title: Primary Cardiac Epithelioid Angiosarcoma with Sustained Remission following Surgical Resection: A Case Report
case_summary:
A 45-year-old woman with iron deficiency anemia and untreated stage I papillary thyroid carcinoma presented with chronic fatigue, night sweats, body aches, and joint pain. Hemoglobin was 6.6 g/dL. She denied chest pain, weight loss, hematochezia, melena, or hematemesis. She had a positive fecal occult blood test and a history of Barrett's esophagus. On hospital day 2, she developed palpitations and was found to be in atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response (heart rate up to 150 bpm), remained hemodynamically stable, and converted to sinus rhythm after intravenous metoprolol. The patient then reported a two-year history of palpitations and occasional syncope, which she had attributed to an experimental immunotherapy she had received. 
CT of the chest showed a 6.6 ? 5.2 cm mass inferior to the heart with a moderate to large circumferential pericardial effusion. Transthoracic echocardiography estimated an ejection fraction of 70%, with no signs of tamponade and identified a

title: Simultaneous Real-Time Intravascular Ultrasound-Guided Transcatheter Pulmonary Embolectomy Improves Procedural Accuracy, Efficacy, and Safety
case_summary:
A 46-year-old man presented with acute onset of dyspnea, chest pain, and near syncope. Three weeks earlier, he had undergone liver transplantation complicated by heart failure requiring an intra-aortic balloon pump, renal failure requiring hemodialysis, and prolonged ventilation requiring tracheostomy. At the time of presentation, his heart failure had resolved with normal biventricular function; he no longer required dialysis and was stable on a tracheostomy collar.

On examination, he was normotensive, tachypneic at 30 breaths/min, tachycardic at 120 beats/min, and had worsened hypoxia. The electrocardiogram was unremarkable.

His past medical history was notable for alcoholic cirrhosis. The differential diagnosis included pulmonary embolism, heart failure, and hospital-acquired pneumonia.

Investigations:
? Computed tomography angiography revealed filling defects in the proximal right pulmonary artery and the main left pulmonary artery, with an RV-to-LV diameter ratio of 1.8:1, cons

title: An unusual presentation of anomalous left coronary artery from the pulmonary artery (ALCAPA) syndrome in a 70-year-old man: a case report
final_diagnosis: Anomalous left coronary artery from pulmonary artery
case_summary:
A 70-year-old Caucasian man presented with moderate dyspnea on exertion (NYHA class II) without chest pain, palpitations, or syncope. His medical history was notable for well-controlled hypertension; he denied diabetes, hypercholesterolemia, smoking, or excess alcohol use. His father died at age 55 of an unspecified heart condition. Current medications included ramipril.

On examination, blood pressure was 140/80 mmHg, pulse 58 beats/min, and BMI 30.8 kg/m2. The lungs were clear, and there were no cardiac murmurs. Mild lower-extremity pitting edema was present. Neurologic and abdominal examinations were unremarkable.

Laboratory tests were within normal limits except for an elevated brain natriuretic peptide level of 1441 pg/ml. Transthoracic echocardiography showed marked concentric left ventricular hypertrophy with a normal cavity size, preserved segmental and global wall motion, a borderline ejection fraction of 55%, left atrial enlargement, and mild mitral regurgitation.

To evalua

title: A case report of severe cardioinhibitory reflex syncope associated with coronavirus disease 2019
case_summary:
A 35-year-old man presented with a 10-day history of syncope occurring each time he changed position from supine to sitting. One day before presentation he developed fever to 39  degreesC but denied cough or dyspnea. He was unable to sit or stand without transient loss of consciousness. In the emergency department, when he assumed an upright seated posture his heart rate initially rose to 110 bpm in sinus rhythm with maintained blood pressure; over the next 2-3 minutes his rate gradually declined to marked sinus bradycardia, at which point he yawned, experienced dizziness and visual blurring, and then lost consciousness. There was no PR-interval prolongation and no AV-conduction abnormality on ECG. His only past medical history was asthma treated with salbutamol; there was no family history of unexplained syncope or sudden cardiac death.

On examination, temperature was 39  degreesC, heart rate 107 bpm, blood pressure, respiratory rate, and oxygen saturation were normal, and bilateral

title: Hydralazine-induced antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody-associated vasculitis with pulmonary-renal syndrome: a case report
case_summary:
A 64-year-old African American woman with hypertension and a known heart murmur presented with 4 days of acute onset shortness of breath, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, and a non-bloody cough productive of clear sputum. She also reported 1 week of fatigue, anorexia, bilateral leg swelling, and lightheadedness. She denied fever, chest pain, recent hospitalizations, new medications, or travel.  
Her home medications included hydralazine, amlodipine, losartan, metoprolol succinate, amiodarone, aspirin, gabapentin, cetirizine, trazodone, zolpidem, cyclobenzaprine, chlorzoxazone, fluticasone, docusate sodium, pantoprazole, ferrous sulfate, ibuprofen, and pravastatin.  
On examination, she was afebrile; blood pressure 158/76 mmHg; heart rate 90/min; oxygen saturation 96% on room air. She had mild respiratory distress, pale conjunctiva, and bilateral pitting lower-extremity edema. Cardiac auscultation revealed a grade 3/6 holosystolic murmur at the apex. Pulmonary examination showed

title: Hampton's Hump: A Notable Radiographic Finding in a Patient with Infectious Endocarditis
case_summary:
A 35-year-old woman presented with three months of progressive exertional dyspnea and bilateral lower extremity edema. She noted a gradual increase in dyspnea to the point of being unable to climb one flight of stairs without resting. She also endorsed a nonproductive cough, pleuritic chest pain, occasional orthopnea, and a 20-lb unintentional weight loss over one month. She denied fevers, chills, night sweats, gastrointestinal symptoms, bleeding, and known risk factors for tuberculosis. She had no past medical history, denied intravenous drug use, and had no history of structural heart disease.

On examination, she was nontoxic, mildly tachycardic (109 bpm), afebrile, with normal blood pressure and respiratory rate. Lung auscultation was clear bilaterally; she spoke in 4-5-word sentences with mild tachypnea. Cardiac auscultation showed tachycardia without murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distention or carotid bruits were present. She had 2+ pitting edema to the midshins bi

title: Thrombus or tumor? a case of fibroelastoma  as indicated during the submission process
case_summary: A 50-year-old woman with a history of mild idiopathic dilated cardiomyopathy (NYHA class III), chronic atrial fibrillation, and hypertension was admitted for worsening heart failure. On examination, her blood pressure and heart rate were normal, and her temperature was 36.5  degreesC. The external jugular veins were distended; there was mild hepatomegaly; and a systolic murmur was heard at the fourth intercostal space along the left sternal border. Laboratory studies were unremarkable, with no elevation in inflammatory markers. A chest radiograph showed an enlarged cardiac silhouette, and the ECG confirmed atrial fibrillation. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed mild dilated cardiomyopathy, severe biventricular dysfunction, and a small (1.3 ? 1.0 cm) mass adherent to the noncoronary cusp of the aortic valve. Because of atrial fibrillation, the mass was initially presumed to be a thrombus, and the patient received thrombolysis and intravenous heparin; however, the lesion did not cha

title:
A life-threatening reverse Takotsubo syndrome in a young breastfeeding woman:
a case report
case_summary:
A 36-year-old woman, 8 months postpartum and still breastfeeding, with no prior medical history, presented to the emergency department with sudden onset of dyspnea, palpitations, and oppressive chest pain radiating to her left shoulder for more than 1 hour. She denied recent emotional stress, infection, or medication use. There was no family history of cardiovascular or autoimmune disease. 

On arrival, she was lethargic, diaphoretic, hypotensive (systolic blood pressure 60 mmHg), and tachycardic (heart rate 145 bpm). Cardiac examination revealed a grade 2/6 apical systolic murmur; pulmonary crackles were present. The remainder of the examination was unremarkable.

Electrocardiogram showed atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, left anterior hemiblock, poor anteroseptal R-wave progression, and diffuse ST-segment depression with normal QTc. The patient underwent electrical cardioversion to sinus rhythm without hemodynamic improvement.

Laboratory tests revealed hemoglobin

title: A failing right heart in an eggshell: A case report of idiopathic constrictive pericarditis
case_summary:
A 73?year?old woman with no significant past medical history presented with 2 weeks of abdominal pain, abdominal bloating, and bilateral lower?extremity edema. She also reported sharp, substernal chest pain radiating to the back intermittently over the past year. She denied gastrointestinal symptoms, fevers, or chills. She emigrated from the Democratic Republic of the Congo 5 years earlier.  
On examination, her temperature was 98.8 degreesF, heart rate 77/min, respiratory rate 16/min, blood pressure 112/88 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 94% on room air. Laboratory studies showed NT?proBNP 2425.9 pg/mL (normal <900), high?sensitivity troponin 11 ng/L (normal <45), and normal TSH.  
Chest radiography demonstrated central pulmonary vascular congestion, bibasilar atelectasis, small to moderate right?sided pleural effusion, small left pleural effusion, and a mildly enlarged cardiac silhouette. CT of the abdomen and pelvis without contrast revealed large?volume ascites, small bilateral pleural

title: A case report of severe pulmonary arterial hypertension after nivolumab, an IgG4 anti-PD1 monoclonal antibody
case_summary:
A 65?year?old man with stage IV gastro?oesophageal junction adenocarcinoma diagnosed 8 months ago presented after a 2?minute witnessed syncope at home. He had received FOLFOX chemotherapy since diagnosis and nivolumab 240 mg every 2 weeks for the past 7? months (12 doses, no prior immune?related adverse effects). Over the preceding 4 weeks, he noted progressive dyspnoea with minimal exertion. 

On admission, vital signs were: heart rate 85 beats/min, blood pressure 128/70 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 90% on 5 L/min supplemental oxygen. Examination revealed clear lungs and bilateral pitting oedema of the legs. 

An electrocardiogram showed a right ventricular strain pattern with new right bundle branch block. High?sensitivity troponin I was negative. B?type natriuretic peptide was elevated at 115 pmol/L (normal < 9.5 pmol/L). A CT pulmonary angiogram showed no pulmonary embolism or lung disease. After diuresis, his oxygen requirement improved to 2 L/min. 

Transthoracic echocardiography

title: A case of slowly progressive malignant pericardial mesothelioma suggesting the involvement of BAP1 loss
case_summary:
A 70-year-old man with atrial fibrillation, chronic kidney disease, chronic heart failure, and hypertension presented with chest tightness. He had a remote smoking history (25 pack-years) and possible low-level asbestos exposure during a brief period of industrial work. Two years earlier, chest radiography had shown cardiac enlargement, and initial pericardial drainage yielded class IIIb cytology without a definitive diagnosis. His symptoms improved transiently but recurred every two weeks, necessitating pericardial drainage approximately every three months.

On examination, he was euvolemic with no signs of heart failure. Laboratory tests showed blood urea nitrogen 29.1 mg/dL, creatinine 1.62 mg/dL, and brain natriuretic peptide 103.7 pg/mL; serum lung tumor markers were negative. Chest radiography and echocardiography confirmed massive pericardial effusion with normal ventricular function. CT and 18F-FDG PET-CT demonstrated small nodular FDG uptake along the pericardium around the ri

title: Premature myocardial infarction presenting with acute pulmonary embolism: a case report
case_summary:
A 33-year-old Caucasian man presented with sudden onset of severe pleuritic chest pain and haemoptysis. He reported progressive dyspnoea and malaise for one month, unresponsive to three courses of antibiotics prescribed for a presumed chest infection. His medical history was notable only for a pyloromyotomy in infancy; he had an 18 pack-year smoking history and no other cardiovascular risk factors or drug use.

On examination, heart rate was 120 bpm, respiratory rate 36 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 90% on room air, and blood pressure was normal. The physical examination was otherwise unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 13.9 g/dL, troponin-I <0.04 ng/mL, creatine kinase 17 U/L, and D-dimer 800 ng/mL. Electrocardiogram demonstrated right ventricular strain. Chest radiograph revealed ill-defined patchy shadowing in the left mid-zone.

Given the clinical history, raised D-dimer, and ECG findings, a computed tomography pulmonary angiogram was performed, showing bilateral pu

title: Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy Associated with High-Grade Atrioventricular Block and Ventricular Fibrillation: A Case Report
case_summary:
A 56-year-old woman with hypertension and obesity presented with 1 day of dyspnea and adynamia. Two days earlier she had experienced intense emotional stress. She denied syncope, chest pain, or palpitations. Her medications included spironolactone, losartan, and amlodipine. On examination, heart rate was 50 beats per minute, blood pressure 140/90 mm Hg, oxygen saturation 97 %, and there were no signs of heart failure.  
Electrocardiography showed second-degree (2:1) atrioventricular block, right bundle-branch block, left anterior fascicular block, biphasic and inverted T waves in multiple leads, QRS duration of 142 ms, and a corrected QT interval of 689 ms. Troponin I was 396 ng/L (normal < 40), and B-type natriuretic peptide was 307 pg/mL (normal < 100). Renal function, electrolytes, and thyroid function tests were normal.  
Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 40 %, with basal hypercontractility and midventricular and apical ballooning. Coro

title: The first post-cardiac injury syndrome reported following transcatheter aortic valve implantation: a case report
case_summary:
A 79-year-old woman with hypertension and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation presented with fatigue, pleuritic chest pain worsened by inspiration, dyspnea at rest, nonproductive cough, and low-grade fever for several days. She had undergone an uncomplicated transcatheter aortic valve implantation (TAVI) 6 months earlier. 

On examination: temperature 38.0? degreesC, heart rate 112 bpm, blood pressure 140/80?mmHg, respiratory rate 22/min, oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Heart and lung sounds were normal; jugular venous pressure was not elevated.

Laboratory studies showed WBC 14.4 ? 10^9/L (3.6-10.5), ESR 86?mm/h (<30), procalcitonin 0.05?ng/mL (<0.5), and CRP 177?mg/L (0-5). Electrocardiogram demonstrated a new left bundle branch block and PR-segment elevation in aVR. Cardiac enzymes were within normal limits: CK 45 U/L (0-170), CK-MB 13 U/L (0-24), troponin-T 15 pg/mL (<14).

Chest X-ray and CT scan revealed only a minimal left pleural effusion. Transesophageal echocardiography showed

title: Thoracic Computed Tomography Scan and Bronchoscopy Appearance of Mounier-Kuhn Syndrome: A Case Report
journal: Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports
case_summary:
A 45-year-old African American man, nonsmoker, with hypertension, iron deficiency anemia, and atrial fibrillation had a 10-year history of recurrent community-acquired pneumonias requiring ICU admission. HIV, cystic fibrosis, tuberculosis, and primary immunodeficiencies had previously been ruled out; family history for similar diseases was negative. He had chronic dry cough and dyspnea (mMRC 3/4) on home oxygen.

He presented with increased productive cough with purulent sputum, worsened dyspnea (mMRC 4/4), and functional decline. Vital signs: T 38.0  degreesC; BP 130/85 mm Hg; HR 100 bpm; RR 18/min; SpO? 93% on room air. He was dyspneic at rest with accessory muscle use. Cardiac exam: regular tachycardia without murmurs. Pulmonary exam: diminished breath sounds bilaterally and tubular murmur at the bases. Digital clubbing was present. No peripheral edema.

Laboratory studies: WBC 7.4 ? 10?/uL; neutrophils 3.17 ? 10?/uL; lymphocytes 2.77 ? 10?/uL; hemoglobin 9 g/dL; hematocrit 34%; CRP

title: An enormous right atrial myxoma highlights the role of echocardiography in heart failure pathways: a case report
case_summary: A 66-year-old woman with a history of hypertension presented with a 4-week history of progressive breathlessness and ankle swelling. Initial measurement of N-terminal pro B-type natriuretic peptide was elevated at 2829 ng/L (normal <125 ng/L). On Day 10 she was assessed by the heart failure service. Vital signs were within normal limits. Physical examination revealed mid-shin oedema, a grade III mid-diastolic murmur, raised jugular venous pressure, and clear lung fields. The ECG showed normal sinus rhythm. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a large, mobile, echo-dense, heterogeneous mass arising from the right atrium, attached to the interatrial septum, and extending into the right ventricle, measuring 90 ? 42 mm. The tricuspid valve was held open by the mass; the basal right ventricular dimension was 43.5 mm and the tricuspid annular dimension was 49.7 mm. The inferior vena cava measured 19.1 mm with minimal respiratory variation. Chest radiography showed a dilated right atri

title: A Rare Case Report of Mid Cavitary Takotsubo: The Role of Magnetic Resonance Imaging
case_summary:
A 69?year?old obese woman with a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia presented with midsternal chest pain radiating to her right arm and right jaw, accompanied by dyspnea. Physical examination was unremarkable. Initial laboratory studies, including a complete blood count, chemistry panel, TSH, and coagulation studies, were within normal limits except for elevated cardiac enzymes: CK 248 IU/L, CK?MB 32.6 ng/mL, and troponin I 1.18 ng/mL (normal <0.04). She was diagnosed with a non-ST?segment elevation myocardial infarction and treated with dual antiplatelet therapy, anticoagulation, a beta blocker, an ACE inhibitor, and a statin. Overnight, her chest pain resolved and cardiac enzymes down?trended.

The next morning, left heart catheterization demonstrated no angiographic evidence of disease in the epicardial coronary arteries, but revealed mid?cavitary akinesis with apical and basal hyperkinesis and an ejection fraction of 37%, along with a mildly elevated left ventricular end?di

title: Chronic myelomonocytic leukemia primarily presenting as life?threatening pericardial effusion, Eldoret, Kenya: A case report
case_summary:
An 82-year-old man presented with a 4-week history of progressive dry cough, dyspnea on mild exertion, bilateral lower-limb swelling, abdominal distension, early satiety, and unintentional weight loss. He had intermittent dry cough since June 2021 without orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, fevers, or prior edema. He denied known chronic illnesses. 

On examination, he had bilateral non-tender pitting edema to the knees. Vital signs: blood pressure 120/70 mm Hg, pulse 79 bpm, respiratory rate 18 bpm, temperature 36.8  degreesC, and oxygen saturation 75% on room air (improved to 96% on 7 L oxygen via non-rebreather mask). Cardiovascular exam revealed warm extremities, normal pulses, muffled heart sounds without murmurs. Abdominal exam showed distension, hepatomegaly 4 cm below the costal margin, and shifting dullness; spleen was not palpable. Respiratory and neurologic exams were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies revealed WBC 75.32 ? 10^9/L (neutrophils 67.6%, monocytes 24.0%, lymp

title: Percutaneous coil embolization of a post-traumatic left anterior descending coronary artery pseudoaneurysm: a case report
case_summary:
A 78-year-old man with triple-vessel coronary disease status post coronary-artery bypass grafting (LIMA to mid-LAD, SVG to first diagonal, and SVG to second obtuse marginal), heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, and hypertension was admitted after a mechanical fall complicated by left chest pain. On examination, he had a hematoma and swelling of the left chest wall but denied cardiac pain or dyspnea. An initial chest CT angiogram was unremarkable except for an incidental abdominal aortic aneurysm. 

Fourteen days later, he developed moderate, pressure-like chest pain and dyspnea. He was mildly tachycardic without hypoxia. ECG showed normal sinus rhythm with frequent premature atrial complexes. Laboratory studies revealed a high-sensitivity troponin I level of 140 ng/mL (reference 0-12 ng/mL) and a BNP of 3363 pg/mL (reference 0-450 pg/mL). A pulmonary embolism was suspected; a repeat chest CTA was negative for PE but demonstrated a new hyperattenuating structure in the left

title: Acute Amiodarone Pulmonary Toxicity after Drug Holiday: A Case Report and Review of the Literature
case_summary:
A 57-year-old Caucasian man with hypertension and sick sinus syndrome had a pacemaker placed 8 months ago. One month later, he developed recurrent atrial fibrillation and was started on amiodarone 400 mg daily, metoprolol, and oral anticoagulation. After maintaining sinus rhythm for 6 months, amiodarone was discontinued. Three weeks later, he presented with a 2-day history of right lateral sharp chest pain and worsening shortness of breath. He denied fever, chills, weight loss, palpitations, or leg swelling and had a remote tobacco history. 

On examination, he appeared ill and tachypneic (respiratory rate 28 breaths/min), afebrile, blood pressure 115/74 mmHg, heart rate 60 bpm, and oxygen saturation 86% on room air. Cardiac auscultation was normal. Breath sounds were diminished at both lung bases. The abdomen was soft, and the legs showed no edema or tenderness. There was no rash, clubbing, or lymphadenopathy.

Laboratory studies showed leukocytosis with neutrophilia and elevated infl

title: Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Churg-Strauss syndrome) masquerading as acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction with complete resolution after immunosuppressive therapy: a case report
case_summary: A 42-year-old man presented with 4 hours of central chest pain radiating to his left shoulder, associated with nausea. His history included childhood asthma (not requiring inhalers) and 6 months of progressive nasal congestion and anosmia; he was a non-smoker with no family history of premature cardiovascular disease. On examination, his voice was hyponasal; blood pressure was 126/74 mmHg, pulse rate 85 b.p.m., jugular venous pressure was normal, and there was no peripheral edema. Cardiac auscultation revealed normal heart sounds and lung bases were clear. An electrocardiogram showed ST-segment elevation in the inferior leads with reciprocal ST-segment depression laterally. Bedside transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated inferior and apical hypokinesia with preserved left ventricular function. Cardiac troponin I was >39 000 ng/L (normal <16 ng/L). Laboratory tests showed a white-cell count of 9.37?10?/L with eosinophils of 3.13?10?/L (normal 0-0.4?10?/L), a C-reactive protein level

title: Repetitive out of hospital cardiac arrests following pregnancy: a case report of an unfortunate presentation of mitral annular disjunction
case_summary:
A 29-year-old woman was referred at two weeks' gestation in her second pregnancy for evaluation after surviving an out-of-hospital cardiac arrest four months after delivering her first child. At that time, she underwent implantation of an ICD for secondary prevention following a working diagnosis of peripartum or dilated cardiomyopathy versus primary electrical disease. Initial transthoracic echocardiography and cardiac MRI revealed only mild myxomatous mitral valve prolapse with mild regurgitation, a mildly reduced left ventricular ejection fraction, and no other abnormalities. 

When seen in our center, she was asymptomatic. Blood pressure was 112/76?mmHg, oxygen saturation 98%, and an apical mild systolic murmur was noted without signs of heart failure. A 12-lead ECG demonstrated sinus rhythm with frequent premature ventricular complexes showing a right bundle-branch block morphology. Exercise testing reproduced regular PVCs without ischemic changes and yielded 81% of predicted work

title: Differential diagnosis and treatment of wide QRS tachycardia in an Ebstein anomaly patient
case_summary:
A 20-year-old man with a known diagnosis of asymptomatic Ebstein anomaly presented to the emergency department with palpitations and dizziness. On examination, he had skin pallor, cyanosis, diaphoresis, and a heart rate of 188 beats per minute. An electrocardiogram showed a wide QRS complex tachycardia, and the patient underwent immediate electrical cardioversion. After restoration of sinus rhythm, the ECG demonstrated an intraventricular conduction delay.

Transthoracic echocardiography revealed apical displacement of the septal and posterior tricuspid leaflets consistent with Ebstein anomaly, severe tricuspid regurgitation, "atrialization" of a large portion of the right ventricle, and right ventricular dysfunction. Coronary computed tomographic angiography showed no coronary obstruction. Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging confirmed a septal tricuspid leaflet apical displacement of 33 mm relative to the mitral valve plane, elongated and redundant tricuspid leaflets leading to severe

title: Critical role of cardiac magnetic resonance in the diagnosis of left-dominant arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy: A paradigmatic case in a recreational middle-aged athlete
case_summary:
A 54-year-old recreational male trail runner (?5 hours per week) presented with several months of palpitations at rest and during exercise. He denied chest pain, dyspnea, dizziness, syncope, stimulant use (including caffeine), illicit drugs, supplements, or recent infection. His medical history was unremarkable; he had no hypertension, diabetes, or smoking history. Family history included a mother who died suddenly at 72 (no autopsy) and a father who died at 60 of chronic illness. 

On examination, blood pressure was 124/81 mm Hg, heart rate 66 beats/min; cardiac and pulmonary exams were normal. Laboratory tests were within normal limits, with no elevation of creatine kinase or troponin. A 12-lead ECG showed sinus rhythm, normal atrioventricular and intraventricular conduction, and low QRS voltage (<0.5 mV in limb leads). 

Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated nondilated ventricles and atria, preserved systolic and diastolic function, and no valvular disease. On exercise stress

title: Chilaiditi syndrome: A structural displacement in a heart failure patient
case_summary: A 79-year-old man with a history of atrial fibrillation (on aspirin), type 2 diabetes mellitus, well-controlled asthma, and hypertension, who tested positive for COVID-19 one month earlier, presented with progressive bilateral lower-extremity edema and dyspnea over one month. He reported two-pillow orthopnea but denied chest pain, fever, cough, or gastrointestinal symptoms. On examination, vital signs were normal; there were bibasilar crackles, jugular venous distention to 9 cm, and 2+ pitting edema of the legs. Laboratory evaluation showed a pro-B-type natriuretic peptide level of 8458 pg/mL (previously 3301 pg/mL) and an unremarkable D-dimer. A chest radiograph demonstrated bilateral ground-glass opacities, pulmonary congestion, and interposition of the right hepatic flexure of the colon between the subdiaphragmatic space and the right dome of the liver (Chilaiditi's sign), which was not present on a radiograph one month earlier. Electrocardiography showed atrial fibrillation without

title: When the heart and tumours intertwine: pulmonary hypertension associated with a neuroendocrine tumour--a case report
case_summary:
A 38-year-old man with no cardiovascular risk factors was referred for evaluation of progressive dyspnoea and pulmonary hypertension. Twelve months earlier he noted exertional dyspnoea that gradually worsened until he could walk <100 m. On admission he was normotensive, heart rate 76 bpm, O? saturation normal on room air. Examination revealed jugular venous distension, hepatomegaly, and bilateral lower?limb oedema.  

An ECG showed sinus rhythm, borderline right?axis deviation (+90 degrees), and an S1Q3T3 pattern. Twelve months prior he had a severe pleural and pericardial effusion at the referring hospital; a pericardial window and biopsy yielded haematic fluid with lymphomononuclear cells and inflammatory tissue without malignancy. The initial differential diagnosis included heart failure with pulmonary hypertension, biventricular dysfunction, and chronic pulmonary thromboembolism.  

During this admission, repeat ECG showed complete right bundle?branch block. Transthoracic echocardi

title: Subclavian Artery Perforation and Mediastinal Hematoma Following Transradial Percutaneous Coronary?Intervention
case_summary:
A 69-year-old man presented with a 1-day history of chest pain. He was diagnosed with a non-ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction on the basis of elevated high-sensitivity troponin T levels and was referred for urgent invasive coronary angiography via a right transradial approach. Significant tortuosity of the subclavian artery necessitated use of a hydrophilic guidewire to advance the catheter into the aortic root, and a heavily calcified ostial obtuse marginal lesion was successfully stented.

Immediately postprocedure, he developed marked hypotension (70/20 mm Hg in the right arm; 95/40 mm Hg in the left arm), tachycardia to 120 beats/min, diaphoresis, and dull central chest discomfort. He remained alert and oriented. Despite 2,000 mL of intravenous 0.9% saline, hypotension persisted, although vasopressors were not administered.

His preprocedure hemoglobin was 102 g/L, which fell to 81 g/L postprocedure. A 12-lead ECG showed sinus tachycardia with normal QRS voltages and no ST

title: Cardiac sarcoidosis with severe involvement of the right ventricle: a case report
case_summary:
A 60-year-old woman presented with exertional dyspnea, with mild limitation during ordinary physical activities. Electrocardiography showed a complete left bundle branch block and sinus tachycardia. Doppler echocardiography revealed a severely dilated left ventricle (61 mm in systole and 71 mm in diastole), a mildly dilated left atrium (40 mm), and an ejection fraction of 30%. Cardiac catheterization confirmed left ventricular dilatation and normal coronary arteries. Chagas disease serology was negative. She was started on carvedilol, losartan, and furosemide, with clinical improvement.  

Her history was notable for cholecystectomy for biliary lithiasis complicated by pancreatitis, hypothyroidism treated with levothyroxine, and no smoking or alcohol use. Two siblings had dilated cardiomyopathy, and a brother died suddenly in adolescence.  

One year after symptom onset, she was admitted to the ICU with cardiogenic shock and treated with dobutamine. Following discharge, she underwent c

title: A giant and double-walled left atrial ball thrombus complicating a mitral stenosis: case report--a truck tire into the heart
case_summary:
A 75-year-old woman with a history of mild rheumatic mitral stenosis not followed for 20 years and not on anticoagulation presented to the emergency department with near-syncope followed by acute dyspnea. Shortly after arrival, she developed haemodynamic instability with cardiogenic shock (cold and wet profile, SCAI stage C) and pulmonary oedema on chest X-ray. ECG showed atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response. Immediate electrical cardioversion restored sinus rhythm. Arterial blood gas analysis revealed severe metabolic acidosis (pH 7.04), lactate 16?mg/dL, and pO2 50?mmHg. Despite IV diuretics, she remained unstable and was intubated, mechanically ventilated, and started on norepinephrine (0.5?ug/kg/min) and dobutamine (0.5?ug/kg/min).

Urgent transthoracic echocardiography showed:  
? Severely reduced LVEF (15%) with diffuse hypokinesia.  
? Normal-sized right ventricle with severe systolic dysfunction, severe tricuspid regurgitation, and pulmonary systolic pressure of

title: Cardiac left ventricular thrombus in protein C deficiency
journal: Nigerian Medical Journal : Journal of the Nigeria Medical Association
case_summary: A 33-year-old woman presented with severe dyspnea. She had no history of thromboembolic events. On admission, her left ventricular ejection fraction was 60%. Vital signs showed a heart rate of 95 bpm and blood pressure of 110/70 mmHg. Cardiovascular and pulmonary examinations were unremarkable. Initial blood tests including complete blood count, electrolytes, liver function tests, and cardiac biomarkers were within normal limits. The electrocardiogram demonstrated sinus tachycardia. Chest radiography showed normal cardiac silhouette without pulmonary venous congestion. Transesophageal echocardiography revealed a large, rounded mass attached to the anterior wall of the left ventricle, consistent with a mural thrombus. Coronary angiography showed no obstructions. The patient was started on parenteral heparin.

title: Atrioventricular nodal reentrant tachycardia and the dilemma of reentry circuit components: A proof of concept
case_summary:
A 42-year-old woman was referred for an electrophysiological study because of recurrent palpitations refractory to beta-blockers. She reported palpitations since adolescence, frequently terminated by a 6 mg adenosine bolus. She had no other comorbidities. A 12-lead ECG during symptoms showed a narrow-complex supraventricular tachycardia. Resting ECG in sinus rhythm, transthoracic echocardiography (normal left ventricular function), and 24-hour Holter monitoring (no sustained arrhythmias) were unremarkable.

Under conscious sedation, catheters were placed in the right atrium/right ventricle, at the His bundle, and in the coronary sinus. Incremental ventricular pacing revealed 1:1 ventriculoatrial conduction (earliest activation in proximal coronary sinus) down to a cycle length of 600 ms. Incremental atrial pacing showed an atrioventricular Wenckebach point at a cycle length of 380 ms. There was no pre-excitation on the surface ECG, and the HV interval was normal.

Programmed atrial sti

title: Pulmonary embolism in an ESRD patient following minimal venotomy and milking for salvage of dysfunctional autogenous arteriovenous fistula
case_summary:
A 41-year-old woman with end-stage renal disease secondary to long-standing hypertension has been on maintenance hemodialysis via a left brachiocephalic arteriovenous fistula (AVF) for 6 months. She presented with 6 hours of dizziness, 1 day of shortness of breath, and 2 weeks of chest pain with orthopnea and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea. She last dialyzed 2 days earlier. There was no trauma to the AVF.

On examination, temperature was normal. Heart rate was 46/min, blood pressure 60/40 mm Hg, respiratory rate 16/min, and oxygen saturation 86% on room air. She was drowsy but oriented. Jugular venous distension was present; heart sounds were muffled. There was no bruit over the AVF.

Initial investigations showed ECG with a prolonged PR interval and tall T waves. Serum potassium was 6.6 mmol/L and creatinine 10.2 mg/dL. Cardiac enzymes were not elevated. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed moderate pericardial effusion without tamponade, dilated inferior vena cava, normal left ventr

title:
The rising appendicitis mimicker: A case report of Multisystem
Inflammatory Syndrome in Children presenting with acute abdomen
case_summary:
A previously healthy 12-year-old boy presented with 1 day of progressive right lower quadrant abdominal pain and fever to 40 degreesC. Three days before admission, he had vomiting and persistent fever of 38 degreesC treated with antipyretics and antibiotics by a pharmacist. He was taken to surgery without prior imaging or laboratory studies and underwent an open appendectomy; the appendix appeared normal macroscopically.

Two days after surgery, a COVID-19 RT-PCR test was negative. On postoperative day 5, because of unremitting fever and new conjunctivitis, wound and blood cultures were obtained, and broad-spectrum antibiotics were started; the wound culture grew Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and blood cultures were negative. On postoperative day 10, he developed diffuse abdominal pain. An abdominopelvic CT scan with contrast showed a few enlarged mesenteric lymph nodes, minimal free fluid in the right iliac fossa, and a liver edge enlarged to 15 cm. A rapid SARS-CoV-2 test was positive for

title: Reversible hypertension associated with complete heart block in a 6-year-old boy
case_summary:
A previously healthy 6-year-old boy was transferred for evaluation of incidentally discovered hypertension and bradycardia during workup for a 1-week history of abdominal pain, cough, congestion, frontal headache, and decreased appetite. He had received a 2-day course of prednisolone 4 days earlier, which was discontinued when hypertension was noted. There was no use of over-the-counter decongestants. His perinatal course was notable only for transient tachypnea of the newborn. He had undergone multiple corrections for bilateral club feet and reportedly slept more than peers. Review of prior records showed mild hypertension and low heart rate since age 4? years. Family history included early-onset hypertension in his father (age 29) and premature cardiac deaths on the maternal side; no renal or thyroid disease was reported.

On examination, he was alert and afebrile. Heart rate was 54 beats per minute, blood pressure was 160/91 mmHg, respiratory rate 24 breaths per minute, and oxygen s

title: Subaortic Membrane Papillary Fibroelastoma
case_summary: A 61-year-old man underwent a routine health maintenance examination. His medical history was notable for type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and IgM monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance. He reported no cardiovascular or systemic symptoms and exercised regularly. Vital signs were blood pressure 139/81 mm Hg and heart rate 70 beats per minute with a regular rhythm. Cardiac auscultation revealed no murmurs, gallops, or rubs. The remainder of the physical examination was unremarkable. A baseline electrocardiogram showed minor nonspecific abnormalities. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated normal left ventricular size and function but incidentally identified a subaortic membrane with a highly mobile mass arising from it. Transesophageal echocardiography confirmed a 9 mm by 3 mm mobile mass on the subaortic membrane within the left ventricular outflow tract without significant obstruction. A preoperative cardiac computed tomography angiogram showed o

title: Calcified amorphous tumor of the heart with mitral annular calcification: a case report
case_summary: A 70-year-old Asian man with end-stage renal disease on hemodialysis for 20 years presented with chest discomfort during exercise. He had hypertension, hyperuricemia, and reflux esophagitis. He was afebrile, blood pressure 114/50 mmHg, pulse 61/min, and oxygen saturation 96% on ambient air. Examination revealed no rales, murmurs, or focal neurologic deficits. Laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 14.0 mg/dL, WBC 4700/mm?, platelets 134 000/mm?, total bilirubin 0.4 mg/dL, AST 24 IU/L, ALT 34 IU/L, BUN 54.2 mg/dL, creatinine 11.1 mg/dL, CRP 0.16 mg/dL, normal serum calcium and phosphorus, and BNP 605.1 pg/mL. Blood cultures were negative. Chest radiograph demonstrated mild cardiomegaly without pulmonary congestion. ECG showed left ventricular hypertrophy with nonspecific ST-T changes. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed severe mitral annular calcification and a highly mobile, 8?6 mm echogenic mass attached to the ventricular side of the posterior mitral leaflet, with similar echogenicit

title: A case of vaccine?associated myocarditis following pneumococcal immunization leading to acute mitral regurgitation
case_summary:
A 69-year-old woman with a history of hyperlipidaemia, local reactions after influenza vaccination, and pollen allergy presented with dyspnoea 2 days after receiving a 23-valent pneumococcal polysaccharide vaccine (PPSV23). One day after vaccination she developed painful erythema and oedema at the injection site and fever, which progressed to exertional dyspnoea. Pre-admission medications included rosuvastatin and tranexamic acid without recent changes.  
On admission, temperature 39.7  degreesC, blood pressure 130/67 mmHg, heart rate 82 b.p.m., and oxygen saturation 89% on room air. Examination showed a swollen, warm left upper arm with a painful rash extending beyond the injection site. Chest radiography and portable echocardiography were unremarkable.  
Laboratory studies revealed leukocytosis (19 200/?L) with eosinophilia (8.5%; absolute count 1632/?L), C-reactive protein 15.5 mg/dL, procalcitonin 3.1 ng/mL, total protein 5.9 g/dL, and albumin 2.5 g/dL. She was treated empirically

title: Idiopathic Clubbing Confined to Lower Limb Digits and Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis: An Unusual Association
case_summary:
? A 62-year-old housewife presented with a four-month history of breathlessness on exertion. She denied drug or environmental inhalational exposures, chest pain, palpitations, ankle swelling, syncope, gastrointestinal symptoms, smoking, or alcohol use.  
? Past medical records revealed no previous clubbing or major illness. Menopause at age 45; two full-term vaginal deliveries; no family history of clubbing.  
? On examination: pulse 80/min, regular; respiratory rate 15/min; blood pressure 130/80 mm Hg; afebrile; SpO? 98-100% on room air. Bulbous enlargement of all toes bilaterally; fingernails unaffected. No swelling or skin changes at wrists or ankles. Chest auscultation disclosed bilateral fine end-inspiratory crepitations at the lung bases.  
? Laboratory tests: hemoglobin 12.5 g/dL; WBC 9400/mm? (61% neutrophils); ESR 12 mm/h; normal serum chemistries; thyroid and liver function normal; ANA and rheumatoid factor negative; HIV negative.  
? Microbiology: sputum AFB smear negative;

title: Atrial Giant Cell Myocarditis as a Cause?of?Heart Failure
case_summary: A 79-year-old woman presented with four weeks of exertional dyspnea, palpitations, a 4-kg weight loss, night sweats, and low appetite. She was afebrile; blood pressure was 120/70 mm Hg, and heart rate was 126 beats/min. Electrocardiography showed newly diagnosed persistent atrial fibrillation. Her past medical history was unremarkable, with no recent infections and up-to-date cancer screening. N-terminal pro-B-type natriuretic peptide was elevated at 3063 pg/mL. There was no leukocytosis, and C-reactive protein, procalcitonin, interleukin-6, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate were normal. An extensive infective work-up, including IgM and IgG for myocarditis-relevant viruses, and a rheumatologic panel were negative. Thoracic and abdominal CT, gastroscopy, and colonoscopy ruled out malignancy. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a 7-mm pericardial effusion, biatrial dilatation, and atrial free-wall thickening (6 mm bilaterally). Transesophageal echocardiography confirmed marked thick

title: Aortic dissection associated with blunt chest trauma diagnosed by elevated D-dimer
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 61-year-old previously healthy man presented to the emergency department with chest discomfort and shortness of breath one day after being struck in the chest by a snowboarder while skiing. He initially experienced left-sided sternal pain that resolved, then developed a "hollow sensation" in his chest worsened by deep inspiration and mild exertional dyspnea. He denied nausea, vomiting, diaphoresis, back pain, fever, or neurologic symptoms. He had no past medical history, took no medications, and had no family history of cardiac or connective tissue disease.

On examination: blood pressure 100/80 mm Hg, heart rate 70 beats/min, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 100% on room air, temperature 37.6  degreesC. Heart sounds were normal without murmurs; lungs were clear; there was no tenderness to the sternum or ribs and no external chest trauma; the abdomen was soft and non-tender; a small hematoma was noted on the left calf; neurologic exam was normal.

An electrocardiogr

title: A Tongue Lesion as a Sign of a Systemic Disease
case_summary:
A 77-year-old woman with hypertension, gastroesophageal reflux disease, hypercholesterolemia, and a remote episode of pericarditis presented in February 2014 with progressive dyspnea, bilateral lower?extremity edema, and a painful lesion on the left side of her tongue. Over the preceding months, she had developed generalized fatigue, declining ability to perform daily activities, lower?extremity edema, and transient red bullae on her lips that resolved spontaneously. She was evaluated by a rheumatologist for nonspecific arthritis; electromyography showed bilateral carpal?tunnel syndrome and right ulnar?nerve injury. Laboratory tests were notable for positive antinuclear antibodies and anti-double?stranded DNA antibodies, and she was started on hydroxychloroquine and prednisolone. She later developed anemia and was referred to a hematologist. 

When the tongue lesion worsened and interfered with eating, she was brought to the emergency department. On examination, she was obese and in re

title: PET-Driven, Voltage-Guided Atrial Endomyocardial Biopsy Clinches the Diagnosis of Cardiac Sarcoidosis
case_summary:
A 57?year?old woman was referred for evaluation of progressive dyspnea on minimal exertion. She had been treated for pneumonia that precipitated an exacerbation of heart failure; after stabilization, attention turned to identifying the cause of her nonischemic cardiomyopathy.

Past medical history included a transient ischemic attack in 2013, chronic kidney disease, and persistent atrial fibrillation. In 2019, she suffered cardiac arrest from ventricular tachycardia and fibrillation, required multiple defibrillations, was found to be in complete heart block, and underwent dual?chamber implantable cardioverter?defibrillator placement. Her family history was unremarkable.

One month earlier, coronary computed tomography angiography showed no significant coronary stenosis. On this admission, transthoracic echocardiography revealed an ejection fraction of 30%. Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging could not be performed because of her non-MRI?compatible device.

title: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy and coronary artery disease: value of cardiac magnetic resonance imaging for diagnostic confirmation: a case report
case_summary: A 65-year-old post-menopausal woman with essential hypertension and a history of inferior-wall myocardial infarction treated 5 years earlier with primary angioplasty and two drug-eluting stents underwent uneventful sigmoid colon tumour resection. Approximately one hour postoperatively, she developed chest pain. Electrocardiography showed ST-segment elevation in inferior and anterolateral leads. Vital signs were blood pressure 112/63 mmHg, heart rate 108 bpm, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Cardiac examination was unremarkable, and lung auscultation was clear. Laboratory testing revealed a peak troponin-I of 657 ng/mL (normal <14 ng/mL), total creatine phosphokinase 450 U/L (normal <192 U/L), and CK-MB mass 20.45 ng/mL (normal <3.61 ng/mL). Postoperative haemoglobin was 12.3 g/dL. She received aspirin, clopidogrel, atorvastatin, bisoprolol, opioids, and intravenous nitroglycerine, with resolution of pain and ST-segment elevation. The next day, B-type natriuretic peptide was eleva

title: Ebstein Anomaly With Right Atrial Clot
case_summary: A 17-year-old woman presented with progressive exertional dyspnea, worsening fatigue, and bilateral pedal edema over several months. She denied chest pain or syncope. On examination, she was hemodynamically stable (blood pressure 136/75 mm Hg), in atrial fibrillation with an average ventricular rate of 68 bpm, and had elevated jugular venous pressure. Auscultation revealed a variably split first heart sound and normal inspiratory splitting of S2; there were no murmurs. Laboratory evaluation, including a thrombophilia panel, was unremarkable. Lower-limb venous Doppler ultrasonography showed no deep vein thrombosis. A 12-lead electrocardiogram confirmed atrial fibrillation at a rate of 81 bpm. Chest radiography demonstrated cardiomegaly (cardiothoracic ratio 0.70) with right atrial enlargement. Transthoracic echocardiography disclosed a massively dilated right atrium, an "atrialized" right ventricle with small functional chamber, apical displacement of the septal tricuspid leaflet by 2.3

title: Non-ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction as the Initial Manifestation of Calreticulin-Positive Essential Thrombocythemia: A Case Report
journal: Journal of Cardiovascular Development and Disease
case_summary:
A 62-year-old man, lifelong nonsmoker with no history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, diabetes, or coronary artery disease, presented with a 6-week history of episodic midsternal "burning" chest pain radiating intermittently to his back and associated with dysphagia, abdominal bloating, and palpitations. The pain initially responded to antacids but became persistent and unresponsive. On the day of admission, his prolonged chest discomfort resolved before arrival. 

On presentation, blood pressure was 138/89 mm Hg, heart rate 68 bpm, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. An electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm with lateral ST-segment and T-wave abnormalities consistent with a non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction. Serial high-sensitivity troponin T levels rose from 84 ng/L to a peak of 123 ng/L before trending to 104 ng/L. Physical examination was unremarkable. 

A chest radiograph was normal. Transthoracic echocardiography showed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 60% with no

title: Unicuspid aortic valve replacement with development of complete heart block: a case report
case_summary:
A 46-year-old Caucasian man presented with a 7-month history of exertional dyspnoea, fatigue, and occasional palpitations. He had no known medical history and had not seen a physician since age 16. On examination, his blood pressure was 135/95 mm Hg, and heart rate was 75 bpm. Cardiac examination revealed a grade II/IV systolic ejection murmur, delayed peripheral pulse upstroke, a laterally displaced apical impulse, and faint bibasilar crackles. 

Electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm at 90 bpm, right bundle branch block with QRS duration of 126 ms, and no voltage criteria for left ventricular hypertrophy. Transthoracic echocardiography was interpreted as bicuspid aortic valve with mild left ventricular hypertrophy, a preserved ejection fraction of 61%, a peak/mean transvalvular gradient of 42.5/24.1 mm Hg, an aortic valve area of 1.1 cm2 (index 0.56 cm2/m2), and mild to moderate aortic regurgitation (pressure half time 547 ms). Two-dimensional imaging also noted an aortic root diamete

title: Severe Aortic Valvular Incompetence From IgG4-Related Disease
case_summary: A 53-year-old woman presented with a 6-month history of dyspnea, palpitations, and fatigue. She was afebrile and hemodynamically stable (blood pressure 134/76 mm Hg; pulse 89 beats/min; respiratory rate 19 breaths/min; oxygen saturation 98% on room air; temperature 36.8  degreesC). Cardiac examination revealed an early diastolic murmur at the left sternal border. Electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm with first-degree atrioventricular block. Laboratory studies demonstrated mild anemia (hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL), an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (35 mm/h), and an elevated C-reactive protein level (9 mg/dL); white blood cell count, renal function, and liver function tests were normal. Blood cultures were sterile. Transthoracic echocardiography showed severe aortic regurgitation involving an abnormal noncoronary cusp of a trileaflet valve, with preserved left ventricular function. Transesophageal echocardiography confirmed an echogenic mass at the base of the noncoronary cusp with s

title: Pulmonary Hypertension due to a Pulmonary Artery Leiomyosarcoma: A Case Report
case_summary:
A 64-year-old woman presented with 6 months of progressive generalized weakness and dyspnea. She had no risk factors for thromboembolism. Her history was notable for headache, weight loss, nonproductive cough, and one presyncope episode the previous year.

Vital signs on admission: temperature 38.0 degreesC; blood pressure 120/70 mmHg; pulse rate 90/min; respiratory rate 24/min; oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Arterial blood gas showed PaO? 26 mmHg and PaCO? 40 mmHg. On examination, there was no evidence of deep-vein thrombosis. Cardiac auscultation revealed a grade III/VI systolic murmur at the lower left sternal border.

Laboratory studies showed normal serum CA-125, CA-99, and CEA levels. Electrocardiography was unremarkable.

Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated severe pulmonary hypertension (estimated pulmonary artery pressure 120 mmHg), severe right ventricular hypertrophy and dysfunction, moderate to severe tricuspid regurgitation, and a large nonhomogeneous mass occup

title: A case of incessant bundle branch reentry tachycardia occurring after phase 3 right bundle branch block as a first manifestation of left ventricular noncompaction cardiomyopathy in a patient with bicuspid aortic valve
case_summary:
A 49-year-old man presented with acute chest pain and palpitations. He had a history of congenital aortic stenosis but no prior records, and no family history of sudden death or syncope. On examination he was diaphoretic, with a radial pulse of 240 beats per minute, blood pressure 80/-- mm Hg, and a mild systolic murmur at the left second intercostal space; the remainder of the exam was unremarkable.

A 12-lead ECG showed a broad-complex tachycardia with right bundle branch block morphology, a right superior ("north-west") axis, and negative QRS complexes in leads V2 through V6. Intravenous adenosine was administered three times without change in the rhythm. Multiple attempts at electrical cardioversion restored sinus rhythm transiently, but the tachycardia reinitiated within a few beats; between attempts, the ECG demonstrated diffuse ST-segment depressions and ST elevations in leads V1 and aVR. Intravenous amiodarone (600 mg) followed by two 200-mg oral doses successfully suppressed t

title: Cardiac amyloidosis mimicking acute coronary syndrome: a case report and literature review
case_summary: A 72-year-old man presented with a 2-day history of chest pain localized near the sternum. Over the preceding month he had developed progressive exertional dyspnoea but remained well at rest, with no fever or peripheral oedema. He had no history of coronary artery disease, hypertension, dyslipidaemia, or other comorbidities. On arrival, vital signs were stable (blood pressure 110/65?mmHg; pulse 70?b.p.m.; respiratory rate 20?breaths/min). Cardiac and pulmonary examinations were unremarkable. An electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm, low?amplitude QRS complexes in the limb leads, and a right bundle branch block. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed concentric left ventricular thickening, biatrial enlargement, a small pericardial effusion adjacent to the right atrium, and a preserved ejection fraction with predominant basal hypokinesis. Serial measurements of high?sensitivity cardiac troponin T were elevated (388?pg/mL on admission and 467?pg/mL at 3?h; normal ?24.9?pg/mL), whereas cr

title: Spontaneous coronary artery dissection masquerading as Takotsubo cardiomyopathy: a case report
case_summary:
A 66-year-old woman with hypertension, hyperlipidaemia, and migraines but no known coronary artery disease presented with sudden onset of headache and left jaw pain that progressed to central chest pressure with left arm numbness. She denied nausea or diaphoresis and had no prior similar episodes. She lived independently and had no recent infections or travel; she was a non-smoker with a negative family history of premature cardiovascular disease.  

On arrival, her temperature was 97 degreesF, heart rate 90 bpm, blood pressure 133/81 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. The cardiovascular and pulmonary examinations were unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed a D-dimer of 394 ng/mL (normal <500) and a Troponin-T of 0.33 ng/mL (normal <0.01). The initial ECG demonstrated normal sinus rhythm with 1 mm ST-segment elevations in leads V1, V2, and aVR.  

She received aspirin, high-intensity statin therapy, sublingual nitroglycerine--which relieved her chest

title: A young female presenting with heart failure secondary to eosinophilic myocarditis: a case report and review of the literature
case_summary:
A 21-year-old previously healthy Sri Lankan woman was admitted with a 1-week history of progressive dyspnea, initially on exertion and later at rest, accompanied by low-grade fever, malaise, and profuse sweating. She denied weight loss, rash, allergies, recent drug exposure, or systemic symptoms. She had no cardiovascular risk factors, no history of allergy, asthma, or connective tissue disease, and no recent medication use.

On examination, temperature was mildly elevated, heart rate 120 bpm, blood pressure 100/70 mmHg, and respiratory rate elevated. She was dyspneic at rest, with jugular venous pressure of 5 cm above the sternal angle and no Kussmaul sign. Cardiac auscultation revealed slightly muffled heart sounds with a gallop rhythm. Lung examination showed bilateral basal fine inspiratory crackles. She had mild, non-tender hepatomegaly and splenomegaly. There was no peripheral edema, rash, or lymphadenopathy.

Laboratory studies showed leukocytosis with an absolute eosinophil cou

title: An Unusual and Severe Thyrotoxicosis in a Twin Pregnancy: Fortune Favors the Brave
case_summary: A 24-year-old woman, gravida 2 para 1, at 15 weeks of a dichorionic twin pregnancy presented with weakness, repeated vomiting, and inability to tolerate oral intake. Since early pregnancy, she had severe nausea managed with doxylamine/pyridoxine and metoclopramide. On examination, she was mildly agitated with a temperature of 37.5 degreesC, heart rate 120 bpm, blood pressure 115/80 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation 97% on room air; no tremors were noted. An electrocardiogram showed sinus tachycardia at 104 bpm, ST-segment elevation in the inferolateral leads, and a prolonged QTc interval (603 ms). Laboratory studies revealed severe hypokalemia (K+ 1.9 mmol/L) and hyponatremia (Na+ 118 mmol/L), which were corrected promptly. Cardiac enzymes were elevated (troponin T 9082 ng/L, CK-MB 420 ug/L). Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated inferior wall hypokinesia with an ejection fraction of 55%. Over the next day, she developed sustained ventricular tachycardia requiring defibrillation, pers

title: Acute Myocardial Infarction Complicating Active Ulcerative Colitis: A Case Report
case_summary:
A 27-year-old Afro-Caribbean man with a 5-year history of ulcerative colitis was admitted with a 1-week history of intermittent stabbing chest pain radiating to the neck and left arm, which worsened over the previous 24 hours. He reported no dyspnea or pleuritic component. Two and a half weeks earlier, during a flare of bloody diarrhea and malaise, his therapy was changed from steroids to azathioprine after a normal thiopurine?methyltransferase assay; he was also taking mesalazine. He denied smoking, hypertension, dyslipidemia, diabetes, illicit drug use, and had no family history of coronary artery disease.

On examination he was afebrile; blood pressure 110/70 mm Hg; heart rate 80 bpm; respiratory rate 18 breaths/min; oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Heart sounds were normal; jugular venous pressure was not elevated; lungs were clear. The abdomen was soft with diffuse tenderness. He had reduced abduction of the right hip consistent with sacroiliitis; no rashes were present.

Electr

title: Unveiling the invisible culprit: a multimodality approach for myocardial infarction with non-obstructive coronary arteries diagnosis--a case report
case_summary:
A 48-year-old woman presented to the emergency department with severe chest pain. Her electrocardiogram was normal, but high-sensitivity troponin I rose from 45 to 1133 pg/mL (upper reference, 38). She had mild dyslipidaemia, diet-controlled glucose intolerance, and smoked 10 cigarettes per day. Her medical history was unremarkable, and she took no regular medications; she reported allergies to ceftriaxone and betamethasone. 

On examination, vital signs were within normal limits. There were no signs of heart failure, breath sounds were normal, jugular venous pressure was not elevated, and the neurologic examination was unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed a normal haemogram and creatinine of 0.95 mg/dL (reference range, 0.55-1.02). A chest radiograph was unremarkable. Bedside echocardiography revealed mild lateral and apical left ventricular hypokinesia with an ejection fraction of 55%, without pericardial effusion or right ventricular overload.

She received aspirin and high-inten

title: Expecting the unexpected: right atrial mass in a transplant patient
case_summary:
A 71-year-old man presented to his cardiologist with a two-week history of night sweats and chills. He had undergone orthotopic heart transplantation 24 years earlier for idiopathic dilated cardiomyopathy and was maintained on sirolimus, cyclosporine, and prednisone. His medical history included cardiac allograft vasculopathy, end-stage renal disease on dialysis, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and treated localized prostate cancer in remission. Two months before presentation, he underwent left and right heart catheterization with placement of a drug-eluting stent in the left anterior descending artery.
On examination, his temperature was 99.5 degreesF, blood pressure 135/73 mm Hg, and heart rate 87 beats per minute. A II/VI holosystolic murmur was heard at the left sternal border. Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 11.4 K/mcL, hemoglobin 9.7 g/dL, platelets 172 K/mcL, and serum creatinine 8.21 mg/dL. Electrocardiography revealed normal sinus rhythm with a right bundle branc

title: Vasospastic angina preceding diagnosis of arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy in a young athlete
case_summary: A 12-year-old girl, an avid long-distance runner and basketball player, awakened at midnight with chest pain lasting over 30 minutes. She had several intermittent attacks over the next two nights. She had no history of Kawasaki disease, no family history of cardiomyopathy or sudden cardiac death, and no prodromal infection or inflammatory symptoms. On presentation four days after onset, her ECG showed ST-segment elevations in leads II and III and atypical T waves in V4. Troponin I was 30 324 pg/mL (normal ?15.6). Echocardiography demonstrated normal coronary origins, preserved left ventricular ejection fraction (>60%), and no pericardial effusion. Holter monitoring revealed rare premature ventricular contractions (0.14% of beats) without conduction block. C-reactive protein and procalcitonin were normal. On hospital day 8, coronary angiography showed no stenoses; left ventriculography revealed an apical ventricular aneurysm, and right ventriculography showed a dilated right ventricle w

title: Paroxysmal Atrial Fibrillation Presenting as Acute Lower Limb Ischemia
case_summary: A 69?year?old woman presented with a 6?day history of sudden?onset right calf muscle pain and paresthesia. She also reported a single episode of dizziness with 2-3 seconds of blacked-out vision, as well as low back and bilateral knee pain. Her medical history was notable for 35 years of diabetes with retinopathy and 15 years of hypertension; she denied peripheral vascular disease, intermittent claudication, valvular heart disease, atrial fibrillation, hypercoagulable disorder, and smoking. On examination, blood pressure was 130/90 mm Hg, pulse 76 beats/min, temperature 36.8  degreesC, and respiratory rate 20 breaths/min. The right foot was cold with mottled skin and had weak popliteal and dorsalis pedis pulses; the right calf circumference was smaller than the left. Cardiac examination revealed normal heart sounds without murmurs. Laboratory studies showed a normal complete blood count, normal lipid and chemistry panels except for an elevated blood sugar and HbA1c of 7.3%. Coagulation

title: Role of cardiac magnetic resonance in MINOCA of unclear etiology: a case report of a suspicious paradoxical coronary embolism
case_summary:
A 68-year-old woman presented with sudden-onset oppressive chest pain lasting 15 minutes, radiating to the back. Her ECG showed subtle nonspecific ST-segment changes in lateral leads, and her high-sensitivity troponin I was elevated (peak 3700?ng?l??; normal <12?ng?l??). Her history was notable for hypertension, active smoking, and a similar NSTEMI three months earlier, during which invasive angiography--including tests for epicardial coronary spasm and microvascular dysfunction--had shown fully normal coronary arteries. She was discharged on dual antiplatelet therapy, which she continued. She also reported a recent transient right lower-limb edema and recurrent dizziness over the past months.

On admission, she was asymptomatic with unremarkable vital signs and physical examination. Lab tests revealed normal blood counts and C-reactive protein; SARS-CoV-2 testing was negative. Transthoracic echocardiography showed normal chamber morphology and function without valvular or pericardial

title: Rare case of infective endocarditis from invasive aspergillosis encasing the pulmonary valve: a case report
case_summary:
A 50-year-old Caucasian man presented with progressive dyspnea and palpitations for two days, along with one week of lower?extremity swelling and two months of fatigue, anorexia, and weakness. He denied fever, chills, and cough. His medical history was notable for myasthenia gravis treated with thymectomy and mediastinal radiotherapy, recurrent metastatic thymoma managed with multiple chemotherapeutic regimens, and ongoing eculizumab therapy. He also had paroxysmal atrial fibrillation on rivaroxaban. 

Two months earlier, chest CT angiography revealed a filling defect in the main pulmonary artery and new pulmonary metastases; anticoagulation was changed to enoxaparin. Two weeks before this admission, he was treated empirically for pneumonia without improvement.

On current admission, temperature was 37.0 degreesC, heart rate 147 beats/min (irregularly irregular), blood pressure 115/80 mmHg, and respiratory rate 22 breaths/min. Examination showed tachypnea, irregular rhythm, bilateral p

title: Intra-cardiac tumour and bicuspid aortic valve in a patient with neurofibromatosis type 1--rare associations: a case report
case_summary: A 53-year-old man with known neurofibromatosis type 1 and essential hypertension was referred preoperatively after detection of an ejection systolic murmur. He had residual cervical myelomalacia and resultant neuromuscular atrophy from prior cervical laminectomy but no symptoms of ischemia, syncope, or heart failure. On examination, a loud ejection systolic murmur was heard best at the right upper sternal border with radiation to the neck; peripheral pulses were normal. Electrocardiography showed normal sinus rhythm without conduction abnormalities. Transthoracic echocardiography suggested a bicuspid aortic valve with mixed valve disease: peak velocity 3.2 m/s, maximal and mean gradients 41 and 24 mmHg, and an aortic regurgitation pressure-half-time of 488 ms; ventricular dimensions and wall thickness were within normal limits. Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging confirmed fusion of the left and right aortic valve leaflets with mild aortic stenosis (peak velocity 2.6 m/s) and moderate a

title: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy following ultrasound-guided renal cyst aspiration
case_summary: A 48-year-old woman with autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease, hypertension, hyperlipidaemia, premature ventricular contractions, and anxiety underwent elective right renal cyst aspiration for right upper-quadrant pain. Under ultrasound guidance and moderate sedation, an 18-gauge needle yielded 170 mL of clear yellow fluid. She reported symptomatic relief and was initially stable. One hour later, she developed severe abdominal pain. Examination revealed a soft abdomen and cool, dry skin. Vital signs showed bradycardia and hypotension. Intravenous fluids and Trendelenburg positioning were started, but she became unresponsive and pulseless. Advanced cardiac life support was initiated with CPR and intravenous epinephrine. After return of spontaneous circulation, she complained of severe chest pain. Bedside ultrasound showed no right flank haematoma or pericardial effusion. An ECG demonstrated ST-segment elevations in the anterolateral leads and depressions in the inferior leads. A

title: Relevant pericardial effusion caused by cytomegalovirus infection in an immunocompetent patient: a case report
case_summary: A 72-year-old woman presented with 2 weeks of progressive shoulder pain and 1 week of dyspnea on exertion. Her history included hypertension treated with cilazapril and atenolol/chlorthalidone, dyslipidemia on atorvastatin, and a 30-year history of smoking five cigarettes per day. On admission she was afebrile (37.3  degreesC), blood pressure 91/59 mmHg, and heart rate 82 beats/min. Examination revealed a 2/6 systolic murmur and diminished breath sounds on the left, without jugular venous distention or peripheral edema; neurologic findings were normal. Laboratory tests showed CRP 256.7 mg/L, leukocytes 21.5 ? 10^9/L with monocytosis, elevated AST, ALT, alkaline phosphatase, and GGT; renal function transiently improved with hydration. Chest radiography had shown a large left pleural effusion; thoracentesis yielded an exudate (4 ? 10^9 cells/L, 85.5% neutrophils, pH 7.57, glucose 7.5 mmol/L, protein 39 g/L, LDH 213 U/L) with no microbial growth. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a

title: The way to a man's heart is through his stomach? A unique case report of transient constrictive pericarditis secondary to infarction of herniated omentum following bariatric surgery
case_summary:
A 38-year-old man presented with a 2-day history of central sharp chest pain radiating to the shoulder, associated with breathlessness, fever, and chills. He had undergone an elective laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy 5 days earlier. He denied abdominal pain, vomiting, or obstructive symptoms. His medical history included obesity, diet-controlled type II diabetes mellitus, dyslipidaemia, obstructive sleep apnoea, non-alcoholic steatohepatitis, gout, and childhood asthma. He also reported a remote upper abdominal stab wound ?20 years earlier. 

On examination, he was tachycardic (heart rate 118 bpm), blood pressure 103/62 mmHg, temperature 37.9 degreesC, and oxygen saturation 95% on room air. There was no jugular venous distension. Heart sounds were dual and soft, without murmurs or pericardial rub. Lung auscultation revealed reduced air entry and bilateral basal crepitations. The abdomen was soft and non-tender, with no distension or signs of obstruction; surgical wounds appeared unremar

title: Atrial tachycardia ablation at the pulmonary outflow tract in a patient with congenitally corrected transposition of the great arteries
case_summary:
A 50-year-old woman with known congenitally corrected transposition of the great arteries of the {I, D, D} type and situs inversus presented with a 6-year history of intermittent palpitations that became more frequent over the past 6 months. Episodes were sudden in onset and termination, without chest pain or syncope. Transthoracic echocardiography confirmed {I, D, D} ccTGA, and CT scan demonstrated situs inversus. A standard 12-lead ECG during tachycardia showed a narrow QRS complex; the RP? interval exceeded the P?R interval; P? waves were positive in leads V1, II, III, and aVF and negative in leads I and aVL. Based on the ECG, the differential included atrial tachycardia, fast-slow or slow-slow AV nodal reentrant tachycardia, and orthodromic AV reentrant tachycardia.  

Electrophysiological testing was performed with catheters in the coronary sinus, morphologic left ventricle, and intracardiac echo guidance of cardiac structures. During ventricular pacing at a cycle length of 400 ms

title: Nonbacterial Thrombotic Endocarditis of Bioprosthetic Aortic Valve Presenting as Cardioembolic Stroke in a Patient without Predisposing Systemic Disease
case_summary:
A 73-year-old man with ischemic heart disease and a bioprosthetic aortic valve implanted in 2009, type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, stage 4 chronic kidney disease, and peptic ulcer disease presented with a 2-week history of worsening exertional dyspnoea, orthopnoea, and intermittent presyncope. His medications included lifelong clopidogrel, antihypertensives, statin therapy, and insulin.

Initial ECG showed a stable borderline left bundle branch block without ischemic changes. He was admitted for intravenous diuresis. A few days later, he awoke with new right-sided visual disturbance. Vital signs were within normal limits. Neurologically, he had a right homonymous hemianopia; tone, reflexes, cranial nerves, strength, and sensation were otherwise normal. Cardiovascular examination revealed a regular rate with no rub, gallop, or murmur; there were coarse crepitations at the lung bases and bilateral ankle pitting oedema. Abdominal and dermatologic findings were

title: A case report of giant cell myocarditis after a syncope-related motor vehicle accident: an atypical presentation for a life-threatening condition
case_summary:
A 59-year-old Vietnamese woman presented to the emergency department after a motor vehicle accident that followed a syncopal episode. Two days before presentation, she had experienced vomiting and abdominal pain without fever or diarrhea. She denied chest pain, palpitations, dyspnea, pre-syncope, urinary incontinence, and tongue biting. She had received her first Pfizer COVID-19 vaccine two weeks earlier. Her past medical history was significant only for thalassemia; she had no cardiovascular risk factors or history of seizures.

At triage, her heart rate was 114 beats per minute, blood pressure 120/77 mmHg, respiratory rate 16 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, and she was afebrile. She was alert, oriented, euvolemic, and cardiac examination revealed no murmurs.

An initial ECG showed Q waves, fixed ST-segment elevation anteriorly, and low QRS voltages. Cardiac troponin I was 24?000 ng/L (normal <12); C-reactive protein was 10 mg/L and erythrocyte sedimentation rat

title: An unusual cause of syncope: Inferior vena cava compression by a massive renal cyst
case_summary:
An 83-year-old man with metastatic prostate cancer (inguinal lymph nodes and bone), a seizure disorder, and a prior right thalamic infarct with residual left-sided weakness was being transported home after an oncologist visit when he was noted to be unresponsive with a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 6. He arrived in the emergency department mildly confused but alert and oriented. He recalled feeling extremely warm before losing consciousness and upon awakening felt very confused. He also described several falls over the past 6 months while walking to his mailbox, which he had attributed to leg weakness. He denied chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, or speech difficulties but endorsed generalized weakness, abdominal distention, constipation, and bilateral leg swelling.  
Vital signs: blood pressure 156/76 mm Hg, heart rate 45-52 bpm, temperature 98.7  degreesF, respiratory rate 13/min, oxygen saturation 100% on room air. Examination revealed moderate abdominal distention, bilateral lower ex

title: Catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome presenting with a stroke as a first presentation: case report
case_summary:
A 31-year-old man presented to the emergency department with a one-hour history of sudden onset left-sided weakness of both upper and lower extremities following a fall while seated. He also reported a frontal headache, slurred speech, and nonradiating, burning epigastric pain. His past medical history was notable only for recurrent left flank pain previously treated as renal colic with NSAIDs. He smoked 18 pack-years and denied alcohol or drug use.  

On examination, he was alert and oriented; blood pressure and heart rate were normal and oxygen saturation was 98% on room air. Neurologic examination revealed nonfluent speech with intact comprehension and repetition, preserved sensation, and marked left-sided weakness (1/5 strength in both upper and lower limbs) with a muted left Babinski sign. Cranial nerves were intact.  

Initial laboratory tests showed elevated cardiac enzymes, normocytic anemia (hemoglobin 7 g/dl), reticulocytosis, thrombocytopenia, prolonged aPTT, normal PT/INR,

title: Tracheopericardial fistula in lung cancer masquerading as acute myocardial infarction: a case report
case_summary: A 72?year?old man presented with a 4?hour history of fever, chest tightness, and dull precordial chest pain unrelieved by rest or nitroglycerin. His temperature was 39.7  degreesC, pulse 136 beats/min, respiratory rate 23 breaths/min, and blood pressure 98/67 mmHg. He had a history of right upper lobectomy for stage IIB lung adenocarcinoma in 2016, adjuvant chemotherapy, nodal recurrence treated with chemotherapy in 2018, tracheal tumour involvement treated with cryotherapy and radiotherapy in early 2024, and most recently three cycles of pemetrexed, carboplatin, and bevacizumab for progressive disease. On examination, lung sounds were slightly coarse without rales; heart rate was 136/min with distant, dull heart sounds and no murmurs. Laboratory tests showed WBC 10.05?10^9/L with 93.2% neutrophils, hemoglobin 110 g/L, platelets 187?10^9/L, CRP 130 mg/L, D?dimer 7.83 mg/L, and normal cardiac enzymes including troponin I and BNP. ECG showed sinus tachycardia, abnormal Q waves, and ST?se

title: Systemic lupus erythematous as an unusual cause of intussusception in Sudanese woman: A case report
case_summary:
A 40?year?old Sudanese woman presented with a 2?week history of facial skin rash, hair loss, and diffuse arthralgias involving both small and large joints. She also reported palpitations, shortness of breath, and pleuritic chest pain. On admission, she developed acute, severe, cramping periumbilical abdominal pain with distension, vomiting, and infrequent episodes of bloody diarrhea.

Vital signs were notable for blood pressure 150/100 mm Hg, pulse 100 beats/min, and temperature 37.5  degreesC. Examination revealed a malar rash sparing the nasolabial folds with hyperpigmented patches, broken hair in the frontal and temporal regions, and no nail changes.
