Cardiac auscultation identified a short, localized systolic murmur at the mitral area. Chest examination showed decreased air entry bilaterally with dullness to percussion. The abdomen was distended with a tender left periumbilical mass. Musculoskeletal examination demonstrated active synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal joints bilatera

title: Cyclical sinus bradycardia and atrioventricular block induced by ticagrelor
case_summary: A 75-year-old woman with a history of coronary artery disease (balloon angioplasty and stenting of the left anterior descending and circumflex arteries 7 years earlier), hypertension, dyslipidemia, diabetes mellitus, and obesity presented with chest pain. An electrocardiogram during pain showed diffuse ST-segment depression. She received a loading dose of ticagrelor 180 mg and aspirin 325 mg and underwent urgent coronary angiography, which revealed chronic total occlusion of the right coronary artery and severe stenosis of the left main and circumflex arteries. Two drug-eluting stents were placed with excellent angiographic results. Postprocedural left ventricular ejection fraction was 50% with mild mitral regurgitation. A few hours later, telemetry showed Cheyne-Stokes respiration without overt heart failure, marked sinus arrhythmia with sinus tachycardia during apneic phases and severe sinus bradycardia (<30 beats/min) during hyperpneic phases, and cyclical transient complete atriove

title: "Never Trust to General Impressions, My Boy, but Concentrate Yourself upon Details": An Unusual and Challenging Presentation of Pheochromocytoma
journal: Journal of Cardiovascular Development and Disease
case_summary: A 45-year-old woman with no prior cardiac disease was admitted for rapidly worsening dyspnea, orthopnea, and fatigue. On arrival, her systolic blood pressure was 80 mmHg and she was oliguric. Electrocardiography showed rapid atrial fibrillation (150 bpm) with nonspecific repolarization abnormalities. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a normal left ventricular dimension and severely depressed ejection fraction (?20%) with diffuse hypokinesis. Laboratory studies demonstrated a slight increase in myocardial necrosis markers without a significant time-activity slope, leukocytosis, acute renal and hepatic failure, and metabolic acidosis with elevated lactate. Coronary angiography showed no obstructive lesions. She was managed with an intra-aortic balloon pump and dopamine infusion, and acute renal failure was treated with renal replacement therapy. On hospital day 1, she developed coagulopathy consistent with disseminated intravascular coagulation and experienced multiple episodes of

title: Massive aortic root dilation in a Young male with previously undiagnosed acromegaly: a case report and review
case_summary:
A 23-year-old man with no significant past medical history was referred for evaluation of unexplained dyspnea. He had been in his usual state of health until 2 weeks before admission, when he developed fever, nasal congestion, and rhinorrhea. Three days later, he experienced vomiting, diarrhea, and abdominal pain. After admission for these symptoms, he developed marked palpitations, dyspnea, and orthopnea.

His parents both have diabetes, his grandfather died of myocardial infarction at age 74, and his maternal uncle has acromegaly. He had no known connective tissue disorder or previous cardiovascular disease.

On examination, temperature was 37.9  degreesC, blood pressure 150/90 mmHg, heart rate 110 beats/min, and respiratory rate 22 breaths/min. Lung auscultation revealed fine basal crepitations and mild expiratory rhonchi bilaterally. Cardiac examination disclosed an early diastolic murmur; neck veins were distended without visible pulsations. There was no peripheral edema or ascite

title: Multifocal Severe Coronary Artery Vasospasm Mistaken for Diffuse Atherosclerosis: A Case Report
case_summary: A 50-year-old Caucasian woman with hypertension and a history of smoking presented with an acute exacerbation of longstanding anginal chest pain at rest. She had assumed her symptoms were due to a chest infection and was taking antibiotics. Her daily medications included clopidogrel, dipyridamole, ramipril, nicorandil, and sublingual glyceryl trinitrate, which only marginally relieved her pain. On examination, heart rate and blood pressure were within normal limits; cardiac auscultation was unremarkable, and diffuse expiratory wheezes were heard in the lungs. Laboratory results showed a normal CK-MB level and a mildly elevated cardiac troponin I of 1.1 ng/mL. The ECG demonstrated inferolateral ST-segment elevations. She was prepared for primary percutaneous coronary intervention. Selective coronary angiography revealed several obstructive stenoses in the right and intermediate coronary arteries, and complex intervention was contemplated.

title: An incidental finding of testicular seminoma in the context of acute pulmonary embolism: a case report
case_summary:
A 37-year-old previously healthy white man presented with acute chest pain and dyspnea that markedly limited his exercise tolerance. He denied cough, infective symptoms, deep-vein thrombosis symptoms, syncope, or constitutional complaints. He had no personal or family history of venous thromboembolism and no identifiable risk factors. His medications were fluoxetine and promethazine; he was a nonsmoker, drank within recommended limits, and worked as a light technician.

On examination, temperature was 37.8  degreesC, oxygen saturation 92% on room air, respiratory rate 24 breaths/min, heart rate 120 beats/min, and blood pressure 132/84 mm Hg. He appeared pale but was alert; cardiac and pulmonary examinations were normal. Abdominal and neurologic examinations were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 15 g/dL, white-cell count 16.9 ? 10^9/L (neutrophils 15.3 ? 10^9/L), lymphocytes 0.8 ? 10^9/L, platelets 290 ? 10^9/L, sodium 145 mmol/L, potassium 3.7 mmol/L, creatinine 105

title: Acute posteromedial papillary muscle rupture secondary to aortic valve endocarditis: a case report
case_summary: An 81-year-old man with hypertension presented with 4 days of fever and rapid visual deterioration of his left eye. Two days before admission he had noted left-eye pain and worsening eyesight, followed by fever and fatigue. On examination at admission, temperature was 38.7 degreesC, blood pressure 132/49 mmHg, pulse 60 beats/min regular, respirations 20/min, and oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Cardiovascular examination revealed a grade 2 aortic diastolic murmur. Funduscopic examination showed a Roth spot in the left eye. White blood cell count was 17 720 cells/mm3, and procalcitonin was 14.08 ng/mL. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated mild mitral regurgitation, moderate aortic regurgitation with a pressure half-time of 347 ms, no holodiastolic flow reversal in the abdominal aorta, a left ventricular ejection fraction of 53% with diffuse global hypokinesis, and mild LV dilation (diastolic dimension 55 mm; systolic dimension 38 mm). Transesophageal echocardiography revealed a

title: Phantom Tumor of the Lung: Localized Interlobar Effusion in Congestive Heart Failure
case_summary: An 83-year-old man with type II diabetes mellitus, hypertension, atrial fibrillation, and chronic congestive heart failure (treated with insulin, methyldigoxin, furosemide, and oral potassium supplements) presented with a 5-day history of progressive shortness of breath, bilateral leg swelling, and dry cough. On examination, his respiratory rate was 32 breaths per minute, heart rate 99 beats per minute, and blood pressure 165/105 mm Hg. Lung auscultation revealed fine moist subcrepitant inspiratory sounds at the left base and diminished breath sounds at the right base. A 3/6 systolic murmur was heard at the aortic area. Arterial blood gas analysis showed pH 7.448, pCO2 4.33 kPa, pO2 7.67 kPa, HCO3- 22.0 mmol/L, base excess ?1.1 mmol/L, and oxygen saturation 91.4%. Laboratory studies revealed a normal complete blood count, serum creatinine of 143 ?mol/L, and gamma-glutamyl transferase three times the upper limit of normal. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated concentric left vent

title: Unilateral absent pulmonary artery in an adult - A diagnostic and therapeutic challenge
case_summary:
A 53-year-old woman with well-controlled hypertension and hypothyroidism presented with sudden-onset dyspnea and palpitations. On arrival, her blood pressure was 60/40 mmHg, pulse 160 beats per minute, and she was cyanosed. Respiratory examination was unremarkable. Cardiovascular examination showed tachycardia, a loud pulmonary component of the second heart sound, and a tricuspid regurgitation murmur. 

An electrocardiogram revealed supraventricular tachycardia, which was terminated by synchronized electrical cardioversion. The post-conversion ECG demonstrated right-axis deviation, an S?Q?T? pattern, a qR pattern in lead V?, T-wave inversion in leads V?-V?, and p-pulmonale. 

She was started on low-molecular-weight heparin and intravenous amiodarone, and admitted to the intensive care unit. Transthoracic echocardiography showed a dilated right ventricle with severe tricuspid regurgitation and an estimated pulmonary artery pressure of 120 mmHg; the left atrium and ventricle were normal

title: Heart Failure in a Young Adult with a Fine-Lubinsky Syndrome: An Unknown Comorbidity
case_summary:
A 25-year-old man with known Fine-Lubinsky syndrome presented with two weeks of dyspnea on exertion and bilateral lower extremity swelling. He reported a one-month history of a dry, nonproductive cough. He denied fever, myalgias, chest pain, recent travel, or sick contacts. A prior COVID-19 test was negative. Outpatient chest radiography showed cardiomegaly, prompting referral.

On examination, he was afebrile, breathing nonlaboriously, with blood pressure 106/74 mmHg and heart rate 106 beats/min. Jugular venous distension was noted, and auscultation revealed an S3. His abdomen was distended, and he had trace pitting edema of the lower extremities.

His past medical history was notable for Fine-Lubinsky syndrome, with cranial abnormalities, early auditory problems, intellectual disability, speech impairment, and multiple intracranial fibrous dysplasia resections. Family history included hypertension in both parents and a grandfather with early myocardial infarction and heart failure. H

title: A 44-year-old man with recurrent ST-segment elevation: a case report of two presentations of Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis
case_summary:
A 44-year-old man presented with sudden onset atypical chest and jaw pain. He was otherwise healthy, a non-smoker, and had a normal CT coronary angiogram two years earlier. Over the preceding weeks he had fatigue, nasal congestion, and red eyes; imaging of his chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed pancreatic and pulmonary masses. A pulmonary biopsy demonstrated granulomata with acid-fast bacilli, and he was started on anti-tuberculous therapy.

On examination, he was febrile with a normal cardiac examination. Twelve-lead ECG showed widespread ST-segment elevation. High-sensitivity troponin T was 1089?ng/L (reference 0-14). Transthoracic echocardiography revealed normal LV systolic function, no regional wall-motion abnormalities, but an echogenic antero-septal myocardium. Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging demonstrated multiple focal areas of high signal intensity on T2-weighted imaging with corresponding late gadolinium enhancement, consistent with acute myocarditis, and round infiltrati

title: A case report of myocarditis secondary to eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis
case_summary: A 60-year-old man with a 3-year history of allergic rhinitis and therapy-resistant asthma presented with severe central chest pain radiating to the back, nausea, sweating, neck pain, fatigue, and generalized weakness. He had no prior cardiac disease or cardiovascular risk factors. On examination, blood pressure was 100/60 mm Hg, heart rate 130 beats/min, heart sounds were normal, and the lungs were clear. A 12-lead ECG showed sinus tachycardia with globally flat T waves and no dynamic ST-segment changes. Laboratory tests revealed a white-cell count of 18.16 ? 10^9/L with 27.3% eosinophils (5.0 ? 10^9/L), C-reactive protein 92 mg/L, erythrocyte sedimentation rate 22 mm/h, troponin T 1267 ng/L, and NT-proBNP 6470 ng/L. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated segmental anterior left ventricular wall-motion abnormalities, mildly reduced ejection fraction, and a small pericardial effusion. Invasive coronary angiography showed unobstructed coronary arteries. Cardiovascular magnetic reson

title: Allergic myocardial ischemia causing reversible hemodynamic collapse during gastroscopy
case_summary:
A 54-year-old woman with occult anemia was scheduled for screening gastroscopy and colonoscopy under sedation. She had no known drug allergies, no significant cardiac history, and was a heavy smoker but had no other coronary risk factors. Baseline vital signs were normal (BP 112/65 mm Hg; HR 72 beats/min; RR 12/min; SpO? 100% on room air). Sedation was induced with meperidine 50 mg, midazolam 2 mg, and propofol 40 mg IV, with continuous monitoring and oxygen by nasal cannula.

Ten minutes into gastroscopy, she developed bradycardia (SpO? 97%) that progressed to asystole without any rash. She received IV lidocaine 60 mg, atropine 2 mg total, epinephrine 0.01 mg, and hydrocortisone 250 mg, with 100% face-mask ventilation. She then converted to sinus tachycardia (HR 112 beats/min) with BP 70/42 mm Hg and maintained spontaneous breathing.

A few minutes later, she regained consciousness with acute chest pain, dyspnea, pulmonary edema, and hypoxia (SpO? 60%). The procedure was aborted, and

title: Perimyocarditis as First Manifestation of Systemic Lupus Erythematosus Successfully Treated with Heart Failure and Immunosuppressive Therapy
journal: Journal of Cardiovascular Development and Disease
case_summary:
A 30-year-old woman was admitted for recurrent biliary colic with suspected acute calculous cholecystitis. Initial laboratory tests showed mildly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. She was started on analgesics, spasmolytics, and antibiotics. Three days later, she developed fever to >38.5  degreesC, a maculopapular rash on the arms and torso, a malar rash, and dyspnea. N-terminal pro-b-type natriuretic peptide was 9373 ng/L (normal <208 ng/L), and high-sensitivity troponin I was 293 ng/L (normal <39 ng/L). ECGs showed sinus tachycardia without ischemic changes. Laboratory markers of inflammation included low albumin, elevated fibrinogen, and normal ferritin.  
Abdominal ultrasound showed no signs of acute cholecystitis. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed hypocontractility of the basal anterior, septal, and lateral walls with preserved wall thickness and left ventricular (LV) diameter, mildly reduced ejection fraction (45%), reduced global longitudinal

title: Atrioventricular junctional tachycardia with exit block in an adolescent
final_diagnosis: atrioventricular junctional tachycardia with exit block
case_summary:
A 17-year-old girl presented with a 2-year history of post-exercise palpitations, shortness of breath, and chest tightness lasting up to 45 minutes and resolving with rest. An ambulatory heart rhythm monitor showed sinus rhythm during her symptomatic episodes and, separately, episodes of second-degree (Mobitz type I) AV block as well as advanced, second-degree AV block with asymptomatic pauses up to 4.8 seconds. 

On admission, her physical examination, vital signs, and a 15-lead ECG were normal. Laboratory studies, including serum electrolytes, complete blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, antinuclear antibody panel, Lyme antibody panel, and streptolysin O antibodies, were all within normal limits. Transthoracic echocardiography and cardiac magnetic resonance imaging demonstrated normal cardiac structure and function without evidence of myocarditis.

During telemetry, she continued to have intermittent episodes of advanced second-degree AV block, particularly when startled awa

title: Mapping catheter-related mitral valve injury: a case report
case_summary:
A 69-year-old woman with paroxysmal atrial fibrillation and typical atrial flutter underwent an elective combined pulmonary vein isolation and cavotricuspid isthmus ablation. During the procedure, a circular mapping catheter became entangled in the mitral valve apparatus but was freed with sheath advancement; immediate on?table transthoracic echocardiography showed no significant mitral regurgitation or pericardial effusion. The patient was discharged the next day in sinus rhythm.

Twelve days later, she presented with worsening exertional dyspnea, nonproductive cough, and lethargy. On examination, jugular venous pressure was raised to the level of the tragus. Stony dullness and decreased air entry were noted at the right lung base. A new grade 3/6 pansystolic murmur was heard at the apex radiating toward the aortic region. There were no peripheral edema or stigmata of infective endocarditis.

Laboratory studies revealed a white blood cell count of 18.36 ? 10^9/L with neutrophilia, hem

title: Mycotic Coronary Aneurysm Associated With Biventricular Fistula
case_summary: A 53-year-old man with hypertension, end-stage renal disease on hemodialysis, pulmonary embolism, and type 2 diabetes mellitus presented with increasing dyspnea, malaise, low-grade fever, and signs of heart failure. Blood pressure was 123/71 mm Hg, heart rate 72 beats/min, and peripheral oxygen saturation 97%. He was afebrile and had no cutaneous signs of endocarditis or skin infection. Electrocardiogram showed an anterior myocardial infarction of indeterminate age with abnormal ST-segment and T-wave changes suggesting lateral ischemia. Chest radiograph revealed a right pleural effusion, cardiomegaly, and interstitial edema. White blood cell count was 5,200 ?10^9/L (normal 4.5-11), C-reactive protein 88 mg/L (normal <0.3), and procalcitonin 0.09 ug/mL. Complete blood count, creatinine, and cardiac enzymes were normal. Blood cultures were negative. Transthoracic echocardiography showed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 35% with apical and anterolateral akinesia and pleuro-pericard

title: Hyponatraemia-induced Takotsubo syndrome secondary to idiopathic syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone: a case report
case_summary: An 84-year-old overweight woman with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, hypothyroidism, and osteoporosis was taking levothyroxine, metformin, linagliptin, losartan, indapamide, pregabalin, calcitriol, and denosumab. One month before admission, her sodium level was normal. She presented with anginal chest pain and two epileptic seizures. On exam, blood pressure was 110/70 mmHg, heart rate 85 bpm, jugular venous pressure was normal, there was no peripheral oedema, and heart sounds were normal. Laboratory tests showed a serum sodium of 119 mmol/L, NT-proBNP 53 175 pg/mL, and high-sensitivity troponin I 1657 ng/L; complete blood count, liver and renal function, thyroid function, and cortisol were normal. The initial ECG showed sinus rhythm without ST-segment or T-wave abnormalities. Cranial CT was unremarkable. She received 60 mL of 20% saline in 500 mL of 0.9% saline at 1 mL/kg/h, with gradual correction of sodium to >130 mmol/L over 48 hours. Approximately 48 hours after admission, repeat EC

title: Severe iron overload cardiomyopathy manifested as acute myopericarditis: A case report
case_summary:
A 21-year-old woman with ?-thalassemia/hemoglobin E disease complicated by secondary hemochromatosis, requiring biweekly red blood cell transfusions and oral deferasirox, presented with 4 days of acute chest pain, dyspnea, and orthopnea, preceded by a 2-week low-grade fever. Her serum ferritin over the past year ranged from 8000 to 15 000 ng/mL. Five years earlier, CMR T2* had shown mild cardiac iron (T2* 16 ms) with preserved ventricular function (LVEF 56%) and no scar; liver T2* was 0.9 ms.

On examination, blood pressure was 110/64 mm Hg, heart rate 93/min, respiratory rate 20/min, temperature 36.8 degreesC. She appeared pale, jaundiced, and had a bronze skin tone. Jugular venous pressure was 12 cm H?O. The apex beat was displaced downward and leftward; P2 was prominent; a grade II/VI systolic murmur was heard at the left parasternal border. Fine crackles were present bilaterally, with pitting edema of both legs; the liver edge was palpable 4 cm below the right costal margin.

ECG s

title: Spontaneous Dissection of a Septal Coronary Artery Mimicking Myocarditis
case_summary:
A 38-year-old man presented with persistent chest pain during exercise and at rest, along with worsening dyspnea. He had a 12-pack-year smoking history but no other cardiovascular risk factors. One month earlier, he had been evaluated for weakness and dry cough and diagnosed with a viral respiratory infection.  
On admission, blood pressure was 150/103 mm Hg, heart rate 60 beats/min, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. The baseline ECG showed up to 1 mm of J-point elevation in leads V3 to V5 with peaked T waves, findings that were unchanged from ECGs obtained during his earlier viral illness. Repeat ECGs remained unchanged, and blood pressure normalized.  
Laboratory tests revealed a negative D-dimer result, normal inflammatory markers, and a negative SARS-CoV-2 PCR. Cardiac biomarkers were elevated: creatine kinase 219 U/L, high-sensitivity troponin T 101 ng/L rising to 350 ng/L over 24 hours, and N-terminal pro-B-type natriuretic peptide 182 ng/L.  
Transthoracic echocardiography

title: Unstable angina early after aortic valve replacement surgery in a female patient with normal coronary arteries preoperatively - a case report
case_summary:
A 60-year-old obese woman was evaluated for progressive dyspnea on exertion. Physical examination revealed a 3/6 crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at the aortic area. Preoperative evaluation included:
? ECG: Normal sinus rhythm.  
? Echocardiogram: Severely calcified aortic valve, valve area 0.8 cm2, mean gradient 55 mmHg, preserved left ventricular ejection fraction, left ventricular wall thickness 14 mm.  
? Coronary angiography: Completely normal coronary arteries.  

She underwent aortic valve replacement via mini-sternotomy with a Sorin 23 mm mechanical prosthesis. Myocardial protection was achieved with cold antegrade and retrograde St. Thomas' cardioplegic solution. Early postoperative recovery was unremarkable.

Twelve weeks after surgery, she reported severe chest pain on mild exertion and at rest. On admission:
? ECG: T-wave inversions in leads V2-V5 and aVL; during pain episodes, marked ST-segment depression in V2-V5.  

Coronary angiography demonstrated a 90% diameter r

title: A rare presentation of primary cardiac myxofibrosarcoma: Case report and literature review
case_summary: A 40-year-old man presented with sudden, profuse bright-red hemoptysis while cycling. He denied chest pain, dyspnea, fever, weight loss, or fatigue. His 10-pack-year smoking history was otherwise unremarkable. On examination, he was stable: heart rate 100 bpm, oxygen saturation 96% on room air. Lung bases had decreased breath sounds; cardiac exam revealed a loud S1 murmur; there was no edema. ECG showed sinus rhythm. Laboratory studies revealed normal D-dimer and NT-proBNP levels, without evidence of inflammation, anemia, or renal or hepatic dysfunction. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a 75 ? 28 mm mass in the left atrium extending into the left ventricle, with mild mitral regurgitation and moderate to severe functional mitral stenosis; left ventricular ejection fraction was 55% with normal chamber size. Chest CT showed no pulmonary parenchymal or mediastinal abnormalities; CT angiography excluded pulmonary embolism. Bronchoscopy was unremarkable. Cardiac MRI revealed an 80

title: Acute breathlessness with frank hemoptysis following catheter ablation for atrial fibrillation, a cause not so obvious
case_summary:
A 71?year?old man with persistent symptomatic atrial fibrillation underwent elective repeat catheter ablation under general anesthesia. His medical history included hypercholesterolemia; he had previously maintained sinus rhythm for 9 months after his first ablation and had taken amiodarone for 6 months in 2015 without complications. Medications on admission included warfarin, bisoprolol, and atorvastatin.

In July 2017, the ablation lasted 2 ? hours with pulmonary vein re?isolation, complex fractionated electrogram ablation, and linear roof and mitral lines. Two hours postprocedure, amiodarone was started at 200 mg three times daily, planned to taper to 200 mg daily over three weeks.

That night, he developed cough, chest pain, and hemoptysis with clots. He was afebrile, blood pressure and oxygen saturation were normal on room air, and physical examination was notable only for blood?stained sputum. Laboratory studies showed:  
- Hemoglobin 120 g/L (preprocedure 159 g/L)  
- White bloo

title: A case report of high-output heart failure due to arteriovenous shunt without bowel: how to address?
case_summary:
A 70-year-old man with a history of colorectal cancer complicated by intestinal ischaemia underwent haemicolectomy with resection of the ileum and jejunum, resulting in a terminal jejunostomy and complete dependence on parenteral nutrition via a long-standing vena saphena magna arteriovenous shunt. He also had hypertension, chronic hepatitis B infection, and microcytic anaemia. Over the past 6 weeks, he experienced progressive dyspnoea and fatigue. On examination, he had rales bilaterally and peripheral oedema; blood pressure was 130/70 mmHg and heart rate 76 b.p.m. Repeated ECGs showed atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 70-100 b.p.m. NT-proBNP was 315 pmol/L. He was admitted for intravenous loop diuretics. 

Transthoracic echocardiography showed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 35% with dilated atria and a left ventricular end-diastolic diameter of 5.6 cm. Coronary angiography revealed no significant coronary artery disease. After decongestion, right-heart catheteri

title: A case report: a rare case of severe aortic incompetence
case_summary:
A 37-year-old man presented with dyspnoea and palpitations (New York Heart Association class II-III) for 6 months. He had no history of chest trauma or fever. On examination, he was afebrile with mildly elevated jugular venous pressure, a blood pressure of 140/70 mmHg, and a heart rate of 90 beats per minute. Chest auscultation was unremarkable. Cardiac examination revealed apical displacement to the left sixth intercostal space, an ill-sustained left ventricular heave, a third heart sound, and an early diastolic murmur (grade IV/IV) over the left second intercostal space. Abdominal and neurologic examinations were normal.

Electrocardiography showed sinus tachycardia with left bundle branch block. A chest radiograph demonstrated cardiomegaly with a prominent aortic shadow. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a large mobile sac-like structure with irregular borders prolapsing into the left ventricular cavity during each cardiac cycle, associated with severe aortic regurgitation. Th

title: Cardiac injury, arrhythmia, and sudden death in a COVID-19 patient
case_summary: A 72-year-old man with hypertension presented to the emergency department with acute chest tightness and a feeling of impending doom. He reported several days of shortness of breath, cough, fatigue, and intermittent diarrhea, and had not sought regular medical care. En route, paramedics noted an afebrile but distressed patient with chest pain and hypoxemia; a field electrocardiogram was interpreted as an acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction. On arrival, his blood pressure was 158/110 mm Hg, and he was placed on high-flow oxygen and moved to a respiratory isolation room while preparations were made for emergent cardiac catheterization. Minutes later, he became unresponsive and pulseless. During resuscitation, he was intubated and briefly regained spontaneous circulation, at which time a second electrocardiogram was obtained. He remained hypotensive, became pulseless again, and after 45 minutes of resuscitative efforts was pronounced dead. A postmortem nasopharyngeal sample tested po

title: New Onset Cardiac Murmur and Exertional Dyspnea in an Apparently Healthy Child: A Rare Localization of Obstructive Myxoma in the Right Ventricle Outflow Tract without Pulmonary Embolization--A Case Report and Literature Review
journal: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
case_summary:
A 13-year-old previously healthy boy presented with three weeks of mild dyspnea, chest pain on exertion, and a newly detected cardiac murmur. He denied syncope, palpitations, fever, weight loss, recent infections, and had no history of central lines or tattoos. Family history was negative for congenital heart disease, cardiomyopathy, sudden death, or thrombophilia.

On examination, he was afebrile, heart rate 95 bpm, blood pressure 96/72 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 100%. He had a grade 3/6 systolic murmur at the left upper sternal border, normal S1 and S2, no gallops or rubs, and mild hepatomegaly. Peripheral pulses were normal. 

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 12.9 g/dL, aspartate aminotransferase 43 U/L, normal renal function, coagulation profile, C-reactive protein, and troponin, but an elevated NT-proBNP of 4019 pg/mL. Chest radiograph was unremarkable. 

Electrocardiogram demonstrated sinus rhythm, right bundle-branch block with repolarization abnormalities in the right prec

title:
Acute Liver Failure due to Altered Fontan Circulation in a Patient
With Tricuspid Atresia: A Case Report
journal: Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports
case_summary:
A 33-year-old man with tricuspid atresia status post-modified Fontan procedure approximately 30 years earlier and a history of atrial flutter presented with 1 week of progressive fatigue and malaise. He also reported body aches, anorexia, subjective fevers, chills, nausea, and two episodes of nonbilious, nonbloody emesis. Two weeks before presentation he had received a second dose of the Pfizer COVID-19 vaccine and undergone dental work without antibiotic prophylaxis. He denied chest pain, palpitations, dyspnea, cough, diarrhea, leg swelling, night sweats, or weight loss. His home medications included sotalol and aspirin; he denied tobacco, alcohol, and illicit drug use.

On examination, temperature was 98.2 degreesF, blood pressure 105/85 mmHg, heart rate 193 beats per minute, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. He appeared dehydrated with dry mucous membranes and scleral icterus. Cardiac examination revealed tachycardia with an irregular rhythm; no murmurs were noted.

Laboratory

title: Acute torrential mitral regurgitation during transcatheter aortic valve replacement: a case report
case_summary:
A 90-year-old man with symptomatic severe aortic stenosis was referred for transapical transcatheter aortic valve replacement. His history included endovascular repair of an abdominal aortic aneurysm. Preoperative ECG showed normal sinus rhythm without conduction block. Transthoracic echocardiography confirmed severe aortic stenosis (valve area, 0.58 cm2; peak velocity, 4.1 m/s), preserved left ventricular ejection fraction (54%) without regional wall-motion abnormalities, and mild mitral regurgitation. Coronary angiography demonstrated 90% stenosis of the ostial left circumflex artery; he had no angina and no inducible ischemia, so no intervention was performed.

Under general anesthesia, an extra-stiff guidewire was passed through the aortic valve via the apex. Shortly thereafter, new-onset left bundle-branch block developed, accompanied by moderate mitral regurgitation and an increase in pulmonary arterial pressure. A 26-mm Sapien XT valve was deployed under rapid ventricular pacing

title: False-positive troponin elevation due to an immunoglobulin-G-cardiac troponin T complex: a case report
case_summary:
A 53-year-old South Asian man presented with more than 24 hours of chest pain and left-arm numbness that began while driving but resolved upon arrival. He had no abnormal findings on examination (heart rate 65 bpm; blood pressure 108/76 mmHg; respiratory rate 16 breaths/min; O? saturation 98% on room air). His history included type 2 diabetes, hypertension, hypercholesterolaemia, non-alcoholic steatohepatitis, and hepatitis B. He had two prior admissions for cTnT-positive chest pain (2002 and 2015), each with unobstructed coronaries and a working diagnosis of myocarditis.

An admission ECG showed no ischaemic changes. Initial cTnT was 1588 ng/L (normal <15 ng/L) and 1842 ng/L on repeat. Renal function was normal (creatinine 102 ?mol/L) and hemoglobin was 137 g/L. Serial troponins remained above 1600 ng/L. Creatine kinase was borderline elevated at 338 U/L (normal 40-320 U/L). Transthoracic echocardiography revealed no regional wall motion abnormalities and an LVEF of 60-65%. A CT aorta

title: Cardiac Sarcoidosis or Giant Cell Myocarditis? On Treatment Improvement of Fulminant Myocarditis as Demonstrated by Cardiovascular Magnetic Resonance Imaging
case_summary: A 42-year-old African-American man presented with a 2-week history of rapidly progressive exertional dyspnea. He had no significant past medical history, used tobacco occasionally, and consumed six beers weekly. On evaluation, cardiac enzymes were negative, B-type natriuretic peptide was 843 pg/mL, and D-dimer was 3.7 ?g/mL. HIV, hepatitis viral panel, and urine drug screen were negative. Rheumatoid factor was normal, antinuclear antibody was mildly positive (speckled pattern), and serum angiotensin?converting enzyme was elevated at 70 U/L (normal, 7-46). Parvovirus B19 IgG was positive, and IgM was negative. Electrocardiography showed sinus rhythm with low voltage and poor R?wave progression in the inferior and anterior leads. Chest radiography revealed pulmonary edema, cardiomegaly, and a peripheral nodular pattern. Contrast?enhanced chest CT showed mediastinal, hilar, and right peridiaphragmatic lymphadenopathy, small pleural and pericardial effusions, and bilateral multifocal conso

title: Giant androgen-producing adrenocortical carcinoma with atrial flutter: A case report and review of the literature
case_summary:
A 66-year-old Caucasian woman presented with a 24-hour history of rapid, regular palpitations and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, superimposed on a one-year history of progressive dyspnea on exertion, generalized edema, abdominal discomfort after meals, and gradual onset of hirsutism. She had no past medical history, never smoked or used exogenous hormones, and experienced menopause at age 52.  

On examination, blood pressure was normal, heart rate 150 beats/min regular, with enlarged cardiac dullness, a 3/6 pansystolic mitral murmur, jugular venous distension, right basal crackles, diminished breath sounds at the left pulmonary base, massive generalized edema, hirsutism (Ferriman-Gallwey score 14), minimal frontal balding, and a painless left hypochondriac abdominal mass. BMI was 33.3 kg/m^2.  

Electrocardiogram showed typical atrial flutter with 2:1 atrioventricular conduction at 150 bpm and left bundle branch block. Laboratory studies revealed hemoconcentration, new-onset diabetes (

title: A case report of constrictive pericarditis following COVID-19 vaccination
case_summary: A 19-year-old previously healthy man reported a 4-month history of abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting beginning 2 weeks after his second dose of an mRNA COVID-19 vaccine. He had no prior medical history. He presented on three occasions to outpatient and emergency settings for these symptoms and was treated with antacids and antiemetics. Outpatient right upper quadrant ultrasound showed cholelithiasis, ascites, and a right pleural effusion. Laboratory tests revealed a total bilirubin of 3.9 mg/dL, hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate 22 mm/h, and C-reactive protein 7.8 mg/dL. A chest radiograph demonstrated a borderline enlarged cardiac silhouette. Over the next weeks, his symptoms progressed to include diarrhea, shortness of breath, and pleuritic chest pain. On examination, he was tachypneic (26 breaths/min) and tachycardic (123 beats/min) without jugular venous distension, pericardial rub, or peripheral edema. A 12-lead electrocardiogram showed sinus tachycardia with

title: Hemorrhagic pericardial effusion superimposed on total anomalous pulmonary venous connection: First-reported case
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 29-year-old man presented with acute midsternal, sharp, severe chest pain radiating to the back that worsened when supine and improved by leaning forward. He also reported palpitations, shortness of breath, and cough. Vital signs were: temperature 37 degreesC, heart rate 104 beats/min, respiratory rate 35 breaths/min, blood pressure 118/90 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 88% on room air (on oxygen supplementation thereafter). On examination, lung fields were clear. Cardiac auscultation revealed normal heart sounds and a grade 2/6 systolic murmur at the left sternal border; there was no jugular venous distension, peripheral edema, or clubbing. 

Laboratory studies showed a white blood cell count of 8.11 ? 10^3/uL, hemoglobin 13.3 g/dL, negative high-sensitivity troponin, and a respiratory alkalosis on arterial blood gas. Electrocardiography demonstrated sinus rhythm with a right bundle branch block. A chest radiograph revealed an enlarged cardiac silhouette and widened superior mediastinu

title: First case report of myopericarditis linked to Campylobacter coli enterocolitis
case_summary: A 51?year?old woman was admitted with a 3?day history of continuous, dull, non?pleuritic, left?lateral chest pain, associated with diaphoresis, nausea, palpitations, and presyncope. The pain was partially relieved by leaning forward and by paracetamol with codeine. Four days before the chest pain, she had developed an acute gastrointestinal illness, passing 3-4 loose stools daily, which was still ongoing. She had no significant past medical history or recent travel. On examination she was afebrile; heart rate 106 bpm; blood pressure 120/76 mm Hg. Heart sounds were normal without murmur or rub; jugular venous pressure was normal; lungs were clear. The abdomen was soft and non?tender. Laboratory tests showed C?reactive protein 30.0 mg/L (normal <5.0), troponin I 70 ng/L (normal <16), normal creatine kinase, ALT 65 U/L (normal <55), ALP 142 U/L (normal 20-110), normal full blood count and electrolytes. ECG demonstrated concave ST?segment elevations in leads I, II, III, aVF, and V6, PR ele

title: Bradycardia and syncope as sole manifestations of a?cranial lesion: a case report
case_summary:
A 69-year-old Caucasian man presented to the emergency department with a year-long history of episodic lightheadedness. He described a sensation beginning in his feet, spreading upward, and then feeling lightheaded, hot, and clammy; each episode lasted a few minutes, occurred randomly without identifiable triggers, and was not associated with focal neurologic symptoms. He also reported general malaise over the past year, including fatigue, weakness, and reduced appetite without weight loss.

His medical history was significant only for hypertension, treated with ramipril. He had previously undergone evaluation for ischemic heart disease; an exercise?tolerance test was negative for ischemic changes, and echocardiography demonstrated good global systolic and diastolic function. Outpatient monitoring with a 7?day ECG recording showed sinus rhythm with 12 episodes of bradycardia in 24 hours (lowest rate, 23 bpm), ventricular ectopic beats, and pauses in cardiac activity of up to 2.5 second

title: A case report of malignant primary pericardial mesothelioma with atypical imaging appearance: multimodality imaging with histopathological correlation
case_summary:
A 45-year-old man presented with a 1-year history of dyspnea, palpitations, chest pain, peripheral numbness, weight loss, and hypotension. Six months earlier, a mediastinal mass had been detected on CT; a CT-guided biopsy was non-diagnostic and complicated by cardiac tamponade. He declined further intervention and received dexamethasone for symptom relief. He had no other medical history.

On examination, he had bilateral lower-leg edema, a loud systolic heart murmur, heart rate 66 b.p.m., respiratory rate 23 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 93% on room air, and blood pressure 100/60 mmHg. Laboratory tests were unremarkable except for C-reactive protein 15.9 mg/L (normal <3 mg/L) and troponin I 0.16 ug/L (normal <0.04 ug/L). ECG showed sinus rhythm, ST-segment depression in leads II, III, and aVF, and T-wave inversion in leads I, aVL, and V4-V6. Chest radiography revealed a left pleural effusion, left lower lobe consolidation, and an enlarged cardiac silhouette.

Transthoracic echocardio

title: Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging in a patient with temporary external pacemaker: a case report
case_summary:
A 59-year-old man with chronic back pain presented with a 5-day history of central chest pain and several weeks of malaise, dry cough, and breathlessness. He was pale and clammy, with a blood pressure of 147/76 mm Hg, heart rate 59 b.p.m., oxygen saturation 99% on room air, and no fever. Cardiac examination revealed a regular rhythm and normal heart sounds, and lung auscultation was normal.  
Electrocardiogram showed complete atrioventricular block with inferior Q-waves and a ventricular rate of 58 b.p.m. He had transient loss of consciousness with hypotension, and telemetry confirmed intermittent long pauses due to atrioventricular block.  
Coronary angiography showed atheromatous changes but no obstructive lesions. A temporary transvenous pacemaker was inserted via the right femoral vein and programmed to VVI 60. Prophylactic cloxacillin was started.  
Troponin-T was 17 ng/L on admission (ULN < 15 ng/L) and peaked at 49 ng/L; CK-MB and myoglobin were normal. BNP peaked at 1078 ng/L (

title: Double superior vena cava incidentally found during permanent catheter placement: A case report and literature review
case_summary:
An 86-year-old woman with chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine, 2.5-3.4 mg/dL), asthma, valvular heart disease, and a history of rectal carcinoma was admitted after her family noted progressive sleepiness. She takes nifedipine and hydralazine for hypertension and inhaled symbicort and ipratropium for asthma. She has no allergies and is a lifelong nonsmoker.

On initial evaluation: temperature 36.8  degreesC; blood pressure 128/74 mm Hg; pulse irregular at 110 beats/min; respiratory rate 18 breaths/min; oxygen saturation 93% on ambient air. ECG showed atrial fibrillation. A noncontrast head CT scan was unremarkable. Chest radiography revealed bilateral pleural effusions. Laboratory studies demonstrated hyponatremia and hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis.

The patient was admitted, received IV fluids, bisoprolol, and enoxaparin. Ultrasound-guided left pleurocentesis was complicated by pneumothorax, and a chest tube was placed. Echocardiography showed left ventricular hypertrophy an

title: A case report of successful primary percutaneous coronary intervention to an occluded anomalous left main coronary artery arising from the right coronary sinus
final_diagnosis: Anomalous aortic origin of the left main coronary artery
case_summary: A 65-year-old man presented with sudden onset retrosternal chest pain while riding his bicycle uphill. He was a non-smoker with untreated hypertension and dyslipidaemia. On examination, blood pressure was 168/83 mmHg, pulse was irregular at 108 b.p.m., and oxygen saturation was 94% on room air; crackles were heard at both lung bases. An electrocardiogram showed atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 108 b.p.m., 3 mm of ST-segment elevation in leads I and aVL, and reciprocal ST-segment depression in II, III, aVF, and V2-V6. High-sensitivity troponin-I was 25 ng/L (reference < 4 ng/L). He was diagnosed with a Killip class II STEMI, loaded with aspirin, ticagrelor, and intravenous heparin, and taken for primary percutaneous coronary intervention via right radial access. A Judkins Right 4 catheter engaged the right coronary artery and demonstrated minor diffuse disease in a large dominant vessel. An attempt to engage the left main coronary artery with an Extra Backup Left 3.5 guide

title: Lutembacher syndrome presenting as heart failure in a young Nigerian at Obafemi Awolowo University Teaching Hospitals Complex (OAUTHC), Ile-Ife: a case report
case_summary:
A 28-year-old man presented with 2 months of progressively worsening dyspnea on exertion, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, and bilateral leg swelling. He also reported a 2-week history of cough productive of whitish, frothy sputum. There was no fever, facial swelling, or reduction in urine output, and no history of recurrent sore throats. He denied hypertension or diabetes. He consumed approximately 120 g of alcohol daily for 5 years and did not smoke.

On examination, he was dyspneic and cyanotic (respiratory rate 30/min, SpO? 78% on room air). Pulse was 110/min and regular; blood pressure was 110/60 mmHg. He had bilateral pitting edema to the knees and jugular venous pressure elevated to the angle of the jaw. The apex beat was heaving and displaced to the 7th left intercostal space, anterior axillary line. A left parasternal heave and palpable P? were present. Auscultation revealed an S?, a grade 3/6 pansystolic murmur at both the mitral and tricuspid areas, a systolic ejecti

title: Woven Coronary Artery Disease Successfully Managed with Percutaneous Coronary Intervention: A New Case Report
case_summary: A 54?year?old man presented with a 2-month history of exertional chest tightness and palpitations. On examination, resting ECG showed Q waves and extrasystoles in leads D3 and aVF. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed akinesia of the inferior and posterior walls and an ejection fraction of 44%. Coronary angiography via the right femoral approach demonstrated nonobstructive plaques in the left anterior descending artery and 50% stenosis of the proximal circumflex artery. In the midportion of the right coronary artery, numerous small tortuous channels coalesced distally, with TIMI grade III flow beyond this segment. No other lesions explained his symptoms. Thallium myocardial perfusion scintigraphy showed inferior wall ischemia involving approximately 14% of the left ventricle. Despite optimal medical therapy, his angina persisted, and Holter monitoring detected nonsustained ventricular tachycardia.

title: Cardiac electrical instability in Erdheim-Chester disease: a case report
case_summary:
A 39-year-old woman presented after an accidental fall causing an occipital laceration. She reported months of fatigue, polyuria, polydipsia, intermittent abdominal pain, and severe weight loss. On examination, blood pressure was 110/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, and cardiac auscultation revealed no murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Surface ECG showed sinus rhythm at 85 bpm with diffuse low QRS-wave amplitude and QT prolongation (600 ms by Bazett's formula). Laboratory studies revealed a white blood cell count of 18,590/mm3 (70% neutrophils), C-reactive protein of 154 mg/L, procalcitonin of 2.64 ng/mL, and creatinine of 1.75 mg/dL. 

Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a large pericardial effusion without tamponade and marked thickening of the right atrial free wall (1.65 cm) and interatrial septum; left ventricular systolic function was preserved. Abdominal CT revealed bilateral hydronephrosis, thickening of the perirenal soft tissue, and adrenal gland enlargement.

Durin

title: Dizziness in an avid cyclist: an unusual presentation of a common problem
case_summary: A 75-year-old man with a history of prostate cancer treated by prostatectomy 8 years earlier presented with presyncopal symptoms occurring predictably during vigorous exertion, alleviated by brief rest. He denied chest pain, dyspnoea, or palpitations. He had no cardiovascular risk factors except age and male sex, and no family history of premature coronary artery disease. Physical examination was unremarkable, with normal heart sounds, no murmurs, normal jugular venous pressure, and no peripheral oedema. An electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm with normal PR, QRS, and QTc intervals. A 24-hour ambulatory ECG demonstrated sinus rhythm throughout, good heart rate variability (61-112 beats/minute), and no atrioventricular block. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed normal biventricular systolic function and valvular function. During an exercise treadmill test, he exercised for 9 minutes 19 seconds of the Bruce protocol (11.1 METS), achieved 97% of target heart rate, and developed <1 mm

title: Cardiac interventricular septum hemangioma in a colon cancer patient treated with Capecitabine: A case report and review of literature
case_summary: A 21-year-old man with stage IIIB microsatellite-instability-high sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma, recently completing eight cycles of adjuvant XELOX chemotherapy, presented with atypical chest pain and shortness of breath. On examination, he was tachypneic (RR 24/min), hypertensive (150/90 mmHg), and tachycardic (HR 108 bpm), with oxygen saturation 93% on room air. Lung auscultation revealed decreased breath sounds bilaterally, and cardiac auscultation revealed a grade II systolic ejection murmur at the left sternal border. ECG showed sinus tachycardia with nonspecific ST-T changes, and troponin was normal. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a 2.7 ? 4.7 cm hyperechoic mass with irregular borders in the basal to mid interventricular septum and an ejection fraction of 35%. Contrast echocardiography revealed a well-defined vascular mass within the septum. Cardiac MRI confirmed an intramyocardial septal mass measuring 2.5 ? 3.8 cm that was iso-intense on T1-weighted images, hyperint

title: A rare presentation of spontaneous atheroembolic renal disease: A case report
case_summary:
A 62-year-old woman with stage IIIa chronic kidney disease (baseline creatinine 1.5 mg/dL) was referred for rapidly worsening renal function (creatinine now 5.62 mg/dL over 4 months) associated with hyperkalemia and metabolic acidosis. Two weeks earlier she had noted bilateral lower?extremity edema and dyspnea on exertion; she denied hemoptysis, chest pain, nausea, or vomiting. One month prior, she had two episodes of epistaxis. She had no history of invasive vascular procedures, contrast exposure, or anticoagulant or thrombolytic therapy.

Her medical history included poorly controlled hypertension, hyperlipidemia, transient ischemic attack, diastolic heart failure, reactive airway disease, nephrolithiasis post?lithotripsy, and osteoarthritis treated with NSAIDs. She was a non?smoker and did not drink alcohol. On examination, vital signs were normal; there was bilateral lower?extremity pitting edema and diminished breath sounds at the lung bases.

Laboratory tests showed normocytic, n

title: Case report: human granulocytic anaplasmosis causes acute myopericarditis with atrial fibrillation
case_summary:
A 65-year-old man with hypertension and depression presented in July with 1 week of fevers and malaise. Outpatient evaluation included an unremarkable chest x-ray, a negative nasopharyngeal swab for COVID-19, and an ECG showing sinus rhythm with an incomplete right bundle branch block and left anterior fascicular block. Symptoms persisted, and he was referred from an ambulatory respiratory unit to the ED.  

On arrival: temperature 97.7  degreesF, heart rate 63 bpm, blood pressure 74/44 mm Hg, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Examination revealed crackles at the right lung base and a II/VI holosystolic murmur at the apex.  

Laboratory studies: AST 89 IU/L, ALT 103 IU/L, proBNP 16 093 pg/mL, troponin T 0.79 ng/mL, CRP > 300 mg/L, creatinine 2.6 mg/dL (baseline 1.1), and lactate 3.4 mmol/L. ECG showed atrial fibrillation, a non-specific intraventricular conduction delay, and minimal ST-segment elevation in the lateral leads.  

He lives in a wooded

title: A Rare Case of Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Bladder Presenting as a Metastatic Right Ventricular Mass
final_diagnosis: Squamous cell carcinoma of the bladder
case_summary:
A 74-year-old woman presented with a 6-month history of bilateral lower-extremity swelling and progressive dyspnea on exertion. She also noted occasional mild cough with scant hemoptysis but denied chest pain, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, weight loss, or fevers. Her medical history was notable for non-insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus and hypertension; she had never smoked and drank alcohol only occasionally. Two weeks before transfer, an outside hospital evaluation including echocardiography and cardiac catheterization was normal, but she returned with persistent symptoms. A contrast-enhanced CT of the chest showed a filling defect in the right ventricle extending into the right ventricular outflow tract, occlusions of bilateral segmental pulmonary arteries, a small to moderate pericardial effusion, and small bilateral pleural effusions. Transthoracic echocardiography confirmed a right ventricular mass.  

On transfer, she was afebrile; blood pressure was 103/56 mmHg, pu

title: A rare case report of catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia with an uncommon CALM2 mutation
case_summary: A 21-year-old woman with autism spectrum disorder (on risperidone) and intellectual disability was brought to the hospital after a witnessed collapse. She had no prior history of syncope, cardiac arrest, or known cardiovascular disease, and her family denied any illicit drug or alcohol use. While at a noisy auto mechanic shop, she became pale and lost consciousness; bystander ECG showed ventricular fibrillation. Return of spontaneous circulation was achieved after two defibrillations. On arrival, her blood pressure was 93/70 mmHg, pulse 86 bpm, and oxygen saturation 100% (bag-valve-mask). An intravenous amiodarone infusion (1 mg/min) and oral amiodarone 400 mg were administered. On examination she was comatose (GCS 3), with agonal respirations; cardiovascular examination was unremarkable. In the intensive care unit, her differential diagnosis included channelopathies, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, and metabolic abnormalities. Laboratory tests showed normal chemistry panel and electrolyt

title: Clozapine-induced hypersensitivity myocarditis presenting as sudden cardiac death
case_summary:
A 54-year-old Caucasian man with anxiety, bipolar disorder, and stage III chronic kidney disease secondary to hypertensive nephrosclerosis was referred for evaluation of shortness of breath and tachycardia of 6 weeks' duration. He attributed his dyspnea to anxiety and denied chest pain, palpitations, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, or peripheral edema. He had been treated with clozapine for the previous 6 weeks. Vital signs were: heart rate 110 beats per minute, respiratory rate 20 breaths per minute, blood pressure 118/78 mmHg, temperature normal, and oxygen saturation 96% on room air. Jugular venous pressure was normal.

An electrocardiogram showed sinus tachycardia with nonspecific ST-T-wave changes. Chest radiography demonstrated minimal lineal bibasilar atelectasis. Laboratory studies revealed: troponin T < 0.01 ng/mL (normal < 0.01), NT-proBNP 750 pg/mL (normal 0-125), D-dimer 3.1 ug/mL (normal 0.00-0.50), normal white blood cell count with a normal eosinophil count, and

title: Myocarditis with concomitant tuberculosis infection presenting with solitary ventricular tachycardia: a case report
case_summary: A 36-year-old man presented with a 1-week history of palpitations and dyspnea. He had stable vital signs and an unremarkable physical examination. ECG showed sinus rhythm with frequent monomorphic ventricular extrasystoles and negative T waves in the inferior leads. High-sensitivity troponin T was mildly elevated (23-26 ng/L; normal < 13 ng/L). Chest radiography was normal. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed mild left ventricular hypertrophy, preserved ejection fraction, and no segmental wall-motion abnormalities. Continuous monitoring captured symptomatic runs of slow non-sustained ventricular tachycardia. Coronary angiography was normal. Laboratory tests showed normal electrolytes, ferritin, renal and thyroid function; N-terminal pro-brain natriuretic peptide was 55 ng/L; C-reactive protein was mildly elevated at 7.8 mg/L. Despite escalating bisoprolol, he had a hemodynamically tolerated sustained ventricular tachycardia and was started on amiodarone, which reduced the arrhythmi

title: Splenic Abscesses in a Returning Traveler
case_summary: A 68-year-old man with coronary artery disease, hypertension, type II diabetes mellitus, and paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia returned to the U.S. one month after spending nine months in the Philippines. Five months into his stay, he had a myocardial infarction treated with angioplasty and stenting. Three months later, he was hospitalized there for fevers and left upper quadrant abdominal pain, which resolved with an unknown course of oral antibiotics. One month after his return, he presented with progressively worsening left upper quadrant pain. His temperature was 97.5 degreesF (36.4 degreesC), blood pressure 127/69 mmHg, pulse 117 beats/min (irregularly irregular), respiratory rate 27/min, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Physical examination revealed tenderness in the left upper quadrant. Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen and pelvis showed multiple ill-defined hypodense lesions in the spleen. He was started on oral ciprofloxacin and metronidazole, later changed to in

title: Catecholamine-induced cardiomyopathy in a patient with pheochromocytoma and polycystic kidney and liver disease: a case report
case_summary:
A 47-year-old Caucasian woman presented with a 1-day history of retrosternal burning chest pain, diffuse abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, and fatigue. Her blood pressure was 200/100?mmHg and heart rate 88?b.p.m.; examination revealed right hypochondriac tenderness but no murmurs, no jugular venous distension, and clear lung fields. She had a history of hypertension treated intermittently with amlodipine and indapamide, and 6 months earlier had presented with similar chest pain, ST changes, mildly elevated troponin, global left ventricular hypokinesis (EF 36%), and a normal coronary angiogram; that episode was labeled "acute myocarditis." Her mother and aunt had polycystic kidney disease.  

Laboratory studies showed a white blood cell count of 18?10^3/L and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 46?mm/h; electrolytes, troponin I, and renal function were normal. Electrocardiography demonstrated 1.0-2.5?mm ST depression in leads II and V2-V6 and >0.5?mm ST elevation in aVR. Chest radiogra

title: Atypical twin atrioventricular nodal re-entrant tachycardia in a congenitally corrected transposition of the great arteries patient with a surgical repair of a ventricular septal defect: a case report
case_summary:
A 15-year-old teenager with complex congenital heart disease presented to the emergency department with incessant palpitations and dizziness. On arrival, a regular narrow-complex tachycardia at 125 beats per minute was observed, with a long RP? interval and negative P waves in the inferior leads. The tachycardia was briefly terminated by intravenous adenosine but immediately recurred. In sinus rhythm, the QRS-axis remained unchanged, and P waves became positive in the inferior leads, indicating atrial activation from the sinus node.

The patient was born with situs solitus congenitally corrected transposition of the great arteries, mild sub-pulmonary stenosis, and a perimembranous ventricular septal defect. He underwent pulmonary artery banding at 3 months and surgical closure of the VSD at age 3. He continued to have residual pulmonary arterial hypertension and hypoxia (85-90%) from a residual right-to-left shunt. Apart from the arrhythmia episodes, he was New York Heart Association C

title: Rapid effect of benralizumab in fulminant eosinophilic myocarditis in the setting of uncontrolled eosinophilic asthma
case_summary:
A 51-year-old man with poorly controlled asthma, diabetes mellitus, and hypertension presented with a 1-week history of productive cough, worsening dyspnea, and chest pain. The chest pain was constant, worse when supine, and relieved by leaning forward. He had been hospitalized for SARS-CoV-2 pneumonia one month earlier. 

On examination, he was afebrile; blood pressure was 176/105 mmHg, heart rate 117 beats/min, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 87% on room air. Findings included jugular venous distention, bilateral lower extremity pitting edema, tachypnea, and bilateral expiratory wheezing.

Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 26.6 ? 10^3/?L with 32% eosinophils (8,512 cells/?L) and a troponin I level of 1.45 ng/mL (reference 0.00-0.05 ng/mL). Electrocardiography revealed subtle ST-segment depressions in the anterolateral leads. He was started on a heparin infusion for presumed non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction, and coronary angiography demonstra

title: Infective endocarditis following percutaneous balloon mitral valvuloplasty: A case report of a 25?year?old female from Jimma Medical Center of Ethiopia
case_summary:
A 25-year-old woman with known chronic rheumatic valvular heart disease presented in her third trimester with worsening exertional dyspnea, orthopnea requiring two pillows, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, and lower-extremity edema. She also had a one-year history of episodic cough productive of blood-tinged sputum and recent pleuritic chest pain. Transthoracic echocardiography before intervention showed thickened and doming mitral leaflets with restricted motion, a mitral valve area of 1.2 cm2, a mean transmitral gradient of 24 mm Hg, pulmonary hypertension (systolic pressure 164 mm Hg), and preserved left ventricular systolic function; all other valves were normal. Her Wilkins score was <8. She underwent percutaneous balloon mitral valvuloplasty using a 25 mm Mansfield balloon, with resolution of her symptoms. She delivered a healthy term infant without complications.

Forty-five days after the procedure, she developed high-grade persistent fever, easy fatigability, weight loss, headach

title: Flagellate dermatitis: An atypical skin finding in adult?onset Still's disease
case_summary: A 23?year?old woman with no relevant past history presented with a 3?week history of daily fevers up to 39.4 degreesC, profound fatigue, odynophagia, loss of appetite, and polyarthralgia involving the elbows, wrists, hands, knees, and ankles. She reported no photosensitivity, malar rash, muscle weakness, chest pain, or palpitations at that time. On examination, she was febrile (39.4 degreesC) with mild swelling and tenderness of the wrists and interphalangeal joints. There was a diffuse, nonpruritic macular erythematous eruption appearing with fever spikes on the neck, trunk, and upper limbs. Initial ECG was normal. Laboratory studies revealed leukocytosis (30,350/?l with 95% neutrophils), hemoglobin of 9.3 g/dl, CRP of 286 mg/L, ESR of 150 mm, and ferritin of 18,000 ng/ml. Liver function tests were normal. ANA, rheumatoid factor, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibody were negative, as were parvovirus B19 IgM, CMV, HIV, and hepatitis B and C serologies. Chest radiograph, abdom

title: Therapeutic Potential of a Vasopressin V2 Receptor Antagonist for Calcium Channel Blocker-Associated Edema with Vasospastic Angina
final_diagnosis: Calcium channel blocker associated peripheral edema
case_summary:
A 78-year-old man was admitted with progressive bilateral lower extremity swelling that was severe enough to prevent him from wearing his usual shoes. He also noted swelling of his face and upper extremities. His history was notable for vasospastic angina diagnosed two months earlier (with transient ST-segment elevation relieved by sublingual nitroglycerin and catheter-cine coronary angiography demonstrating reversible left anterior descending stenosis). He had chronic kidney disease and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. His medications included nifedipine 80?mg daily, diltiazem 200?mg daily, and a nitroglycerin patch.

On admission, blood pressure was 126/62?mmHg, pulse 83?beats/min, and body weight had increased by 10?kg from his baseline. Physical examination showed pitting edema of both lower extremities; jugular veins were not distended. Laboratory tests revealed creatinine 1.25?mg/dL and B-type natriuretic peptide 128?pg/mL, both unchanged from his usual values. Serum albumin, live

title: Isolated right ventricular myocardial infarction: a case report
case_summary:
A 61-year-old woman with a history of smoking and hypercholesterolaemia presented with sudden onset of shortness of breath, dizziness, and chest pain while walking. Her ECG showed ST-segment elevation in the inferior leads and V1-V3 with transient right bundle branch block and junctional bradycardia. She received aspirin, ticagrelor, and prophylactic enoxaparin. Transradial coronary angiography revealed a normal left coronary system and a dominant left circumflex artery without obstructive disease; the right coronary artery could not be engaged despite multiple catheter attempts. A left ventriculogram showed normal left ventricular systolic function.

On examination, she had an elevated jugular venous pressure, clear lung fields, a pansystolic murmur, hypotension, bradycardia, oliguria, and signs of hypoperfusion. Her NEWS score was 10 (RR 27/min, SpO2 88% on room air, T 35.9 degreesC, BP 98/54?mmHg, pulse 53?bpm). She was started on 4?L/min oxygen and intravenous dobutamine, which imp

title: Reversible Tricuspid Regurgitation Due to?Beriberi
case_summary:
A 76-year-old man was referred with 20 days of progressive dyspnea and generalized edema. He was hemodynamically stable (heart rate 72 beats/min, sinus rhythm), well perfused, with jugular venous distension, bibasilar crackles, a systolic murmur at the tricuspid area, voluminous ascites, and marked peripheral edema. His BMI was 27.8 kg/m2. On admission, transthoracic echocardiography showed right ventricular systolic dysfunction (fractional area change 33%), severe tricuspid regurgitation (vena contracta 14 mm), preserved left ventricular function, a normally functioning aortic bioprosthesis, and a cardiac index of 8.0 L/min/m2. A TTE 9 months earlier had been normal. His history included hypertension, hypothyroidism, chronic alcoholism, low meat intake with weight loss over 6 months, aortic valve bioprosthesis, and coronary bypass grafting. 

Investigations included 3D transesophageal echocardiography, which confirmed severe tricuspid regurgitation (effective regurgitant orifice area

title: Eosinophilic myocarditis during treatment of acute myeloid leukaemia: cardiac magnetic resonance in the very early phase mimicking triple-vessel coronary artery disease: a case report
case_summary: A 57-year-old woman with no prior cardiac history was admitted for newly diagnosed acute myeloid leukemia. On arrival, laboratory studies showed a leukocytosis of 31.9?10^9/L with an eosinophil count of 7.66?10^9/L and normocytic anemia. Baseline echocardiography demonstrated preserved biventricular function without valvular disease. She began induction chemotherapy with cytarabine and daunorubicin. Two days after starting systemic chemotherapy, she developed chest pain. She had cardiovascular risk factors of hypertension and a smoking history. An ECG showed anterior and inferior ST-segment depressions, and troponin I was elevated at 690?ng/L (normal <14?ng/L) alongside an eosinophil count of 33.3?10^9/L. Because of thrombocytopenia, she underwent cardiac magnetic resonance imaging instead of immediate catheterization. Stress perfusion CMR revealed a new left ventricular ejection fraction of 32% with global hypokinesia and extensive sub-endocardial hypoperfusion in all coronary territo

title: Thyroid Storm Superimposed on Gestational Hypertension: A Case Report and Review of Literature
case_summary:
A 38-year-old G2P1 woman with a dichorionic twin pregnancy at 35+5 weeks was brought for an emergency cesarean section because of failure to progress. She had a 7-year history of hyperthyroidism treated with propylthiouracil 50 mg twice daily throughout pregnancy; her free T4 level had ranged from 1 to 3 ng/dL, and 6 days before surgery it was 2 ng/dL. She was diagnosed with gestational hypertension at 24 weeks, with systolic pressures rising from 145 mmHg to 175 mmHg by 35 weeks; proteinuria was absent. 

On arrival in the operating room, she reported abdominal pain and mild nausea. Vital signs were notable for heart rate 124 beats/min, blood pressure 210/117 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 95% on room air. During preparation for spinal anesthesia she developed dyspnea; oxygen saturation fell to 90% despite 10 L/min via mask, and arterial PaO2 was 58 mmHg. Electrolytes were within normal limits. Blood pressure acutely rose to 240/120 mmHg with heart rate 200 beats/min, and she became irri

title: MitraClip detachment after electrical cardioversion: a case report
case_summary:
An 86-year-old woman presented with several months of exertional dyspnoea, malaise, and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea. Her medical history included hypertension (on ramipril), paroxysmal atrial fibrillation (on rivaroxaban and bisoprolol), and a COVID-19 infection 7 months earlier. She was first noted to have moderate mitral regurgitation (MR) in 2013, with stable disease until 2021, when she developed severe MR with apical tethering of the leaflets and moderate tricuspid regurgitation (RV systolic pressure 38 mmHg). A SPECT thallium dipyridamole scan showed no ischemia. Physical examination revealed a mitral systolic murmur; the remainder was unremarkable.

Because of high operative risk, a heart team recommended transcatheter edge-to-edge repair (TEER). Transoesophageal echocardiography (TOE) showed severe MR with a jet originating from the medial commissure. Baseline parameters were: regurgitant volume 71 mL, effective regurgitant orifice area 0.37 cm?, vena contracta 0.65 cm; pulmo

title: A case report of eosinophilic granulomatosis and polyangiitis myocarditis presenting as ST elevation myocardial infarction and showing positive response to immunotherapy
case_summary: A 44-year-old Caucasian woman with a 6-month history of asthma treated with inhaled corticosteroid and ?-agonist presented with sudden onset central crushing chest pain, shortness of breath, and collapse. An ECG showed anterior ST elevation in leads V2-V5. She had no conventional risk factors for ischemic heart disease. On examination, cardiovascular findings were unremarkable; respiratory examination revealed bi-basal crepitations, more prominent on the left. Troponin-T was 772?ng/L (normal <14?ng/L). A chest radiograph showed extensive left-sided consolidation. Urgent coronary angiography demonstrated unobstructed coronary arteries, with diffuse coronary spasm that resolved with intracoronary nitrate. A working diagnosis of myopericarditis secondary to pneumonia was made, and the patient received intravenous co-amoxiclav with supportive care. Viral swabs and urinary antigens for atypical pneumonia were negative, and serological viral and autoimmune screens for myocarditis were negati

title: Cushing syndrome cardiomyopathy: an unusual manifestation of small?cell lung cancer
case_summary: A 64-year-old man with a history of coronary artery disease (inferolateral MI in 2008 with left?circumflex stent), well?controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension, obstructive sleep apnoea, and tobacco use presented with 2 weeks of shortness of breath, 20 lb weight gain, lower extremity oedema, and hyperglycaemia. He had preserved left ventricular ejection fraction and no prior congestive heart failure. On examination, blood pressure was 189/101 mmHg; jugular venous distention, bilateral rales, and pitting oedema were noted. Transthoracic echocardiography showed new global hypokinesia with an ejection fraction of 30-35%, mildly increased wall thickness (interventricular septum 1.1 cm, posterior wall 1.1 cm), normal cavity size (end-diastolic diameter 5.7 cm; volume 135 mL; indexed 61.6 mL/m2), normal relative wall thickness (0.39), and normal LV mass index (96.2 g/m2). Biplane ejection fraction was 31%, and global longitudinal strain was reduced at -11.4%. Diastolic parameter

title: Ivabradine toxicity: a case report
case_summary:
A 43-year-old woman presented with several days of dizziness culminating in a near-syncopal episode. She had been taking ivabradine 5 mg twice daily for symptomatic inappropriate sinus tachycardia (baseline heart rate ~110 bpm) but stopped it 10 days before presentation when her heart rate dropped to 50 bpm. On arrival, she was afebrile; heart rate 55 bpm; blood pressure 92/55 mmHg; oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Orthostatic vitals were normal. Physical examination was unremarkable except for a regular bradycardic pulse.  
Electrocardiogram showed an ectopic atrial rhythm with heart rate 41 bpm (PR interval 120 ms, QRS 80 ms, QTc 397 ms). Overnight telemetry demonstrated sinus arrest and intermittent junctional rhythm. A recent baseline ECG had shown normal sinus rhythm.  
Her medical history included qualitative platelet dysfunction with recurrent thromboses on apixaban, granulosa cystic ovarian tumor, right nephrectomy, chronic pancreatitis, adrenal insufficiency, and hypertensio

title: Masquerade of an emergency: cardiac tamponade as a deceptive presentation of primary cardiac diffuse large b-cell lymphoma--a case report
case_summary: An 80-year-old man was admitted with severe, progressive dyspnoea without chest pain. On examination, blood pressure was 75/54 mmHg, heart rate 57 beats/minute, and oxygen saturation 95% on 15 L/min via non-rebreathing mask. Heart sounds were muffled and extremities were cold, pale, with early marbling; jugular veins were not distended. His history included chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and recurrent paroxysmal atrial fibrillation after failed pulmonary vein isolation, treated with a factor Xa inhibitor, a beta-blocker, an angiotensin receptor blocker, and a beta2-agonist. The electrocardiogram showed sinus bradycardia with ST-segment elevation in leads V2-V3 and T-wave inversions in leads II, III, and aVF. Troponin T was elevated at 0.044 ug/L, and lactate was 3.0 mmol/L. Initial transthoracic echocardiography performed by the emergency physician revealed a pericardial mass, raising suspicion of a covered perforation or left ventricular free-wall rupture due to myocardial infa

title: Post-cardiac injury syndrome after transcatheter mitral valve repair using MitraClip system: a case report
case_summary: A 79-year-old woman with idiopathic dilated cardiomyopathy, atrial fibrillation, and arterial hypertension underwent successful transcatheter mitral valve repair with one MitraClip. Transthoracic echocardiography immediately and 1 day after the procedure showed no pericardial effusion. On post-procedural Day 3, she reported fatigue, malaise, and non-specific epigastric pain. Laboratory studies showed C-reactive protein 70 mg/L (normal <5), white blood cell count 9/nL, and procalcitonin 0.16 ng/mL (normal <0.5). Chest X-ray revealed new bilateral pleural effusions without congestion or infiltrate. Electrocardiogram was unchanged, and blood cultures remained sterile. On Day 5, TTE demonstrated a mild circular pericardial effusion (8 mm); by Day 7, this progressed to 13 mm without hemodynamic compromise. Cardiac CT showed pericardial and pleural effusions of similar low density (~10 HU), suggesting serous fluid.

title: Fat embolism syndrome: A severe case diagnosed early using dual?energy CT and saved with VA?ECMO and inhaled nitric oxide
case_summary: An 86-year-old man was brought to the emergency department after being found in cardiac arrest 6 hours after a fall at home. His medical history included hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and a pacemaker for complete atrioventricular block. On arrival, his Glasgow Coma Scale was 3 (eyes 1, verbal 1, motor 1); blood pressure, 110/70 mmHg; heart rate, 100 beats/min; oxygen saturation, 50% on 15 L/min by bag-valve-mask with agonal respirations; and temperature, 35.4 degreesC. The extremities were warm without edema, and neurologic reflexes were intact. ECG showed sinus rhythm with atrial-sensing ventricular pacing and no ST-segment changes. Laboratory data revealed anemia, elevated inflammatory markers, metabolic acidosis with a pH of 7.21, PaO2 of 17.6 mmHg on high-flow oxygen, lactate of 8.22 mmol/L, and a D-dimer of 14.6 ug/mL. Chest radiography showed bilateral segmental infiltrates and pleural effusions. The patient was intubated for severe respiratory failure. CT of the head w

title: Pulmonary tumor thrombotic microangiopathy and pulmonary veno-occlusive disease in a woman with cervical cancer treated with cediranib and durvalumab
case_summary:
A 45-year-old woman with metastatic squamous cell carcinoma of the cervix refractory to standard chemotherapy was enrolled in a clinical trial of daily cediranib and biweekly durvalumab. After four cycles she reported progressive dyspnea on exertion and fatigue over one month. On admission, temperature was 37  degreesC, heart rate 120 beats/min, blood pressure 90/72 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 93-97% on room air. Examination revealed normal jugular venous pressure, regular heart rhythm without a prominent P2, clear lung fields, and no peripheral edema.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, platelet count 159 k/?L, D-dimer 0.98 ?g/mL, fibrinogen 517 mg/dL, pro-BNP 4541 pg/mL, and troponin-T 0.022 ng/mL. Chest radiography demonstrated hazy bibasilar interstitial markings. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed right ventricular dilation with TAPSE 8 mm (normal ?17 mm) and estimated right ventricular systolic pressure of 67 mmHg (new from baseline). Cardiac MRI confirmed a righ

title: Noteworthy Cardiovascular Involvement with Sporadic Late-onset Nemaline Myopathy
case_summary:
A 66-year-old woman was referred for management of right-sided heart failure. She had severe scoliosis diagnosed at age 14 and bradycardia (heart rate ~30/min) at age 27. At 39, she experienced cardiac arrest due to complete atrioventricular block and underwent dual-chamber pacemaker implantation after a negative work-up, including myocardial biopsy with conventional staining. She had additional cardiac arrests at ages 40, 46, and 47 despite a functioning pacemaker. At 62, she was diagnosed with sleep apnea and treated with CPAP.  
On admission at 66 years, transthoracic echocardiography showed an ejection fraction of approximately 20% and pulmonary hypertension (estimated pulmonary artery pressure ~60 mmHg); left ventricular function later improved, but mitral regurgitation and pulmonary hypertension persisted. Pacemaker interrogation revealed loss of atrial capture and sinus node dysfunction, prompting reprogramming to VVIR mode.  
Arterial blood gas analysis in room air showed PaCO2

title: Cardiac light-chain deposition disease and hints at diagnosing: a case report
case_summary: A 65-year-old Japanese man with end-stage renal disease on haemodialysis was referred for evaluation of chronic heart failure.
