His medical history included hypertension, dyslipidaemia, and chronic kidney disease, with progressive elevation of serum creatinine to 3.4 mg/dL over 7 years. Eight years before presentation, urinary immunoelectrophoresis revealed 1.1 g/day of ? type Bence-Jones protein and serum free-light-chain assay showed markedly elevated ? light chain, but renal biopsy was not performed. Five years ago he began haemodialysis. Four years ago he had his first congestive heart failure (CHF) episode; despite dry-weight reduction and initiation of bisoprolol, he developed a second episode three years ago. Echocardiography over this period showed progressive diastolic dysfunction, increasing left ventricular wall thickness, and high-voltage ECG findings. One year ago, left ventricular ejection fraction fell from 71% to 50.9%. Six months ago he developed fatigue, anorexia, and

title: Endomyocardial fibrosis presented by ventricular tachycardia: case report
case_summary: A 45-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and no history of hypertension or cardiac disease presented with 5 days of recurrent episodes of rapid, regular palpitations. On the day of admission, the palpitations persisted and were accompanied by nausea and sweating. His blood pressure was unrecordable, and his heart rate was 180 beats per minute. Electrocardiography showed ventricular tachycardia at 180 beats per minute with a left bundle-branch-block morphology and an inferior axis. Urgent direct cardioversion restored sinus rhythm at 80 beats per minute, with a right bundle-branch block, left-axis deviation, and inverted T waves in the right precordial leads. Laboratory studies, including cardiac biomarkers and a complete blood count, were within normal limits, with no eosinophilia. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a dilated right atrium and right ventricle with obliteration and retraction of the right ventricular apex, mild tricuspid regurgitation, preserved biventricular systolic

title: Long?term renal survival of ?3?heavy?chain deposition disease complicated by heart failure: A case report
case_summary:
A 52-year-old woman presented with a 3-month history of progressive peripheral edema, heavy proteinuria, and worsening dyspnea. One year earlier, a renal biopsy had shown nodular glomerulosclerosis, and she was treated with valsartan for 3 months without improvement. During this interval, serum creatinine increased from 0.8 to 1.7 mg/dL and 24-hour urinary protein rose from 1.51 to 2.19 g. 

On admission, blood pressure was 169/97 mmHg, heart rate 62/min, and BMI 22 kg/m2. Examination revealed bilateral pitting edema of the lower limbs; there was no jaundice, rash, or lymphadenopathy. 

Laboratory studies showed BUN 23.25 mg/dL, creatinine 1.92 mg/dL, eGFR 29.5 mL/min/1.73 m2, albumin 27.9 g/L, and 24-hour proteinuria 4.71 g. Urinalysis demonstrated 3+ protein, 40-60 RBCs/high-power field, and 40 WBCs/high-power field without RBC casts. Complement levels were decreased (C3 0.4 g/L [0.7-1.4], C4 0.04 g/L [0.1-0.4], C1q 121.6 mg/L [160-230]). Serologic tests for ANA, anti-ENA, ANCA, and

title: Nonfatal pulmonary embolism associated with the use of compression stockings in the lithotomy position after spinal anesthesia
case_summary:
A 73-year-old man (ASA II; height 156 cm; weight 51 kg) with benign prostatic enlargement was scheduled for transurethral resection of the prostate under spinal anesthesia. His medical history was notable only for ureterorenoscopic stone manipulation one month earlier under spinal anesthesia, without complications or hypersensitivity reactions to bupivacaine, midazolam, or cefazolin. He had no history of cardiovascular disease or thromboembolism. 

Preoperatively, his blood pressure was 155/98 mmHg, heart rate 66 beats/min, respiratory rate 12 breaths/min. Physical examination revealed no leg swelling or tenderness. ECG, chest radiograph, and coagulation tests were unremarkable. Spinal anesthesia was established with 12 mg of 0.5% hyperbaric bupivacaine, achieving a sensory block to T6, and 400 mL of crystalloid was infused without hemodynamic instability. Cefazolin was administered, and 100% O? at 6 L/min was delivered by mask. 

Before surgery, compression stockings were applied and

title: Retrieval of Entrapped Catheter-Mounted Axial Flow Pump From Mitral Subvalvular Apparatus Using a Snare Catheter
case_summary:
A 67-year-old man presented with a 4-day history of dull central chest pain and shortness of breath. He was hemodynamically stable, and physical examination was unremarkable.  
Electrocardiogram showed Q waves in leads V1 and V2 with ST-segment depression in the inferolateral leads. High-sensitivity troponin I was elevated at 11,720 ng/L.  
Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a left ventricular ejection fraction of approximately 35% with thinning and akinesis of the mid and apical anterior wall segments.  
The patient was diagnosed with non-ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction and underwent emergent coronary angiography, which showed severe distal left main stem disease involving the ostia of the left anterior descending and left circumflex arteries, severe proximal left anterior descending disease, and moderate mid right coronary artery disease.  
He was admitted for high-risk percutaneous coronary intervention with hemodynamic support. Via a single femoral-access technique,

title: Mechanism of Drug-Eluting Stent Thrombosis Diagnosed by Histopathological Evaluation of Directional Coronary Atherectomy Specimen
case_summary:
An 89-year-old man was brought in with dyspnea at rest. He was afebrile; blood pressure 180/110 mm Hg; heart rate 110 beats/min; respiratory rate 30 breaths/min; oxygen saturation 90% on 10 L/min O?. Laboratory tests showed B-type natriuretic peptide 1,757.5 pg/mL and troponin I 818.7 pg/mL, with no creatine kinase elevation. ECG revealed atrial fibrillation without ST-segment elevation. Chest radiography demonstrated pulmonary congestion. Transthoracic echocardiography showed hypokinesis of the left ventricular anteroseptal wall. He was diagnosed with non-ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction and acute decompensated heart failure.

His history was notable for diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidemia, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, and chronic kidney disease. Twelve years earlier, he had a sirolimus-eluting stent (SES) placed in the left anterior descending artery. Fourteen months before admission, he underwent balloon dilatation and paclitaxel-coated balloon (PCB) treatment for an i

title: Diagnosis and Treatment of Paroxysmal Sympathetic Hyperactivity in Medical ICU, University of Gondar Hospital, Northwest Ethiopia: A Case Report
case_summary:
A 35-year-old woman with chronic rheumatic heart disease (severe mitral stenosis with pulmonary hypertension) underwent percutaneous balloon mitral valvotomy 6 weeks earlier. A left atrial thrombus was detected afterward, and she was started on warfarin, titrated to an INR of 2.5. She self-discontinued warfarin 3 days before presentation.

She presented to the emergency department with 1 day of severe headache and recurrent non-bloody vomiting, a recent history of nasal bleeding, and a 1-week history of fever, malaise, and generalized weakness. On examination, vital signs were stable. Cardiovascular exam showed an accentuated P2 and a mid-diastolic plus pan-systolic murmur at the mitral area. Neurologic and meningeal signs were normal.

Laboratory studies revealed WBC 25,000/uL (85% neutrophils), hemoglobin 12 g/dL, platelets 350,000/uL, PT 35 sec, PTT 53 sec, INR 2.5; LFTs and RFTs were within normal limits. ECG showed P-mitrale, RBBB, and ST-T changes consistent with left atrial enla

title: A case report of isolated right ventricular lymphocytic myocarditis
case_summary:
A 22-year-old Caucasian woman was first evaluated in 2014 for dyspnoea, chronic cutaneous eruptions, and symmetrical polyarthritis. High-resolution chest computed tomography showed pulmonary fibrosis, and lung biopsy led to a diagnosis of hypersensitivity pneumonitis in the setting of significant mould exposure. Skin biopsies were consistent with spongiotic dermatitis (eczema). Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated right ventricular dilatation with mild tricuspid regurgitation and normal left ventricular size and function. Right heart catheterization revealed normal pulmonary arterial pressures.

Over the next 4 years, she received methotrexate, sulfasalazine, and intermittent prednisone. In mid-2018, she reported dyspnoea on walking 50 m and four episodes of syncope at rest. On examination, blood pressure was 130/70 mmHg, heart rate 88 b.p.m. (regular), and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Jugular venous pressure was elevated with prominent V waves, and there was a right ventr

title: Conservatively treated intramyocardial dissecting haematoma of the interventricular septum as a rare complication of acute myocardial infarction: a case report
case_summary: A 60-year-old man with hypertension, dyslipidemia, and a family history of heart disease presented with 6 hours of moderate chest pain. He had a history of episodes of brief dull left shoulder pain over the preceding month and was blind from hereditary retinopathy. On arrival, blood pressure was 150/100 mmHg. Cardiac auscultation and respiratory examination were unremarkable. ECG showed sinus rhythm at 80 beats/min, 3 mm ST-segment elevation, and formed Q waves in the anterior and lateral leads. High-sensitivity troponin was markedly elevated. The patient received 300 mg aspirin and 180 mg ticagrelor and was taken for urgent catheterization. Coronary angiography revealed occlusion of the proximal left anterior descending artery; two drug-eluting stents were placed with restoration of TIMI 3 flow and no visible complications. A 70% stenosis in the mid right coronary artery was left untreated. He remained symptom-free on dual antiplatelet therapy, a proton-pump inhibitor, and a high-inte

title: Intermittent complete atrioventricular block in a 20-year-old woman with cardiac sarcoidosis: a case report
case_summary:
A 20-year-old Caucasian woman presented with exertional dyspnoea and recurrent dizziness. A 12-lead ECG showed complete atrioventricular (AV) block with a ventricular escape rhythm at 34 bpm. On admission, she was asymptomatic at rest. Vital signs: temperature 37.3  degreesC, blood pressure 135/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 15/min, oxygen saturation 100%. Physical examination was unremarkable; no peripheral lymphadenopathy, skin lesions, murmurs, or oedema. There was no personal or family history of cardiac arrhythmias.

Laboratory studies: normal blood counts, cardiac enzymes, and inflammatory markers. Electrolytes showed sodium 134 mmol/L and potassium 4.70 mmol/L on admission, which normalized on repeat testing. Renal, liver, and thyroid function tests were normal. PCR for SARS-CoV-2 was negative. Serologic testing for Lyme disease and multiple viruses was negative.

Transthoracic echocardiography: left ventricular ejection fraction 60%, mild mitral and tricuspid regurgitation, no regio

title: Anesthetic management of a rare case of extra-adrenal pheochromocytoma
case_summary: A 14-year-old boy presented with a 5-month history of intermittent headache, vomiting, and generalized weakness, accompanied by unintended weight loss. He denied palpitations or syncope. On examination, his pulse was 120 beats/min; blood pressure was 160/110 mm?Hg supine and 150/100 mm?Hg standing. Airway, cardiovascular, respiratory, abdominal, and neurologic examinations were unremarkable. Initial laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 12.4 g/dL, white blood cell count 12 200 cells/?L, platelets 225 000/?L, random blood sugar 116 mg/dL, creatinine 0.6 mg/dL, sodium 140 mEq/L, potassium 5.4 mEq/L, and calcium 9 mg/dL. Chest radiograph and thyroid function tests were normal. ECG demonstrated sinus tachycardia with tall T waves. Echocardiography revealed concentric left ventricular hypertrophy and an ejection fraction of 60%. Abdominal ultrasonography identified a 48 ? 45 ? 30 mm heterogeneous para-vertebral mass with cystic areas just above the umbilicus, suggestive of pheochromocytoma.

title: Amaurosis fugax associated with congenital vascular defect
case_summary:
A 68-year-old woman presented with a sudden, temporary loss of vision in her left eye 1 day earlier. She described a "black window shade" pulled over the bottom half of her vision while reading; the episode lasted approximately 1 minute and resolved spontaneously. She denied chest pain, palpitations, headache, flashes, floaters, or recent illness. Her medical history included rheumatoid arthritis and migraines without aura; she took low-dose aspirin daily. She had an uncomplicated cataract extraction in both eyes 2 years ago. Family history was notable for cardiovascular disease but no personal history of atrial fibrillation or glaucoma. She did not smoke and drank alcohol socially.

On examination, blood pressure was 140/80 mmHg and pulse was 80 beats per minute, regular. Best-corrected visual acuity was 20/25+2 in the right eye and 20/20 in the left. Pupils were equal and reactive without an afferent pupillary defect. Extraocular movements and confrontational visual fields were full.

title: Unusual case of cerebral embolism after initiation of selexipag for sarcoidosis-related pulmonary hypertension: a case report
case_summary: A 63-year-old woman with a 5-year history of sarcoidosis involving the eyes and skin presented with progressive dyspnea and palpitations on minimal exertion. An ECG showed nonsustained ventricular tachycardia and frequent ventricular extrasystoles. Her NT-proBNP level was 729 pg/mL. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated an elevated right atrial-right ventricular pressure gradient; no thrombus was seen in the left atrium, left ventricle, or atrial appendage. Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest revealed no thrombus in the pulmonary arteries or major veins. FDG-PET did not show myocardial uptake, excluding cardiac sarcoidosis. Right heart catheterization showed a mean pulmonary artery pressure of 44 mm Hg, a pulmonary artery wedge pressure of 5 mm Hg, and a cardiac index of 2.2 L/min/m2. Holter monitoring revealed sinus rhythm without atrial fibrillation. She was diagnosed with pulmonary arterial hypertension related to sarcoidosis and was started on warfarin and selexipag. A few days

title: Scimitar syndrome: A rare disease with unusual presentation
journal: Lung India : Official Organ of Indian Chest Society
case_summary:
A 14-year-old girl presented with seven days of hemoptysis. She denied fever, cough, sputum production, chest pain, or extrapulmonary symptoms. On examination, she was comfortable, afebrile, with no lymphadenopathy or clubbing. Vital signs: pulse 86/min, blood pressure 100/70 mm Hg, respiratory rate 16/min. Chest examination revealed a small right hemithorax with reduced breath sounds and occasional crepitations on the right. Cardiac examination showed the apex beat in the right fourth intercostal space without murmurs. Routine blood counts, coagulation profile, renal and hepatic function tests were normal. 

A posteroanterior chest radiograph showed a small right hemithorax, rightward shift of the trachea and heart, and increased background reticulations in the right lung field. Electrocardiogram was normal except for an R/S ratio >1 in V1. Transesophageal echocardiography (bicaval view) revealed no atrial septal defect; the main and left pulmonary arteries were visible, but the right

title: Life-threatening hemoptysis following mitral valvuloplasty for rheumatic mitral stenosis
case_summary: A 58-year-old African American woman presented with progressive dyspnea and scant hemoptysis that acutely escalated to massive life-threatening hemoptysis, necessitating intubation. Her medical history included COPD, hepatitis C, congestive heart failure, atrial fibrillation (on warfarin), and complicated rheumatic heart disease. At age 36, she underwent mechanical aortic valve replacement for rheumatic disease; at age 53, she had balloon mitral valvuloplasty for mitral stenosis associated with episodic hemoptysis. She was a 42-pack-year smoker. Post-intubation, a portable chest x-ray showed right-sided pleural effusion, opacities obscuring the right hemidiaphragm and cardiac border, and cephalization of pulmonary vessels. Bronchoscopy revealed bilateral large airway submucosal varices and a clot in the left mainstem bronchus. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated thickened mitral leaflets with commissural fusion, a peak gradient of 47 mmHg, a mean gradient of 21 mmHg, mitral val

title: Spontaneous coronary dissection: A rare etiology of acute coronary syndrome
case_summary:
A 43-year-old man presented to the emergency department with prolonged, infractoid chest pain that began earlier that day. He had no conventional cardiovascular risk factors and no prior surgeries; his only significant history was a spontaneous distal anterior interventricular artery dissection in 2020, treated medically. He was normotensive and hemodynamically stable on arrival, with a heart rate of 72 beats/min, blood pressure 140/90 mm Hg in both arms, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Physical examination was unremarkable, with no signs of heart failure.

The electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm at 63 beats/min, a PR interval of 182 ms, a QRS duration of 103 ms, and no repolarization abnormalities. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a nondilated left ventricle with normal wall thickness and a left ventricular ejection fraction of 60%. Laboratory studies showed an ultra-sensitive troponin level of 82 ng/L (approximately five times t

title: An Unusual Case of Refractory Hypoxia on the ICU
case_summary:
A 68-year-old man presented with a six-month history of progressive dyspnea that worsened over five days. His medical history included mild Parkinson disease, insulin-dependent type II diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and angina; he was a minimal drinker and an ex-smoker. He had recently been started on diuretics for presumed heart failure. 

On arrival in the emergency department, his respiratory rate was 30 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation was 54% on room air, improving to 72% with 15 L oxygen via a non-rebreather mask. Blood pressure and heart rate were normal. An arterial blood gas on oxygen showed pH 7.37, PaO2 5.3 kPa, PaCO2 6.4 kPa, base excess ?2.7 mEq/L, and lactate 4.4 mmol/L. Chest auscultation revealed fine bibasal inspiratory crepitations; chest radiography demonstrated pulmonary oedema.

Laboratory studies showed an elevated troponin T of 141 ng/L on admission, rising to 1118 ng/L (normal <28 ng/L); C-reactive protein was 20 mg/L with a normal neutrophil count. ECG reve

title: Ventricular predominance in biventricular arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy: Should new subtype criteria be recognized?
case_summary: A 68-year-old man was admitted after chest discomfort, palpitations, and light-headedness followed by a syncopal episode. He had a history of progressive exercise intolerance, heart failure, and ventricular tachycardia treated with cardioversion one year earlier. Current medications were bisoprolol, spironolactone, aspirin, and amiodarone. Family history was unremarkable. On arrival, heart rate was 66 bpm, blood pressure 103/64 mmHg, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, and respiratory rate 12/min. Laboratory studies were normal except for mild hyponatremia (Na+ 134 mEq/L). Continuous monitoring captured a sustained monomorphic ventricular tachycardia, which was pharmacologically cardioverted to sinus rhythm. The post-conversion ECG showed low QRS voltages in the limb leads and T-wave inversions in leads V4-V6. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed an LVEF of 30%, RV systolic dysfunction (TAPSE 17 mm; S' 5.2 cm/s; RV fractional area change 27%), biatrial enlargement, and biventricular

title: Cardiac Memory-induced T-wave Inversions
journal: Clinical Practice and Cases in Emergency Medicine
case_summary:
A 29-year-old man with second-degree Mobitz type II atrioventricular block had a dual-chamber right ventricular pacemaker placed 5 weeks earlier. He presented with 1 week of sharp, intermittent, unprovoked left-sided chest pain over the pacemaker site.  
On arrival: pulse 78/min, blood pressure 171/99 mm Hg, respiratory rate 16/min, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, temperature 98.3 degreesF. He appeared comfortable but had reproducible chest pain over the device.  
A 12-lead ECG showed deep, symmetric T-wave inversions in multiple leads. Electrolytes and complete blood count were within normal limits, and troponin I was undetectable. He received aspirin and a heparin drip and was admitted to the coronary care unit.  
Four hours later, a repeat ECG showed a ventricular-paced rhythm. Troponin I remained undetectable on three measurements over 11 hours, and the heparin drip was discontinued. A transthoracic echocardiogram demonstrated normal left ventricular size and function without wa

title: Double Connection of Left-Sided Partial?Anomalous Pulmonary Vein Return in a Young Man
case_summary:
A 40-year-old man presented with a 2-year history of progressive shortness of breath and palpitations. He denied chest pain, syncope, smoking, alcohol use, or allergies, and had no family history of cardiac disease. Vital signs were within normal limits. Cardiovascular and pulmonary examinations were unremarkable, and there was no peripheral edema.

An ECG showed a right bundle branch block pattern. Chest radiography revealed enlargement of the right cardiac border. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a normal left ventricular size and systolic function, a dilated right ventricle, and an estimated systolic pulmonary pressure of 50 mm Hg. There was no evidence of an atrial septal defect or any other intracardiac shunt, and valvular function was normal. A thin fibromuscular membrane dividing the left atrium into proximal and distal chambers gave the appearance of cor triatriatum sinister. Color Doppler imaging showed flow between the left atrial subdivisions. In the suprasternal

title: Giant Adrenal Neuroblastoma in Adults: Surgical Management and Comprehensive Review
case_summary:
An 18-year-old African woman presented with a 6-month history of progressive, painless right-sided abdominal swelling that later became intermittently painful. She also reported loss of appetite and early satiety but denied nausea, vomiting, gastrointestinal bleeding, or jaundice. Three months before admission, she developed gradual-onset throbbing frontal headaches unassociated with exertion. The headaches were relieved by opioids and accompanied by nonexertional palpitations and generalized diaphoresis. There was no syncope, seizures, vision changes, or sleep disturbance. She had no significant medical or family history and did not use tobacco or alcohol.

On examination, her blood pressure was 159/95 mmHg, pulse was  normal, and she had diaphoresis and tachycardia without edema. Abdominal examination revealed a firm, irregular, tender mass in the right hypochondrium extending into the right lumbar and umbilical regions, about 10 ? 10 cm below the costal margin, with limited upward m

title: Non-bacterial thrombotic endocarditis in a patient with COVID
case_summary:
A man in his late 40s with hypertension and a mildly symptomatic COVID-19 infection one month earlier presented after a gunshot wound to his left arm. He reported a transient episode of left-sided weakness and facial droop about 10 days before admission, for which he did not seek medical attention. He denied tobacco, alcohol, or intravenous drug use and was unvaccinated against COVID-19; his sister had died from a pulmonary embolism in the setting of COVID-19. 

On examination, he was tachycardic but otherwise afebrile and normotensive, with no audible murmurs, no neurological deficits, and no skin or nail lesions. Two days into hospitalization, he developed chest pain, fever, and tachycardia. A CT scan of the chest showed multiple acute subsegmental pulmonary emboli in the right middle and lower lobes. Cardiac troponin was 1.85?ng/mL, and coronary angiography for a non-ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction revealed a 100% occlusion of the proximal right coronary artery with left-

title:
A rare case of rapidly progressive atrial myxoma masquerading as
cough syndrome
case_summary:
A 39-year-old woman presented with progressive shortness of breath and a dry, nonproductive cough of 4 months' duration. She had seen her primary care provider multiple times and was treated with antitussives without improvement. She denied fever, chills, night sweats, arthralgias, myalgias, or leg edema. Outpatient chest radiographs, respiratory viral panels, and laboratory tests had been repeatedly negative. Over the preceding weeks, she developed exertional dyspnea progressing to dyspnea at rest, orthopnea, and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea.  

She had undergone transthoracic echocardiography 8 months earlier for evaluation of a patent foramen ovale; the study had shown a normal atrium and ventricle. Two weeks before presentation, a chest CT scan performed in the outpatient setting showed a 4.6-cm mass in the left atrium and an enlarged left subcarinal lymph node.  

On arrival to the emergency department she was afebrile, heart rate 103 beats/min, blood pressure 95/63 mm Hg, respira

title: Homozygous Familial Hypercholesterolemia
case_summary:
A 67-year-old African-American woman with lifelong severe hypercholesterolemia was evaluated for persistent corneal arcus and tendon xanthomas.  
? At age 11, she developed bilateral arcus and tendinous xanthomas; total cholesterol was >1,000 mg/dL. Rapid xanthoma growth led to resection of elbow and knee lesions at age 12. A partial ileal bypass at age 12 lowered cholesterol to 608 mg/dL, and she was placed on a low-cholesterol diet.  
? Psychosocial history included parental abuse, suicidal ideation, and becoming a ward of the state at age 13 years.  
? Family history: father had arcus, hypercholesterolemia, and fatal myocardial infarction at age 60; mother had hypercholesterolemia; three siblings presumed heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia; one daughter with heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia.  
? Lipid-lowering treatments:  
  - Age 21: clofibrate and probucol (discontinued for rash and pregnancy)  
  - Age 21-26: cholestyramine (paused for hemorrhoids), restarted w

title: Cardiomyopathy Related to a Weight Loss Supplement: A Case Report and Review of Literature
journal: Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports
case_summary: A 28-year-old woman with a history of bronchial asthma was transferred for further management of newly diagnosed systolic heart failure. One week earlier, she had presented with chest discomfort. Initial laboratories showed a brain natriuretic peptide level of 572 pg/mL and a troponin level of 54.5 ng/mL. On admission, her heart rate was 99 beats/min, blood pressure 118/69 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. An electrocardiogram demonstrated sinus tachycardia with nonspecific ST changes in leads II, III, and aVF. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed an ejection fraction of 30-35% with hypokinesia of the inferior wall. Cardiac catheterization showed clean coronary arteries, a left ventricular ejection fraction of 20-25%, and moderate diffuse hypokinesis of the left ventricle. She had no recent upper respiratory symptoms, no family history of coronary artery disease or sudden cardiac death, and no history of rheumatic heart disease. Further history disclosed that she had beg

title: Wild-Type Transthyretin Cardiac Amyloidosis in a Transplanted Heart
case_summary:
An 88-year-old woman presented after a mechanical fall. Her heart rate was 150 beats per minute, blood pressure 147/92 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Examination was notable for a regular tachycardia and mild confusion. Laboratory studies showed a B-type natriuretic peptide level of 962 ng/L and a troponin level of 9.8 ng/L (normal range, 0-53.7 ng/L). Serum potassium was 3.7 mmol/L, magnesium 2.1 mg/dL, creatinine 1.4 mg/dL, and glucose 110 mg/dL. An electrocardiogram demonstrated atrial flutter with 2:1 ventricular conduction. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 40% to 45%, down from 65% three months earlier.

Her medical history was significant for ischemic cardiomyopathy treated with orthotopic heart transplantation 21 years earlier, an ischemic stroke six years ago without residual deficits, shoulder and hip arthropathies, and recently diagnosed dementia. She had missed doses of tacrolimus, leading to subtherapeutic levels

title: Too much of a good thing: a case report of traumatic drop attacks and syncope due to orthostatic hypertension
case_summary:
A 67-year-old Caucasian woman with a history of hypercholesterolaemia, depression, fibromyalgia, and migraine (on propranolol) was referred for evaluation of drop attacks over the past 8 years. These episodes occurred monthly within minutes of standing or during early walking, sometimes with transient loss of consciousness lasting seconds, and were accompanied by headaches, light-headedness, dyspnoea, and chest pain. She sustained multiple fractures from the attacks. Between episodes, she experienced daily orthostatic symptoms. 

Physical examination was unremarkable. Laboratory tests, including thyroid and adrenal function, were normal. Neurological evaluation (EEG, CT, MRI) was normal. ECG, echocardiography, and 72-hour ambulatory rhythm monitoring showed no abnormalities. Exercise testing to 80 W reproduced her dyspnoea, chest pain, and light-headedness with a modest hypertensive response (peak BP 197/67 mmHg) but no ischaemia. An implanted loop recorder documented normal sinus rhyt

title: A case of isolated sinus bradycardia as an unusual presentation of adrenal insufficiency
case_summary: A 65-year-old man with a history of hypothyroidism and hyperlipidemia presented with two months of recurrent lightheadedness and dizziness at rest. He denied chest pain, palpitations, diaphoresis, dyspnea, focal neurologic symptoms, exertional or positional triggers, medication use including AV-nodal blockers, or recent illness. There was no family history of heart disease or sudden death. In the emergency department, his blood pressure was 70/50 mm Hg and heart rate 30-50 beats/minute. ECG showed sinus bradycardia at 47 beats/minute, an incomplete right bundle-branch block pattern, and a PR interval of 200 ms. He remained hypotensive despite fluid resuscitation and required a dopamine infusion. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed an ejection fraction of 60%, mild left ventricular hypertrophy, and normal right ventricular size and function. Physical examination was otherwise unremarkable. Laboratory studies were notable for sodium 121 mEq/L, hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, MCV 89 fL, ferritin

title: Incessant narrow-QRS tachycardia mediated by ventriculo-fascicular accessory pathway
case_summary:
A 34-year-old man was referred for incessant, narrow-QRS tachycardia and symptoms of congestive heart failure; echocardiography showed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 25%. He had intermittent episodes over several years that were resistant to verapamil, flecainide, propafenone, and sotalol; adenosine did not terminate the tachycardia. A 12-lead ECG showed a rate of 200 beats/min, atrioventricular dissociation, and onset after two normally conducted sinus beats, with subtle QRS changes (lower R-wave in V1, loss of Q waves in V5-V6, slight leftward axis shift) consistent with right-to-left septal activation.

An electrophysiological study used multipolar catheters in the coronary sinus, His-bundle region, right ventricular septum, and apex. In sinus rhythm, AH and HV intervals were 65 ms and 55 ms, respectively; during tachycardia (cycle length, 310 ms), the HV interval was 50 ms, and His electrograms preceded each QRS. His activation was proximal-to-distal in sinus rhythm but si

title: Chest pain after elective percutaneous coronary intervention as trigger of takotsubo syndrome--a case report
case_summary: A 67-year-old man with hypertension, dyslipidaemia, stage 3 chronic kidney disease, active smoking (35 pack-years), a family history of ischaemic heart disease, and prior percutaneous coronary interventions (2008, 2017, 2022) for non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction presented with Canadian Cardiovascular Society Class III angina. A recent exercise stress test induced angina at 50 W without ECG changes. On admission, he appeared anxious; the physical examination was unremarkable. A 12-lead ECG showed normal sinus rhythm, Q waves in lead III, and biphasic T waves in leads III and aVF. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) of 65% without regional wall-motion abnormalities. Coronary angiography demonstrated patent stents in the circumflex artery, non-significant disease in the left anterior descending artery, and significant ostial right coronary artery stenosis with in-stent restenosis in the mid-segment, which was treated with three drug-el

title: Fulminant myocarditis after the second dose of COVID?19 mRNA vaccination
case_summary:
A 50-year-old man presented with syncope and resting chest pain 10 days after his second dose of the BNT162b2 COVID-19 vaccine. He was admitted to an outside hospital and then referred for hypotension and persistent chest pain. On arrival, he was receiving inotropic support; his blood pressure was 115/66 mmHg, heart rate 104 beats per minute, and oxygen saturation 94% on 8 L/min oxygen. He had no significant medical history, did not smoke or drink alcohol, and had no recent infections or allergies. Reverse transcriptase-PCR for SARS-CoV-2 was negative, and anti-SARS-CoV-2 IgG was positive. 

Electrocardiography showed sinus tachycardia (97 beats/min) with ST-segment elevations in leads V1-V4 and a complete right bundle branch block. Laboratory tests revealed a troponin-I level of 14 644 ng/L (normal <40), creatine kinase-MB 72 IU/L, and brain natriuretic peptide 248 pg/ml. Viral serologies for Epstein-Barr virus, cytomegalovirus, adenovirus, respiratory syncytial virus, varicella zoste

title: Atypical presentation of lipomatous hypertrophy of the interatrial septum: a case report
final_diagnosis: lipomatous hypertrophy of the interatrial septum
case_summary: A 64-year-old man with hypertension, diabetes mellitus, and coronary artery disease treated with multiple percutaneous coronary interventions (most recently 2 weeks earlier) presented with sudden-onset precordial chest pain and dyspnoea on exertion similar to his prior angina. The pain was initially relieved by sublingual nitroglycerine but recurred, prompting presentation to the emergency department. On examination, his heart rate was 75 beats per minute, blood pressure 167/72?mmHg, respiratory rate 20 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air; his body mass index was 35?kg/m2. Physical examination showed a regular rate and rhythm, clear lungs, and jugular venous pressure of 9?cm H2O. Serial cardiac enzymes were normal (troponin T 0.02?ng/mL, normal 0-0.03?ng/mL), and electrocardiography demonstrated sinus rhythm with right bundle branch block and lateral T-wave inversions unchanged from prior studies. His home medications included aspirin, clopidogrel, atorvastatin, m

title: A Case of Ventricular Tachycardia Caused?by a Rare Cardiac Mesenchymal?Hamartoma
case_summary:
A 33-year-old woman with a 3-year history of asymmetric cardiac hypertrophy and sustained ventricular tachycardia (VT) had multiple antiarrhythmic drugs and an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) placed. She experienced recurrent VT despite metoprolol, amiodarone, sotalol, and dofetilide, requiring several ICD shocks. Endocardial radiofrequency ablation and subcutaneous ICD array placement were unsuccessful. Six months earlier, she underwent cardiac sympathetic denervation and renal artery denervation because her VT was stress-precipitated. 

On the current admission, she had symptomatic recurrent VT and received 22 ineffective ICD shocks before a 200 J external shock restored sinus rhythm. At our center, her heart rate and blood pressure were normal. Physical examination was unremarkable. Laboratory tests showed a troponin level of 11.1 ng/ml and normal electrolytes. A 12-lead ECG demonstrated normal sinus rhythm with T-wave inversions in the inferior and septal leads, extrem

title: A case report of ivabradine used for heart rate control of atrial fibrillation in acute decompensated heart failure
case_summary:
A 95-year-old woman with ischaemic cardiomyopathy (LVEF 20%), prior TAVR for aortic stenosis, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, mitral regurgitation, hypertension, and chronic kidney disease presented with 2 days of progressive dyspnoea on exertion, leg swelling, orthopnoea, and weight gain. Four months earlier, a subdural haematoma had prompted discontinuation of all heart-failure medications and anticoagulation. In clinic, she was started on furosemide. A transthoracic echocardiogram showed unchanged LVEF of 20% and normally functioning prosthetic valve. ECG showed sinus rhythm with left bundle-branch block. 

On hospital admission: BP 123/87 mmHg, RR 21 breaths/min, SpO? 96% on room air, HR 130-160 b.p.m. (irregular), afebrile. Examination revealed bilateral pulmonary rales, jugular venous distention to the mandible at 45 degrees, an irregularly irregular tachycardia, cool extremities, and pitting oedema to the upper thighs. ECG confirmed atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular res

title: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy secondary to deficiency in lysosome-associated membrane protein-2: A case report
case_summary:
An 18-year-old man was admitted with a 3-year history of chest tightness and dyspnea that acutely worsened 1 week before presentation, when he developed orthopnea and anuria. Four years earlier in Beijing, he had been diagnosed with non-obstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, a small pericardial effusion, and chronic bilateral lung inflammation; he also experienced a cerebral infarction at that time.

He denied any family history of genetic disorders. On examination, blood pressure was 92/62 mmHg. He had normal development and cognition. Muscle strength initially was grade 5 in all limbs, but at this admission the right upper limb was grade 4 and the other three limbs were grade 1. There was no retinal abnormality. Resting ECG showed atrial and ventricular premature beats and ventricular blocks but no pre-excitation.

Laboratory studies showed high-sensitivity troponin T 0.187 ng/mL, NT-proBNP 6554 pg/mL, creatine kinase 936-1545 U/L, creatine kinase-MB 58-63 U/L, lactate dehydrogenas

title: Very late perforation of a passive fixation lead 8 years after pacemaker implantation
case_summary: An 89-year-old man with a DDD pacemaker implanted 8 years earlier for sick sinus syndrome and atrioventricular block presented with chest pain and presyncopal episodes. He described sudden-onset chest pain while gardening 2 weeks prior. A 12-lead ECG showed severe bradycardia at 20-40 bpm with intermittent ventricular pacing failure. Pacemaker interrogation revealed a ventricular pacing threshold of 4.25 V at 0.4 ms, lead impedance of 425 ?, and inability to measure R-wave sensing due to a slow intrinsic rate. His history included hypertension, COPD, and regular device checks every 6 months, during which ventricular impedance had gradually declined over the past 3 years but other parameters were unchanged. On examination, blood pressure was 128/97 mm Hg, pulse 40 bpm, and SpO2 98% on room air. Chest radiograph demonstrated that the ventricular lead tip had advanced to the distal cardiac silhouette. Pacing failure occurred frequently during inspiration. Transthoracic echocardiography sh

title: Anomalous Right Coronary Artery: A Multimodality Hunt for the Origin
final_diagnosis: anomalous right coronary artery from pulmonary artery
case_summary: A 48-year-old previously healthy woman presented with sudden onset of right scapular and right thoracic chest pain, as well as exertional shortness of breath. Her only cardiac risk factor was a four-pack-year smoking history. Vital signs and cardiovascular examination were normal, with no murmurs or signs of heart failure. She had tenderness to palpation below her right scapula but a normal shoulder examination. Serial cardiac enzymes were negative. A resting ECG showed T-wave inversions in leads V1-V3. Chest radiography showed no cardiomegaly or pulmonary edema. The scapular pain responded to analgesics and was attributed to repetitive strain, but her chest pain and exertional symptoms remained unexplained. She recalled a similar episode of right thoracic chest pain two years earlier during travel abroad, with no definitive diagnosis. Since then she had experienced frequent exertional shortness of breath and chest discomfort relieved within five minutes of rest. A prior exercise-stres

title: ECG changes after electroconvulsive therapy, cause or consequence?
case_summary: A 67-year-old man with hypertension, dilated abdominal aorta, diabetes mellitus, and severe depression underwent electroconvulsive therapy. He was admitted to the cardiology service because of low oxygen saturation and persistent electrocardiographic abnormalities. He denied chest pain, dyspnea, or other cardiac symptoms. On examination, blood pressure was 140/80 mmHg and heart rate 67 beats/min. Pulmonary and cardiac auscultation were unremarkable. ECG demonstrated normal sinus rhythm with left axis deviation and new, deep negative T waves in leads V1 through V5. Laboratory studies showed a troponin-T of 0.06 ?g/L (normal < 0.05 ?g/L), an elevated D-dimer, and otherwise normal blood chemistries. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a normal left ventricular ejection fraction with mild concentric hypertrophy.

title: Challenges in diagnosing and treating Liddle syndrome in resource-limited settings: A case report from Indonesia
case_summary:
A 35-year-old woman presented with a one-month history of bilateral limb weakness that began in her legs and gradually involved all extremities. The weakness was constant, did not fluctuate with activity or rest, and progressed to difficulty walking two days before admission. She denied sensory changes, speech difficulties, palpitations, sweating, polyuria, edema, or bulbar symptoms. She had a history of hypertension diagnosed previously but had not taken any antihypertensive medications. Her mother died of hypertension-related complications. She was a nonsmoker, did not drink alcohol, and consumed a high-salt, high-fat diet.

On examination, blood pressure was 190/100 mmHg, heart rate 79/min, respiratory rate 20/min, temperature 36.4 degreesC, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. There was no conjunctival pallor. Cardiac and pulmonary examinations were unremarkable. Mild right upper quadrant tenderness was noted without guarding or rebound. Extremities were warm, with normal capilla

title: Nonbacterial thrombotic endocarditis as the sole manifestation of stage IV gastric cancer: a case report
case_summary:
A 36-year-old Caucasian man presented after an episode of presyncope. He had sudden dizziness in the bathroom, lay down for 15 minutes, and then improved. There was no history of seizures, incontinence, weight loss, night sweats, or high-grade fever, but he noted chills a few hours earlier.

One week earlier, he had been admitted elsewhere for chest pain; cardiac catheterization was negative, but abdominal ultrasound showed ascites, prompting referral.  

In the emergency department, vital signs showed mild tachycardia. Examination revealed a soft grade II/VI systolic ejection murmur at the left sternal border and splinter hemorrhages under the nails; the remainder of the exam, including neurologic assessment, was normal.  

Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a 0.8 ? 0.6 cm vegetation on the mitral valve. CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis showed multiple lytic bone lesions, splenic and renal infarcts, malignant-appearing ascites, and omental caking.  

He was started on intrav

title: Chronic rheumatic heart disease with recrudescence of acute rheumatic fever on histology: a case report
case_summary: A 59-year-old woman with a 10-year history of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes and hypertension presented with 2 months of progressive dyspnea, declining exercise tolerance, orthopnea, bipedal edema, and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea. Symptoms progressed from NYHA class II to IV over 3 months. She was afebrile. Blood pressure was 140/60 mmHg, heart rate 98 bpm, and jugular venous pressure was elevated. Cardiovascular examination revealed a displaced apical impulse, a soft S1, normal S2, an S3 gallop, a 3/6 pansystolic murmur at the apex consistent with mitral regurgitation, and a 2/4 early diastolic murmur with a widened pulse pressure. The remainder of the examination was unremarkable. Twelve-lead ECG showed abnormal QRS wave pattern, repolarization changes, and first-degree atrioventricular block (PR 330 ms). Chest radiography revealed cardiomegaly, bilateral pleural effusions, and upper-lobe pulmonary venous diversion. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a mildly dilated lef

title: Non?dissecting large thoracic aortic aneurysm leading to chronic aortic insufficiency presenting as acute heart failure
case_summary: A 31-year-old Japanese-American man presented with a five-day history of new-onset dyspnea on exertion, orthopnea, and nonproductive cough. One year earlier, a "heart murmur" was noted but never evaluated. His sister had a spontaneous pneumothorax, and both siblings were taller than their parents. He had no known family history of cardiac disease. On examination, he was a thin, tall man (185 cm) with a 2/6 systolic murmur and a 2/4 diastolic murmur. An ECG showed normal sinus rhythm, evidence of left atrial enlargement, and left ventricular hypertrophy with strain, with no ischemic changes. A chest X-ray revealed marked cardiomegaly. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated global hypokinesis with an LVEF of 28%, an LVEDV of 479 mL, a trileaflet aortic valve with severe aortic regurgitation, mild-moderate pulmonary hypertension, mild atrial and right ventricular enlargement, and a 9.4 cm ascending aortic aneurysm without dissection at the level of the sinuses of Valsalva. A CT angio

title: Aorto-ventricular tunnel with three orifices: a unique case report diagnosed by transthoracic echocardiography
case_summary:
A 64-year-old man presented with six months of chest discomfort and exertional dyspnea. His past medical history was unremarkable. On examination, blood pressure was 124/66 mmHg and heart rate was 86 beats/min. Cardiac auscultation revealed a grade 4/6 continuous biphasic murmur along the left sternal border and a grade 3/6 systolic murmur at the apex. There were no stigmata of Marfan syndrome.

Electrocardiography showed atrial fibrillation, ventricular premature beats, left ventricular hypertrophy, and ST-T-wave abnormalities. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated:
- A dilated heart with a left ventricular ejection fraction of 42%.  
- An abnormal channel between the ascending aorta and left ventricle, visualized in the parasternal long-axis view.  
- Color Doppler imaging revealed anterograde systolic and retrograde diastolic flow between the aorta and left ventricle, with retrograde diastolic flow also seen in the apical five-chamber view.  
- On a modified parasternal long-a

title: Vasospastic Angina With ST-Segment Elevation Seen During Mobile Cardiac Outpatient Telemetry (MCOT) Monitoring
journal: Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports
case_summary: A 54-year-old man with hypertension, dyslipidemia, and gout presented with recurrent episodes of burning chest discomfort, profuse diaphoresis, and a syncopal event occurring in the mornings before restroom use. He denied exertional triggers and walked 10 miles daily without limitation. Since 2015, his evaluation had included a normal 24-hour Holter monitor, exercise stress test, tilt-table test, and transthoracic echocardiography. Because of continued palpitations and sweating during symptomatic episodes, a mobile cardiac outpatient telemetry device was placed. While wearing the device, he experienced chest burning and diaphoresis coinciding with marked ST-segment elevations. He was brought emergently to the hospital. On admission, an ECG showed sinus rhythm without ST-segment deviation. High-sensitivity troponin I was 3.5 pg/mL (reference 0-14.4 pg/mL). Echocardiography revealed normal left ventricular function without wall-motion or structural abnormalities. Coronary angiography dem

title: Single-loop biatrial tachycardia mimicking typical atrial flutter in a patient without a history of cardiac surgery or ablation
case_summary: A man in his 60s with HIV on antiretroviral therapy and a history of pacemaker implantation for complete heart block (3 years earlier) and subsequent ICD upgrade for ventricular tachycardia presented with 4 months of exertional dyspnea without palpitations. Echocardiography, CT angiography, endomyocardial biopsy, and gallium scintigraphy were unremarkable. A 12?lead ECG showed atrial flutter with sawtooth waves in the inferior leads and a tachycardia cycle length of 330 ms. During electrophysiological study under sedation, catheters were placed in the coronary sinus, right atrial septum, and tricuspid annulus. Intracardiac electrograms showed craniocaudal activation in the lateral right atrium, caudocranial activation in the septal right atrium, and proximal-to-distal activation in the coronary sinus. Entrainment pacing from the high and low lateral right atrium and the ostial coronary sinus yielded post-pacing intervals matching the tachycardia cycle length, whereas pacing from the di

title: Guillain-Barre syndrome after myocardial infarction: a case report and literature review
case_summary:
A 75-year-old Chinese woman with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, atrial fibrillation, and prior cerebral infarction experienced sudden loss of consciousness while eating. After sublingual nitroglycerin, her consciousness returned but chest tightness persisted. An electrocardiogram showed ST-segment elevation in leads II, III, aVF, V3R-V5R, V7-V9, reciprocal ST depression in I and aVL, and a IIIA-V block, indicating inferior, anterior, posterior, and right-ventricular MI. Coronary angiography revealed 90% stenosis of the proximal left anterior descending artery, occlusion of the distal posterior left ventricular branch, and diffuse 85% stenosis in the mid right coronary artery with thrombus autolysis. She underwent emergency PCI of the mid right coronary artery.

Twenty hours before transfer, she noted subacute bilateral upper-limb weakness (MRC sum score 54), progressing distally to proximally with numbness, followed by similar bilateral lower-limb weakness and urinary retention. Crania

title: Percutaneous Treatment of Severe?Aortic?Regurgitation After Surgical Mitral?Valve Repair
case_summary:
A 54-year-old woman was admitted with worsening dyspnea, now NYHA class II. Her blood pressure was 130/60 mm Hg, heart rate 85 beats/min, and body mass index 38 kg/m2. Physical examination revealed a holodiastolic murmur and mild basal lung crackles. She was taking a direct oral anticoagulant and furosemide. Laboratory tests showed a normal white blood cell count, mild anemia, normal renal and liver function, and a negative C-reactive protein level. An electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm, normal atrioventricular conduction, and a narrow QRS complex.  

Transthoracic echocardiography revealed trivial mitral regurgitation with a mean gradient of 4.75 mm Hg, a severely enlarged left atrium, preserved left ventricular ejection fraction, and severe aortic regurgitation. There was moderate tricuspid regurgitation, preserved right ventricular function, elevated pulmonary pressure, and no pericardial effusion.  

Transesophageal echocardiography confirmed severe regurgitation through

title: Intima Sarcoma of the Pulmonary Artery Mimicking Takayasu Disease
case_summary:
A 31-year-old woman was referred for persistent dyspnea on exertion (NYHA class II) and presumed chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension. Four years earlier, she had acute chest pain and dyspnea; a second similar episode occurred three months ago and was attributed to pulmonary embolism. She had a history of hookworm infection; she used oral contraceptives and smoked.

On examination, she had digital clubbing, a prominent pulmonary component of the second heart sound, and a left parasternal systolic ejection murmur; blood pressure was 99/60?mmHg bilaterally, and the remainder of the examination was unremarkable. Routine laboratory tests showed no inflammation; ECG was normal.

Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a thickened mitral valve, moderate tricuspid regurgitation with an estimated systolic pulmonary arterial pressure of 60?mmHg, and a markedly dilated, mildly hypocontractile right ventricle. Right-heart catheterization showed a mean pulmonary arterial pressure of 2

title: Cardiac sarcoidosis presenting with syncope and rapidly progressive atrioventricular block: a case report
case_summary:
A 50-year-old man with a remote history of treated Lyme disease, and no hypertension, hyperlipidemia, or diabetes, presented after four episodes of syncope over 24 hours. Six weeks earlier, he had a syncope while exercising; initial workup was unrevealing. Outpatient ECG then showed a new left bundle branch block. Exercise stress testing provoked presyncope with a brief burst of atrial tachycardia. A pharmacological nuclear stress test demonstrated an LVEF of 42% with a moderate-size area of inferior wall ischaemia and scar, but he had no chest pain or heart failure symptoms.

On current presentation, vital signs were blood pressure 140/66 mmHg, heart rate 54 bpm, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, temperature 36.2 degreesC, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Physical examination was unremarkable. A 12-lead ECG showed sinus rhythm at 57 bpm, first-degree AV block, and a prolonged QTc. He had a brief burst of atrial tachycardia that resolved with IV metoprolol. Cardiac enzymes, thyroi

title: Myocardial Bridging Leading to Cardiac Collapse in a Marathon Runner
journal: Journal of Cardiovascular Development and Disease
case_summary:
A 30-year-old man suddenly collapsed at mile 17 of a marathon. Bystanders initiated CPR for approximately 10 minutes until emergency medical services arrived; he regained spontaneous circulation and was intubated due to a Glasgow Coma Scale of 4. On admission, he had generalized tonic-clonic seizures that resolved with benzodiazepine.  

He had a history of anxiety-related palpitations since childhood and recurrent left shoulder dislocations but no prior chest pain, cardiac disease, or family history of sudden death. He trained 5 days per week, up to 19 miles, and had one episode of dizziness without syncope at 12 miles a month earlier. He denied smoking or drug use.  

Initial investigations included:  
- Cranial and cervical CT scans, CT angiography of cerebral arteries, spine radiographs, and brain MRI: no acute structural lesions.  
- Toxicology: negative.  
- ECG: sinus rhythm at 69 bpm, no ST-T changes.  
- Cardiac biomarkers: high-sensitivity troponin T 0.115 ng/mL (normal <0.01

title: A case report of cardiac amyloidosis presenting with chronic pericardial effusion and conduction block
case_summary:
A 51-year-old woman with no prior comorbidities presented with a 1-year history of persistent, left-sided, non-pleuritic chest pain and gradually progressive dyspnoea. Eight months earlier she had two syncopal episodes and was found to have complete heart block, for which a permanent pacemaker was implanted. She was also diagnosed with pericardial effusion and was treated empirically with anti-tuberculosis therapy for 8 months without improvement. 

On examination, she was afebrile; blood pressure was 100/70 mmHg without pulsus paradoxus; heart rate 78 beats/min; respiratory rate 18 breaths/min; oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Cardiovascular examination revealed muffled heart sounds without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; other systems were unremarkable.

Chest radiography showed cardiomegaly with a pacemaker in situ. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a large pericardial effusion (maximum 27 mm posteriorly), global left ventricular systolic dysfunction (ejection fraction 41.3%)

title: Accelerated idioventricular rhythm as anginous substrate in elderly: Report of an unprecedented case
case_summary:
A 68-year-old man with hypertension on losartan presented with intermittent, nonradiating retrosternal chest pain described as squeezing, lasting 5-10 minutes both at rest and with exertion. He had no associated symptoms and no relieving factors. An ECG before consultation showed sinus rhythm with diffuse, asymmetric T-wave inversions without other abnormalities. During the visit, he developed chest pain; a repeat ECG revealed a wide-QRS rhythm at 55 beats per minute consistent with accelerated idioventricular rhythm (AIVR) with left bundle branch block morphology and an inferior axis, which coincided with his anginal symptoms.

Earlier evaluation included a treadmill test during which he experienced angina; the ECG at that moment showed AIVR with left bundle branch block morphology and a superior axis, self-limited with symptom resolution. He was hospitalized for comprehensive evaluation. Coronary angiography showed no atherosclerotic disease. Serial troponin levels remained normal. T

title: Rumpel-Leede phenomenon in a hypertensive patient due to mechanical trauma: a case report
case_summary: A 58-year-old woman presented with left-sided chest pain worsened by exertion, accompanied by shortness of breath, nausea, vomiting, and diaphoresis. Her history included type 2 diabetes mellitus, prior myocardial infarction, atrial fibrillation on warfarin, heart failure, and stage 4 chronic kidney disease. On arrival, blood pressure was 207/140 mmHg, and she had clinical signs of decompensated heart failure. Electrocardiography showed ST depression and T-wave inversion in the lateral leads. Chest radiography confirmed pulmonary edema. Bedside echocardiography demonstrated moderate to severe left ventricular systolic dysfunction, left ventricular hypertrophy, and a calcific but unobstructive aortic valve. High-sensitivity troponin I was elevated, confirming an acute coronary syndrome. She was treated with aspirin, clopidogrel, fondaparinux, a high-dose statin, intravenous glyceryl trinitrate, and diuretics, and amlodipine was added for persistent hypertension. Coronary angiography was

title: Vanishing Bile Duct Syndrome in a Patient With Recurrent Hodgkin Lymphoma
case_summary:
A 40-year-old man with hypertension, alcohol abuse, and a history of classical Hodgkin lymphoma (HL) treated with ABVD 4 years earlier presented with one week of worsening shortness of breath, cough, and exertional chest pain without diaphoresis or palpitations. He was afebrile, blood pressure 131/73 mm Hg, heart rate 109 bpm, respiratory rate 18/min, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Physical examination revealed jaundice, scleral icterus, and decreased breath sounds at the left lower lung field.

Two months prior, he had two admissions for suspected HL recurrence. A mediastinoscopy with lymph node biopsy was negative for disease; flow cytometry was inconclusive. He developed transaminitis and a marked alkaline phosphatase elevation. MRCP showed no biliary obstruction, and a liver biopsy showed no lymphoma involvement. He was lost to follow-up before ERCP.

On this presentation, CT of the abdomen showed a normal liver without biliary dilation but new peri-aortic and para-diaphrag

title: Atypical Chest X-Ray Calcification in an Idiopathic Constrictive Pericarditis Case
case_summary: A 50-year-old woman presented with a 6-month history of progressive dyspnea on exertion, abdominal swelling, and lower extremity edema. Three months earlier, she had been treated for presumed liver disease with furosemide, but her swelling and functional limitation worsened, and she developed orthopnea. She had no prior medical history. On examination, blood pressure was 100/60 mm Hg, pulse 100 beats/min and irregular, respiratory rate 24 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 92% on room air. She appeared pale but not cyanotic. Jugular venous distention extended to the mandibular angle, and hepatojugular reflux was positive. The liver was palpable 5 cm below the right costal margin, and there was bilateral pitting edema to the knees (+++/++++). Cardiac auscultation revealed an irregular rhythm with normal S1 and S2; lung bases had diminished breath sounds and crackles. Laboratory tests, including cardiac enzymes, were normal; brain natriuretic peptide was 140 pg/mL (reference 0-125 pg/mL)

title: Feminizing Adrenal Carcinoma Presenting with Heart Failure and Ventricular Tachycardia
case_summary:
A 54-year-old African American man with a 2-year history of hypertension, heart failure, and diabetes mellitus presented with progressive dyspnea and edema. He had episodes of nonsustained ventricular tachycardia. An echocardiogram showed dilated left-sided chambers with moderate global hypokinesis and an ejection fraction of 35-40%. He was treated, stabilized, and transferred for consideration of an implantable defibrillator. Further history revealed a 20-lb weight loss, decreased libido, erectile dysfunction, and breast development over 1-2 years. 

On examination, vital signs were stable (blood pressure 122/66 mm Hg, pulse 100 beats/min, regular) and BMI was 23. There were no rales, S3 gallop, or peripheral edema. He had gynecomastia, a hyperdynamic precordium, and a firm mass in the left upper quadrant of the abdomen. Testes were soft, 20 mL bilaterally. 

Chest radiograph showed an elevated left hemidiaphragm. CT of the abdomen revealed a 12 ? 11 ? 17 cm mass in the left upper qua

title: Elusive Cardiac Angiosarcoma in a Young Pregnant Female: Rare Presentation With Fatal Outcome
case_summary:
A G2P1 woman in her thirties at 18 weeks of gestation presented with progressive dyspnea. Her medications included prenatal vitamins, folic acid, and an occasional bronchodilator inhaler.  
Vital signs were: temperature 36.3  degreesC, pulse 110 bpm, respiratory rate 22 /min, blood pressure 90/59 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation 97% on room air. On cardiac examination, heart sounds were distant with pulsus paradoxus; there was no murmur or pericardial rub. The remainder of her physical examination was unremarkable.  
Laboratory studies showed a hemoglobin concentration of 89 g/L and a platelet count of 223 ? 10^9/L; both antinuclear antibodies and anti-double-strand DNA antibodies were negative. Coagulation parameters and thyroid-stimulating hormone were within normal limits. An electrocardiogram demonstrated sinus tachycardia.  
Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a large pericardial effusion with signs of impending tamponade. The patient underwent pericardiocentesis, with removal of

title: Left coronary ostial isolation in a young boy caused by a dysplastic aortic valve: a case report
final_diagnosis: isolation of the left coronary ostium
case_summary:
A 12-year-old boy was referred with a four-month history of episodic, progressively severe, atypical left-sided chest pain causing significant functional disturbance. Pain occurred at rest and with exertion; he adopted back-arching postures for relief. He had no other cardiac symptoms and no family history of cardiac disease. Initial echocardiography at the referring centre showed a dysplastic aortic valve with mild aortic regurgitation and left ventricular hypertrophy. Serial troponin-I measurements in the week before referral revealed one mildly elevated value (32?ng/L; upper limit 26?ng/L). Prior local presentations were managed as presumed pericarditis without improvement on anti-inflammatory therapy. Differential diagnoses considered included musculoskeletal pain, gastro-oesophageal reflux (treated with a proton-pump inhibitor), and non-organic pain; these interventions led to only mild symptom improvement.

On assessment: heart rate 80/min, blood pressure 115/60?mmHg, oxygen satu

title: Giant cell myocarditis following COVID?19 successfully treated by immunosuppressive therapy
case_summary: A 46-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of new-onset heart failure. She reported exertional dyspnea (NYHA class II) and fatigue. Her history was notable for lichen planus dermatitis treated with immunosuppression several years earlier and TPO antibody-positive hypothyroidism on levothyroxine. One month earlier, she had mild COVID-19 confirmed by RT-PCR, with no hypoxemia or pulmonary involvement, which resolved with outpatient therapy. Three weeks later, she developed severe chest discomfort and shortness of breath. ECG showed ST-segment elevations in leads V2-V6 and troponin-I was elevated, but coronary angiography revealed normal epicardial arteries. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a normal LV size, apical hypokinesia, and an EF of 35-40%; RV size and function were normal. Cardiac MRI showed mildly enlarged LV size (LVEF 35%), a thin and aneurysmal apex, and widespread patchy subepicardial to mid-wall fibrosis in inferolateral and septal segments with subendocardi

title: A dynamic variant of Takotsubo cardiomyopathy mimicking apical hypertrophic cardiomyopathy: a case report
case_summary:
A 74-year-old Caucasian woman presented with retrosternal chest pain that developed after emotional stress; the pain resolved spontaneously by the time of arrival.  
Vital signs were within normal limits: oxygen saturation 97% on room air, blood pressure 105/57 mmHg, respiratory rate 17/min, heart rate 75/min, temperature normal. Physical examination was unremarkable, with no signs of heart failure.  
Initial troponin was elevated at 2008 ng/L, peaking at 2232 ng/L (reference <18 ng/L). Bedside echocardiography showed apical ballooning of the left ventricle with an ejection fraction of 45%.  
Her past medical history included systemic sclerosis, hypoparathyroidism, hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia, a body mass index of 14, and remote colon cancer resection leading to short bowel syndrome. Medications included flecainide, prednisolone, omeprazole, and calcium/vitamin D replacement; she had a history of paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia managed in the past.  
Given concern for

title: Pericardiectomy for Constrictive Pericarditis in a Young Patient With Erdheim-Chester Disease
case_summary:
A 34-year-old woman presented with several months of progressive shortness of breath on exertion, reduced exercise capacity, and signs of fluid overload, including peripheral edema and ascites. Her heart rate was 103 beats/min, blood pressure 119/77 mm Hg, respiratory rate 20 breaths/minute, temperature 36.3  degreesC, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Physical examination revealed a jugular venous pressure of 6 cm above the sternal angle, muffled heart sounds, and bilateral peripheral edema.  

Ten years earlier, at age 25, she had developed polyuria and polydipsia from diabetes insipidus, tenosynovitis with joint effusions, and skin lesions. Lymph node and skin biopsies at that time were interpreted as histiocytosis with S100 positive and CD1a positive cells, and she was diagnosed with Langerhans histiocytosis. She received methotrexate and intermittent prednisone.  

Current investigations included biopsy of a skin lesion, which showed foamy histiocytic cells in loose clusters

title: Late stent thrombosis as a complication of T and small protrusion bifurcation stenting: a case report
case_summary: A 64-year-old man presented with a 5-hour history of severe, sudden-onset central chest pain. On admission, his ECG showed normal sinus rhythm without acute ischemic changes. Over the next 12 hours, he developed Wellen's type A changes followed by Wellen's type B changes. Initial high-sensitivity troponin I was 4177 ng/L (normal < 5) and rose to 99 394 ng/L six hours later. He had undergone coronary stenting for stable angina two years earlier, but procedural details were initially unavailable. A standard non-ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction regimen was started, including aspirin and ticagrelor. Diagnostic coronary angiography demonstrated TIMI?3 flow in all vessels, a patent left anterior descending artery (LAD) stent, mild diffuse attenuation within the second diagonal (D2) stent, and an ambiguous mid-D2 appearance suggestive of intraluminal thrombus; intravascular imaging was not performed. Takotsubo cardiomyopathy was considered in the differential. Transthoracic echocard

title: A Case Report of Pericardial Effusion with False-Positive Mesothelioma and Adenocarcinoma Markers as the Initial Presentation of Systemic Lupus Erythematous
case_summary: A 54-year-old African American woman with poorly controlled hypertension, a 15 pack-year smoking history, and alpha-thalassemia anemia presented after 3 weeks of progressive blurred vision, fatigue, headache, and orthopnea. She was referred for hypertensive retinopathy evaluation, where her blood pressure measured 220/130 mmHg and she had cotton-wool spots on funduscopic exam. In the emergency department, her systolic pressure reached 230 mmHg. On examination, jugular venous pressure was 14 cm above the sternal angle; heart sounds were distant without pulsus paradoxus; lungs were clear; and there was no peripheral edema. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 7.9 g/dL (baseline 10 g/dL), creatinine 1.3 mg/dL, C-reactive protein 11 mg/L, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate 110 mm/hr. Chest radiography demonstrated a markedly enlarged cardiac silhouette with mild pulmonary congestion. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a large circumferential pericardial effusion (2.24 cm) with early r

title: Severe functional tricuspid regurgitation due to wild-type transthyretin amyloid cardiomyopathy without left ventricular hypertrophy: a case report
case_summary: A 75-year-old woman presented with exertional dyspnea and presyncope associated with bradycardia. Five years earlier, she had undergone carpal tunnel release, and she had been using ?-blocker eye drops for normal-tension glaucoma for 20 years. On examination, her blood pressure was 122/76 mmHg, and pulse was irregular at 42 beats per minute. Jugular venous distention and bilateral lower-leg edema were noted. A grade II/VI systolic murmur was heard at the right sternal border, fourth intercostal space, and lung auscultation was clear. Chest radiograph showed a cardiothoracic ratio of 61.8%. The ECG demonstrated bradycardia with an ectopic atrial rhythm. Laboratory testing revealed a brain natriuretic peptide level of 306.6 pg/mL (normal ? 18.4) and a high-sensitive troponin I level of 9.3 pg/mL (normal ? 26.2). Transthoracic echocardiography showed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 68.9% and no left ventricular hypertrophy (interventricular septal thickness 8.8 mm, posterior wall

title: MRI findings of epipericardial fat necrosis: As a rare cause of acute chest pain in a healthy man
case_summary: A 37-year-old man presented with sudden-onset pleuritic pain in his right anterior lower chest that radiated to the upper abdomen. The symptoms began a day earlier, shortly after a powerlifting session. He rated the pain 6 of 10. He denied fever, chills, palpitations, syncope, cough, or shortness of breath. On examination, vital signs were normal. Heart sounds were normal without murmurs or friction rub. Breath sounds were clear without wheezing or crackles. He had no significant past medical history or cardiovascular risk factors. An electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm with no ST-segment or T-wave changes. Laboratory tests were normal, including a D-dimer of 100 ng/L (reference <500 ng/L), troponin I <0.01 ng/L (reference <0.016), and C-reactive protein 2.0 mg/L (reference <8.0). Transthoracic echocardiography revealed normal global systolic left ventricular function without regional wall motion abnormalities and no significant pericardial effusion. A broad differential diagn

title: Case report: DSP truncation variant p. R1951X leads to arrhythmogenic left ventricular cardiomyopathy
case_summary:
A 21-year-old university student experienced syncope shortly after exercise. He reported no chest pain or dyspnea but had a self-limited diarrheal illness two weeks earlier. In the emergency department he was afebrile, blood pressure was 127/78?mmHg, heart rate 81?beats/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Examination was unremarkable. His mother had idiopathic cardiomyopathy; his maternal grandmother died of sudden cardiac arrest.

On telemetry, he developed sustained monomorphic ventricular tachycardia with a cycle length of 220?ms, treated with intravenous metoprolol, amiodarone, and lidocaine. In the coronary care unit, he had polymorphic VT that degenerated into ventricular fibrillation; he was successfully defibrillated.

Serum chemistry was normal. Initial troponin I was 0.23?ng/mL (normal <0.04?ng/mL) and subsequently decreased. Pre-arrest ECG showed sinus rhythm with premature ventricular complexes of left bundle branch block, inferior axis morphology; there were no eps

title: Coronary Arteriovenous Fistula Causing Hydrops Fetalis
journal: Case Reports in Obstetrics and Gynecology
case_summary: A 22-year-old primigravida at 26 weeks' gestation was referred for fetal echocardiography after routine obstetric ultrasonography detected pericardial effusion. The mother's medical history was unremarkable, and prenatal care had been irregular. Fetal echocardiography revealed pericardial, pleural, and abdominal free fluid, skin edema, an increased cardiothoracic ratio (heart one-half the thoracic cavity), severe holosystolic tricuspid regurgitation, and a dilated coronary sinus. Severe fetal bradycardia was present throughout the examination. These findings suggested hydrops fetalis and severe fetal heart failure, prompting immediate referral for emergency cesarean delivery. The newborn male weighed 1050 g, had no spontaneous respirations, a heart rate < 60 bpm, and was hypotonic. He underwent cardiopulmonary resuscitation, was intubated, and transferred to the neonatal ICU. On examination, he was pale with generalized edema; lung auscultation revealed fine rales; heart sounds were mu

title: Coronary Embolism despite CHA?DS?-VASc Score of Zero: Should We Reconsider Anticoagulation?
case_summary:
A 53-year-old obese man presented with sudden-onset, pressure-like chest pain and lightheadedness lasting one hour. This was his first episode of chest pain and was not exertional. He had a history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation diagnosed one year earlier, but was not on anticoagulation because his CHA2DS2-VASc score was 0. He was a lifelong nonsmoker and had no notable family history. 

On admission, vital signs were stable. Physical examination showed an irregularly irregular rhythm with a borderline tachycardic rate and normal heart sounds without murmurs, rubs, or gallops. An electrocardiogram revealed coarse atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular rate and premature ventricular contractions. Chest radiography was unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed mild thrombocytopenia, normal coagulation tests, mild hyperglycemia, and sequential troponin T levels of 0.02, 0.03, and 0.80?ng/ml (upper limit of normal, 0.10?ng/ml). ProBNP was 1104?pg/ml. Thyroid testing revealed an und

title: Probable idiopathic hypereosinophilic syndrome: A case report of severe multi-organ eosinophilic involvement in a young male presenting with heart failure
case_summary:
A 37-year-old firefighter with a history of refractory chronic cough, eosinophilic bronchitis, and chronic sinusitis presented with 2 weeks of worsening cough, scant hemoptysis, fever, night sweats, dyspnea, and orthopnea. He reported intermittent use of oral prednisone that provided transient relief but no gastrointestinal or joint symptoms. Two weeks earlier, he had syncopal episodes and was found to have a small pericardial effusion with hemodynamic compromise; he was treated for presumed myopericarditis with nonsteroidal antiinflammatory drugs.

On examination, he was afebrile, heart rate 110 bpm, blood pressure 115/70 mm?Hg, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 94% on room air. Lung examination revealed diffuse crackles without wheezes; cardiac examination showed distant heart sounds; there was no rash or lymphadenopathy.

A chest radiograph showed increased interstitial thickening and ground-glass changes. Contrast-enhanced chest CT demonstrated bilateral ground

title: Focal atrial tachycardia mimicking catecholaminergic polymorphic ventricular tachycardia: a case report
case_summary:
A 26-year-old Caucasian woman was referred for evaluation of recurrent broad complex extrasystoles and nonsustained wide-complex tachycardia. She first experienced palpitations at age 12 and had an exercise-related syncope while jumping on a trampoline. Initial transthoracic echocardiography revealed a structurally normal heart; resting ECG showed polymorphic wide complex extrasystoles. She was started on beta-blockers, which were later discontinued because of persistent palpitations. At age 20, she had another syncope while standing, without further workup.

In February 2022, outpatient evaluation for reduced exercise tolerance following symptomatic SARS-CoV-2 infection again revealed wide complex extrasystoles and nonsustained wide complex tachycardia with varying QRS axes, prompting referral. Family history was negative for sudden or unexplained death. Physical examination was normal aside from frequent extra beats; vital signs and body habitus were within normal limits.

title: Stuck between a rock and a hard place: heart failure with bilateral atrial appendage thrombi and disseminated intravascular coagulation--a case report
case_summary: A 69-year-old Caucasian man with hypertension and type 2 diabetes was admitted with progressive dyspnea and new-onset diarrhea. He had orthopnea and bilateral pleural effusions on chest radiograph. Examination revealed a grade 2 systolic murmur at the apex, bilateral lower-extremity edema, and lung crackles. ECG showed new-onset atrial fibrillation with inverted T waves in the inferior and lateral leads. NT-proBNP was 35 000 pg/mL; troponin I was ?200 ng/L. In the emergency department, he was tachypneic (RR 21/min), hypoxemic (SpO? 89% on room air), febrile (37.5  degreesC), with AF and a median heart rate of 65 b.p.m., and blood pressure of 100/65 mmHg. Laboratory tests revealed WBC 11 750/mm? (95.7% neutrophils), hemoglobin 13.6 g/dL, platelets 32 000/mm?, CRP 166.7 mg/L, procalcitonin 9.43 ng/mL, INR 2.04, fibrinogen 80 mg/dL, D-dimer 13 380 ng/mL, normal liver function, and creatinine 2.06 mg/dL. Blood and urine cultures grew Morganella morganii. An ISTH DIC score was 6, consistent

title: Acute pericarditis, Graves' disease, and thymic hyperplasia: a case report
case_summary:
An 18-year-old woman with a history of Graves' disease treated with methimazole presented with sharp chest pain at rest, radiating to the left arm and trapezius ridge.
