The pain was relieved by sitting up and leaning forward and worsened when supine, with deep inspiration, or coughing. She denied fever, respiratory symptoms, or recent systemic illness. 

On examination, blood pressure was 141/63 mmHg and temperature 36.5  degreesC. Heart sounds were normal without friction rub; there was no jugular venous distension or pulsus paradoxus. 

Electrocardiogram showed sinus tachycardia at 128 b.p.m. and diffuse, concave ST-segment elevations with PR-segment depression in all leads except aVR and V1. Laboratory tests revealed normal cardiac markers, D-dimer, complete blood count, C-reactive protein, and procalcitonin. Thyroid?stimulating hormone was suppressed (< 0.0008 mIU/L) and free thyroxine 4 was elevated (61.24 pg/mL); methimazole was increased and propranolol was initiated. 

Autoimmun

title: An Unusual Case of Suspected Microvascular Angina in a Newborn
case_summary:
A term female newborn (3.700?kg) was born vaginally without prenatal complications. Family history was negative for heart disease. Before discharge, transthoracic echocardiography showed moderate tricuspid regurgitation due to valvular dysplasia; all other findings were normal. She was discharged for follow-up.

On day 6 of life she was admitted with tachypnea, sweating, and failure to thrive; weight was 3.220?kg. Signs of heart failure and respiratory distress were present: O2 saturation 96% on 6?L/min, venous blood gas revealed metabolic acidosis (pH 7.03, pO2 46?mmHg, pCO2 29?mmHg, HCO3-?12.3?mmol/L, BE -19). Mechanical ventilation was initiated. On arrival: BP 70/30?mmHg, HR 130?bpm, diuresis preserved. Examination showed a grade 3/6 systolic murmur, weak femoral pulses, and hepatomegaly (liver palpable 3?cm below costal margin).

Laboratory studies: high-sensitivity troponin T 1001?ng/L, CK-MB 37.4?mcg/L; venous blood gas on admission pH 7.1, pO2 65?mmHg, pCO2 28?mmHg, lactate 14.

title: Zero-Fluoroscopy Ablation for Atrial Re-Entry Via a Vein of Marshall Connection Using a Visible Sheath
case_summary:
A 76?year?old woman was referred for repeat catheter ablation because of highly symptomatic recurrence of atrial fibrillation. She had undergone pulmonary vein isolation, cavotricuspid isthmus ablation, and complex fractionated atrial electrogram ablation in 2018. Her medical history included surgical closure of a sinus venosus atrial septal defect and redirection of an anomalous right upper pulmonary vein at age 65. On admission, blood pressure was 130/64 mm Hg, heart rate 70 beats/min, and oxygen saturation 97% on room air. 

A 7?day Holter recording documented atrial fibrillation with a mean heart rate of 86 beats/min. Transthoracic echocardiography showed normal biventricular size and function, mild left ventricular diastolic dysfunction, and moderate left atrial enlargement (indexed volume 44 mL/m2). Preprocedural computed tomography demonstrated patent pulmonary veins draining normally to the left atrium.

A zero?fluoroscopy procedure was performed under general anesthesia. After

title: New-onset Takayasu's Arteritis as Acute Myocardial Infarction
case_summary:
A 25-year-old Japanese woman was brought to the emergency department with severe chest pain and transient loss of consciousness. She had no history of smoking, hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidemia, connective-tissue disease, or prior cardiac disease. Four days before presentation, she noted exertional chest discomfort that gradually worsened, sometimes with diaphoresis. On the day of admission, she developed severe chest pain with diaphoresis, fainted at a local clinic, and was transferred to a nearby hospital. 

Electrocardiography showed marked ST-segment elevation in lead aVR and widespread ST-segment depression elsewhere. Echocardiography revealed diffuse left ventricular hypokinesis with preservation of basal inferior wall motion. On arrival at our hospital, she was hypotensive and agitated (GCS E1V1M5), heart rate 113 bpm and irregular, jugular venous distension, coarse crackles bilaterally, and no murmurs. 

Laboratory studies revealed WBC 19,400/?L, CRP 8.18 mg/dL, CK 1100 IU/L

title: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus Presenting as Myopericarditis with Acute Heart Failure: A Case Report and Literature Review
case_summary: A 49-year-old Jamaican woman with no significant past medical history presented with 4 weeks of progressive dyspnea. On examination, she had marked jugular venous distension, bilateral basal crackles, a distended tender abdomen with positive fluid shift and hepatojugular reflux, and bilateral 2+ lower-leg pitting edema. Laboratory studies showed anemia, elevated troponin level, markedly increased N-terminal pro-B-type natriuretic peptide, elevated transaminases, mildly elevated TSH, and increased C-reactive protein. An ECG showed atrial flutter with a ventricular rate of 126 beats/min. A chest radiograph demonstrated a markedly enlarged cardiac silhouette with a water-bottle appearance. Transthoracic echocardiography on hospital day 1 revealed a severely dilated right ventricle, severe tricuspid regurgitation, moderate mitral regurgitation without structural abnormalities, a left ventricular ejection fraction of 30-40%, and a large pericardial effusion. CT of the chest with a pulmonary

title: Successful Treatment of Severe Paravalvular Leak by Repositioning a Self-Expandable Percutaneous Aortic Valve Bioprosthesis (Evolut PRO+) Using the "Double Snare" Technique
case_summary:
A 74-year-old man presented with progressive exertional dyspnea and orthopnea over 24 hours. He was afebrile (37.4 degreesC), hemodynamically stable (heart rate 81 beats/min, blood pressure 121/58?mmHg) with a wide pulse pressure (63?mmHg), and tachypneic (22 breaths/min) without hypoxia (oxygen saturation 96%). Examination revealed central congestion, a grade 3 diastolic decrescendo murmur at the left upper sternal border, and warm extremities without edema. He had undergone transfemoral transcatheter aortic valve replacement 3 days earlier with a 34?mm self-expanding bioprosthesis and was discharged the next day without complications.

His medical history included mesenteric low-grade follicular B-cell lymphoma, nonobstructive coronary artery disease, and severe aortic stenosis with a left ventricular ejection fraction of 20-25%. 

Electrocardiography showed sinus rhythm without new ischemic changes. High-sensitive troponin levels were elevated (399 -> 362 -> 420?ng/L; normal <22?ng/

title: Rheumatic heart disease of the mitral valve alongside the papillary fibroelastoma of the aortic valve: A case report
case_summary:
A 47-year-old woman presented with worsening dyspnea over 2 weeks. She denied chest pain, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, hemoptysis, and right-upper-quadrant pain. She had no history of coronary disease, pulmonary disease, cancer, or prior cardiac surgery. Family history was noncontributory.

On examination, temperature was 36.5 degreesC, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, heart rate 82 beats/min. A 4/6 mid-diastolic murmur was heard at the apex. There was bilateral pitting edema of the lower extremities and expiratory wheezing. The New York Heart Association functional class was III.

Laboratory studies, including a complete blood count with differential, cardiac enzymes, and three sets of blood cultures, revealed no evidence of infection. Chest radiography demonstrated displacement and widening of the carina, elevation of the left main bronchus, Kerley B lines, cardiomegaly with a straightened left border, and prominent pulmonary vasculature. Electrocardiography showed atrial fibr

title: Looking beyond the heart in cardiac magnetic resonance imaging: an interesting case report of Takayasu arteritis presenting with left ventricular apical aneurysm
case_summary:
A 35-year-old woman of Southeast Asian (Indian) origin presented with sudden-onset retrosternal chest pain for 8 hours, associated with dyspnea and palpitations. The pain began at rest and was not precipitated by exertion. She had no prior medical or surgical history.

On examination, heart rate was 115 beats per minute (sinus tachycardia), blood pressure was 90/60 mmHg, and jugular venous pressure was elevated to the angle of the mandible. Cardiac auscultation revealed an S3 gallop; bilateral basal lung crackles were present.

Electrocardiogram showed ST-segment elevation in the antero-inferior leads. Troponin I level was 1.2 ng/mL. Chest radiograph revealed mild cardiomegaly (cardiothoracic ratio 0.62) and grade I pulmonary venous hypertension.

She underwent emergent coronary angiography for presumed acute myocardial infarction; the coronaries were angiographically normal. A left ventriculogram demonstrated a large apical aneurysmal outpouching. Transthoracic echocardiography confir

title: Complement inhibition for the treatment of COVID-19 triggered thrombotic microangiopathy with cardiac failure: a case report
case_summary:
A 74-year-old Caucasian man was admitted with 2 days of malaise, dyspnea, and cough. On presentation, temperature was 37.6 degreesC, blood pressure 124/70 mmHg, pulse 135/min and irregular, and oxygen saturation 91% on 6 L/min oxygen. He had bilateral crackles and basal interstitial infiltrates on lung auscultation, and atrial fibrillation on electrocardiogram. A nasopharyngeal swab confirmed SARS-CoV-2 infection; he was started on remdesivir and dexamethasone.

On hospital day 5, he developed acute chest pain, ST-elevation in leads II, III, and aVF, and troponin I elevated to 116 ng/L. Coronary angiography revealed only chronic thrombotic occlusion of marginal branches of the left circumflex artery. He was admitted to the ICU in cardiogenic shock (cardiac index 1.2 L/min/m?, pulmonary capillary wedge pressure 27 mmHg, mean pulmonary artery pressure 33 mmHg). Echocardiography showed a normal-size left ventricle with severely reduced ejection fraction (15%) and global akinesia, and seve

title: Doxorubicin?associated takotsubo cardiomyopathy in a patient with adult T?cell leukemia/lymphoma
case_summary:
A 53-year-old Afro-Caribbean woman with no history of cardiac disease presented with 3 weeks of confusion, anorexia, a 20-lb weight loss, and fatigue. She denied fever, headache, chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, or edema. In the emergency department her blood pressure was 92/52 mm Hg, heart rate 129 beats/min, and she was lethargic but without focal neurologic deficits. Examination revealed small, painless supraclavicular and inguinal lymph nodes; heart and lung exams were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed a white-blood-cell count of 88 000/?L, creatinine 4.8 mg/dL, and corrected calcium 21 mg/dL. Peripheral smear demonstrated atypical lymphocytes. Additional studies were consistent with tumor lysis syndrome. Imaging revealed diffuse lytic bone lesions and enlarged lymph nodes. Bone marrow biopsy and flow cytometry confirmed a T-cell clonal neoplasm, and HTLV-1 serology was positive, establishing acute adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma (ATLL). A baseline transthoracic echocardiog

title: Infectious stentitis after treatment of coarctation of the aorta: a case report
case_summary: A 53-year-old man with a history of a small restrictive muscular ventricular septal defect, a bicuspid aortic valve (right-left coronary cusp type), and percutaneous stent placement for coarctation of the aorta in 2016 presented with several weeks of fatigue, night sweats, and chills. On examination, he was febrile (40 degreesC), blood pressure was 153/78 mm Hg, and heart rate was 106 b.p.m.; a known systolic murmur was noted. The ECG showed sinus tachycardia with first-degree atrioventricular block. Laboratory studies revealed a leucocyte count of 11.1 ? 10^9 /L, C-reactive protein of 66 mg/L, and haemoglobin of 8.2 mmol/L. Blood cultures were obtained, and intravenous ceftriaxone was started for presumed endocarditis. Transthoracic echocardiography showed no evidence of endocarditis. Transoesophageal echocardiography demonstrated that the bicuspid valve functioned well, with a peak velocity of 1.8 m/s, no regurgitation, and no vegetations. Blood cultures grew Streptococcus gordonii,

title: Severe pulmonary hypertension due to combined pulmonary fibrosis and emphysema: another cause of death among smokers
case_summary:
A 63-year-old Caucasian woman presented with severe dyspnea of 1 month' duration, which had been progressively worsening over 2 years. She had a 102-pack-year smoking history and was treated irregularly for hypertension. During the prior 2 years she made five ED visits for dyspnea, each attributed to decompensated COPD from respiratory infection.  
Eighteen months earlier, chest CT demonstrated marked pulmonary hypertension--enlarged pulmonary trunk and branches, right-heart chamber dilation, moderate pericardial effusion. Pulmonary windows showed ground-glass opacities in the lung bases with subpleural sparing, thin reticulation, and traction bronchiectasis but no honeycomb cysts, consistent with non-specific interstitial pneumonia; paraseptal emphysema was noted in the upper lobes. Mild splenomegaly was also seen.  
Transthoracic echocardiography revealed normal left ventricular systolic function and an estimated pulmonary artery pressure of 100 mmHg, confirmed by right-heart cathete

title: Extraskeletal calcifications in a dialysis-dependent teenager: A novel cause of acquired pediatric complete heart block
case_summary:
A 16-year-old female with peritoneal dialysis-dependent end-stage renal disease secondary to steroid-resistant nephrotic syndrome and calcineurin inhibitor toxicity was noted to be bradycardic at a routine outpatient dialysis clinic visit. Her medical history included anemia of ESRD, malnutrition requiring a gastrojejunal feeding tube, chronic kidney disease-mineral and bone disorder (CKD-MBD), wheelchair dependence for chronic pain and deconditioning, and significant medication nonadherence in the setting of persistent depressive disorder.

An electrocardiogram demonstrated new complete atrioventricular (AV) block with a junctional escape rhythm at 50 beats per minute and a borderline prolonged QTc of 465 ms. Seventeen months earlier, an ECG had shown normal sinus rhythm with PR intervals between 130 and 160 ms; a Holter monitor 13 months earlier similarly demonstrated intact AV conduction. She denied recent fever, viral symptoms, syncope, or near-syncope; she reported mild fatigue bu

title: Parsing a perplexing paroxysmal pathway
case_summary:
A 34-year-old man with hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a 5-year history of intermittent, self-limited palpitations presented with sudden onset of palpitations associated with chest pain and dyspnea. In the emergency department, he was found to be in supraventricular tachycardia at 220 beats per minute. The tachycardia terminated spontaneously, and echocardiography and other imaging showed no structural heart disease. He was referred for catheter ablation.

During electrophysiologic study, electrode catheters were positioned in the right atrium, right ventricle, His-bundle region, and coronary sinus. Baseline intervals were: sinus cycle length 680 ms, atrial-His 67 ms, His-ventricular 32 ms. Pacing the right ventricle at both apex and base revealed ventriculoatrial (VA) dissociation at cycle lengths of 350 to 650 ms. Programmed atrial stimulation showed no ventricular preexcitation or dual AV nodal physiology; the AV Wenckebach cycle length was 280 ms.

Tachycardia was readily induced

title: Pacemaker implantation in a patient with isolated persistent left superior vena cava using a delivery catheter: a case report
case_summary:
A 47-year-old woman with a history of sick sinus syndrome diagnosed 9 years earlier presented with progressive shortness of breath and a decline from New York Heart Association functional class I to II. A Holter electrocardiogram revealed a total heart rate of approximately 55 000 beats per day. Chest radiography showed an increased cardiothoracic ratio, and stress echocardiography demonstrated an impaired heart rate response to exercise. The patient was scheduled for permanent pacemaker implantation. 

On admission, her blood pressure was 109/66 mmHg, heart rate 37 bpm, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Physical examination revealed clear lung sounds, no heart murmurs, and no lower extremity edema. Her medical history included diabetes mellitus and a treated submucous uterine myoma. Laboratory studies showed an N-terminal prohormone of brain natriuretic peptide level of 132 pg/mL (normal <55 pg/mL), with normal creatinine and electrolytes. 

Chest radiography revealed no lung inf

title: Recurrent Coronary Vasospasm in a 50-Year-Old Woman with Granulomatous Polyangiitis: A Case Report
case_summary:
A 50-year-old woman was transferred at 23:00 for acute-onset, severe, retrosternal squeezing chest pain and dyspnea. She had a history of bilateral peroneal neuropathy of unknown origin and a cerebral hemorrhage 6 years earlier (decompressive craniotomy). She had well-controlled hypertension on long-term diltiazem and no diabetes, dyslipidemia, smoking, or family history of coronary artery disease.

An ECG from the referring hospital showed ST-segment elevation in the inferior, anterolateral, and posterior leads, and dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin plus ticagrelor) was started. On admission, vital signs were stable and the cardiovascular exam was unremarkable. A repeat ECG showed persistent ST-segment elevation in the same leads. Laboratory tests revealed troponin T 50 ng/l and myoglobin 89 ng/l. Complete blood count, liver and renal function, lipid profile, C-reactive protein, and erythrocyte sedimentation rate were normal or negative. Echocardiography showed a left ventricular eje

title: Type A Aortic Dissection in a Previously Healthy Pregnant Patient: A Challenging Dilemma, Case Report, and Literature Review
case_summary:
A 24-year-old woman, gravida 6 para 4 with one abortion, at 25 weeks of gestation presented with sudden-onset midsternal chest pain and two days of fatigue. She had received no prenatal care and her only notable history was lens dislocation requiring prior ophthalmic surgery.  
On examination, her temperature was 36.8  degreesC, pulse 88 beats/min, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Blood pressure was 120/65 mmHg in the left arm and 90/60 mmHg in the right arm. Cardiac auscultation revealed a diastolic murmur.  
An electrocardiogram showed no pathologic findings. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated mild mitral valve prolapse, mild-to-moderate aortic regurgitation, and a linear flap at the sinuses of Valsalva.  
Laboratory evaluation, including a complete blood count, coagulation profile, and renal and hepatic function tests, was within normal limits.  
Fetal sonography revealed a male fetus appropriate for gestational age, uterine tocography

title: Sarcoidosis-induced pericarditis in a patient with portopulmonary hypertension: a case report
case_summary:
A 51-year-old Caucasian man was admitted with a 20-day history of progressive dyspnea and fatigue without chest pain, cough, or fever. He had a history of Wilson's disease diagnosed 8 years earlier, treated successfully with D-penicillamine, complicated by well-compensated cirrhosis. Six and a half years later, he was found to have pulmonary hypertension on echocardiography and treated with bosentan and sildenafil.

On examination, he had jugular venous distention, peripheral edema, vascular spiders, a systolic tricuspid murmur, accentuated and split second heart sounds in the pulmonary area, and splenomegaly. ECG showed right-axis deviation and right bundle branch block. Chest radiography revealed a "water bottle"-shaped cardiac silhouette and bilateral hilar enlargement. Echocardiography demonstrated a large pericardial effusion, dilated right heart chambers, mild tricuspid regurgitation, and an estimated RV systolic pressure of 75-80 mmHg (normal <25 mmHg).

Laboratory tests showed

title: Necrotizing fasciitis resembled acute coronary syndrome: A case report
case_summary:
A 60-year-old man without prior medical history presented with 4 hours of severe, continuous, compressive left-sided chest pain radiating to the left hand, aggravated by exertion, relieved by nitroglycerine, and accompanied by diaphoresis and nausea. He denied fever, dyspnea, palpitations, or trauma. He quit smoking 10 years ago and stopped opium 3 months ago. He arrived hypotensive (85/55 mmHg on norepinephrine), tachycardic (103 bpm), and afebrile (36.9 degreesC), with cold lower extremities and an otherwise unremarkable chest, abdominal, heart, and lung exam.

An initial ECG showed ST depression in V5 and V6, which later resolved with T-wave inversion. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a normal-sized left ventricle with severe systolic dysfunction (LVEF 15-20%) and global hypokinesia, an enlarged right ventricle with mild dysfunction, mild mitral regurgitation, and mild to moderate tricuspid regurgitation. Coronary angiography demonstrated significant mid-left anterior descend

title: Acute myocardial infarction complicated by purulent pericarditis: A case report
case_summary:
An early-80s man with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and dyslipidemia (no smoking history) was brought in for progressive dyspnea since that morning. On arrival: BP 140/70?mmHg, SpO2 94% on humidified oxygen, bradycardia, and no fever. Electrocardiography showed ST-segment elevation in leads II, III, and aVF. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated normal left ventricular function and no pericardial effusion. Cardiac biomarkers were elevated (CK-MB 290?ng/ml; troponin I 91.69?ng/ml), and acute inferior myocardial infarction was diagnosed. The patient received aspirin 200?mg, prasugrel 20?mg, and underwent temporary pacing for bradycardia. Coronary angiography revealed complete occlusion of the right coronary artery, which was successfully revascularized. Post-PCI medications included aspirin, prasugrel, a proton-pump inhibitor, statin, linagliptin, empagliflozin, and a continuous heparin infusion.  

That night, he developed fever and tachypnea with worsening respiratory status. Th

title: Spectrum of cardiac manifestations from aconitine poisoning
case_summary:
A 62-year-old Hmong man presented with sudden-onset chest pain, dizziness, and palpitations. He appeared mildly distressed and weak. Initial vital signs: blood pressure 60-80/?, heart rate 150-220 bpm. ECG showed sinus rhythm with low-amplitude P waves and junctional beats with narrow QRS complexes, followed by ventricular ectopic beats with right bundle branch block morphology and left axis deviation, diffuse ST-segment depression, a prolonged QT interval, and ectopics on the terminal portion of the T wave.  
Laboratory results: potassium 2.9 mmol/L, pH 7.21, anion gap 17, lactate 9.7 mmol/L, troponin T <0.01 ng/mL, digoxin level 0.1 ng/mL. Intravenous saline was administered.  
Bedside echocardiography revealed hyperdynamic left ventricular function without regional wall motion abnormalities or pericardial effusion, with no evidence of Takotsubo cardiomyopathy. Urgent coronary angiography showed nonobstructive coronary atherosclerosis with slow flow; ventriculography confirmed hyperd

title: A case report of non-lamin A/C dilated cardiomyopathy presenting in a patient with Najjar-Malouf syndrome
case_summary:
A 45-year-old woman was admitted with decompensated heart failure. Her history included:
? Postnatal cardiomegaly and a ventricular septal defect with a 40% left-to-right shunt.  
? At age 13, psychomotor developmental delay; at age 19, hypergonadotropic hypogonadism treated with oestradiol and norethisterone.  
? At age 31, despite exertional fatigue, myalgias, arthralgias, and a positive Sokolow index on ECG, transthoracic echocardiography was normal.  
? No family history of cardiac or other relevant disease; parents are unrelated.  

Current symptoms developed over weeks and included thoracic tightness, dyspnoea on exertion, orthopnoea, rapid fatigability, ankle and eyelid oedema, and unproductive cough. Examination:
? Blood pressure 106/79 mmHg; pulse 90/min regular with frequent extrasystoles.  
? 2/6 band-shaped systolic murmur at the apex radiating to the axilla.  
? Signs of decompensation: jugular venous distension, ankle oedema, cool extremities; breath sounds diminished at

title: Accelerated idioventricular rhythm after left atrial tachycardia ablation as a marker of acute coronary ischemia
case_summary: A 43-year-old man with persistent atrial tachycardia and three prior left atrial ablation procedures for atrial fibrillation presented for repeat ablation. He had no other medical history. Preprocedure echocardiography showed a structurally normal heart without regional wall motion abnormalities. The procedure was performed on uninterrupted warfarin. After induction of anesthesia and transesophageal echocardiography to exclude left atrial appendage thrombus, transseptal punctures were made and an electroanatomic map of the left atrium was generated. Endocardial RF energy failed to block a perimitral flutter, so epicardial RF energy was delivered within the distal coronary sinus (maximum 20 W, temperature limit 43 degreesC, irrigation 25 mL/min) for a total of 103 seconds, achieving an average power of 16 W and temperature of 39 degreesC. The perimitral tachycardia terminated, and subsequent arrhythmias were successfully ablated. Anticoagulation with heparin maintained an activated clot

title: Transesophageal echo diagnosis of perioperative unusual transient left ventricular apical ballooning syndrome
case_summary:
A 22-year-old, 48-kg woman with a 6-month history of temporomandibular joint pain was scheduled for bilateral temporomandibular meniscopexy. Her previous anesthesia included an uneventful appendectomy at age 10. On arrival, HR was 92 bpm, RR 16/min, BP 128/78 mmHg, and SpO? 91% on room air. Anesthesia was induced with midazolam 2 mg IV, remifentanil infusion, propofol 140 mg IV, and rocuronium 35 mg IV. Following topical vasoconstrictor and local anesthetic, nasotracheal intubation was confirmed uneventfully.

Shortly after securing the airway, the patient developed sinus bradycardia with ST changes that rapidly progressed to supraventricular tachycardia (HR 168 bpm), hypotension, desaturation, piloerection, and persistent ST changes. Airway pressures rose with rhonchi, and bronchospasm was suspected; she received four puffs of salbutamol and 100 mg IV hydrocortisone. Bloody fluid then appeared in the endotracheal tube, and chest radiography showed severe bilateral pulmonary edema.

An

title: Acute Polyserositis with Cardiac Tamponade and Bilateral Refractory Pleural Effusion after ChAdOx1 nCoV-19 Vaccination
case_summary:
A 94-year-old man presented to the emergency department with acute onset, rapidly progressive dyspnea and fatigue. His symptoms began two days after receiving his first dose of the ChAdOx1 nCoV-19 (AstraZeneca) vaccine and worsened over four days before presentation. He had a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia treated with amlodipine and olmesartan; he had no history of heart failure, coronary artery disease, renal disease, cirrhosis, autoimmune disease, or malignancy.

On examination, he was hypotensive (blood pressure 99/51 mmHg), heart rate 61 beats per minute, respiratory rate 22 breaths per minute, oxygen saturation 94% on 3 L of nasal cannula oxygen, and afebrile (35.5  degreesC). Cardiac examination revealed distant heart sounds; pulmonary examination showed decreased breath sounds over the bilateral lower lung fields.

Laboratory studies demonstrated a white blood cell count of 4500 /uL, blood urea nitrogen 26 mg/dL, creatinine 1.11 mg/dL, C-reactive protein 0.19 mg/dL,

title: Stokes-Adams syndrome, a rarely reported disease
case_summary: A 78-year-old man with type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, chronic arterial disease, and prior right coronary artery stenting presented with 15 days of dizziness and loss of postural tone. He experienced episodes of loss of consciousness lasting about 1 minute, with clonic movements of all four extremities, upward gaze deviation, and a brief postictal somnolence. On admission, his blood pressure was 130/70 mmHg, heart rate 40 beats per minute, and other vital signs were normal. Laboratory tests, including complete blood count and electrolytes, were within normal limits. An electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm with bradycardia and a 2:1 atrioventricular block without ischemic changes. Noncontrast head CT demonstrated cavernous-segment internal carotid angiosclerosis and age-related changes without acute infarction. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed moderate concentric left ventricular hypertrophy, type I diastolic dysfunction, preserved ejection fraction of 63%, and mitral an

title: COVID-19 Induced Cardiomyopathy Successfully Treated with Tocilizumab
case_summary:
A 39-year-old woman with well-controlled hypertension had a mild SARS-CoV-2 infection confirmed by PCR one month earlier. She now presents with a 3-day history of worsening generalized body aches, chills, fever, watery diarrhea, nausea, mild dry cough, mild dyspnea, and nonspecific chest pain. On admission, her blood pressure was 82/44 mmHg, heart rate 132 beats per minute, temperature 101.5 degreesF (38.6 degreesC), and oxygen saturation remained above 90% on 2 L nasal cannula. Cardiovascular examination revealed an S3 gallop, 2+ peripheral pulses, warm extremities, and bilateral pitting edema. Pulmonary examination was unremarkable.  
Laboratory studies showed positive SARS-CoV-2 RNA by PCR, troponin-I 4.295 ng/mL, B-type natriuretic peptide 2291 pg/mL, and elevated inflammatory markers including LDH, ferritin, C-reactive protein, procalcitonin, and interleukin-6. Blood cultures were negative. Electrocardiogram demonstrated sinus tachycardia without ST-segment changes. Chest radiogra

title: Erdheim-Chester Disease presenting with constrictive pericarditis: A case report and review of the literature
case_summary: A 62-year-old man of Chinese origin presented with a 2-month history of cough, expectoration, dyspnea, bilateral lower-extremity edema, and a 5-kg weight loss over the past month. He had a history of coronary artery disease and carotid artery stenosis. On examination, he was tachycardic with jugular venous distension, no Kussmaul's sign, and symmetric concave lower-extremity edema. Laboratory tests showed an elevated NT-proBNP (4051 pg/mL; normal <125 pg/mL) and C-reactive protein (7.89 mg/dL; normal <0.8 mg/dL), with normal blood counts, liver, and renal function. Electrocardiography revealed sinus tachycardia, low?voltage limb leads, and T-wave changes. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated pericardial thickening up to 9.4 mm without effusion, ventricular septal bounce, biatrial enlargement, reduced LVEF (42%), diastolic dysfunction, and plethoric IVC with reduced inspiratory collapse. Contrast-enhanced cardiac CT showed pericardial thickening without calcification, a dilated a

title: A rare cause of acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction: case report of native aortic valve thrombosis
case_summary: A 69-year-old woman with no history of cardiovascular disease or risk factors woke with acute, typical chest pain and called for an ambulance. Prehospital ECG showed acute anterolateral ST-elevation myocardial infarction. On arrival at the catheterization laboratory, her heart rate was 91 b.p.m., blood pressure 133/97 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 96% on room air. Shortly after arrival, she developed pulmonary edema and cardiogenic shock, with pulseless electrical activity requiring intermittent CPR supported by a mechanical compression device. During CPR, coronary angiography showed no evidence of obstructive coronary artery disease. A transthoracic echocardiogram (image quality limited by the patient's critical state) excluded pericardial effusion, significant left-sided valvular disease, and revealed no direct or indirect signs of pulmonary embolism or aortic dissection. Despite continued resuscitation, she died of cardiogenic shock of unknown origin.

title: Persistent Unexplained Dyspnea: A Case of Hepatopulmonary Syndrome
case_summary:
A 53-year-old man was admitted for progressive dyspnea (NYHA class III). His history included a congenital ventricular septal defect repaired in childhood, subsequent aortic valve and septal patch replacements, permanent atrial fibrillation on anticoagulation, chronic hepatitis C-related liver disease, and prior admission for heart failure with preserved ejection fraction. On examination, he had jugular venous distension, irregular rhythm, a 2/6 systolic murmur at the aortic and mitral areas, decreased breath sounds at the right lung base, and peripheral edema.  
ECG showed atrial fibrillation with left bundle branch block. Chest radiography demonstrated pulmonary congestion and a right basal pleural effusion. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed: a dilated left ventricle with preserved ejection fraction (60%), a dilated right ventricle with normal systolic function, no residual septal shunt, a normally functioning mechanical aortic prosthesis but elevated transprosthetic gradients

title: Diagnostic significance of adenosine deaminase in pleural effusate for primary effusion lymphoma-like lymphoma
case_summary:
A 74-year-old man was evaluated for dyspnea on exertion of 4 days' duration and a 5-kg weight gain over 1 week. His medical history included chronic heart failure from cardiac amyloidosis, hypertension, deep venous thrombosis, chronic kidney disease, and dyslipidemia. Medications included tolvaptan, edoxaban, bezafibrate, and bisoprolol. 

On initial admission, dyspnea progressed to resting dyspnea. Chest radiography showed a large left pleural effusion with rightward mediastinal shift. Chest CT confirmed a left pleural effusion without lymphadenopathy. Echocardiography showed normal cardiac function and no pericardial effusion. Thoracentesis over 4 days yielded 3300 mL of hemorrhagic fluid, with symptomatic relief; the patient was discharged after 1 week. Cytologic examination noted atypical lymphocytes, and the effusion gradually recurred.

One month later, he presented with recurrent dyspnea, weight gain, low-grade fever, night sweats, anorexia, malaise, and chest pain. Temperature

title: Massive myocardial edema and inflow cannula obstruction due to epicardial surgical ventricular tachycardia cryoablation at time of left ventricular assist device implantation
case_summary:
A 61-year-old man with nonischemic idiopathic cardiomyopathy and recurrent ventricular tachycardia refractory to multiple endocardial ablations was admitted for acute decompensated heart failure. Given his intractable arrhythmias and advanced heart failure, a multidisciplinary team elected to perform simultaneous surgical arrhythmia ablation and left ventricular assist device (LVAD) implantation. 

Intraoperative course:
? Surgical epicardial cryoablation was performed on cardiopulmonary bypass, targeting inducible VT substrates in the basal lateral and inferior left ventricle.  
? A HeartWare HVAD was implanted; the patient was successfully weaned from bypass at 2700 rpm with flows of 3.0 L/min.  
? Intraoperative transesophageal echocardiography (TEE) at the end of the procedure demonstrated low-normal left ventricular size and normal wall thickness.  

Postoperative course in the cardiothoracic ICU:
? Initially stable, the patient subsequently developed persistently low VAD flows (<

title: Endomyocardial fibrosis of the right ventricle in a patient with schistosomiasis: a case report
case_summary: A 50-year-old fisherman from northeastern Brazil, with past contact with contaminated fresh water, was referred for evaluation of several years of lower-limb edema, ascites, and jugular venous distention. He had a history of atrial fibrillation, arterial hypertension, and liver disease of unknown cause. On examination, there was a 3/6 pansystolic murmur at the left lower sternal edge. Laboratory studies showed thrombocytopenia (75 000/mm3), normal renal and liver enzymes, and a brain natriuretic peptide level of 198 pg/mL (?35). The electrocardiogram showed atrial fibrillation. Chest radiography revealed cardiomegaly with right atrial enlargement. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a normal-sized left ventricle with preserved ejection fraction; the right ventricle had small cavity size and its apical region was occupied by a homogeneous hypoechoic mass causing obliteration and restriction, with severe tricuspid regurgitation due to annular dilatation and leaflet tethering; an

title: A Case of Chylothorax in a Hemodialysis Patient with Left Innominate Venous Stenosis
case_summary:
A 45-year-old man with end-stage renal disease secondary to obstructive nephropathy presented with 2 months of swelling of the left side of his neck, face, and left upper limb. He denied recent trauma, fever, or chills. His medications included amlodipine, pantoprazole, carvedilol, calcium carbonate, and calcitriol. He had been on hemodialysis for 1? years via a left internal jugular catheter. After failure of a left radiocephalic arteriovenous fistula, a left brachiocephalic fistula was created, after which he developed severe venous hypertension of the left arm.

He denied chest pain, night sweats, or weight loss. On examination, he was afebrile with a blood pressure of 140/90 mmHg. There was diffuse edema over the left face, neck, chest, and left upper limb. The arteriovenous fistula showed no signs of infection. There was no thyroid enlargement or lymphadenopathy. Jugular veins were not distended, cardiac auscultation was normal, and lung fields were clear.

Laboratory studies show

title: Pannus formation: a rare culprit of early bioprosthetic valve dysfunction--a case report
case_summary:
An 84-year-old man presented with progressive exertional dyspnoea 3 years after surgical aortic valve replacement (SAVR) with a 19 mm bioprosthetic valve for severe aortic stenosis. His medical history included hypertension and hyperuricaemia managed without medication. Preoperative echocardiography had shown an annulus diameter of 19 mm; a 19 mm valve was implanted after a 21 mm sizer would not pass through the annulus, yielding an effective orifice area index of 0.97 cm2/m2 in a patient with body surface area (BSA) 1.52 m2.

On postoperative day 11, transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) showed a trans-prosthetic peak velocity of 3.0 m/s and a mean pressure gradient (PG) of 20.8 mmHg. Two years later, TTE demonstrated a peak velocity of 4.7 m/s and mean PG of 54 mmHg but no paravalvular leakage. The patient was treated with oral furosemide and spironolactone, yet his dyspnoea progressed.

At readmission, he had a harsh systolic murmur at the right upper sternal border, bilateral lung c

title: Heparin-resistance in AL amyloidosis: a case report
case_summary:
A 70-year-old man with a 6-month history of progressive dyspnea and retrosternal pain was evaluated and found to have severe aortic stenosis (peak gradient 76 mmHg, mean gradient 51 mmHg, dimensionless index 0.22) with concentric left ventricular hypertrophy (EF 60%) and significant two-vessel coronary artery disease (LAD and circumflex). His medical history was unremarkable apart from a recent thromboembolic event of the right retina attributed to a 50-69% stenosis of the right internal carotid artery; clopidogrel was initiated.

On day 1, he underwent planned aortic valve replacement with on-pump CABG. Preoperative labs included baseline ACT 115 s (target 480 s). He received sequential boluses of unfractionated heparin totaling 75 000 IU (approximately 1057 IU/kg) and two doses of antithrombin-III concentrate (500 IU each). Despite these measures, the ACT never exceeded 310 s. Antithrombin-III activity measured before and after supplementation was within normal limits. The procedure

title: Brugada Pattern in a Child with Severe SARS-CoV-2 Related Multisystem Inflammatory Syndrome
case_summary:
A previously healthy 7-year-old boy presented with 7 days of fever, abdominal pain, and vomiting. On arrival he was febrile (38.3  degreesC), tachycardic (150 bpm), tachypneic (26 breaths/min), and normotensive (95/55 mmHg); oxygen saturation was 99% on room air. He appeared pale with bilateral conjunctival injection and periorbital edema; his abdomen was tender with guarding in the periumbilical area. There was no rash or lymphadenopathy. His medical history was notable only for a mild SARS-CoV-2 infection two months earlier. There was no personal history of syncope; a paternal cousin had died suddenly at age 35, without a known cause.

Initial laboratories showed leukocytosis (16 700/uL; neutrophils 85.2%), lymphopenia (10.8%), hyponatremia (Na+ 130 mEq/L), hypoalbuminemia (2.6 g/dL), elevated inflammatory markers (procalcitonin 0.99 ng/mL, C-reactive protein 53.9 mg/L, ferritin 452 ng/mL, interleukin-6 14? upper limit), elevated cardiac markers (troponin 23 ng/L, BNP 763 pg/mL), D-d

title: Rupture of sinus of Valsalva in a young laborer: A rare case report
case_summary:
A 26-year-old male laborer presented with sudden onset of severe chest pain, shortness of breath, and dizziness occurring 3 months after an initial cough that progressed to dyspnea. He reported these symptoms began immediately after strenuous work involving heavy lifting. He denied any past cardiac history or family history of congenital or hereditary disorders.

On examination, he was pale and diaphoretic. Temperature was 98 degreesF, heart rate 120 beats per minute, blood pressure 148/100 mmHg, respiratory rate 30 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Cardiovascular examination revealed a harsh pansystolic murmur best heard at the right upper sternal border. Lung fields were clear, and the remainder of the exam was unremarkable.

Electrocardiogram showed sinus tachycardia without ischemic changes. Chest radiography demonstrated cardiomegaly with an increased cardiothoracic ratio. Routine laboratory studies, including complete blood count, metabolic panel, coagula

title: Adult-onset Still's disease masquerading as acute coronary syndrome: a case report and review of the literature
case_summary:
A 36-year-old Hispanic man with no significant past medical history presented with two weeks of cough, shortness of breath, and persistent sore throat. He was treated outpatient with azithromycin and prednisone; a rapid strep test was negative. He then developed spastic back pain radiating to his left leg, followed days later by 8/10 substernal chest pain and exertional dyspnea. Over the next week he spiked fevers to 38.9-40  degreesC daily. On the third day of fever he presented to an outside emergency department, declined lumbar puncture, and returned two days later with abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, chest pain, and dyspnea at rest.  
Initial studies showed a neutrophilic leukocytosis of 44 ? 10^3/uL, C-reactive protein of 41 mg/dL, elevated troponin, and an electrocardiogram concerning for an ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction. He underwent left and right heart catheterization, which revealed clean coronary arteries, but he developed severe hypotension requiring vasopres

title: Tako-tsubo cardiomyopathy after administration of ergometrine following elective caesarean delivery: a case report
case_summary: A 28-year-old healthy woman at 37 weeks of gestation was admitted for elective cesarean delivery. Intravenous methylergometrine (0.2 mg) and oxytocin (30 IU) were administered post-delivery to facilitate placental release and prevent postpartum hemorrhage. Thirty minutes after delivery she developed acute chest pain and respiratory distress. On examination, her blood pressure was 100/60 mmHg, heart rate 100 beats/min, and pulmonary rales were present bilaterally (Killip class II). Arterial pO2 was 52 mmHg on room air. ECG showed sinus tachycardia without ST-segment changes. Chest radiography demonstrated marked pulmonary consolidation, and NT-proBNP was 3900 pg/mL. Oxygen supplementation and intravenous loop diuretics rapidly improved her respiratory status. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed moderately reduced left ventricular systolic function with midventricular hypokinesia; the left ventricular end-diastolic diameter was normal, and ejection fraction was 38% by Simpson's metho

title: A case report of primary cardiac sarcoma: a diagnostic and therapeutic challenge
case_summary: An 18-year-old woman with no prior medical history presented with a one-month history of fatigue and lethargy and 2-3 days of progressive dyspnoea. She reported presyncope, severe dyspnoea on minimal exertion, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea, chest tightness, and palpitations. She was a non-smoker without relevant family history. On examination, she was afebrile; respiratory rate, 22 breaths per minute; oxygen saturation, 98% on room air; heart rate, 140 beats per minute in sinus rhythm; blood pressure, 120/80 mmHg. There was no peripheral oedema or jugular venous distension. Cardiac auscultation revealed a grade II/VI pan-diastolic murmur; breath sounds were decreased at both lung bases. Laboratory tests showed a d-dimer of 6.6 ?g/mL (normal <0.5 ?g/mL) and haemoglobin 13.7 g/dL; other blood counts and biochemistry were normal. An ECG demonstrated sinus tachycardia with a normal P-wave axis and a narrow QRS complex. Chest radiography revealed mild bilateral costophrenic-angle blunting.

title: Dual atrial rhythms: a case report of an unusual cause of pacemaker syndrome
case_summary:
An 87-year-old woman presented with 1 week of dyspnoea and fatigue. Two weeks earlier, she underwent revision of her right atrial pacemaker lead for loss of sensing and capture. She has a history of Ebstein's anomaly with prior tricuspid valve surgery, sinus-node dysfunction, paroxysmal atrial tachycardia, and atrial flutter, and 3 years ago was upgraded to a dual-chamber pacemaker programmed to DDDR with automatic mode switching to VDIR for atrial rates >170 b.p.m. Her medications included nebivolol, warfarin, and amiodarone (held for 2 months).

On examination, blood pressure was 128/78 mmHg, heart rate 70 b.p.m., respiratory rate 16 per minute, temperature 98.2  degreesF, and oxygen saturation 95% on room air. There was a grade 3/6 pansystolic murmur at the left parasternal border and fine crackles at both lung bases; there was no jugular venous distension or peripheral oedema. Laboratory tests showed a B-type natriuretic peptide level of 925 pg/mL and normal troponin I. Chest radio

title: Case Report: Post-operative mitral valve replacement complicating with a large cardiac mass and the role of TEE in Imaging
case_summary:
A woman in her 50s presented with one year of progressive breathlessness (NYHA II->III) and three months of exertional palpitations relieved by rest. She had rheumatic heart disease managed conservatively and episodes of paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea but no orthopnea or familial cardiac disease.  
On examination, blood pressure was 130/78 mmHg and pulse 90 /min. The apex beat was displaced to the 6th intercostal space, 1 cm lateral to the midclavicular line, hyperdynamic and well localized. Auscultation revealed a non-ejection click at the apex, soft S1, widely split S2, and a grade 3/6 mid-systolic murmur at the apex radiating to the mid-axillary line; no S3 or S4.  
ECG showed sinus arrhythmia with notched P waves in inferior leads and biphasic P in V1, suggesting biatrial enlargement. Chest radiograph demonstrated cardiomegaly, straightening of the left heart border, and a double-density shadow.  
Transthoracic echocardiography revealed bileaflet mitral valve prolapse with an eccentr

title: Dual left anterior descending coronary artery (type IV): a rare coronary artery anomaly
final_diagnosis: Dual left anterior descending coronary artery
case_summary:
A 66-year-old man presented with three episodes of substernal chest pain over 2 days and was diagnosed with unstable angina. His medical history was notable for longstanding hypertension managed with beta-blockers and a history of chronic smoking; there was no family history of coronary artery disease. On examination, vital signs were stable. An electrocardiogram demonstrated dynamic ST-T changes in the anterior precordial leads. Cardiac enzymes were within normal limits, and two-dimensional echocardiography showed normal left ventricular function without regional wall-motion abnormalities. Routine laboratory tests were unremarkable.

Because of recurrent anginal episodes during hospitalization, coronary angiography was performed. Left coronary angiography revealed a left anterior descending artery (LAD) arising from the left main coronary artery that was unusually short and terminated prematurely in the anterior interventricular sulcus (AIVS), raising suspicion for mid-LAD occlusion.

title: Obstructive accessory mitral valve tissue in an adult patient: a case report
case_summary: A 72-year-old Caucasian man presented with progressive dyspnea over 3 months, worsening during the week before admission, and atypical chest pain. He was a heavy smoker with no known medical history; a family history of hypertension was noted. On examination, blood pressure was 155/75 mmHg, pulse 95 bpm; cardiac auscultation revealed a severe systolic murmur best heard in the aortic area, radiating to the left parasternal border and diminishing at the apex; lung fields were clear. A chest radiograph showed a normal cardiac silhouette and clear lungs. Electrocardiography revealed T-wave inversion in lateral leads and horizontal ST-segment depression in leads V4-V6. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated an oval, well-marginated tissue attached to the proximal anterior leaflet of the mitral valve, causing left ventricular outflow tract obstruction during systole, with a peak gradient of 55 mmHg; the aortic cusps were normal, left ventricular size and wall motion were normal, pulmona

title: Pseudo-hypotension with acute pulmonary oedema due to simultaneous bilateral subclavian artery stenosis in a patient with coronary artery bypass graft surgery using bilateral internal mammary arteries: a case report
case_summary:
A 75-year-old woman presented with acute severe dyspnea. Eight and thirteen years earlier, she had undergone coronary artery bypass grafting: the left internal mammary artery (LIMA) to the left anterior descending artery, the right internal mammary artery (RIMA) to an obtuse marginal branch, and a saphenous vein graft to the distal left circumflex artery. Her medical history included hypertension, diabetes mellitus (HbA1c 6.5%-6.7%), hyperlipidemia (LDL 100 mg/dL), and a bilateral neck bruit noted two years ago. MR angiography at that time showed <50% stenosis of both carotid arteries and 50% stenosis of both subclavian arteries. Ankle-brachial indices were elevated (ABI 1.62 on both sides), attributed to arterial stiffness, and she was asymptomatic without inter-arm blood pressure difference or arm claudication.

Six months before admission, she developed exertional chest discomfort. Two months later, her systolic blood pressure fell from 126/70 mmHg to 94/60 mmHg. ECG showed worsenin

title: Word of caution: clinically apparent coronary spasm following pulsed field cavotricuspid isthmus ablation despite nitroglycerin prophylaxis - a case report
case_summary:
A 75-year-old woman with a history of symptomatic paroxysmal atrial fibrillation and typical atrial flutter was referred for catheter ablation. She reported palpitations and dyspnea during arrhythmia episodes and one episode of chest discomfort during atrial flutter. She had not undergone prior evaluation for ischemia or coronary artery disease. Her medical history included paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, atrial flutter, and a 70% stenosis of the left internal carotid artery. Physical examination was notable only for a carotid bruit. Preprocedural laboratory studies, including hemoglobin, INR, thyroid-stimulating hormone, and creatinine, were within normal limits. A transesophageal echocardiogram excluded atrial thrombus.

The patient was brought to the electrophysiology laboratory in sinus rhythm. Pulmonary vein isolation was performed using a penta-spline pulsed field ablation (PFA) catheter, with 48 applications delivered in total via a single transseptal access. After successful pu

title: Early-onset post-cardiotomy severe constrictive pericarditis: a case report
case_summary:
A 68-year-old man presented 4 weeks after coronary artery bypass grafting to the left anterior descending and posterior descending arteries and bioprosthetic aortic-valve replacement, with a 1-week history of dyspnoea on exertion, orthopnoea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea, and progressive oedema. His medical history included multivessel coronary artery disease, severe aortic stenosis (status post-AVR), paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, hypertension, and hypercholesterolaemia. He had no prior pericardial or myocardial disease, autoimmune conditions, or history of arrhythmia symptoms.

On examination, he was euvolaemic except for pitting oedema extending to the abdomen, jugular venous pressure markedly elevated with a positive Kussmaul's sign, and reduced breath sounds at both lung bases. Heart sounds were dual without murmurs.

Laboratory studies showed an N-terminal pro-B-type natriuretic peptide level of 3034 ng/L (normal < 126 ng/L). A chest radiograph demonstrated bilateral pleural ef

title: Massive myocardial infiltration by primary anaplastic T-cell lymphoma: a case report
case_summary: A 35-year-old man presented with 2 months of progressive dyspnea and weight loss. His history included acute myeloid leukemia treated with allogeneic bone marrow transplant, subsequent relapse managed with chemotherapy, and complete remission on azacitidine and venetoclax. On admission: temperature 37.1 degreesC; heart rate 87 bpm; blood pressure 130/80 mmHg; oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Physical examination showed bilateral lower-extremity pitting edema and normal heart sounds without murmur. ECG demonstrated Q waves in inferior leads, diffuse T-wave inversion, and low-voltage QRS complexes. Laboratory studies revealed leukopenia (2.9 ? 10^3 /uL), normal renal and liver function, no blasts on peripheral smear, and elevated high-sensitivity troponin I. Transthoracic and transesophageal echocardiography showed inferior and septal hypokinesia (LVEF 45%), circumferential pericardial effusion without tamponade, abnormal right ventricular wall thickening, and a mobile LV apical thromb

title: Going under the bridge: unmasking ischaemia and endothelial dysfunction of myocardial bridging: a case report
case_summary: A 74-year-old man presented with 2-3 months of exertional chest pain and shortness of breath. He had a history of hypertension, hyperlipidaemia, and obstructive sleep apnoea. On examination, blood pressure was 121/63 mmHg and heart rate 80 b.p.m.; cardiac examination was unremarkable. Baseline electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm. A computed tomography coronary artery calcium score was elevated at 404 (266 in the left anterior descending artery and 138 in the left circumflex artery). Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a normal ejection fraction and no significant valvular abnormality. Due to ongoing symptoms and risk factors, coronary angiography was performed, revealing mild proximal left anterior descending artery disease with mid?LAD myocardial bridging and no significant lesions in the left circumflex or right coronary arteries. Right?heart catheterization showed a normal cardiac output of 6.0 L/min, pulmonary capillary wedge pressure of 11 mmHg, and mean pulmonary

title: A rare giant atrial septal cardiac blood cyst in an adult: a case report
case_summary:
A 59-year-old Chinese woman with hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and a transient ischaemic attack presented with 3 days of intermittent, non-exertional, central chest discomfort. She denied dyspnea, palpitations, lower limb swelling, or fever and had no other cardiovascular risk factors.

On examination, blood pressure was 125/85 mmHg, heart rate 110 beats/min, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Jugular venous pressure was elevated. Cardiac auscultation revealed a pan-systolic murmur at the left lower sternal edge. Lung fields were clear, and there was no pedal edema.

Laboratory tests showed normal full blood count, renal, liver, thyroid, and coagulation panels; cardiac troponins were not significantly elevated. NT-proBNP was 105 pg/mL (reference ?99 pg/mL).

Chest radiography demonstrated mild cardiomegaly with an uptilted cardiac apex and clear lung fields. Electrocardiography showed sinus tachycardia, extreme axis deviation, p pulmonale, and featu

title: Navigating the Diagnostic and Management Challenges of Suspected Cardiac Amyloidosis in Resource?Limited Settings: Ethiopia Experience
case_summary:
A 54-year-old man presented with progressively worsening shortness of breath over 1 month, on a background of exertional dyspnea for 1 year and increasing fatigue. Recently, he developed orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, and progressive bilateral leg swelling. He was treated with furosemide 40 mg twice daily and enalapril 5 mg twice daily with no improvement; enalapril caused dizziness and worsened fatigue. He has no personal or family history of hypertension or diabetes and denies syncope, neurologic symptoms, joint pain, or autonomic symptoms.

On examination, blood pressure was 100/60 mm Hg, pulse 110 bpm, respiratory rate 23 /min. Lung auscultation revealed bibasilar dullness and decreased air entry, more on the right. The jugular venous pressure was elevated; the precordium was quiet, and there was a systolic murmur at the left sternal border. The liver was enlarged 6 cm below the costal margin and tender, with shifting dullness; there was grade ++ bilateral pitting edema.

title: Reverse takotsubo cardiomyopathy- life-threatening symptom of an incidental pheochromocytoma: a case report
case_summary:
A 47-year-old man was referred for mechanical circulatory support for severe cardiogenic shock with multiorgan failure of unknown cause. One day earlier, he had undergone excision of a benign thyroglossal duct cyst, during which he experienced recurrent hypertensive episodes. After extubation, he developed profound hypotension and pulmonary oedema. Transthoracic echocardiography showed severely impaired left ventricular (LV) systolic function. Coronary angiography revealed no evidence of relevant coronary artery disease, and toxicological screening was negative. His medical history included chronic polyarthritis and treated arterial hypertension.

On transfer, he was invasively ventilated (PaO2/FiO2 30?mmHg), receiving norepinephrine at 2.5?ug/kg/min. Blood pressure was 75/49?mmHg, heart rate 180?b.p.m., with bilateral rales and basal dullness on chest percussion. He experienced pulseless electrical activity requiring 5?minutes of resuscitation. Bedside echocardiography demonstrated ak

title: Fulminant myocarditis in a COVID-19 positive patient treated with mechanical circulatory support - a case?report
case_summary:
A 43-year-old non-smoking man with mixed connective tissue disease on hydroxychloroquine for 5 months presented with 4 days of fever and cough, followed by acute chest pain. On arrival, he was afebrile; blood pressure was 100/- mmHg, heart rate 130 b.p.m., and oxygen saturation 90-100% on 10-15 L/min oxygen. Physical examination showed no murmurs or rales. Electrocardiogram revealed sinus tachycardia with diffuse ST-segment elevation. A chest CT scan showed no pulmonary pathology. High-sensitivity troponin T was 590 ng/L (ULN 15 ng/L). He was initially treated for NSTEMI with aspirin, ticagrelor, and fondaparinux, then for presumed perimyocarditis with cefotaxime and colchicine. Nasopharyngeal PCR for SARS-CoV-2 was positive. On day 2, NT-proBNP was 6100 ng/L (ULN 300 ng/L). Echocardiography showed a normal-sized left ventricle with severely depressed systolic function (EF 10-15%) and a 5 mm pericardial effusion. Central venous O? saturation was 53%.

He was transferred to the CCU with

title: Biatrial thrombi resembling myxoma regressed after prolonged anticoagulation in a patient with mitral stenosis: a case report
case_summary:
A 54-year-old man presented with palpitations. His pulse was 85 beats/min with an irregular rhythm; blood pressure was 120/80 mm Hg, and he was afebrile. Cardiac examination revealed a loud first heart sound and a soft mid-diastolic rumbling murmur (grade 2/6) at the mitral area without signs of heart failure. Electrocardiography showed atrial fibrillation. Laboratory tests, including complete blood count, renal and liver function, and urinalysis, were normal.

Transthoracic and transesophageal echocardiography demonstrated severe rheumatic mitral stenosis with a mitral valve area of approximately 0.9 cm2, a peak gradient of 14 mm Hg, and a mean gradient of 9 mm Hg. Both atria were dilated, and spontaneous echocardiographic contrast was present in the left atrium. A large, mobile, homogeneous round mass measuring 40 ? 35 mm was seen in the right atrium. A similar large left atrial mass adherent to the interatrial septum protruded into the left ventricle; no discrete stalk was identifie

title: STEMI associated with SARS-CoV-2 infection and the use of ECMO as a potential therapeutic approach in addition to the PCI
case_summary:
A 55-year-old man presented in April 2020 with sudden-onset, left-anterior chest pain of 4 hours' duration. The pain was 5/10, radiated to the left arm, and was not accompanied by dyspnea. He also reported a 2-week history of dry cough and fever. He is a police officer; two coworkers tested positive for SARS-CoV-2.

His medical history is notable for hypertension, hyperlipidemia, coronary artery disease status post-percutaneous coronary intervention with a drug-eluting stent in 2005 and quadruple coronary artery bypass grafting in 2008, untreated type II diabetes mellitus, and polycythemia.

On presentation, he was diaphoretic; heart rate was 100 bpm, blood pressure 148/100 mm Hg, and he was hypoxic on room air. Initial laboratory testing showed a cardiac troponin I level of 0.02 ng/L (reference < 19 ng/L). Reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction testing for SARS-CoV-2 was positive.

Chest radiography demonstrated severe diffuse bilateral pulmonary infiltrates with air bronchog

title: An unusual presentation of prominent crista terminalis mimicking a right atrial mass: a case report
case_summary:
A 54-year-old man was referred after an incidental finding of a right atrial mass on echocardiography. He was asymptomatic with no fever, chest pain, dizziness, palpitations, or history of heart disease or tumor. He exercised regularly, was a non-smoker, consumed 50 mL of liquor daily for 30 years, and had no family history of sudden death.  
On examination, vital signs were stable: temperature normal, heart rate 68 bpm, blood pressure 128/79 mmHg. Serum tumor markers and D-dimer were normal. ECG showed normal sinus rhythm. Chest radiograph demonstrated normal cardiac size and clear lung fields.  
Transthoracic echocardiography revealed normal ventricular function (EF 66%), normal chamber sizes, and only mild tricuspid and aortic regurgitation. However, in the apical four-chamber view, there was a smooth, echogenic, 9 ? 11 mm structure in the right atrium attached near the tricuspid valve, suggestive of a thrombus or tumor.  
To further characterize the lesion, the patient was referred

title: The role of multidetector CT scan in the management of prosthetic aortic valve thrombosis: A case report
case_summary:
A 61?year?old man presented with three weeks of dizziness and dyspnea at rest and with exertion. He reported running out of his medications about one month earlier. On examination, he was afebrile, in New York Heart Association functional class III, with a pulse of 77 beats/min, respiratory rate of 22 breaths/min, and blood pressure of 100/82 mm Hg. An electrocardiogram showed atrial flutter at a rate of 79 beats/min with right bundle branch block. Laboratory tests, including high?sensitivity troponin I and complete blood count, were normal. Notable findings included an NT-proBNP level greater than 3000 pg/mL and a subtherapeutic INR of 1.2. A chest radiograph demonstrated mild pulmonary vascular congestion.

His history was significant for a modified Bentall procedure and mechanical aortic valve replacement three years earlier, with concomitant ventricular septal defect repair. He also had heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, coronary artery bypass grafting, paroxysmal atri

title: A haemodynamic conundrum: a case report of a patient with concurrent pulmonary arterial hypertension and hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy
case_summary:
A 49-year-old Caucasian woman with a 6-year history of obstructive hypertrophic cardiomyopathy presented with progressively worsening New York Heart Association class III dyspnoea over 6 months. She had a history of Raynaud's phenomenon and was under evaluation for possible scleroderma. Medications included metoprolol succinate 100 mg daily; she also had an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator placed for primary prevention.

On examination, she had hand telangiectasias without sclerodactyly and a crescendo systolic murmur that increased with the Valsalva manoeuvre. There was no lower-extremity oedema.

Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated asymmetric septal hypertrophy with maximal septal thickness of 2.2 cm, systolic anterior motion of the mitral valve, a dynamic left ventricular outflow tract gradient of 56 mmHg at rest and 125 mmHg with Valsalva, mildly reduced right ventricular function, and an estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure of 75 mmHg.

Simultaneous left and

title: The Role of TEE in Diagnosing Hepatopulmonary Syndrome and Cryptogenic Cirrhosis
case_summary: A 54-year-old woman with a 15-pack-year smoking history presented with 7 months of NYHA class III exertional dyspnea without cough, wheezing, chest tightness, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, or peripheral edema. She had no rashes, arthralgias, myalgias, travel, or exposure history. Oxygen saturation was 91% on 3 L/min oxygen; blood pressure 115/76 mm Hg; heart rate 82 beats/min. Pulmonary examination revealed normal breath sounds without wheezes, crackles, or pleural rubs. Cardiac examination showed normal S1 and S2 and an early-peaking grade II/VI crescendo-decrescendo murmur at the right upper sternal border radiating to the carotids. No stigmata of chronic liver disease were present. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a left ventricular ejection fraction > 60% and mild to moderate subaortic stenosis without other valvular abnormalities or shunts on color Doppler. Transesophageal echocardiography with agitated saline injection revealed more than 20 bubbles in the le

title: Early amiodarone pneumonitis: A case report
case_summary:
A 50-year-old heavy ex-smoker with hyperlipidemia and hypertension presented with chest pain, sweating, nausea, and progressive dyspnea. Initial vital signs were temperature 37.5 degreesC, blood pressure 96/70 mm Hg, pulse 95 beats/min, respiratory rate 26 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 90% on room air. Lung auscultation revealed bilateral crackles, more pronounced on the right. ECG showed ST-segment elevations in the precordial leads. Chest radiography demonstrated peri-hilar "bat-wing" consolidations consistent with pulmonary edema. 

Urgent coronary angiography revealed total occlusion of the left main artery; stenting of the LAD and balloon angioplasty of the ramus intermedius were performed. During and after the procedure, the patient received a total of over 1000 mg of IV amiodarone in 24 hours, IV furosemide, heparin, epinephrine infusion, and dobutamine. On hospital day 2, dyspnea improved and amiodarone (200 mg BID) and furosemide (40 mg BID) were switched to oral. Echocar

title: Cardiac Syncope with Anoxic Seizure Activity
case_summary:
A 44-year-old woman presented to the emergency department after an episode of syncope on standing from a crouched position. She reported dizziness and lightheadedness before loss of consciousness; witnesses described 3-5 minutes of limb jerking, eye rolling, and urinary incontinence. On recovery she complained of a bifrontal headache. Her history included asthma, psoriasis, and recent initiation of citalopram for possible depression. She had previously been evaluated for ventricular bigeminy. She was a nonsmoker and drank minimally.

On examination, blood pressure was 95/61 mmHg and heart rate was 50 bpm; the remainder of her cardiac and neurologic examinations was unremarkable. A 12-lead ECG on admission showed sinus rhythm with monomorphic premature ventricular complexes. Shortly after arrival, she had a second collapse characterized by stertorous breathing, hypertonia of the upper limbs, bradycardia, and transient postictal hypertension; she denied chest pain or palpitations prior t

title: Case report of eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangitis presenting as acute myocarditis
case_summary:
A 63-year-old Caucasian man presented with chest pain, shortness of breath, palpitations, and syncope that began during a return flight from Cyprus two days after developing fever and fatigue. On ambulance electrocardiogram, he was in atrial fibrillation at 160 beats/min with new-onset complete right bundle branch block. At admission he was dyspneic, hypotensive (systolic blood pressure 80 mmHg), and complained of chest pain. His medical history included hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and spinal stenosis; medications were losartan, atorvastatin, omeprazole, and naproxen. He was a former smoker, with no relevant family history.
Laboratory studies showed a white blood cell count of 19.1?10^9/L, hemoglobin 126 g/L, high-sensitivity troponin T of 400 ng/L, C-reactive protein 76 mg/L, and NT-proBNP 6440 ng/L. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed mild left ventricular hypertrophy, a left ventricular ejection fraction of 50-60%, small pericardial effusion without hemodynamic significance, a

title: Case report of rare highly aggressive cardiac tumour: the intimal sarcoma
case_summary: A 60-year-old man presented with one month of night sweats and malaise and one week of increasing exertional dyspnoea. He had a history of congenital aortic stenosis treated with aortoplasty at age 17, mechanical aortic-valve replacement at age 35, and aortic stenting at age 58. He was taking losartan and warfarin, and his self-monitored INR had fluctuated between below 2 and above 5 over the prior five weeks. On examination, he was dyspnoeic at rest, blood pressure was 92/66 mmHg, there was a holosystolic murmur in the left second intercostal space, bibasal crackles, and mild peripheral oedema. Laboratory tests showed haemoglobin 9.2 g/dL, LDH 260 U/L, and CRP 129 mg/L. The ECG demonstrated normal sinus rhythm, left axis deviation, and poor R-wave progression. Chest X-ray revealed pulmonary congestion, Kerley B lines, and bilateral pleural effusions. Transthoracic echocardiography showed large masses in the left atrium causing functional mitral stenosis (mean gradient 13 mmHg) and pro

title: Synchronous Left Ventricular and Endocranial Mass
case_summary:
A 26-year-old man from the Democratic Republic of Congo was transferred from a refugee camp for evaluation of progressive dyspnea, orthopnea, and generalized peripheral edema of one week's duration. His medical history was unremarkable.  
On examination, he was hemodynamically stable (blood pressure 125/80 mm Hg, heart rate 100 bpm) and had bilateral lung rales and a holosystolic murmur best heard at the cardiac apex. Peripheral edema was present.  
Laboratory studies showed a D-dimer of 1303 ng/mL and an elevated C-reactive protein of 58.6 mg/L; other values were unremarkable. ECG demonstrated sinus tachycardia at 102 bpm with left-ventricular hypertrophy and strain. Chest radiography revealed mild pulmonary congestion.  
While in the emergency department, he developed generalized tonic-clonic seizures that progressed to status epilepticus, requiring intubation. A noncontrast brain CT showed a 14 mm right frontal cystic lesion; subsequent brain MRI confirmed a homogenous, cerebrospina

title: Postcardiac injury syndrome caused by radiofrequency catheter ablation of persistent atrial fibrillation: severe pulmonary arterial hypertension with severe tricuspid regurgitation: a rare case report and literature review
case_summary:
A 70-year-old man with persistent atrial fibrillation refractory to drugs underwent radiofrequency catheter ablation with pulmonary vein isolation, roof and bottom lines in the left atrium, and cavo-tricuspid isthmus ablation. Preprocedure CT angiography of the pulmonary veins was normal. Baseline echocardiography showed an ejection fraction of 56%, left atrial diameter 40 mm, a calcified aortic valve, and mild mitral and tricuspid regurgitation.

He was discharged on rivaroxaban and remained in sinus rhythm. On postablation day 5 he developed progressive dyspnea, fatigue, and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea without fever. Heart rate was < 110 bpm. Examination revealed a pansystolic murmur at the left sternal border and decreased breath sounds at the lung bases.

Labs showed normal leukocyte and neutrophil counts but 79.6% neutrophils, ESR 18 mm/h (0-15), CRP 13.8 mg/L (0-6), IL-6 126.6 pg/mL (0-7), NT-proBNP 2200 ng/L (0-125), and negative high-sensitivity troponin T. Oxygen saturation w

title: Acute fibrinous constrictive pericarditis and large pericardial effusion as the first manifestation of systemic lupus erythematsous disease in an adult male patient
case_summary:
A 36?year?old Iranian man presented with a 1-month history of pleuritic chest pain, dyspnea, fatigue, and, over the preceding 10 days, intermittent fevers and chills. The chest pain was sharp, radiated to the left shoulder, worsened when supine, and improved when leaning forward. He described exertional dyspnea corresponding to NYHA class III-IV. He denied rash, alopecia, oral ulcers, arthralgia, or morning stiffness.  

He had no recent infections. A chest CT scan performed by a pulmonologist showed an enlarged cardiac silhouette, severe pericardial effusion, and a mild to moderate left pleural effusion.  

On examination, his temperature was 39.5 degreesC; pulse, 82 bpm; blood pressure, 110/70 mmHg; respiratory rate, 14 breaths/min; and jugular venous pressure was elevated. Heart sounds were muffled; breath sounds were decreased at the left base. He had bilateral 1+ pitting edema of the lower legs. Pulsus paradoxus was absent.  

Electrocardiography showed generalized low QRS voltage

title: Atrial dysrhythmias due to extrinsic left atrial compression by an esophageal stent
case_summary:
A 36-year-old man with stage IVa esophageal squamous cell carcinoma complicated by an esophagobronchial fistula underwent placement of a partially covered esophageal stent 5 months earlier. One month prior to presentation, tumor ingrowth prompted placement of a second fully covered esophageal stent overlapping the first by 7.5 cm, with its distal end terminating in the mid to distal esophagus immediately posterior to the superior left atrium. Soon thereafter, he experienced worsening chest pain, palpitations, and failure to thrive and was referred to the emergency department.

On arrival, his temperature was 36.8  degreesC, blood pressure 132/87 mm Hg, respiratory rate 20/min, and oxygen saturation 100% on room air. Physical examination was notable for cachexia. Laboratory studies showed potassium 4.7 mmol/L, magnesium 2.2 mmol/L, thyroid-stimulating hormone 2.472 mIU/mL, white blood cell count 5.5 ? 10^9/L, troponin I < 0.034 ng/mL, D-dimer 169 ng/mL, and an unremarkable urine toxicol

title: Cardiac sarcoidosis mimicking arrhythmogenic right ventricular dysplasia in a patient presenting with monomorphic ventricular tachycardia
case_summary:
A 66-year-old white man presented to the Emergency Department with abrupt onset of dyspnea, palpitations, and lightheadedness. In the ED, he was found to be in monomorphic ventricular tachycardia and was electrically cardioverted into normal sinus rhythm. His medical history included a cerebrovascular accident, Factor V Leiden and prothrombin gene mutations, hypertension, incidentally noted pulmonary nodules, and chronic kidney disease (baseline GFR 40-45 mL/min/1.73 m2). He was taking losartan and warfarin and had no family history of sudden cardiac death.

Initial laboratory studies were within normal limits, and coronary angiography ruled out ischemia. His electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm without ischemic changes, but an epsilon wave in V1, a QRS duration of 140 ms, and T-wave inversions in leads V1-V5. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed preserved left ventricular function and no valvular disease, but a markedly dilated right ventricle with severely reduced systolic

title: Myocardial infarction showing non-ischaemic late gadolinium enhancement pattern in the mid-septum: a case report
case_summary:
A 65-year-old man with dyslipidaemia and psoriatic arthritis presented with angina. ECG showed ST-segment elevations in the inferolateral leads consistent with an inferolateral STEMI. Physical examination was unremarkable. He received 250 mg IV aspirin and underwent urgent coronary angiography, which revealed a subtotal occlusion of the mid left anterior descending artery (LAD) with a large thrombus burden and thrombotic apposition in a recurrent apical branch supplying the inferior and inferior-septal walls, consistent with distal embolization. A drug-eluting stent was implanted in the mid-LAD and balloon angioplasty performed in the distal segment. During the procedure, he received unfractionated heparin (100 IU/kg) and a 60 mg loading dose of prasugrel.

Six days later, cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (1.5 T) showed mild systolic left ventricular dysfunction with akinesis of the inferior and lateral apical walls. Late gadolinium enhancement (LGE) demonstrated a transmural pattern

title: A rare case of nitrofurantoin?induced acute lung injury
case_summary:
A 79-year-old woman with coronary artery disease (status post six stents), hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and type 2 diabetes presented with mid-sternal, nonradiating chest pain that became diffuse over her chest. The pain began 6 hours earlier during a 3-hour drive home. She took aspirin without relief. She reported nausea but denied fever, chills, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, vomiting, diarrhea, headache, tingling, or dizziness. Her home medications included amlodipine, aspirin, atorvastatin, losartan-hydrochlorothiazide, metformin, and metoprolol. Two days before presentation, she had begun nitrofurantoin twice daily for a urinary tract infection; it was discontinued on admission.

On examination, she was alert and oriented. Her temperature, heart rate, and blood pressure were within normal limits; oxygen saturation was 89% on room air. Cardiovascular exam revealed a regular rhythm without murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Her lungs were clear bilaterally without wheezing, rales, or

title: A case of repetitive myocardial infarction with unobstructed coronaries due to Churg-Strauss syndrome
case_summary:
A 70-year-old man with a history of multiple sclerosis, resected colon cancer ten years earlier, and late-onset asthma (age 62) presented with retrosternal chest pain. His ECG showed no ST-T changes, and troponin I was mildly elevated.
