Physical examination was unremarkable, and transthoracic echocardiography revealed normal left ventricular function without regional wall motion abnormalities or valvular disease. He was diagnosed with non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI).

Diagnostic coronary angiography demonstrated a smooth stenosis in the right coronary artery that resolved on repeat imaging and a smooth mid-circumflex stenosis that resolved after intracoronary isosorbide dinitrate; a diagnosis of coronary artery spasm was made, and he was treated with aspirin and oral isosorbide mononitrate. One month later, he experienced an anterior ST-elevation myocardial infarction that promptly resolved with sublingual glyceryl trinitrate. Persistent rest angina led to stepwise addit

title: A unique case of acute brain haemorrhage with left ventricular systolic failure requiring ECMO
case_summary:
A previously healthy 9-month-old boy was found to have generalized tonic-clonic seizures at his childcare centre following a bath and milk feed. His childcare teacher initiated chest compressions and rescue breathing when he appeared cyanotic. Paramedics arrived 20 minutes later, noted a pulse, and provided bag-valve-mask ventilation en route to a general hospital. 

On arrival, his heart rate was 144 beats/min, blood pressure 130/72 mmHg, SpO? 83% on bag-valve-mask ventilation, and axillary temperature 36  degreesC. He had ongoing seizures, was intubated under rapid-sequence induction, and began mechanical ventilation. He received a 10 mL/kg normal saline bolus for persistent tachycardia and poor perfusion and was started on a midazolam infusion. Forty-five minutes later in the Children's Emergency Department, he was tachycardic (HR 180 beats/min), markedly hypertensive (BP 191/120 mmHg), and intermittently desaturated to SpO? 80-90% (FiO? 100%). He was poorly perfused with central pu

title: Ventricular Tachycardia in an Elderly Male With Brugada Syndrome
journal: Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports
case_summary: A 74-year-old man with diabetes, hypertension, and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease was admitted for urinary retention. He reported no chest pain, palpitations, syncope, or family history of sudden cardiac death and denied substance or alcohol use. On examination, he was hemodynamically stable and asymptomatic. Laboratory tests, including serum electrolytes and cardiac biomarkers, were within normal limits. Transthoracic echocardiography showed normal systolic function and no wall motion abnormalities or left ventricular hypertrophy. The initial 12-lead electrocardiogram demonstrated runs of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia and subtle ST-segment elevation in lead V1.

title: Cardiac metastatic melanoma presenting with ventricular tachycardia: a multimodality imaging evaluation case report
case_summary:
A 51-year-old woman with a history of right shoulder melanoma excised seven years earlier presented with chest pain and palpitations. Emergency medical services recorded a wide-complex tachycardia that terminated after vagal maneuvers. On admission, her vital signs and physical examination were normal. Laboratory studies showed a peak high-sensitivity troponin I of 935 ng/L (normal < 49 ng/L), with normal blood counts and metabolic panel. A chest radiograph was unremarkable. The arrhythmia was diagnosed as monomorphic ventricular tachycardia based on right bundle-branch-block morphology not present in sinus rhythm, a monophasic R wave in V1, and an R:S ratio < 1 in V6. Coronary angiography revealed normal coronary arteries. Cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR) imaging was performed to evaluate for scar or infiltration and demonstrated a left ventricular mass, prompting transfer for further evaluation.

Transthoracic echocardiography with contrast showed a small LV chamber without outflow o

title: Role of echocardiography in a patient with suspected acute pulmonary embolism: a case report
case_summary:
A 44-year-old man with no prior medical history presented after 1 week of progressive weakness, dyspnea, nonproductive cough, fever (38.9? degreesC), vomiting, and diarrhea. He was admitted to the ICU with community-acquired pneumonia and respiratory failure. On admission he was febrile (38? degreesC), tachycardic (110 beats/min), blood pressure 120/80?mmHg, tachypneic (28 breaths/min), and had an oxygen saturation of 88% on 50% Ventimask. Lung auscultation revealed bilateral crackles; heart sounds were normal with no murmurs; there was no peripheral edema.

Empiric antibiotics and oseltamivir were started, and influenza A(H1N1) pneumonia was confirmed by PCR. He required mechanical ventilation and remained hemodynamically stable until day 12, when he developed sudden severe hypotension (systolic blood pressure <?80?mmHg), tachycardia (>?140 beats/min), and worsening respiratory status. Arterial blood gas on FiO2 60% showed pH 7.39, PaCO2 26.7?mmHg, PaO2 55.9?mmHg, bicarbonate 15.9?mmo

title: A patient with pulmonary embolism takes a surprising HIT: a case report
case_summary: A 43-year-old previously healthy man of Sephardic-Jewish descent presented with sudden-onset chest pain and exertional dyspnea that began that morning, following a week of anorexia and diarrhea which had resolved one day earlier. In the emergency department, he was diagnosed with acute pulmonary embolism with a saddle embolus, large bilateral thrombotic burden, right ventricular (RV) systolic dysfunction, and mildly elevated serum troponin levels. He was admitted to the cardiac intensive care unit and started on therapeutic unfractionated heparin. Over the next several days, serial echocardiograms showed further deterioration of RV function, and he was transferred to a tertiary center. On arrival, he was asymptomatic with stable vital signs. Laboratory tests showed normal renal function, normal high-sensitivity troponin I, and normal lactate. Physical examination was notable only for jugular venous distention. Electrocardiography demonstrated normal sinus rhythm, a normal QRS axis, and

title: Aortic sinus aneurysm invading ventricular septum and dissection caused by Behcet's disease: a case report and literature review
case_summary:
A 36-year-old man presented with chest distress and shortness of breath for more than 2 months. He reported recurrent oral and external genital ulcers for over 10 years, and a family history of recurrent oral ulcers in his mother, younger brother, and sister. He had hypertension for more than 10 years and underwent left carotid artery stent implantation in 2016 for a carotid aneurysm, and left femoral artery stent implantation later that year for a pseudoaneurysm; he has been taking aspirin since. In January 2019, he developed exertional chest distress, dyspnea, chest pain, and occasional nocturnal orthopnea without fever, cough, or expectoration. At another hospital he was diagnosed with pulmonary infection and rheumatic heart disease. 

On examination, the jugular veins were distended; a palpable thrill was present over the aortic area; heart rate was regular; a grade 4/6 systolic blowing murmur was heard at the aortic area; and grade 3/6 systolic blowing murmurs were heard at the mi

title: Unique ECG Findings in Acute Pulmonary Embolism: STE with Reciprocal Changes and Pathologic Q Wave
case_summary: A 68-year-old man with no prior medical history experienced retrosternal chest pain and presyncope. Emergency medical services arrived after he collapsed and documented a pulseless electrical activity (PEA) cardiac arrest. Spontaneous circulation was restored, and a 12-lead ECG demonstrated ST elevations in leads V1-V3, Q waves in lead V2, and reciprocal ST depressions in lateral and inferior leads. During a second PEA arrest, he received CPR and thrombolytic therapy for a presumptive ST-elevation myocardial infarction. After return of spontaneous circulation, he was taken for emergent coronary angiography, which showed no hemodynamically significant stenosis or evidence of disrupted plaque in any arteries. A left ventriculogram revealed normal contraction of all segments with no dissection, left ventricular aneurysm, or mitral regurgitation. An intra-aortic balloon pump was placed for hypotension support. Despite adequate cardiac output, he remained acidotic and dependent on the ballo

title: Chest Pain in a Psychiatric Patient Due to Clozapine-Induced Myopericarditis
case_summary:
A 29-year-old man with treatment-resistant schizoaffective disorder was hospitalized after an intentional overdose of acetaminophen and lithium and began clozapine therapy. Eight days after starting clozapine, he developed substernal chest pain worse with deep inspiration that progressed over two days. He was transferred to cardiology when troponin and brain natriuretic peptide levels were found to be elevated.  
On examination, he was tachycardic but had no murmurs, rubs, or signs of heart failure; lungs were clear. Laboratory studies revealed leukocytosis, elevated troponin, elevated inflammatory markers, and elevated brain natriuretic peptide. Electrocardiogram showed ST-segment elevations most prominent in leads V2-V3.  
Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging showed global hypokinesis with a left ventricular ejection fraction of 45% and subtle late gadolinium enhancement of the epicardial lateral wall and pericardium with abnormal parametric mapping, consistent with myopericarditis. A

title: Hypertension and Brachydactyly Syndrome
case_summary:
A 20-year-old woman presented with worsening dyspnea on exertion. Her medical history included hypertension since age 4, hyperlipidemia, bicuspid aortic valve with severe aortic insufficiency, aortic root dilation, and left ventricular dilation with preserved ejection fraction. She was born at 37 weeks in a pregnancy complicated by gestational hypertension and diagnosed with bicuspid aortic valve and aortic insufficiency at 3 months. At age 6, evaluation of her short stature and hypertension included karyotyping (46,XX), array CGH (negative), and skeletal survey revealing diffuse brachydactyly.

On examination, she was 58 inches tall, weighed 87 pounds (BMI 18.2), and had a blood pressure of 137/83 mm Hg. She had bilateral brachydactyly of the hands and feet without facial dysmorphism. Transesophageal echocardiography showed a bicuspid aortic valve, left ventricular ejection fraction 60%, severe aortic insufficiency with eccentric left ventricular dilation, mild aortic stenosis, and mo

title: ST-segment elevation in V1-4 in takotsubo cardiomyopathy with ventricular septal perforation: A case report and literature review
case_summary: An 87-year-old woman was referred for progressive dyspnea. Three days earlier she had presented with high-grade fever, dyspnea, and hematuria and received levofloxacin without improvement. Her history included hypertension, diabetes, dementia, and deep vein thrombosis; medications included candesartan, teneligliptin, donepezil, digoxin, and apixaban. On examination, blood pressure was 92/66 mmHg, heart rate 92 beats/min, respiratory rate 24 breaths/min, temperature 36.7  degreesC, and oxygen saturation 97 % on room air. Arterial blood gas analysis showed pH 7.454, PaO2 73.7 mmHg, PaCO2 28.8 mmHg, and bicarbonate 19.7 mmol/L. Cardiac examination revealed a grade 4/6 pansystolic murmur at the apex; the abdomen had mild right-upper-quadrant tenderness. ECG showed sinus tachycardia with ST-segment elevations in leads V1-4, I, and aVL. Chest radiography was unremarkable. Laboratory tests revealed WBC 18,300/uL (78 % neutrophils), C-reactive protein 18.05 mg/dL, BNP 224 pg/mL, and troponin T

title: The Role of Genetic Testing in the Evaluation of Dilated Cardiomyopathies
case_summary:
A 19-year-old adopted African American man presented in cardiogenic shock and acute respiratory failure requiring intubation. He had a several-week history of chest pain and progressive dyspnea. At an outside hospital, he failed noninvasive ventilation, was intubated, developed profound bradycardia with a brief pulseless electrical activity arrest, and was transferred on vasopressors. His mother noted that over preceding months he had become less able to walk to basketball without assistance or to play without resting. He denied palpitations or overt chest pain before decompensation.

Past medical history was notable for developmental delay of unknown etiology, type 2 diabetes, morbid obesity, depression, and anxiety. Family history was unknown.

On examination he was sedated and intubated. Breath sounds were coarse bilaterally. Cardiovascular examination revealed regular tachycardia, normal heart sounds, no murmurs or gallops. Extremities were cool, with mild pretibial pitting edema.

title: Erdheim-Chester disease with multisystem involvement evaluated by multimodal imaging: A case report
case_summary:
A 54-year-old woman presented with a 4-year history of exertional dyspnea, palpitations, fatigue, dizziness, and mild pitting edema of the lower extremities. Her initial blood pressure was 125/77 mm Hg. Her medical history was notable for diabetes insipidus, chronic osteomyelitis, well-controlled hypertension, and hyperthyroidism.  

Laboratory studies showed an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 100 mm/h, a C-reactive protein level of 42.4 mg/l, a B-type natriuretic peptide of 2137 ng/l, an interleukin-6 level of 61.3 pg/ml, a white blood cell count of 15.7 ? 10^9/l, a platelet count of 653 ? 10^9/l, and a serum CA 19-9 of 32.9 U/ml. Her daily urine output exceeded 10 000 ml.  

Cardiac computed tomography revealed a soft-tissue lesion of inhomogeneous density with an unclear boundary infiltrating the right atrial wall, interatrial septum, and atrioventricular sulcus, with a small pericardial effusion; the lesion showed slight enhancement after contrast administration. Coronary CT angi

title: Hemorrhage Pleural Effusion During Percutaneous Coronary Intervention Due?to Pericardial Agenesis
case_summary:
A 67?year?old man presented with an 18?hour history of chest pain that was worsened by exertion. On arrival, heart rate was 70 beats/min and blood pressure was 120/80 mm Hg. The initial electrocardiogram showed ST?segment elevation in leads V2, V3, and aVF. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated hypokinesis of the basal septal and inferior walls with a left ventricular ejection fraction of 49% and no pericardial or pleural fluid.

His history was notable only for heavy smoking. The initial differential diagnosis included acute myocardial infarction and acute aortic dissection with coronary extension. The patient underwent emergent coronary angiography, which revealed acute total occlusion of the right coronary artery (RCA) and 90% proximal stenosis of the left anterior descending artery (LAD). A 3.5 ? 48?mm drug?eluting stent was deployed in the RCA via right radial access, restoring flow without immediate complications.

Thirty minutes postprocedure, he developed severe chest pai

title: Pericardial pseudocyst along atrioventricular groove
case_summary: A 34-year-old physically active man presented with 4 months of exertional dyspnea (NYHA class II) and pedal edema. He denied fever, chest pain, trauma, cough, pancreatitis, or weight loss and had no smoking or alcohol history. Examination revealed elevated jugular venous pressure, a loud P2, and a wide S2 split. ECG was unremarkable, and cardiac enzymes were normal. Transthoracic echocardiography showed an extracardiac heteroechoic mass compressing both ventricles, containing echogenic areas without Doppler flow. Contrast-enhanced CT revealed a 10 cm hypodense cystic lesion between the visceral and parietal pericardium along both atrioventricular grooves, with peripheral wall enhancement and a small calcification; bilateral pleural effusions were noted. Cardiac MRI demonstrated a thick-walled, predominantly hyperintense cystic structure in a horseshoe configuration along the inferior heart, with T1 hyperintensity suggestive of haemorrhage, nonenhancing isointense areas consistent with

title: Aortic valve endocarditis with root abscess causing superior vena cava obstruction: a case report
case_summary:
An 84-year-old man with a tissue aortic valve replacement 12 years earlier, permanent atrial fibrillation, chronic kidney disease stage 4, and prostate cancer presented with several weeks of malaise, fevers, and weight loss. On admission his blood pressure was 95/50?mmHg, heart rate 67?b.p.m., and he was afebrile. Cardiac examination revealed an ejection-systolic murmur in the aortic area without peripheral stigmata of endocarditis. He reported no focal infective symptoms. Laboratory tests showed haemoglobin 8.2?g/dL, white cell count 19.8???10^9/L, and C-reactive protein 51?mg/L. Chest radiograph and bedside urinalysis were normal. ECG confirmed atrial fibrillation. Within 12?h, he developed septic shock requiring inotropic support. 

A transthoracic echocardiogram showed preserved left ventricular function, an abnormal thickening of the aortic annulus and root, and no obvious vegetations. A CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis demonstrated an abnormal aortic root with an adjacen

title: Gastrointestinal Distention Masquerading?as ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction
case_summary:
A 41-year-old man with no significant past medical history was admitted to the medical ICU with 3 weeks of progressive lower-extremity edema, fatigue, and shortness of breath. On presentation, he appeared pale and had melenic stool on rectal examination. He was intubated, sedated with propofol and fentanyl, and required norepinephrine for septic shock. Overnight on telemetry, there was a sudden onset of ST-segment elevations.

Vital signs: blood pressure 101/57 mm Hg; heart rate 112 beats/min; respiratory rate 23 breaths/min; O? saturation 99% on mechanical ventilation. Physical examination was notable for significant abdominal distention; cardiovascular auscultation revealed no murmurs.

A 12-lead ECG showed sinus tachycardia with apparent ST-segment elevations in the precordial leads, deep T-wave inversions, J-point depression in the inferior leads, and marked QT prolongation. Laboratory studies revealed a troponin-T level of <0.01 ng/ml, potassium 4.3 mEq/l, calcium 7.6 mg/dl, and a

title: COVID-19 and ST elevations-keep an open mind: a case report
case_summary: A 48-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, and obesity (BMI 32) presented with several days of fever, cough, nausea, and dyspnoea. She tested positive for SARS-CoV-2 and was initially discharged to self-quarantine. Three days later, she returned with worsening dyspnoea on minimal exertion. On examination, she was tachycardic, tachypnoeic, and hypoxic (oxygen saturation 79% on room air, improving to 93% with 4 L nasal cannula). Lung auscultation revealed no significant crackles, and there was no cardiac murmur. Laboratory studies showed a leukocyte count of 11 700/mL, D-dimer 432 ng/mL, and an eGFR of 106 mL/min. Chest radiography demonstrated bilateral patchy airspace opacities. She was admitted for presumed COVID-19 pneumonia, developed progressive respiratory failure, and was intubated for acute respiratory distress syndrome. Her course was complicated by non-oliguric acute renal failure. On ICU day 6, she became hypotensive requiring norepinephrine and develope

title: Case Report: concurrent myocardial and cerebral infarction due to aortic thrombus
case_summary:
A 38-year-old man experienced severe central chest pain shortly after taking sildenafil. His medical history included intellectual disability and unexplained abdominal pain managed with amitriptyline, metoclopramide, and lansoprazole. He smoked five cigarettes daily and frequently consumed caffeinated energy drinks. He had no known COVID-19 infection. The chest pain lasted about 10 minutes and recurred throughout the day without associated sexual activity.

Six hours after symptom onset, his brother noted slurred speech and called emergency services. A prehospital ECG showed inferolateral ST-segment elevation. On arrival at the Emergency Department, he was pain-free but hypertensive (166/115 mmHg). Neurologic examination revealed dysarthria and a right homonymous hemianopia (NIHSS score 3); there were equal peripheral pulses, no murmurs, and no cardiac failure. Respiratory and abdominal exams were unremarkable. A repeat ECG again demonstrated persistent inferolateral ST-segment elevati

title: Reversible dilated cardiomyopathy caused by idiopathic hypoparathyroidism
journal: The Korean Journal of Internal Medicine
case_summary: A 50-year-old woman was admitted with dyspnea (New York Heart Association class IV) and orthopnea of 5 days' duration. She had been taking neurotonic medication for dizziness for 2 months. On examination, blood pressure was 100/60 mm Hg, pulse 82 beats/min, and there was no cardiac murmur; bilateral lung rales were present, but there was no jugular venous distension or peripheral edema. Neurologic examination was unremarkable. Electrocardiography showed a prolonged QT interval (432 ms) with nonspecific ST-T changes. Chest radiography revealed marked cardiomegaly and pulmonary congestion. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated global hypokinesia, left ventricular ejection fraction 36.5%, left ventricular diastolic and systolic dimensions of 59 mm and 47 mm, and moderate mitral and tricuspid regurgitation. Serum creatine kinase-MB and troponin-I levels were normal, whereas the brain natriuretic peptide level was 1,855.1 pg/mL. She had no history of hypertension, diabetes mellitus, o

title: Doing a double take: when transthoracic echocardiography fails for mitral valve annuloplasty ring endocarditis with Streptococcus pyogenes: a case report
case_summary:
A 66-year-old woman with a history of robotic mitral valve repair with annuloplasty ring and left atrial appendage clipping 3 years earlier, right vertebral artery occlusion stroke attributed to fibromuscular dysplasia 6 months ago, and a second left parietal stroke 1 month later on dual antiplatelet therapy, presented after her smartwatch recorded a heart rate of 160 bpm. She was asymptomatic. Home medications included aspirin, ticagrelor, atorvastatin, bupropion, and fluoxetine.  
On presentation, ECG showed supraventricular tachycardia that spontaneously reverted to sinus rhythm with ST-segment depressions in leads II and V3-V5. Vital signs were notable for a heart rate of 64 bpm, blood pressure 121/63 mm Hg, afebrile status, and oxygen saturation of 99% on room air. Cardiac examination revealed a grade II/VI systolic murmur at the left lower sternal border; no skin lesions were present. High-sensitivity troponin I was 86 ng/L and rose to 777 ng/L (reference 0-20 ng/L). She was admi

title: Late diagnosis of Kawasaki disease with major cardiovascular complications: a case report
case_summary:
A 25-year-old woman presented to a ski resort hospital with chest pain and dyspnoea while skiing. She had no fever or flu-like symptoms. Her only cardiovascular risk factor was smoking five cigarettes per day. Past medical history included a clinical suspicion of myocarditis at age 5. 

On examination, her heart rate was 86 bpm, blood pressure 110/65 mmHg, and respiratory rate 26 breaths/min. Lung auscultation was normal, heart sounds were normal, and there was no peripheral oedema. 

Laboratory testing showed elevated troponin (14 809 ng/L) and creatinine phosphokinase (972 mmol/L). ECG revealed negative T waves. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a mildly reduced left ventricular ejection fraction (58%) with preserved right ventricular function. 

Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging showed active inflammation in the left ventricle, focal inflammation on the diaphragmatic wall of the right ventricle, akinesia of the basal inferior wall, hypokinesia of the mid-inferolateral and

title: Isolated myocardial relapse of Philadelphia-positive acute lymphoblastic leukaemia causing myocarditis: a case report
case_summary: A 39-year-old woman with Philadelphia-positive acute lymphoblastic leukaemia, in remission for 6 months after two allogeneic stem cell transplants and donor lymphocyte infusion, presented with 2 days of fevers, malaise, and central dull chest pain that was neither pleuritic nor positional. She had no known cardiovascular disease. On examination she was dehydrated, tachycardic (heart rate up to 130 b.p.m.), hypotensive (90/65 mmHg), and febrile (38.5  degreesC), with an otherwise unremarkable cardiovascular exam. Laboratory studies showed a C-reactive protein of 72.7 mg/L (normal <10), a Troponin T of 2490 ng/L (0-14), and a white-cell count of 10.4 ? 10^9/L with no precursor cells on peripheral smear; other biochemistry was within normal limits. Blood cultures were negative. Chest radiography was normal. Electrocardiography demonstrated sinus tachycardia and new deep T-wave inversion in leads V3-V6. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed mild-moderate left ventricular hypertrophy, ant

title: Fatal Coronary Artery Anomaly Concealed in Young Athletes with Exertional Syncope
final_diagnosis: Anomalous aortic origin of the left coronary artery with an intramural course
case_summary:
A 14-year-old boy collapsed during a soccer game three days before presentation. He had gripping anterior chest pain immediately before losing consciousness and developed generalized tonic seizures with median ocular fixation lasting about 10 minutes, which resolved spontaneously without bystander resuscitation. On arrival by ambulance, he was nearly alert; vital signs were stable. At a nearby hospital, physical examination revealed no trauma, dehydration, or respiratory, circulatory, or neurological abnormalities. Electrocardiogram showed no arrhythmias or ST-T-wave changes. Head magnetic resonance imaging and general blood tests, including cardiac enzymes, were unremarkable. He was discharged after overnight observation but referred to our facility for further evaluation because a full cardiovascular workup was not performed at the first hospital.

His medical history included mild asthma and no known cardiac disease, arrhythmia, or family history of sudden death. He reported brief e

title:
Ticagrelor-Related Severe Dyspnoea: Mechanisms,
Characteristic Features, Differential Diagnosis and
Treatment
case_summary: A 59-year-old man with arterial hypertension and a prior STEMI treated by right coronary artery angioplasty in 2008 was admitted for another STEMI. He received a 180 mg loading dose of ticagrelor and underwent coronary angioplasty with drug-eluting stent implantation in the left anterior descending artery. Two hours later, he developed sudden episodes of strong dyspnoea associated with anxiety; his breathing pattern resembled Cheyne-Stokes respiration. During each episode, he had bradycardia (heart rate 35-40 beats per minute), presyncope, and intense sweating. Each episode lasted about 1 to 2 minutes, occurred every few minutes, and resolved spontaneously; he was asymptomatic between attacks. He had no pulmonary congestion, no auscultatory features of bronchial obstruction, and no orthopnoea. Transthoracic echocardiography showed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 30% without haemodynamically significant valvular defects or mechanical complications of myocardial infarction.

title: Asymptomatic giant right coronary artery aneurysm in Kawasaki disease: A case report
case_summary:
A 30-year-old Ethiopian woman presented with a 1-week history of dry cough, dyspnea, and wheezing. She reported similar intermittent respiratory symptoms for 10 years, usually triggered by colds. She denied chest pain, palpitations, orthopnea, leg swelling, smoking, or illicit drug use. She had no known childhood febrile illness, connective tissue disease, or family history of cardiovascular disease.

On examination, vital signs were normal and cardiopulmonary and systemic examinations were unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed normal complete blood count, serum chemistry, fasting glucose, lipid panel, thyroid function, coagulation panel, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and C-reactive protein. Serologic tests for hepatitis B and C, HIV, VDRL, rheumatoid factor, and antinuclear antibody were negative.

A chest radiograph revealed a bulge on the right cardiac border, a prominent pulmonary bay, and an inconspicuous aortic arch. Transthoracic echocardiography showed a mildly dilated righ

title: Coronary vasospasm induced by cisplatin for seminoma
case_summary:
A 34?year?old man is evaluated for chest pain on exertion. Two years earlier, he underwent orchiectomy and three cycles of adjuvant bleomycin, etoposide, and cisplatin for stage IIa seminoma. He reports no chest pain during or immediately after chemotherapy. He has no history of smoking; his only cardiovascular risk factor is dyslipidemia.

On examination: height 170 cm, weight 64 kg, blood pressure 110/75 mm Hg, heart rate 65 bpm. Cardiac auscultation is normal; there is no peripheral edema. Resting electrocardiogram shows normal sinus rhythm without ST-T changes. Chest radiograph is unremarkable. Cardiac troponin I is normal. Laboratory studies show total cholesterol 223 mg/dL, LDL cholesterol 146 mg/dL, creatinine 0.86 mg/dL (eGFR 83 mL/min/1.73 m2), and BNP 5 pg/mL. Brachial-ankle pulse?wave velocity is approximately 1230 cm/s bilaterally.

Because of ongoing exertional chest discomfort, coronary angiography is performed and reveals no organic stenosis.

title: Thyroid Storm-induced Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy Presenting as Acute Chest Pain: A Case Report
journal: Clinical Practice and Cases in Emergency Medicine
case_summary:
A 61-year-old woman with a history of pulmonary embolism who was not on anticoagulation presented with an 8-hour history of pressure-like chest pain and dyspnea.  
On arrival, she was afebrile, had a normal oxygen saturation on room air, a blood pressure of 151/85 mm Hg, and a heart rate of 142 beats/min.  
An electrocardiogram showed 1 mm of ST-segment elevation in the anterior and inferior leads. She received aspirin, and interventional cardiology was consulted.  
Her medical history was notable only for a provoked PE more than 20 years earlier, discontinued anticoagulation 4 years ago, and a recent diagnosis of shingles treated with antivirals. She denied any recent psychosocial stressors.  
Point-of-care cardiac ultrasound demonstrated a markedly reduced left ventricular ejection fraction with apical hypokinesis; there was no pericardial effusion, no evidence of right ventricular strain, no aortic flap or dilation, and no deep-vein thrombosis in the lower extremities.  
A single-vi

title: Isolated persistence of the fifth aortic arch in an infant presenting with congestive heart failure
case_summary: A 5-week-old male infant was referred for evaluation of an aortic arch anomaly. He had been born at term (3.07 kg), and after 3 weeks developed poor weight gain and respiratory distress with feeding. On admission, his weight was 3.65 kg (<3rd centile), heart rate 170 beats/min, respiratory rate elevated, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Blood pressure in the right arm and lower limbs was equal (80/40 mmHg). Examination revealed a grade III continuous "machinery" murmur, loudest in systole at the left infraclavicular area, fine wheezing bilaterally, and mild hepatomegaly; femoral pulses were normal. ECG showed sinus rhythm with left ventricular hypertrophy. Chest radiograph demonstrated cardiomegaly with increased pulmonary vascular markings. Transthoracic echocardiography showed normal intracardiac anatomy, left?sided volume overload with moderate functional mitral regurgitation, preserved biventricular function, and a large aberrant vascular connection between the inferior aspec

title: Drug Induced Pneumonitis Secondary to Treatment with Paritaprevir/Ritonavir/Ombitasvir and Dasabuvir (VIEKIRA PAK?) for Chronic Hepatitis C: Case Report of an Unexpected Life-Threatening Adverse Reaction
case_summary:
A 68-year-old Chinese woman with genotype 1b chronic hepatitis C, cirrhosis, type 2 diabetes, hypertension, dyslipidaemia, and atrial fibrillation was started on a 12-week course of VIEKIRA PAK without ribavirin. One week after initiation, she developed 2 days of fever, cough, headache, and palpitations. On admission her temperature was 37.8 degreesC, heart rate 128 /min, blood pressure 144/80 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation 92% on 2 L oxygen. Examination revealed right basal crepitations; heart sounds were dual without murmur; no jugular venous distension or peripheral edema. Laboratory testing showed acute kidney injury and a drop in haemoglobin from baseline, and chest radiograph demonstrated right-sided interstitial changes without consolidation. She was treated as community-acquired pneumonia with cefuroxime and erythromycin, improved rapidly, and was discharged on oral amoxicillin and roxithromycin. 

Three weeks later she was asymptomatic and was restarted on VIEKIRA PAK. Four days

title: Bleeding from ruptured hepatic metastases as a cause of syncope in an octogenarian: a case report
case_summary:
An 85-year-old man was admitted after three episodes of collapse with transient loss of consciousness at home. He reported only mild, diffuse abdominal discomfort and no trauma. His medical history included severe aortic stenosis, type 2 diabetes, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, hypertension, and a prior duodenal ulcer bleed. Medications were aspirin, bisoprolol, omeprazole, and ramipril. He was a former smoker (quit 35 years prior) with minimal alcohol intake and lived independently.

On examination, he was afebrile with an oxygen saturation of 100% on room air, blood pressure of 80/40 mmHg, and remained hypotensive despite aggressive fluid resuscitation. Cardiac exam revealed an ejection systolic murmur; jugular venous pressure was not elevated and there was no peripheral edema. The lungs were clear. Abdominal exam showed mild epigastric discomfort without peritoneal signs. Rectal examination was normal.

Initial laboratory tests showed hemoglobin 11.3 g/dL (reference 13-18), white c

title: Serial Changes in Troponin I in COVID-19 Vaccine-Associated Myocarditis
case_summary:
A 63-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis and mixed connective tissue disease presented to the emergency department with new-onset chest pain 11 hours after receiving her third dose of the Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 vaccine. She denied use of immunosuppressive agents at the time of presentation. Physical examination was unremarkable, with no pericardial rub or murmurs. 

Initial electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm without ST-segment changes. Initial cardiac troponin I was 0.02 ng/mL (upper reference limit <0.03 ng/mL). The following day, she returned with persistent chest pain; the electrocardiogram remained unchanged, and troponin I had risen to 1.60 ng/mL. 

Cardiac computed tomography demonstrated normal coronary arteries, normal left ventricular function, no regional wall motion abnormalities, and a normal pericardium. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed normal cardiac structure and function without pericardial disease. 

Serial cardiac troponin I measurements were obtained

title: A case report of unusual clinical features of a spontaneous coronary artery rupture: pathologic findings in the rupture site
case_summary:
A 62-year-old woman on maintenance dialysis for 27 years (chronic renal failure secondary to malignant hypertension) presented with 2 weeks of chest discomfort that worsened with deep inspiration; she noted palpitations for a few hours before arrival. On presentation, blood pressure was 85/54?mmHg, pulse 150?bpm, SpO2 95% on room air. ECG showed atrial fibrillation, which spontaneously converted to sinus rhythm within 1 hour; the ECG then demonstrated slight ST elevation in broad leads, especially I, II, and aVF. 

Transthoracic echocardiography showed normal left ventricular wall motion, thickness, and size; no valvular dysfunction; and diffuse mild pericardial fluid (maximum thickness 6?mm anterior to the right ventricle) without chamber collapse. 

Laboratory tests revealed normal creatine kinase and CK-MB levels, elevated C-reactive protein (5.2?mg/dL), and elevated high-sensitivity troponin I (86?ng/mL). Plain and contrast-enhanced chest CT showed diffuse pericardial effusion with

title: Autoimmune Reaction Associated With?Long COVID Syndrome and Cardiovascular Disease
case_summary: A 35-year-old Russian woman presented with fatigue, chest pain, joint pain, and dyspnea. One year and ten months before her first Pfizer SARS-CoV-2 vaccination, she was hospitalized for three days with presyncope, palpitations, and a high burden of premature ventricular contractions (PVCs). Three weeks after her second vaccination, she redeveloped lightheadedness, fatigue, frequent presyncope, shortness of breath, and chest pain and was admitted with a high PVC burden. Cardiac magnetic resonance showed borderline cardiomegaly with left ventricular enlargement but no infarct, fibrosis, or amyloidosis; the ejection fraction was 62.3%. She underwent catheter ablation on two consecutive days, after which symptoms improved. Transthoracic echocardiography then showed trace mitral and tricuspid regurgitation and a small pericardial effusion. Two months later, she was treated with ibuprofen for pericarditis. Three weeks after that, she tested positive for SARS-CoV-2 and subsequently developed

title: Cardiac sarcoma presenting as paraneoplastic arthritis and clubbing: a case report and literature review
case_summary:
A 52-year-old woman presented with a 3-month history of severe inflammatory arthralgia affecting both hands, wrists, knees, and ankles. The symptoms began one week after receipt of the BNT162b2 Covid-19 mRNA vaccine and were initially attributed to the vaccine. She received a subsequent ChAdOx1-S Covid-19 vaccine three months later; low-dose prednisolone provided minimal relief. Initial investigations showed a C-reactive protein of 101 mg/L (normal < 5) and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 64 mm/hr (normal < 14); rheumatoid factor, anti-CCP antibody, antinuclear antibodies, and extractable nuclear antigens were negative.

Two weeks later, with persistent symptoms, she was evaluated in person. She reported mild dyspnea but denied chest pain or syncope. Her temperature was 38 degreesC, pulse 100 beats/min, and blood pressure 120/70 mm Hg. Examination revealed bilateral wrist swelling, non-tender bilateral knee effusions, bilateral ankle swelling, marked bilateral fingernail clubbing,

title: Malignant fibrous histiocytoma of the heart: a rare case report of an aggressive cardiac sarcoma mimicking myxoma
case_summary: A 40-year-old woman presented with a 2-week history of malaise, dyspnea, multiple episodes of syncope, and recurrent chest pain. She had type 2 diabetes mellitus and no other significant medical or family history. On examination, there was a grade II/VI systolic murmur at the left fifth intercostal space in the midclavicular line and mild dyspnea (NYHA class II). Electrocardiography showed normal sinus rhythm, left axis deviation, poor R-wave progression, and an rS pattern in leads III and aVF. Chest radiography demonstrated mild bilateral hilar congestion with a normal cardiac index. Transthoracic and transesophageal echocardiography revealed a heterogeneous, lobulated, pedunculated mass in the left atrium measuring 3.43 ? 3.47 cm, with a broad-based attachment to the atrial septum. The mass moved toward the mitral valve during diastole without prolapsing or causing obstruction. Central mitral regurgitation (1.5/4), a dilated left atrium (44 mm in systole), normal ventricular volumes

title: Mesalazine-induced myocarditis in a patient with ulcerative colitis: a case report
case_summary:
A 28-year-old man was admitted with a 2-day history of intermittent, sharp, central chest pain with diaphoresis. The pain was pleuritic and worsened when he sat forward. He denied recent viral symptoms and had not received a COVID-19 booster. His medical history was significant for ulcerative colitis diagnosed 3 weeks earlier, for which he started mesalazine; his bowel symptoms had improved. He took no other medications and had no allergies. He smoked 3-4 cigarettes daily and denied alcohol or drug use. There was no family history of cardiovascular disease.

On examination he was hemodynamically stable; the only abnormal finding was stage 2 finger clubbing. ECG showed sinus rhythm with benign early repolarization in the anterior leads. Chest radiography was unremarkable. Laboratory tests revealed a hemoglobin concentration of 132 g/L (120-170), a low mean corpuscular volume of 75 fL (80-97), and a high-sensitivity troponin T of 275 ng/L (<14) peaking at 632 ng/L on hospital day 2. C-re

title: A polo ball in the right atrium, importance of echocardiographic characteristics of intracardiac myxomas:?a?case report
case_summary:
A 77-year-old Asian man with no significant past medical history presented with a 2-month history of mild, intermittent left trepopnea (shortness of breath when lying on the left side) and palpitations. He denied syncope, fever, arthralgias, weight loss, or fatigue. His only risk factors were tobacco use, age, and a sedentary lifestyle; he was not on chronic medications.

On examination, heart sounds were regular without murmurs; there was no abdominal distension or lower-extremity edema. An electrocardiogram showed atrial tachycardia with an average ventricular rate of 60 beats per minute. Chest radiography demonstrated normal lung fields and an enlarged right lower cardiac contour suggestive of right atrial enlargement. Laboratory studies, including C-reactive protein, complete blood count (hemoglobin 13 g/dL), and serum interleukin-6 (7 pg/mL), were within normal limits.

Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a large, smooth-edged, spheroid mass in the right atrium measuring 86 ?

title: Immunosuppressive therapy ameliorates refractory vasospastic angina, severe pulmonary hypertension, and bronchiolitis in a patient with eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis: a case report
case_summary:
A 34-year-old woman with a smoking history and a past history of asthma, nasal polyps, and sinusitis presented with sudden severe chest pain followed by ventricular fibrillation. After successful resuscitation, her consciousness and vital signs were normal. Cardiac enzymes were elevated (CK-MB 7.1?ng/mL [<5.0?ng/mL]; troponin I 0.23?ng/mL [<0.028?ng/mL]). ECG showed right ventricular hypertrophy without ischaemic changes. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated preserved left ventricular function, right ventricular dilatation, tricuspid regurgitation with a peak gradient of 61?mmHg, and an RV fractional area change of 16.5%. Emergent contrast-enhanced CT excluded pulmonary embolism and aortic dissection. Urgent right heart catheterization revealed a mean pulmonary artery pressure of 66?mmHg, indicating severe pulmonary hypertension. 

During hospitalization, she experienced recurrent chest pains despite therapy with benidipine, nicorandil, and nitrates; ECG during episodes showed S

title: Severe heparin-induced thrombocytopenia: when the obvious is not obvious, a case report
case_summary: A 75-year-old Hawaiian-Chinese woman with a history of aortic stenosis, chronic renal insufficiency, and hypertension was evaluated for pitting edema of the lower extremities. On March 21, she underwent cardiac catheterization, which revealed an ejection fraction of 15% and severe aortic and mitral valvular disease. During the procedure, each arterial and venous sheath was flushed with ~250 units of heparin. Ten days later, she underwent elective aortic valve replacement and mitral valve repair with placement of an intraaortic balloon pump. While on cardiopulmonary bypass, she received 32,000 units of heparin. Her preoperative platelet count of 108?10^9/L fell to 25?10^9/L by postoperative day 3. She was transfused with a total of 12 units of platelets and required norepinephrine for blood pressure support. The drop in platelets was attributed to the intraaortic balloon pump, which was removed, but she subsequently bled from the femoral insertion site and underwent surgical repair, rec

title: Amiodarone-Induced Hypothyroidism Presenting as Cardiorenal Syndrome
case_summary:
A 90-year-old man was evaluated for a one-month history of progressive fatigue and abdominal and lower extremity edema. His medical history was notable for ischemic cardiomyopathy (left ventricular ejection fraction, 15%) with a dual-chamber pacemaker for resynchronization, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation and frequent ventricular ectopy treated with amiodarone (200 mcg/day) initiated six months earlier, and stage IV chronic kidney disease (not on dialysis). He reported full compliance with his heart failure medications and denied dietary indiscretion, alcohol use, or anginal symptoms.

On examination, his weight had increased by 4.6 kg; the heart rate was 60 beats/minute, with jugular venous distension and a positive hepatojugular reflex. Inspiratory crackles were heard at the lung bases, and there was pitting edema to the mid-thigh level. The skin appeared dry, and the thyroid examination was unremarkable.

A chest radiograph showed pulmonary edema and cardiomegaly. Electrocardiography

title: Mitral Annulus Disjunction Causing Ventricular Tachyarrhythmias in a Young Lady With Palpitations, Cardiac Ablation Terminated the Complaints
case_summary: A 21?year?old woman presented with palpitations and dizziness. Her resting ECG showed bigeminal premature ventricular contractions but a normal ST segment, QT interval, and T waves. Twenty?four?hour ambulatory ECG monitoring recorded 34 787 ventricular ectopic beats and multiple non?sustained runs of ventricular tachycardia. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated mitral valve prolapse with minimal mitral regurgitation and a probable abnormal location of the mitral annulus but was otherwise normal. To further evaluate potential structural causes of her arrhythmias, cardiac magnetic resonance imaging was performed; it confirmed mitral valve prolapse and revealed a mitral annulus disjunction measuring up to 10 mm in the posterolateral wall, without any evidence of myocardial inflammation or scarring.

title: Regional pericarditis following uncomplicated catheter ablation procedure: a case report
case_summary: A 66-year-old woman was evaluated for dull, nonradiating retrosternal chest pain that began on the second day after an otherwise uneventful radiofrequency ablation of frequent, symptomatic premature ventricular complexes originating in the right ventricular outflow tract. Her medical history included well-controlled hypertension, diabetes mellitus, and class-1 obesity. Two days after ablation, her blood pressure was 125/80 mm Hg, heart rate 75 bpm, respiratory rate 18/min, and oxygen saturation 100% on room air. Cardiac auscultation revealed muffled heart sounds without a pericardial friction rub; the second heart sound was accentuated at the aortic area. Lung examination was unremarkable. A 12-lead electrocardiogram showed biphasic T waves in leads V1-V4. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated no pericardial effusion or regional wall-motion abnormalities. Complete blood count was normal, and the high-sensitivity C-reactive protein level was 3.38 mg/L (reference range 1-3 mg/L). M

title: Sinus Node Dysfunction After Percutaneous Transcatheter Closure of?Right Coronary Artery-Superior Vena?Cava Fistula
final_diagnosis: Right coronary artery superior vena cava fistula
case_summary: A 48-year-old woman presented with intermittent palpitations but no chest pain, syncope, or dyspnea. She was afebrile, blood pressure was 116/70 mm Hg, and pulse 66 beats/min. Cardiopulmonary examination was unremarkable. Her medical history was notable only for migraines with aura and occasional palpitations; she had no history of thyroid disease, myocardial infarction, or congenital heart disease. A 12-lead electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a moderately enlarged left ventricle with elevated filling pressure and a mildly enlarged right ventricle; right ventricular systolic pressure was 55 mm Hg. Color Doppler imaging demonstrated a patent foramen ovale with bidirectional shunting, with significant right-to-left flow after Valsalva maneuver.  Coronary computed tomography angiography showed a dominant right coronary artery, enlarged proximally, giving rise to a sizable tortuous vessel coursing posterior to the right atrium near the cri

title: Intravascular B-cell lymphoma: case report of a rare cause of pulmonary arterial hypertension
case_summary:
A 53-year-old man with no significant past medical history presented with a 2-month history of progressive dyspnoea, nonproductive cough, fevers, night sweats, anorexia, nausea, and fatigue. He was a 30 pack-year former smoker. Initial evaluation at a local hospital revealed:

- Vital signs: on Bilevel Positive Airway Pressure for respiratory distress; oxygenation improved transiently.  
- Physical examination: clear lungs, normal heart sounds, no jugular venous distention, no peripheral oedema.  
- ECG: sinus tachycardia with non-specific ST-T changes.  
- Chest radiograph: mild pulmonary oedema.  
- Laboratory studies: WBC 6 300 cells/?L, haemoglobin 13.3 g/dL, platelets 226 000/?L, creatinine 1.2 mg/dL; arterial blood gas on BiPAP showed pH 7.44, PaCO? 30 mmHg, PaO? 145 mmHg.  
- Treated empirically for pneumonia with ceftriaxone and azithromycin.

His respiratory status worsened over the following days. A transthoracic echocardiogram showed mild RV enlargement and an estimated pulm

title: A Rare Case of Haemophilus Influenzae Serotype F Endocarditis Complicated by Concurrent Cardiogenic and Septic Shock: A Case of Challenging Management
journal: Journal of Cardiovascular Development and Disease
case_summary:
A 69-year-old man presented with fever and chills one week after discharge for Haemophilus influenzae bacteremia, having completed a two-week course of cefuroxime. During the prior admission, transthoracic echocardiography had shown an ejection fraction of 55%, mild aortic stenosis, and moderate aortic insufficiency, which was not further investigated despite bacteremia. Additional prior studies included a normal chest radiograph, CT of the chest demonstrating minimal tree-in-bud opacities, and MRI of the ankle showing synovitis.

On this presentation, he reported one day of achy, nonradiating, nonreproducible chest pain. Vital signs were temperature 101.0  degreesF, heart rate 127 bpm, blood pressure 67/39 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation 96% on room air. Physical examination revealed a grade 3 systolic ejection murmur at the right upper sternal border without radiation to the carotids and no Janeway lesions or Osler nodes.

Laboratory studies showed leukocytosis and a troponin level of 2

title: Successful resolution of urothorax secondary to non-traumatic uroabdomen in a cat managed with peritoneal dialysis as a bridge to surgery
case_summary: A 9-year-old neutered male domestic shorthair cat was referred for acute hemodynamic collapse. The caretaker had noted weakness and possible signs of lower urinary tract disease over the previous days; there was no known trauma, and the cat was strictly indoors. On presentation, the cat was obtunded, bradycardic (70 beats/min), hypothermic (35  degreesC), with weak femoral pulses, prolonged capillary refill time, and non-detectable Doppler blood pressure. Point-of-care ultrasound revealed a moderate volume of free abdominal fluid in all four quadrants and no pleural or pericardial effusion. ECG showed atrial standstill with idioventricular escape rhythms. A jugular venous sample demonstrated severe metabolic acidosis (pH 7.10), hyperkalemia (K? 8.78 mmol/L), and azotemia (creatinine 510 ?mol/L). Abdominocentesis yielded serosanguineous fluid with a fluid-to-serum creatinine ratio of 6.09 and a potassium ratio of 2.28, consistent with uroabdomen. The cat was managed with IV fluids, calc

title: Laminopathies: should Wenckebach be a cause for concern? A case report
case_summary:
A 42-year-old Caucasian man presented with a 1-week history of lethargy and intermittent dizziness but no syncope. He had no prior medical history or medications. His father had dilated cardiomyopathy and a CRT-D and died at age 75. The patient was a non-smoker with a physically demanding job and no athletic training. 

On examination he was hemodynamically stable; cardiovascular and other systems were unremarkable. Laboratory tests showed normal renal function and normal troponin levels. A chest radiograph was normal, with no hilar lymphadenopathy. 

Electrocardiogram revealed Mobitz type I (Wenckebach) atrioventricular block at 40 b.p.m., with a narrow QRS complex and normal QT interval. He was admitted to the coronary care unit for monitoring. 

Transthoracic echocardiography showed normal LV size, wall thickness, and systolic function (EF 55-65%), with biatrial dilatation. Overnight telemetry captured multiple episodes of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia, though he remained as

title: Myocardial bridging presenting as myocardial ischaemia induced cardiac arrest: a case report
case_summary: A 50-year-old woman with no prior medical conditions or family history of cardiac disease collapsed suddenly while having lunch; a witness immediately initiated CPR. Emergency responders documented ventricular fibrillation, delivered five shocks during 10 minutes of CPR, and achieved return of spontaneous circulation. On arrival, she was intubated, blood pressure was 160/80 mmHg, and her mental status was stuporous. Noncontrast head CT was normal. Lung fields were clear and cardiac auscultation revealed no murmurs. Post-defibrillation ECG showed right bundle branch block with ST-segment elevation and Q-waves in leads V1-V4. Laboratory studies were notable for WBC 14,980 /?L, pro-BNP 998.1 pg/mL, troponin-I 0.054 ng/mL, D-dimer 3,725 ng/mL, with normal CK-MB and potassium. A repeat ECG demonstrated resolution of ST elevation but persistent deep Q-waves in V1-V4. Urgent coronary angiography showed no fixed stenoses and an anomalous high-takeoff right coronary artery. Systolic compression

title: Mahaim pathway tachycardia versus bystander ventricular tachycardia: A distinction without a difference
final_diagnosis: Mahaim pathway tachycardia due to triggered activity
case_summary:
A 41-year-old woman with a history of a right-sided accessory pathway ablated at age 19 presented with recurrent palpitations and a regular wide complex tachycardia (WCT) that had terminated with 6 mg of intravenous adenosine at an outside hospital. One week later, she had a similar episode.  
- ECG during tachycardia: left bundle-branch block pattern, left superior axis, cycle length 280 ms.  
- Sinus-rhythm ECG: no preexcitation.  
- Echocardiogram and cardiac magnetic resonance imaging: normal.  

Electrophysiologic study:  
- Baseline AH interval 65 ms, HV interval 37 ms; dual AV nodal physiology present.  
- Rapid pacing from the proximal coronary sinus reproduced the clinical arrhythmia. Retrograde conduction was concentric; adenosine caused ventriculoatrial block.  
- During atrial pacing at a cycle length of 370 ms, conduction initially over the fast AV nodal pathway produced a narrow QRS; when conduction abruptly switched to the slow pathway (AH increased from 105 to 194 ms),

title: Giant Left Ventricular (Pseudo?) Aneurysm Complicating Anterior Myocardial Infarction
case_summary:
A 55-year-old woman with hypertension and a history of smoking presented with chest pain and was found to have an anterior wall ST-elevation myocardial infarction. She underwent primary percutaneous coronary intervention 1 hour 45 minutes after symptom onset, with successful recanalization of the mid-left anterior descending artery. Initial transthoracic echocardiography showed an LVEF of 30%, akinesia of the anterior, septal, and apical walls, and a small pericardial effusion. She was treated for heart failure (Killip class II) and epistenocardiac pericarditis and discharged on aspirin, ticagrelor, ramipril, bisoprolol, ivabradine, furosemide, spironolactone, and statin/ezetimibe.

Two days after discharge, she was readmitted with worsening heart failure symptoms and pleuritic chest pain. Echocardiography revealed an apical thrombus, a small circumferential pericardial effusion, and worsening LV dilation and dysfunction (LVEF 28%). She was started on warfarin plus dual antiplatelet th

title: A case report: biventricular takotsubo cardiomyopathy with sequential ventricular recovery due to pulmonary hypertension
case_summary: A 69-year-old woman presented with several days of intermittent breathlessness and reduced exercise tolerance, and on the morning of admission reported a brief episode of chest pain. Her history included alcoholic cirrhosis (Child-Pugh A), portal hypertensive gastropathy, hiatus hernia, healed duodenal ulcer, chronic anxiety, a 30-pack-year smoking history, and 3 years of alcohol abstinence. On arrival she was alert; pulse 127 bpm, BP 166/78 mmHg, respiratory rate 34 /min, SpO? 86% on 5 L O?, temperature 36.3  degreesC. Examination revealed bilateral wheeze, normal heart sounds, cold peripheries, and prolonged capillary refill without edema. Arterial blood gas showed acute decompensated type 2 respiratory failure. She was treated for a presumed infective COPD exacerbation with antibiotics, nebulizers, and steroids. Chest radiograph showed hyperinflation with upper lobe diversion. ECG revealed sinus tachycardia, borderline first-degree AV block, Q waves in V1-V4 with marked ST-segment e

title: Acute Spontaneous Coronary Artery Thrombosis as Initial Presentation of HIV Infection in a Young Man
case_summary: A 33-year-old man was referred urgently with severe retrosternal chest pressure and dyspnea that began two hours earlier, on a background of intermittent chest pain over two weeks. He has no history of hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidemia, smoking, recreational drug use, or family history of cardiac disease. Physical examination: BMI 27, afebrile, blood pressure 141/81 mmHg (equal in both arms), heart rate 62 beats/min, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min. Chest clear, no jugular venous distention, heart sounds normal without murmurs. ECG shows ST-segment elevation in leads V1-V6 consistent with an anterolateral STEMI. Initial labs: creatinine 74 ?g/mL, normal CBC and coagulation studies, alkaline phosphatase 154 U/L, ALT 39 U/L, AST 72 U/L, CK-MB mass 13 ng/mL, troponin 0.1 ng/mL, normal lipid panel, urinalysis with proteinuria. Hepatitis B and C serologies were negative. HIV antibody test was positive; CD4+ count was 643 cells/?L.

title: Constrictive Pericarditis in the Presence of Remaining Remnants of a Left Ventricular Assist Device in a Heart Transplanted Patient
case_summary:
A 63?year?old man presented with progressive dyspnea, fatigue, and decreased exercise tolerance over several months. Four years earlier, he had undergone orthotopic heart transplantation with an uneventful postoperative course. He denied chest pain, palpitations, or syncope. Recent coronary angiography showed no graft vasculopathy and preserved left ventricular ejection fraction. He had never experienced cellular or humoral rejection.

Resting ECG demonstrated sinus rhythm at 87 beats/min without abnormalities. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed normal ventricular sizes and systolic function with biatrial enlargement. Pharmacological stress cardiac MRI excluded ischemia but incidentally revealed a foreign body in the epicardial adipose tissue. A noncontrast chest CT confirmed a tubular epicardial structure, identified as a retained inflow pump connection of a left ventricular assist device left in situ at transplantation.

On examination, cardiac auscultation disclosed a pericardi

title: Eosinophilic perimyocarditis associated with eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis: a case report
case_summary:
A 52-year-old woman with a history of asthma since childhood and recurrent fever, arthralgia, and fatigue over the past two years presented with worsening chest pain of several weeks' duration. Twelve months earlier, she had been diagnosed with idiopathic pericarditis associated with eosinophilia and treated with NSAIDs, with rapid symptom remission. Two weeks before admission she experienced angioedema, painful subcutaneous nodules of the arms and legs, and numbness and paresthesia of her toes and hands. 

On presentation, the chest pain was pleuritic and improved by sitting up and leaning forward. Vital signs were normal. Cardiac examination revealed a holosystolic regurgitant murmur at the apex; there were no skin rashes or focal neurologic deficits. ECG showed diffuse T-wave flattening in the inferior, lateral, and precordial leads. Laboratory studies demonstrated C-reactive protein 101 mg/L (normal <16 mg/L), eosinophils 2.14 ? 10^9/L (normal <0.5 ? 10^9/L), high-sensitivity tropo

title: Tachy- and bradyarrhythmia as an initial presentation of human immunodeficiency virus-related primary cardiac lymphoma: a case report
case_summary:
A 27-year-old man with a 9-month history of advanced HIV infection on antiretroviral therapy presented with 2?weeks of intermittent palpitations, chest pain, reduced exercise tolerance, one near-syncope with exertion, fever up to 38.9 degreesC, dry cough, and loose stools. On admission, he was tachycardic (114?bpm), blood pressure 103/63?mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Physical examination was unremarkable: regular heart sounds without murmurs, clear lungs, no peripheral oedema or infective stigmata, and normal abdominal and lymph node examinations. 

The initial ECG showed sinus tachycardia, first-degree AV block, and frequent atrial ectopics. Continuous monitoring revealed intermittent complete AV block with a broad complex escape rhythm at 54?bpm, episodes of junctional tachycardia, and supraventricular tachycardia correlating with palpitations. Chest radiography demonstrated cardiomegaly (cardiothoracic ratio 60%). Blood cultures were negative.

title: Primary Undifferentiated Pericardial Sarcoma after Radi?therapy for Hodgkin Lymphoma
case_summary:
A 55-year-old woman was admitted with progressive weakness, shortness of breath, and swelling of the right forearm, hand, and both lower extremities. Eighteen years earlier, she had mixed cellularity Hodgkin lymphoma IVA involving mediastinal and pericardial nodes; she underwent thoracotomy with partial lung and pericardial resection, combination chemotherapy, and radiotherapy, achieving stable remission. 

In April 2015, she developed dry cough, dyspnea, low-grade fever, and pleural effusion; tuberculosis was excluded and antibacterial therapy improved her symptoms. In late 2016, symptoms recurred, and chest CT showed changes attributed to post-radiation fibrosis. In spring 2017, she was hospitalized for severe dry cough and dyspnea; CT demonstrated extensive fibrous degeneration, pleural effusion, and right lower-lobe infiltration. Echocardiography revealed pericardial adhesions and fibrous changes of the aortic and mitral valves. She was diagnosed with postradiation pulmonary fibros

title: Case report of Ebstein's anomaly in a young female
case_summary:
A 24-year-old woman with a history of chronic heart failure treated intermittently with furosemide presents with 5 days of resting dyspnea, productive cough, low-grade intermittent fever, poor appetite, and fatigue. She notes 2 weeks of orthopnea requiring two pillows, 3 weeks of paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, and 2 weeks of palpitations. Two years earlier, she was admitted to the ICU with class IV heart failure, unstable ventricular tachycardia, cardiogenic shock, and pneumonia. She has missed follow-up appointments and stopped her medications 3 months ago for financial reasons. 

On examination, blood pressure is 90/55 mm Hg, pulse is irregular at 148 beats/min, respiratory rate is 34 breaths/min, temperature is 36.1 degreesC, and oxygen saturation is 46% on room air. Respiratory examination shows severe subcostal and intercostal retractions and coarse crackles posteriorly. Cardiovascular examination reveals a raised jugular venous pressure, a hyperdynamic precordium with apical and p

title: A case report of isolated arrhythmogenic left ventricular cardiomyopathy: phenotypes, diagnosis, and treatment
case_summary:
A 54-year-old man presented with recurrent palpitations and retrosternal pain radiating to the back, accompanied by nausea and vomiting. On initial admission, his heart rate was 130 b.p.m.; blood pressure and other vital signs were stable, and physical examination was unremarkable. He had no history of hypertension, diabetes, dyslipidaemia, or cardiovascular disease, and no family history of syncope, coronary heart disease, malignant arrhythmia, or sudden death.

At a local hospital, ECG showed ventricular tachycardia at 213 b.p.m. Troponin and myocardial enzyme levels were normal. Coronary angiography revealed normal coronary arteries. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated thinning and bulging of the left ventricular (LV) apex with localized dyskinesia. Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (CMRI) confirmed abnormal systolic activity at the LV apex; the right ventricle was normal. Non-coronary obstructive acute myocardial infarction was initially considered, and he was treated with

title: Too Little, Too Late: A Case of a Swift Fatal Culmination of Amiodarone Induced Pulmonary Toxicity in an Adult Male
case_summary:
A 64-year-old man presented with a 6-day history of cough and worsening dyspnea. His cough was intermittently productive of white sputum. He denied fever, chest pain, palpitations, gastrointestinal or neurologic symptoms. 

His medical history included rheumatic heart disease with a mechanical aortic valve, nonischemic dilated cardiomyopathy, atrial fibrillation, hypertension, and hypercholesterolemia. Eleven months earlier he had a ventricular tachycardia treated with oral amiodarone (200 mg twice daily). He was a former heavy smoker (? 40 pack-years) and drank 20 standard alcoholic drinks per week. His medications included sacubitril-valsartan, bisoprolol, dapagliflozin, furosemide, thyroxine, warfarin, budesonide-formoterol, and amiodarone.

On examination, he was afebrile, blood pressure 85/63 mm Hg (lying) and 92/66 mm Hg (standing), heart rate 55 beats/min, respiratory rate 26 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 90% on room air. He appeared thin and mildly cachectic with resting d

title: Multimodality imaging in assessment of mitral valve tumors: An unusual papillary fibroelastoma?
final_diagnosis: Caseous calcification of the mitral annulus
case_summary: A 72-year-old woman with hypertension, dyslipidemia, diabetes mellitus, and a history of coronary artery disease treated with multiple percutaneous coronary interventions presented with a 2-month history of angina on minimal exertion. On examination, her blood pressure was 130/70 mm Hg, heart rate 63 beats/min, and the remainder of the physical examination was normal. An electrocardiogram was unremarkable, and routine laboratory tests showed no abnormalities. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a 21 ? 15 mm echo-dense, spherical, mobile, tumor-like mass in the periannular posterior region near the ventricular side of the posterior mitral leaflet; the mass had sharp, distinct borders and markedly bright echogenicity compared with adjacent myocardium. The posterior leaflet was stretched and arched over the mass, the anterior leaflet was normal, and Doppler mapping showed mild mitral regurgitation without stenosis. The left ventricle was mildly hypertrophic with normal segmental wall

title: Type A aortic dissection during transoesophageal echocardiography: a case report
case_summary:
An 81-year-old man presented with progressive exertional dyspnea, abdominal distension, and vomiting for 3 days. He had a history of heart failure with reduced ejection fraction for 2 years and an infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm identified on CT screening. He denied hypertension, diabetes, hyperlipidemia, and smoking. One week before presentation, furosemide, bisoprolol, and amiodarone were initiated.

On examination, temperature was normal; blood pressure 104/68 mmHg; heart rate 85 beats/min (irregular); respiratory rate 20 breaths/min; SpO? 96% on 3 L/min O?. Cardiac auscultation revealed an irregularly irregular rhythm and a grade III/VI systolic murmur at the apex. ECG showed atrial fibrillation. Chest radiograph demonstrated cardiomegaly and mild pulmonary edema. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a severely dilated left ventricle with an ejection fraction of 20.8% and moderate-to-severe mitral regurgitation. Laboratory tests showed creatinine 2.6 mg/dL (eGFR 25 mL/min/1

title: Large Mobile Left Ventricular Thrombi Formation in a 32-Year-Old Despite?Direct Oral Anticoagulation With?Dabigatran
case_summary:
A 32-year-old man presented with exertional dyspnea and typical angina corresponding to New York Heart Association functional class II and Canadian Cardiovascular Society stage II. His heart rate was 84 beats/min, blood pressure 124/74 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation 98%. The physical examination was unremarkable except for obesity (body mass index, 30.1 kg/m2). He denied missed doses of anticoagulation. 

His history included an unprovoked bilateral pulmonary embolism at age 26 treated with rivaroxaban; testing revealed a heterozygous prothrombin G20210A mutation. At age 30, after a 1-week interruption of anticoagulation, he had an acute ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction due to thrombotic occlusion of the left anterior descending artery; coronary angiography showed no underlying atherosclerosis. He received thrombus aspiration, stent placement, and resumed anticoagulation with dabigatran 150 mg twice daily, along with clopidogrel for 1 year, a ?-blocker, an aldosterone antagonis

title: Hemodynamic variation is a dominant contributing factor of Graves' hyperthyroidism complication: Heart failure and fatal liver dysfunction, a case report and analysis
case_summary:
A 37-year-old woman was referred for an 18-year history of intermittent palpitations and easy fatigability, and 6 weeks of exertional dyspnea with lower extremity edema. She had been diagnosed with Graves disease in 2001 and started on methimazole but had poor adherence; she also took unspecified herbal remedies for 2 years. Four weeks earlier at a local hospital she received intravenous furosemide, glycyrrhizinate, metoprolol, and aspirin, but her condition worsened and her total bilirubin rose to five times normal.

On examination, she was afebrile, blood pressure 116/65 mmHg, heart rate 105 beats/min, with mild jaundice, a diffusely enlarged nontender thyroid, two vascular "red spots" on the chest, an irregular rhythm, a systolic murmur at the left lower sternal border, elevated jugular venous pressure, and mild bilateral pitting edema.

Laboratory studies showed free T3 24.1 pmol/L, free T4 109.13 pmol/L, TSH <0.008 uIU/mL, TSH receptor antibody 37.88 IU/L; WBC 2.7?10^9/L with neut

title: Acute myocarditis after receiving first dose of BNT162b2 mRNA vaccine
case_summary:
A 40-year-old man presented with acute onset of pleuritic chest pain and dyspnea 3 days after receiving the first dose of the BNT162b2 mRNA COVID-19 vaccine. In the days before presentation, he had experienced nausea, myalgia, and subjective fevers. Four months earlier, he had been diagnosed with mild COVID-19 pneumonia, treated symptomatically as an outpatient, and had fully recovered. 

On examination, he was in moderate distress from chest discomfort. Temperature was 36.9 degreesC, blood pressure 142/84 mm Hg, heart rate 107 beats/min, and oxygen saturation 100% on ambient air. Lungs were clear bilaterally. He had mild jugular venous distension without murmurs, rubs, or gallops. Extremities were warm with no peripheral edema. He had no significant past medical history aside from the recent COVID-19 infection and took only testosterone; he denied tobacco, alcohol, and intravenous drug use.

An initial electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm with diffuse ST elevations across all

title: Fluid Overload-Associated Large B-Cell Lymphoma: A Case Report and Review of Literature
case_summary:
An 82-year-old man with atrial fibrillation (status post ablation), atrial flutter, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes, and coronary artery disease status post coronary artery bypass grafting presented with progressive shortness of breath. He denied cough, chest pain, or fever. Physical examination revealed mild lower extremity swelling. Vital signs were: blood pressure 156/78 mmHg, pulse 70/min, respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 36.3  degreesC, and SpO? 97% on room air. His BMI was 29.5 kg/m?.

Laboratory studies showed microcytic anemia and chronic thrombocytopenia; electrolytes and urinalysis were normal. Hepatitis C serology was negative. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated normal left ventricular systolic function (LVEF 60-65%) with only trace valvular regurgitations. A 12-lead ECG showed atrial flutter with 4:1 AV conduction. Chest radiography revealed a large right pleural effusion with associated atelectasis. CT of the chest confirmed a moderate right pleu

title: Case report: Recovery after large intramyocardial dissecting haematoma of the ventricular septum--a rare complication of myocardial infarction
case_summary: A 77-year-old man with a history of coronary artery disease (including bypass surgery in 2009), chronic obstructive pulmonary disease on inhaled steroids, dyslipidaemia, and hypertension presented with persistent left-sided chest pain on exertion for one week and mild dyspnoea. On examination, heart and lung sounds were normal. An electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm at 79 b.p.m., a right bundle branch block, and isolated ventricular extrasystoles. Transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) revealed mildly reduced left ventricular systolic function and moderate tricuspid regurgitation. Cardiac biomarkers were elevated (troponin I 22 114 pg/mL [normal < 57 pg/mL], CK 707 U/L [normal < 190 U/L], CK-MB 44 ng/mL [normal < 3.6 ng/mL]), and a diagnosis of non-ST-segment-elevation myocardial infarction was made. Coronary angiography showed stenosis in the proximal and mid-right coronary artery, treated with drug-eluting stent implantation, and an ostial left anterior descending artery stenosis tha

title: A Rare Case of Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy with Subendocardial Late Gadolinium Enhancement in an Apical Aneurysm with Thrombus
case_summary: A 71-year-old man presented with progressive dyspnea, orthopnea, general malaise, and bilateral lower?limb edema. He had no history of hypertension, diabetes, or familial cardiac disease. On admission, he was in atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response and a complete right bundle branch block; there was no low voltage on ECG. Laboratory tests showed a B-type natriuretic peptide level of 1272.6 pg/mL (normal < 18.4 pg/mL), troponin I of 0.491 ng/mL (normal < 0.045 ng/mL), a white blood cell count of 5830/uL with 1.5% eosinophils, and a C-reactive protein level of 0.48 mg/dL. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a thin-walled, akinetic apical region with thrombus, hypokinesis of the basal and mid-left ventricle, an ejection fraction of 37%, left ventricular diastolic and systolic diameters of 49 mm and 39 mm, posterior and septal wall thicknesses of 12 mm and 17 mm, and a right ventricular free?wall thickness of 10 mm; there was no left ventricular outflow tract obstructi

title: Slow pseudo sinus rhythm and atrial tachycardia of right superior venous pulmonary origin
case_summary:
A 22?year?old man had 4-5 years of exercise?induced palpitations and dizziness. Recorded atrial tachycardia rates reached 250 bpm, with slower ectopic rhythms around 100 bpm and variable degrees of atrioventricular block. Flecainide provided only temporary control. His medical history, physical examination, and echocardiogram were normal.

During an electrophysiological study, supraventricular premature beats were observed. Compared with sinus rhythm, the P wave showed a slight reduction in Lead I amplitude, increased positivity in Leads II, III, and aVF, inversion in aVL, and a shift to positive polarity in V1. A non?sustained atrial tachycardia at 220 bpm with 2:1 block exhibited identical P?wave polarity. At other times, a slow regular atrial rhythm (~45 bpm) showed alternating sinus and ectopic P?wave morphologies.

Intracardiac recordings demonstrated simultaneous activation of the high right atrium and coronary?sinus os, with delayed lateral right?atrium activation. Based on righ

title: Takotsubo syndrome after mitral valve surgery: a case report
case_summary:
A 79-year-old woman with a history of atrial fibrillation (status post-left atrial appendage closure) was admitted for elective mitral valve replacement for asymptomatic severe primary mitral regurgitation due to anterior leaflet prolapse. One month earlier, echocardiography showed a left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) of 56% with normal chamber dimensions, and preoperative CT angiography revealed no coronary lesions. Her only home medication was acetylsalicylic acid. 

The patient underwent implantation of a biological mitral valve without intraoperative complications; intraoperative transesophageal echocardiography confirmed normal prosthetic function, and low-dose noradrenaline (0.05 ?g/kg/min) was required after cardiopulmonary bypass. 

On arrival to the intensive care unit, she developed cardiogenic shock with cold extremities, oliguria, systolic blood pressure of 80 mmHg, metabolic acidosis, and elevated lactate. Vasoactive support with noradrenaline (0.1 ?g/kg/min) and do

title: Novel analysis of ventriculoatrial interval prolongation during a narrow QRS tachycardia using a right atrial nondecremental-midseptal accessory pathway
case_summary:
A 69-year-old man with a 27-year history of intermittent palpitations and no structural heart disease underwent evaluation. His baseline 12-lead electrocardiogram in sinus rhythm showed no delta waves. During episodes of palpitation, surface ECG demonstrated a regular supraventricular tachycardia at 205 beats per minute with negative P waves in the inferior leads. 

An electrophysiologic study was performed with multipolar catheters in the high right atrium, His bundle region, right ventricular apex, and coronary sinus. At baseline, programmed RV pacing revealed concentric, nondecremental ventriculoatrial conduction, and atrial pacing maneuvers did not induce sustained tachycardia. After isoproterenol infusion, atrial pacing provoked a sustained supraventricular tachycardia (SVT) with a cycle length of 240 ms and a concentric atrial activation sequence. 

With constant RV apical pacing at a cycle length of 600 ms in sinus rhythm, three paced stimuli conducted retrogradely over an acces

title: Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy in the Setting of Tension Pneumothorax
case_summary:
A 78-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, osteoporosis, and asthma was brought to the emergency department with severe shortness of breath. She was afebrile, tachypneic at 28 breaths/min, blood pressure 185/105 mmHg, and heart rate 111 bpm. Chest radiography demonstrated a large left-sided tension pneumothorax, and she was intubated for impending respiratory failure.
