A chest tube was inserted, with subsequent reexpansion of the left lung, and the patient was admitted to the ICU.

A troponin I level was elevated at 16.529 ng/mL (reference ?0.1 ng/mL). Serial ECGs over 12 hours showed alternating sinus bradycardia and tachycardia, anterior Q waves, dynamic T-wave changes, and transient ST-segment elevation in the lateral leads. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed global left ventricular hypokinesis sparing the basal segments, apical ballooning, and an LVEF of 13%. The patient was started on aspirin, clopidogrel, a statin, and ramipril and was transferred for coronary an

title: Syphilitic Coronary Artery Ostial Stenosis Resulting in Acute Myocardial Infarction Treated by Percutaneous Coronary Intervention
case_summary:
A 46-year-old man presented with sudden onset of severe chest pain, progressive dyspnea, and diaphoresis. He was a current smoker with no other coronary risk factors and no history of radiotherapy or chemotherapy. On examination, he was tachypneic (26 breaths/min), tachycardic (160 beats/min, regular), blood pressure was 130/80 mmHg, and oxygen saturation was 83% on room air. Cardiac auscultation was unremarkable, but bilateral crackles were present. He was intubated for acute pulmonary edema.  

Electrocardiography showed sinus rhythm with ST-segment elevations in leads I, aVL, aVR, and V1-V5, and ST depressions in leads II, III, and aVF, consistent with anterolateral myocardial infarction. Aspirin and clopidogrel were administered, and the patient underwent emergent coronary angiography, which revealed a severely stenotic ostial lesion of the left main coronary artery with a normal distal coronary bed; subtle aortic regurgitation was also noted. A drug-eluting stent was successfully

title: Tertiary syphilis and cardiovascular disease: the united triad: case report
case_summary:
A 54-year-old man with a 30-pack-year smoking history presented with one year of progressive exertional dyspnea and recent acute respiratory distress. He denied chest pain, fevers, skin rashes, or history of sexually transmitted infections. His cardiovascular examination was notable for a pronounced diastolic murmur at the aortic focus; peripheral pulses were present and symmetrical, and the neurologic examination was normal.  
An ECG showed sinus rhythm with lateral ST-segment depression and T-wave inversion. Coronary angiography revealed an approximately 80% ostial stenosis of the left main stem, with a smooth appearance of the remainder of the coronary arteries. He underwent successful angioplasty with a drug-eluting stent and was discharged on dual antiplatelet therapy, a statin, a ?-blocker, RAAS inhibitors, and an SGLT2 inhibitor.  
At follow-up, he remained in NYHA class III dyspnea without angina or fever. Transthoracic echocardiography showed a globularly dilated left ventricl

title: A Neovascularized Left Atrial Mass
case_summary:
A 43-year-old man with no history of rheumatic fever presented with a 10-month history of dyspnea and palpitations. On examination, his pulse was irregular at 100 beats per minute, respiratory rate 30 breaths per minute, and a prominent apical diastolic murmur continuing into the first heart sound was noted. Electrocardiography showed atrial fibrillation with a right bundle-branch block. Chest radiography revealed an enlarged left atrium and pulmonary congestion.

Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated critical mitral stenosis (mitral valve area 0.7 cm2 by planimetry; peak/mean gradients 16/8 mmHg), mild mitral regurgitation, severe pulmonary arterial hypertension (70 mmHg), and mild functional tricuspid regurgitation. The mitral valve appeared rheumatically deformed with thickened leaflets, commissural fusion, and calcifications. The left atrium was enlarged (area 40 cm2) and contained a well-circumscribed, heterogeneous mass measuring approximately 10 cm2, adherent to the atrial

title: Asymptomatic paroxysmal ventricular standstill in an elderly male following revascularization of multivessel coronary disease: A case report and literature review
case_summary:
A 70?year?old man with a history of multivessel coronary artery disease, multiple prior stents, hypertension, and type 2 diabetes mellitus was admitted electively for coronary artery bypass grafting (CABG). Over the preceding three months, he had progressive chest pain and exertional dyspnea. A nuclear stress test demonstrated anterior and anterolateral reversible defects with a drop in left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) from 47% at rest to 31% on stress and a blood pressure decrease from 125/77 to 106/44?mmHg. A limited echocardiogram showed an LVEF of 40%-45% with anterior and lateral wall hypokinesis.

On admission, he was afebrile, blood pressure 130/84?mmHg, heart rate 87?beats/min, and in sinus rhythm. Physical examination was unremarkable. An initial electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm with frequent premature ventricular contractions and an atypical left bundle branch block.

He underwent triple?vessel CABG (LAD, OM1, RPL1) with cardiopulmonary bypass, systemic cooling

title: Isolated Pulmonic Valve Endocarditis: A Rare Clinical Entity
case_summary:
A 52-year-old man presented with 1 month of progressive generalized swelling beginning in the legs and later involving the abdomen and face. He reported prolonged fever, malaise, anorexia, fatigue, productive cough, dyspnea on mild exertion, and reddish discoloration of urine, without chest pain, hemoptysis, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, palpitations, flank pain, or change in urine volume. He denied intravenous drug use. His only medical history was hypertension managed with hydrochlorothiazide.

On examination, temperature was 38.3  degreesC, blood pressure 140/90 mmHg, pulse 104 bpm, respiratory rate 26/min, and oxygen saturation 90% on room air. He appeared ill with conjunctival pallor. Lungs had reduced air entry and crackles at both bases. Cardiovascular exam revealed jugular venous distension, a displaced apical impulse, an early diastolic murmur at the left upper parasternal area, and a holosystolic murmur at the left lower parasternal area without gallops. There was

title: Biventricular Mechanical Circulatory Support Does Not Prevent Delayed Myocardial Ventricular Rupture following Myocardial Infarction
case_summary:
A 58-year-old man with a history of coronary artery disease presented to an outside hospital with acute inferior?wall myocardial infarction and cardiogenic shock. Emergent cardiac catheterization revealed right coronary artery occlusion, and a bare?metal stent was placed. An intra?aortic balloon pump was inserted for hemodynamic support, and an Impella 2.5 device was also placed via the femoral artery. A transthoracic echocardiogram demonstrated severely dilated ventricles with severe biventricular systolic dysfunction. The patient was transferred to our tertiary center for further management. 

Upon arrival, he continued to require hemodynamic support, so the IABP and Impella were removed and venoarterial extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) was initiated via the femoral vessels. Eight days after the myocardial infarction, persistent cardiac decompensation prompted conversion to surgical biventricular assist devices (BIVADs); cannulas were placed in the right ventricle and pulmo

title: Resolution of new left bundle branch block and ventricular tachycardia with immunosuppressive therapy in a patient with cardiac sarcoidosis
case_summary: A 42-year-old man with a history of hypertension and a bicuspid aortic valve presented with a gradually worsening headache and blurred vision. Brain magnetic resonance imaging showed enhancing extraaxial masses along the anterior right tentorium and the left parietal-temporal convexity. Chest computed tomography revealed hilar lymphadenopathy. Laboratory studies showed an angiotensin-converting enzyme level below the normal range and an elevated C-reactive protein level. Endobronchial ultrasound-guided lymph node biopsy demonstrated noncaseating granulomas, confirming sarcoidosis. He was treated with high-dose prednisone, resulting in shrinkage and stabilization of the intracranial masses. He subsequently reported intermittent palpitations without presyncope or syncope. Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging performed for screening revealed a normal ejection fraction and delayed gadolinium enhancement of the basal inferior septum along the right ventricular aspect. Baseline electrocardiogr

title: Malaise and fatigue following mitral valve repair: case report
case_summary:
A 73-year-old man was admitted with progressively worsening shortness of breath (NYHA class IV) and fatigue two months after surgical mitral valve repair. His postoperative course had been complicated by haemothorax requiring re-thoracotomy, severe enterococcal sepsis treated with vancomycin, and a right frontal watershed stroke with residual left arm paresis. His history also included hypertension, atrial fibrillation, and coronary artery disease treated with bypass grafting at the time of his mitral repair.

On examination, he appeared pale, had pale conjunctivae, and a holosystolic 3/6 apical murmur radiating to the axilla. Mild bibasilar crackles and bilateral lower extremity edema were present. Blood pressure was 130/80 mmHg, heart rate 80 bpm, and temperature 36.6  degreesC. Chest radiography showed a small left pleural effusion without infiltrates.

Laboratory studies revealed haemoglobin 6.6 g/dL (13.6-17.2 g/dL), haematocrit 0.20 (0.39-0.49), reticulocytes 5% (0.5-2%), haptogl

title: Rare Case of Fetal Permanent Junctional Reciprocating Tachycardia Refractory to Prenatal Antiarrhythmic Therapy
journal: Mayo Clinic Proceedings: Innovations, Quality & Outcomes
case_summary:
A 33-year-old G3P2 woman was referred at 19 3/7 weeks' gestation for fetal tachycardia noted on screening ultrasound. Maternal history was notable only for well-controlled depression and anemia. Fetal echocardiography demonstrated a heart rate of 233 beats/min with 1:1 atrioventricular conduction and transient bradycardia with uterine compression, confirming supraventricular tachycardia (SVT). 

The patient was admitted and treated sequentially with oral digoxin, then digoxin plus flecainide, followed by sotalol when digoxin was discontinued for maternal intolerance. Despite this regimen, follow-up at 21 4/7 weeks showed a fetal heart rate of 217 beats/min with moderate tricuspid and trivial mitral regurgitation but no hydrops. At 22 3/7 weeks, intraumbilical and oral amiodarone were added. By 23 3/7 weeks, the fetal heart rate had decreased to 180-200 beats/min, but biventricular function was severely impaired (left ventricular ejection fraction, 25%) with pericardial and pleural effu

title: Thrombus entrapment with left atrial appendage closure to facilitate early cardioversion in tachycardiomyopathy: a case report
case_summary:
A 65-year-old man presented with a 4-week history of palpitations, dyspnoea on exertion, and peripheral oedema. His medical history included hypertensive heart disease with previously normal left ventricular ejection fraction, well-controlled blood pressure, type 2 diabetes mellitus, ascending aortic ectasia, and a history of tobacco use. Medications were perindopril/amlodipine, carvedilol, and metformin.  
On examination, blood pressure was 144/118 mmHg, heart rate 159 b.p.m., and oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Bibasilar crackles and moderate bilateral lower-extremity oedema were present; heart sounds were regular and tachycardic without jugular venous distension.  
Electrocardiogram showed a regular narrow-complex tachycardia consistent with typical atrial flutter with 2:1 conduction. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 15% with diffuse hypokinesia and no significant ventricular hypertrophy or dilatation (left ventricular end-diastolic

title: Hypopituitarism presenting as congestive heart failure
case_summary:
A 35-year-old woman presented with acute pulmonary edema. Two weeks earlier, she had an episode of gastroenteritis treated by her general practitioner. Afterwards she developed pronounced fatigability, syncope, and dyspnea on exertion. On arrival she was pale, cold, and clammy; pulse 110 beats/min (regular, low volume); blood pressure 92/66 mmHg. Bibasilar fine crepitations were present; an S3 gallop was heard without murmurs. She was placed in a sitting position and given high-flow oxygen, IV furosemide, digoxin, and low-dose dopamine. ECG showed sinus tachycardia with diffuse nonspecific ST-T changes; chest radiograph revealed cardiomegaly and pulmonary edema. Hemoglobin was 9.5 g/dL; liver and renal function tests were normal. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed mitral regurgitation with normal cusps and severe left ventricular systolic dysfunction (EF 25%). A diagnosis of dilated cardiomyopathy with LV failure was made and treated accordingly.

Despite resolution of pulmonary e

title: Acute liver failure and seizure: a case report of an unusual presentation of acute painless aortic dissection
case_summary:
A 36-year-old woman was found unconscious with generalized stiffening and urinary incontinence but reported no chest pain. Three weeks earlier, she had a brief gastroenteritis after eating shellfish in Vietnam. She had no family history of seizure or liver disease (including Wilson's), no acetaminophen or excessive alcohol use, and no recreational drugs.  
On examination: BP 99/75 mmHg, HR 117 bpm, RR 17/min, no respiratory distress, JVP not elevated, no jaundice or Kayser-Fleischer rings, and a hepatic flap was present. Abdominal exam revealed mild epigastric tenderness without organomegaly. Heart sounds were regular, no murmur; lungs clear; no edema. Neurologic exam: pupils equal and reactive, cranial nerves II-XII intact, full strength and sensation in all limbs.  
Initial labs showed ALT 388 U/L and AST 378 U/L with normal bilirubin, ALP, INR, and PT. Abdominal ultrasound excluded cirrhosis or biliary obstruction. Viral hepatitis serologies (A, B, C, E; EBV; CMV) and autoimmune scr

title: Lyme carditis presenting with an incessant atrioventricular nodal reentrant tachycardia masking a variable atrioventricular block
case_summary:
A 32-year-old man with no significant medical history presented with a persistent resting heart rate of up to 140 bpm. He reported intermittent bilateral knee pain and swelling but denied palpitations or syncope. Three months earlier he had found an engorged tick on his left knee; Lyme serology at that time was negative. One week after the tick bite he developed a bull's-eye rash over the posterior left knee that resolved with ibuprofen.  

On presentation he was afebrile, heart rate 143 bpm, blood pressure 122/78 mm Hg. Examination revealed only a small area of hyperpigmentation at the left knee; no rash or tick was seen. Laboratory studies, including CBC, metabolic panel, toxicology screen, and thyroid function tests, were normal. Echocardiogram showed normal ventricular function and no structural disease. Chest radiograph was unremarkable.  

Telemetry and serial ECGs were obtained. Initial ECG showed a narrow-complex tachycardia with retrograde P waves and short RP intervals, consi

title: Heart in a shell--a cascade of classical findings: a case report
case_summary:
A 58-year-old Caucasian man presented with a 1-year history of progressive exertional dyspnea and 4 months of generalized swelling that began in his lower extremities and evolved to anasarca. He reported fatigue, weakness, orthopnea, and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea but denied chest pain, cough, sputum production, or syncope. His medical history was unremarkable. He consumed eight beers daily for 30 years; he denied smoking, illicit drugs, or occupational asbestos exposure.  
On examination, blood pressure was 123/76 mmHg, heart rate 94 beats/min, respiratory rate 24/min, and oxygen saturation 90% on room air. Jugular venous pressure was elevated. Heart sounds were normal without murmurs. There was generalized anasarca. Pulmonary auscultation revealed decreased breath sounds and bilateral crackles. There were no signs of connective?tissue disease.  
Laboratory evaluation was notable only for an elevated brain natriuretic peptide of 274 pg/mL; other routine labs were within normal limit

title: Adult-onset MELAS syndrome in a 51-year-old woman without typical clinical manifestations: a case report
case_summary:
A 51-year-old woman was referred for evaluation of progressive cardiomegaly and dyspnoea on exertion. Her history was notable for dual-chamber pacemaker implantation 24 years earlier for high-degree atrioventricular block, Type 2 diabetes mellitus treated for 23 years, right thyroidectomy for papillary thyroid cancer, sensorineural hearing loss requiring hearing aids for 12 years, and a 2-year history of non-tuberculous mycobacterial lung disease on antibiotics. She had no known family history of cardiac or genetic disorders.

On examination, blood pressure was 139/75 mmHg and pulse 89 b.p.m. She appeared chronically ill but had clear lung fields and a regular heart rhythm without murmurs. Chest radiographs over 2 years demonstrated progressive cardiomegaly. Transthoracic echocardiography showed severe global left ventricular hypokinesia, chamber enlargement (LV end-diastolic dimension 56 mm), an ejection fraction of 26%, a "sparkling" myocardial texture with fine bright particles, wal

title: Case report of asymptomatic very late presentation of ALCAPA syndrome: review of the literature since pathophysiology until treatment
final_diagnosis: anomalous left coronary artery from the pulmonary artery
case_summary:
A 67-year-old woman was referred for intermittent, atypical chest pain. She had no significant past medical history except for remote smoking. On examination, blood pressure was 130/67 mmHg, heart rate 70 bpm, and a 3/6 holosystolic murmur was heard over the precordium. Electrocardiography showed sinus rhythm, normal QRS duration, and no ST-segment or T-wave abnormalities. Routine blood tests were normal.

Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a dilated right coronary artery arising from the right sinus of Valsalva and an aneurysmal left main artery arising from a dilated pulmonary artery. Doppler imaging showed diastolic flow from the right coronary artery filling the left coronary arteries via epicardial collaterals. Biventricular ejection fraction was normal, and the mitral valve was myxomatous with mild to moderate regurgitation.

Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging confirmed a mildly dilated left ventricle with an ejection fraction of 60% and showed subtle mid-wall fibrosis of th

title: Acute De Novo Multivessel Spontaneous Coronary Artery Dissection
case_summary:
A 49-year-old woman presented with worsening anginal chest pain over several weeks. Her blood pressure was 202/100 mm Hg and heart rate 102 beats/min; the rest of the physical examination was unremarkable. She had a history of hypertension and an acute coronary syndrome in 2016, when a spontaneous coronary artery dissection of an obtuse marginal branch was misdiagnosed as atherosclerotic coronary disease and treated with stenting. 

An electrocardiogram was normal, and serum troponin I was 0.93 ng/ml. Complete blood count and metabolic profile were within normal limits. She was started on heparin, aspirin, clopidogrel, a statin, a beta-blocker, and an angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitor. 

On hospital day 1, coronary angiography showed a patent obtuse marginal stent and a new spontaneous dissection of the distal right coronary artery with Thrombolysis In Myocardial Infarction (TIMI) flow grade III; her symptoms resolved, and conservative management was continued. 

On day 3, she de

title:
Mitral Valve Endocarditis in Patient Awaiting TAVI: A Case
Report
case_summary:
An 85-year-old man with severe aortic stenosis (valve area 0.8?cm?, mean gradient 47?mmHg, peak gradient 91?mmHg, peak velocity 4.76?m/s), mild mitral regurgitation, moderate mitral stenosis with annular calcification, chronic anemia requiring biweekly red blood cell transfusions and monthly IV iron for recurrent small-bowel angiodysplasias, and New York Heart Association class II-III dyspnea, was awaiting transcatheter aortic valve implantation (TAVI). He presented to a peripheral emergency department with orthopnea.  
Vital signs: blood pressure 113/54 mmHg; heart rate 93 bpm; respiratory rate 18 breaths/min; oxygen saturation 95% on 2 L/min; temperature 35.1  degreesC.  
Cardiac exam: late-peaking systolic murmur at the aortic area radiating to the carotids, absent S2. Pulmonary exam: bibasilar crackles. Extremities: 2+ bilateral pitting edema.  
Laboratory studies: white blood cell count 15.2 ? 10?/L (normal 4.0-11.0), hemoglobin 9.9 g/dL (post-2 units transfused the prior day), tr

title: Fulminant myocarditis during postoperative adjuvant chemotherapy for lung cancer with atezolizumab: a case report
case_summary:
A 49-year-old Asian woman with a history of stage IIB right upper lobe lung adenocarcinoma underwent video-assisted thoracic surgery, followed by adjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy complicated by neutropenia and dysesthesia. Ten weeks after completing platinum-based chemotherapy, she began adjuvant atezolizumab. There was no personal or family history of cardiac disease; baseline cardiac function was normal.

She was observed inpatient during the first cycle of atezolizumab (day 1-8) without issues. On day 9 she developed persistent fever (to 38  degreesC) and by day 13 she presented with a temperature of 40.3  degreesC, blood pressure 134/80 mmHg, pulse 116 bpm, WBC 4300/uL (lymphocytes 18.5%), AST 23 U/L, ALT 27 U/L, and CRP 4.76 mg/dL. Chest radiography was normal. CT of the neck showed an enlarged, tender right cervical lymph node, and subacute necrotizing lymphadenitis was diagnosed.

On day 16 she developed dyspnea on exertion, progressing to dyspnea at rest on day 17. Chest r

title: A case report on acute pulmonary edema induced by adverse drug event immediately after cesarean section
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
case_summary:
A 23-year-old G2P1 woman at 39 weeks and 6 days gestation underwent induction of labor with oxytocin followed by cesarean delivery under spinal anesthesia for fetal tachycardia. Intraoperatively, she received 2000 mL of normal saline and 30 IU of oxytocin after fetal delivery. Thirty minutes after transfer to the recovery room, her blood pressure was 80/56 mmHg, heart rate 64 bpm, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, and SpO2 on room air was 99%. One hour later, she acutely developed shortness of breath and chest discomfort. Her respiratory rate was 40 breaths/min, blood pressure 142/90 mmHg, heart rate 90 bpm, and SpO2 90% on room air. She was alert and oriented. Lung examination showed reduced breath sounds and bilateral crepitations; cardiac examination showed a regular rate without murmurs, rubs, or gallops. The remainder of the physical examination was unremarkable.  
Chest radiograph showed prominent bilateral interstitial pulmonary opacities consistent with pulmonary edema without c

title: Multisystem inflammatory syndrome in adults with cardiac engagement: a case report and literature review
case_summary:
A 38-year-old woman with a history of smoking and recurrent haematuria presented with a 3-day history of sore throat, progressive shortness of breath, lower abdominal pain, and malaise. On arrival, temperature was 37.8 degreesC, blood pressure 98/60 mmHg, heart rate 100 bpm, and oxygen saturation 100% on room air. Examination revealed general abdominal tenderness and visible haematuria. 

Electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm with inferolateral ST-segment elevations. Laboratory tests demonstrated:  
? C-reactive protein 243 mg/L (normal <3)  
? Troponin T 67 ng/L (normal <14)  
? D-dimer 1.2 mg/L (normal <0.25)  
? Leukocytes 19.5?10^9/L (normal 3.5-8.8?10^9/L)  
? NT-proBNP 2790 ng/L (normal <125)  
? SARS-CoV-2 RNA PCR from nasopharynx and serum: negative  

Given the fever, elevated CRP, and mild troponin rise, acute myocardial infarction was considered less likely, and a coronary angiogram was deferred. Initial working diagnosis was fulminant myopericarditis, possibly abdominal in

title: Massive Biventricular Thrombi in a Patient with Endometrial Adenocarcinoma Diagnosed on Computed Tomography Angiography
case_summary:
A 60-year-old woman presented with intermittent pleuritic chest pain in the mid-chest radiating to the back and exertional dyspnea. Her medical history included non-ischemic cardiomyopathy with an ejection fraction of 20%-25%, hypertension, type 2 diabetes with chronic kidney disease, a remote ischemic stroke with residual right-sided weakness, and stage IVB grade 2 endometrial adenocarcinoma with omental metastasis (post hysterectomy and salpingo-oophorectomy). Three months earlier, nuclear perfusion imaging had shown no ischemia or infarction. She was compliant with furosemide and metoprolol and had received adjuvant chemotherapy one month before presentation.  
On arrival, her systolic blood pressure was 90 mmHg; the remainder of her vital signs was unremarkable. Physical examination revealed no murmurs, rubs, or gallops, no lower-extremity edema, and clear lung fields. Laboratory studies showed a troponin I level of 0.158?ng/mL (normal, 0.02-0.03), an absolute neutrophil count of 0

title: Acute myocardial infarction not attributed to coronary artery disease: A seldom initial presentation of a left ventricular myxoma
case_summary:
A 68-year-old man was transferred for further management after an inferior wall ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction. His medical history was notable for malignant fibrous histiocytoma of the right preauricular and left jugular regions, with regional relapse and a focal lung metastasis treated surgically, as well as essential hypertension, hypercholesterolemia, type II diabetes mellitus, and tobacco use. He also had a family history of coronary artery disease. On examination, his blood pressure was 135/85 mm Hg, and cardiac auscultation was normal without murmurs.

Electrocardiography showed normal sinus rhythm and inverted T waves in leads II, III, aVF, and V3-V6. A coronary angiogram revealed no critical stenoses of the coronary arteries. On the following day, he had multiple episodes of witnessed cardiac arrest due to polymorphic ventricular tachycardia, which required defibrillation. Routine laboratory tests, including electrolyte levels, were within normal limits. Transthora

title: Endocarditis and coronary artery fistula: a case report?
case_summary:
A 50-year-old woman was referred in 2013 after an incidental systolic murmur was detected on open-access echocardiography. She was asymptomatic and had no significant medical history. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a dilated vessel arising from the right coronary cusp with turbulent colour flow into the right atrium. Cardiac CT confirmed a giant right coronary artery with a fistulous connection into the right atrium. A myocardial perfusion scan showed no ischaemia, and because of her asymptomatic status, normal biventricular function, and absence of inducible ischaemia, she was managed conservatively with routine follow-up.

Two years later, between scheduled visits, she developed several months of dyspnoea, low-grade fevers, weight loss, and lower back discomfort. On examination she was febrile and tachycardic. Laboratory tests revealed haemoglobin 64 g/L (115-165), mean corpuscular volume 78.9 fL (83-101), and C-reactive protein 142 mg/L (0-5). A chest radiograph was cle

title: Traumatic ischaemic cardiomyopathy in a 27-year-old: a case report
case_summary:
A 27-year-old man presented with several months of progressive exertional dyspnoea and lower extremity oedema that began after a motor vehicle accident 5 months earlier. He denied any preceding viral illness, sick contacts, or travel. His initial laboratory studies showed hepatic injury (aspartate aminotransferase 632?IU/L, alanine transferase 248?IU/L), a normal troponin I level of 0.04?ng/mL, and an elevated B-type natriuretic peptide at 1787?pg/mL. A transthoracic echocardiogram demonstrated a normal left ventricular chamber size (end-diastolic diameter 5.6?cm), severely reduced systolic function (ejection fraction <20%), and an apical thrombus. He was treated with intravenous diuretics with symptomatic improvement. 

On transfer, he reported that during the prior accident he had struck his chest on the steering wheel and experienced musculoskeletal chest discomfort that resolved without evaluation. Over the ensuing months he developed orthopnoea, intermittent chest tightness, and p

title: Multiple intracardiac masses: myxoma, thrombus or metastasis: a case report
case_summary:
A 49-year-old Asian woman with a 2-week history of progressive exertional dyspnea, orthopnea, bilateral lower?limb edema, and palpitations was admitted. She had mild hypertension previously but no other systemic disease. On examination, her blood pressure was 195/138 mmHg, pulse 163 beats per minute and irregular, jugular venous distention was 18 cm, bilateral basal crackles were present, and there was grade II edema of the lower limbs. Cardiac auscultation revealed an irregularly rapid rhythm without murmurs. An electrocardiogram showed atrial flutter. Chest radiography demonstrated a patch over the lower left lung field and bilateral lung infiltrates. Laboratory studies revealed leukocytosis, an elevated D-dimer (14.14 mg/L), elevated creatinine (3.11 mg/dL), high lactate dehydrogenase (1367 U/L), elevated uric acid (20.9 mg/dL), mildly elevated troponin-I (0.067 ng/mL), CK-MB (11.9 ng/mL), and B-type natriuretic peptide (704 pg/mL). 

Transthoracic echocardiography revealed three in

title: Giant left sinus of Valsalva aneurysm as a rare cause of acute myocardial infarction: a case report
case_summary:
A 55-year-old man presented to the emergency department with worsening shortness of breath and retrosternal chest pain radiating to the left arm and jaw. He had a history of hypercholesterolaemia, prior spinal surgery after traumatic neck injury, a remote COVID-19 infection, and was an ex-smoker.

On examination, he was tachycardic (heart rate 120 b.p.m.), hypoxic (oxygen saturation 80% on room air), and tachypnoeic, with a blood pressure of 110/70 mmHg. Chest auscultation revealed normal heart sounds without murmurs and bilateral crepitations. 

The preliminary working diagnosis was a late-presenting STEMI complicated by congestive heart failure. Mechanical complications of myocardial infarction, including acute mitral regurgitation due to papillary muscle rupture and ventricular septal defect, were also considered.

An electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm with upsloping ST-segment elevation, Q waves, and poor R-wave progression in the anterior leads. High-sensitivity troponin I was

title: Two zebras and a cardiac arrest: a case report of concomitant Brugada syndrome and an anomalous coronary artery
case_summary: A 55-year-old man with hypertension had an out-of-hospital cardiac arrest while at rest watching television. He had no preceding symptoms or family history of sudden cardiac death. His wife began CPR immediately. Emergency responders found ventricular fibrillation, delivered two shocks, and achieved return of circulation; he was intubated and transferred to our centre. On arrival his physical examination was unremarkable. Initial laboratory studies showed a potassium of 3.1?mmol/L (reference 3.5-5.3), which was corrected, and troponin I levels of 573 and 303?ng/L (<21). Noncontrast head CT was normal. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed mild left ventricular hypertrophy with normal systolic function, no regional wall motion abnormalities, and no pericardial or valvular pathology. His initial ECG showed sinus tachycardia, a QTc of 490?ms, 1?mm ST depression in leads II, III, aVF, and V6, and subtle, variable <0.5?mm ST?elevation in lead aVR. One hour later, inferolateral ST changes

title: Diagnosis of post-myocardial infarction left ventricular rupture on CT
case_summary:
A 70-year-old woman with treated hypertension and osteoarthritis collapsed while out walking her dog. Bystander CPR for 10 minutes was followed by paramedic-confirmed pulseless electrical activity (PEA). After 20 minutes of CPR and intubation, return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) was achieved. In transit, her blood pressure was 61/10 mm Hg, and after 4 minutes of renewed CPR she had a second ROSC. On arrival, she was bradycardic (30 bpm) and hypotensive. After another 20-minute resuscitation, she regained a pulse and was started on noradrenaline and adrenaline infusions.  
Collateral history noted a mild nocturnal heartburn the evening before but no chest pain. A 12-lead ECG showed ST-segment depression in leads V1-V4 and ST elevation in V6, concerning for an inferobasal myocardial infarction. Posterior leads could not be obtained due to instability. She was deemed unsuitable for thrombolysis but accepted for transfer for possible PCI.  
Laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 60 g/

title: A case report of a giant coronary artery aneurysm masquerading as a ventricular mass
case_summary:
A 55-year-old man with chronic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, hypertension, and diabetes mellitus presented with acute hypoxic respiratory failure. He was afebrile, blood pressure 84/45 mm Hg, heart rate 67 bpm, and oxygen saturation 90% on room air. Examination revealed 1+ pitting edema in the lower extremities, fine bibasilar crackles, and a 3/6 holosystolic murmur at the apex. A CT angiogram with PE protocol showed pulmonary edema, severe coronary atherosclerotic disease, and calcification in the right ventricle. Cardiac troponin T was elevated at 2245 ng/L (normal ?22 ng/L), and the ECG demonstrated ST elevations in the anterolateral leads with poor R-wave progression.

Transthoracic echocardiography estimated an LVEF of 45% with inferior and inferolateral wall hypokinesis and revealed an immobile cystic mass in the right ventricular apex and interventricular septum. Coronary catheterization showed marked aneurysmal dilation of the left main and left anterior descend

title: Chronic anemia complicated by cardiac failure, pulmonary hypertension, and pericardial effusion: a case report
case_summary:
A 44-year-old Nepalese man was referred for management of severe anemia. He had no significant past medical history, took no medications, and denied smoking or alcohol use. He reported 10 days of fatigability and swollen ankles, without fever, night sweats, weight loss, cough, dyspnea, chest pain, or palpitations. He noted a single episode of a small amount of fresh rectal bleeding 2 days prior.  
On examination, blood pressure was 120/56 mmHg, pulse rate 86/min, respiratory rate 19/min, temperature 36.7? degreesC, and oxygen saturation 100% on room air. He appeared markedly pale with clubbing and mild bilateral pedal edema. Heart sounds were normal with a systolic murmur at the tricuspid area. There was no lymphadenopathy.  
Laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 1.8?g/dL, hematocrit 8%, mean corpuscular volume 58?fL, normal white-cell and platelet counts, and normal CRP, haptoglobin, and liver and renal function tests. Iron studies showed ferritin 4.2??g/L, serum iron 2??mol/L, total

title: Prominent myocardial bridging as a first clue to recognize haemorrhagic shock during percutaneous coronary intervention: a case report
case_summary:
A 73-year-old man with stable angina pectoris was admitted for percutaneous coronary intervention of the proximal left anterior descending (LAD) artery. He had hypertension, was an ex-smoker, and had undergone bypass surgery 5 years earlier, but all grafts were now occluded. Blood pressure was 134/80 mm?Hg, heart rate 72 beats/min, and physical examination--including jugular venous pressure, lung and heart auscultation, and leg inspection--was unremarkable. Echocardiography showed a left ventricular ejection fraction of 59%, normal wall motion, and no hypertrophy.

Coronary angiography revealed 75% stenosis of the left main trunk and 90% calcified stenosis of the proximal LAD; myocardial bridging was not evident. A transfemoral approach was performed under ultrasound guidance, with a 7 Fr sheath in the right common femoral artery. After rotational atherectomy, an everolimus-eluting stent was implanted from the left main trunk to the proximal LAD. During stent deployment, his blood pres

title: Primary cardiac lymphoma presenting with cardiac tamponade and complete heart block: case report
case_summary:
An 80-year-old Caucasian woman presented with acute chest pain and one week of progressive dyspnea on exertion on a six-month background of fatigue. Her medical history included hyperlipidemia, hypertension, and depression; medications were a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor, a statin, an antimuscarinic agent, a proton-pump inhibitor, and calcium supplementation. She had no history of autoimmune disease, malignancy, tuberculosis, or HIV.

On examination, she had bilateral peripheral edema, bibasal crackles, and mild epigastric tenderness. Blood pressure was normal and heart sounds were unremarkable without added sounds; there was no jugular venous distension.

Electrocardiography revealed complete heart block with small QRS complexes. CT pulmonary angiography was negative for pulmonary embolism but showed a large pericardial effusion (>3 cm depth), small to moderate bilateral pleural effusions, and two enlarged mediastinal lymph nodes. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated

title: Perimyocarditis following first dose of the mRNA-1273 SARS-CoV-2 (Moderna) vaccine in a healthy young male: a case report
case_summary:
A 22-year-old man presented with sharp, substernal, nonradiating chest pain for two days. Three days earlier, he had received his first dose of the mRNA-1273 (Moderna) COVID-19 vaccine, after which he experienced body aches and subjective fever. He described his chest pain as occasionally pleuritic and worsening, prompting an emergency department visit. He had a mild COVID-19 infection six months earlier and took no regular medications. He denied tobacco, alcohol, or drug use. On examination, blood pressure was 97/57 mm Hg, heart rate 100 bpm, respiratory rate 16/min, oxygen saturation 99% on room air, and temperature 98.6 degreesF. He was tachycardic without murmurs, rubs, or gallops; there was no jugular venous distention or chest-wall tenderness.

Electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm with diffuse ST-segment elevations and PR depressions in leads V3-V6. High-sensitivity troponin was 13 702 ng/L (normal 3-20 ng/L); B-type natriuretic peptide was 48 pg/mL (normal < 100 pg/mL).

title: A case report of refractory multivessel coronary spasm associated with hypereosinophilic syndrome: one cell, one disease?
case_summary:
A 55-year-old woman with a history of bronchial asthma, allergic rhinitis, chronic sinusitis with nasal polyps, aspirin-sensitive asthma, and urticaria presented with a 1-week history of anosmia, cough, and dyspnoea. Laboratory testing showed mild eosinophilia (eosinophil count, 534/?L [9.7%]; reference range, 0-520/?L [0-8.5%]). She had been on multiple anti-allergic medications for 10 years. Five months later, she developed rest chest pain radiating from the left upper extremity to the cervical region in the morning and evening, which worsened over time. 

One month after onset, she was hospitalized for chest pain. During an episode, ECG showed ST-segment elevation in leads II, III, and aVF with reciprocal changes. Emergency coronary angiography revealed focal 75-90% narrowing of the proximal and distal right coronary artery and proximal left anterior descending artery, which resolved with intracoronary nitroglycerin, consistent with coronary vasospasm. She was diagnosed with vasospa

title: Myxomatous degeneration of aortic valve causing severe aortic regurgitation. A rare case report
final_diagnosis: Myxomatous degeneration of the aortic valve
case_summary:
A 37-year-old man presented with three months of progressive dyspnea. One month earlier, he was hospitalized for heart failure and found to have aortic regurgitation. He denied any history of rheumatic fever and had no symptoms or signs of rheumatic heart disease or infective endocarditis. He reported no fever. His Anti?streptolysin O titer and C?reactive protein level were normal. There were no peripheral stigmata of endocarditis, such as Osler's nodes or Janeway lesions. He is 174 cm tall and weighs 70 kg. He has no wrist or thumb signs of Marfan's syndrome, no hyperflexible skin or facial features of Ehlers-Danlos, and no hypertelorism, cleft palate, or aneurysmal features associated with Loeys-Dietz syndrome.

On examination, blood pressure was 130/40 mm Hg in the right arm, heart rate 90 beats/min, with bounding peripheral pulses. Cardiac auscultation revealed an early diastolic murmur (grade 3/6) at the aortic area. ECG showed features of left ventricular volume overload. Transth

title: Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging findings in primary arrhythmogenic left ventricular cardiomyopathy with cardiocutaneous phenotype--Carvajal syndrome
case_summary: A 47-year-old man with newly diagnosed heart failure was referred for further evaluation. He reported one episode of presyncope preceded by palpitations and another of isolated palpitations; he remained active with good exercise tolerance. His history included well-controlled type 2 diabetes, hyperlipidemia, and obstructive sleep apnea; he was a recent ex-smoker with a 30-pack-year history. Family history was notable for four brothers with woolly hair and palmoplantar keratoderma, two of whom died suddenly (one confirmed ARVC on autopsy), and a father who died suddenly without cutaneous features. Examination revealed woolly hair and callused palms and soles; cardiac examination was unremarkable. Transthoracic echocardiography showed mild-to-moderate global left ventricular hypokinesis with an ejection fraction of 40-45% and a normal right ventricle. Nuclear myocardial perfusion imaging demonstrated a large inferior fixed defect with regional hypokinesis, but coronary angiography showed

title: Taken to heart--arrhythmic potential of heart-leaf sida, a banned ephedrine alkaloid: a case report
case_summary:
A 65-year-old man with idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis status post right single lung transplant 14 months earlier and sick sinus syndrome with a dual-chamber pacemaker presented with several months of dizziness and a persistent resting heart rate of ~120 b.p.m. He denied fever, chest pain, dyspnea, or palpitations. His medications included immunosuppression (cyclosporine, mycophenolate mofetil, prednisone), prophylaxis (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole, valganciclovir), atorvastatin, tiotropium-olodaterol inhaler, and furosemide. On detailed review, he also reported taking an over-the-counter herbal supplement marketed for "natural stress relief" for several months, which had not been previously disclosed.

Pacemaker interrogation demonstrated supraventricular tachycardia at 120 b.p.m. A 12-lead ECG showed a narrow?complex, long R-P tachycardia at 117 b.p.m. with upright P-waves in lead V1 and biphasic (+/-) P-waves in the inferior leads.

Based on the P-wave morphology and vector, the diff

title: Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy Coexisting with Acute Pericarditis and Myocardial Bridge
case_summary:
A 42-year-old Iranian woman presented to the emergency department with a sharp, persistent pleuritic chest pain that began after an emotional stressful event. She had no notable past medical history or coronary risk factors.

On examination, her heart rate was 85 beats per minute and blood pressure was 120/80 mm Hg. Cardiac auscultation revealed normal S1 and S2, an S3 gallop, and a triphasic friction rub (one systolic and two diastolic components). 

An initial electrocardiogram demonstrated 1-2 mm of ST-segment elevation in leads I, II, III, aVF, and V4-V6, with ST-segment depression and PR-segment elevation in aVR. Transthoracic echocardiography on admission showed apical akinesia with preserved basal function, mild pericardial effusion, an ejection fraction of 40%, and moderate mitral regurgitation. 

Laboratory tests revealed mildly elevated cardiac enzymes: creatine kinase-MB of 30 ng/mL (normal < 25 ng/mL) and troponin I of 0.89 ng/mL (normal < 0.01 ng/mL).

title: Primary intracardiac leiomyoma: rare case report and literature review
case_summary:
A 60-year-old woman with a history of permanent pacemaker implantation 8 years earlier (switched off 2 years ago) and active smoking underwent a routine occupational checkup. Electrocardiography showed atrial fibrillation with intermittent dizziness and episodes of ventricular tachycardia. She denied chest pain, dyspnea, or other cardiac symptoms. 

On examination, she was afebrile and hemodynamically stable (BP 116/86 mmHg; HR 77 bpm; RR 20/min; SpO? 96% on room air). Cardiovascular and pulmonary exams were unremarkable. 

Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a large, oval, pedunculated, nonmobile mass with smooth margins attached to the apex and occupying most of the left ventricular cavity, causing obstruction of the left ventricular outflow tract and interfering with mitral valve function. Left ventricular systolic dysfunction was present (LVEF 20%). The atria and all other valves were normal; no pericardial effusion was seen. 

Coronary angiography showed normal coronary arteri

title: Nonconstrictive epicarditis mimicking a cardiac mass in a 71-year-old Caucasian man: a case report and review of the literature
case_summary:
A 71-year-old man with type 2 diabetes, coronary artery disease, atrial fibrillation, chronic pericardial effusion, and hypothyroidism presented with 5 weeks of progressive fatigue and 1 day of dizziness. He was afebrile, blood pressure 140/90 mmHg, heart rate irregularly irregular at 102 bpm, respiratory rate 16 breaths per minute, and no pulsus paradoxus. Examination showed pale conjunctiva but no jugular venous distention, murmurs, or rubs. There was no edema, hepatosplenomegaly, or lymphadenopathy.  

Laboratory studies revealed a white blood cell count of 12,900/L, hemoglobin 7.2 g/dL, hematocrit 23.4%, microcytic indices, and iron deficiency; liver function tests and LDH were normal. TSH was 16.49 mcIU/mL with a free T3 of 57 mcg/mL. He was admitted for anemia, received three units of packed red blood cells, and underwent upper endoscopy, which showed a 30 ? 17 mm polypoid mass in the gastric cardia. Biopsy revealed T1N0M0 gastric adenocarcinoma.  

To evaluate his known 8-month

title: Surgical repair of a giant congenital right atrial aneurysm concomitant with Wolff?Parkinson?White syndrome: A case report and literature review
case_summary:
A 35-year-old man with a 20-year history of tachycardia and palpitations presented with worsening symptoms despite daily sotalol therapy. On admission, his pulse rate was 110-160 beats per minute, blood pressure 96/68 mmHg, respiratory rate 15/min, and temperature normal. Electrocardiography showed a short PR interval and a delta wave, consistent with Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome. He received sotalol and underwent direct-current cardioversion, but recurrent tachyarrhythmia prompted further evaluation.

Chest radiography showed an elevated cardiothoracic ratio due to marked right atrial enlargement. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a normal left ventricle with an ejection fraction of 50%, normal right ventricular size but severe right ventricular systolic dysfunction, and a markedly enlarged right atrium compressing both ventricles; there was no significant valvular disease except a mildly increased tricuspid valve gradient (mean 3 mmHg, peak 8 mmHg) and a localized pericardial

title: Extensive cardiovascular involvement in a young boy with Gaucher's disease: a case report
case_summary:
A 19-year-old Iranian man presented with a 2-month history of dyspnea on exertion (NYHA Class II). He had a past history of hypothyroidism treated with levothyroxine. There were no known familial genetic disorders. On examination, his height was 135 cm and weight 53 kg; vital signs were normal. Cardiac auscultation revealed a III/VI mid-systolic, high-pitched crescendo-decrescendo murmur at the base consistent with aortic stenosis, and a IV/VI holosystolic murmur at the apex in the left lateral decubitus position suggestive of mitral regurgitation. Pulses were symmetrical. Abdominal examination showed a spleen palpable 4 cm below the costal margin.

Laboratory studies demonstrated thrombocytopenia (platelet count 62 000 ? 10^3/uL), with normal red and white cell counts and normal thyroid function. ECG showed normal sinus rhythm with high-voltage QRS complexes and secondary ST-T changes, consistent with left ventricular hypertrophy.

Transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) and transesophag

title: Right atrial appendage tachycardia: A rare cause of tachycardia induced cardiomyopathy in a 4-year-old child
case_summary: A 4-year-old girl from Kenya, weighing 18 kg, presented with 6 months of persistent pounding of the precordium and clinical features of heart failure despite treatment with diuretics, digoxin, and beta-blockers. On examination she had tachycardia and signs of low cardiac output. Transthoracic echocardiography showed a dilated heart with severe left ventricular dysfunction; no obvious structural cause was identified. A 12-lead ECG revealed a regular narrow-QRS tachycardia at 280 beats per minute, a RP interval of 90 ms, and upright P waves in the inferior leads. Administration of adenosine resulted in continuation of the tachycardia with transient atrioventricular block, seen as rapid P waves without QRS complexes. The differential diagnosis at this point included atrial tachycardia with 1:1 atrioventricular conduction and clockwise atrial flutter. Medical therapy failed to control the arrhythmia or improve heart failure, leading to consideration of radiofrequency ablation.

title: Fever of Unknown Origin as a Sole Presentation of Subacute Thyroiditis in an Elderly Patient: A Case Report with Literature Review
case_summary:
An 80-year-old Caucasian man presented with a 3-week history of intermittent fevers up to 101.4 degreesF with chills, occipital headache, and recurrent falls. He denied neck pain, rash, visual changes, photophobia, phonophobia, chest pain apart from stable angina managed with sublingual nitroglycerin, palpitations, cough, or weight loss. His medical history included coronary artery disease, prior stroke, patent foramen ovale repair, type 2 diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, benign prostatic hyperplasia, and gout. Medications were aspirin, atorvastatin, finasteride, and tamsulosin.

On examination he was febrile (101.4 degreesF), blood pressure 162/80 mm Hg, pulse 110/min, respiratory rate 18/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Cardiovascular exam showed sinus tachycardia and a grade 2/6 systolic ejection murmur; the thyroid was not enlarged or tender and there was no lymphadenopathy or rash. Neurologic and other system examinations were unremarkable.

Initial investigation

title: Cardiac troponin I elevation with supraventricular tachycardia: two case reports and review of the literature
case_summary: A 64-year-old Chinese man was admitted with chest pain and palpitations of 45 minutes' duration that resolved on arrival. He had no history of hypertension, diabetes, or known heart disease. Physical examination was unremarkable. An electrocardiogram showed ST-segment depression greater than 1.0 mm in leads V3 through V6. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed no abnormalities. On admission, cardiac troponin I was 0.09 ng/ml (normal < 0.08 ng/ml), increased to 0.52 ng/ml on hospital day 2, and returned to the normal range by day 3. He was treated for presumed acute coronary syndrome with aspirin, clopidogrel, low-molecular-weight heparin, and atorvastatin. Coronary angiography was scheduled one week after admission.

title: Cardiogenic shock as the first manifestation of large vessel vasculitis in a young patient: case report
case_summary: A 33-year-old woman presented with 1 month of asthenia, nonproductive cough, and chest pain and was initially treated as acute bronchitis. Three days later, she returned with worsening stabbing chest pain, pleuritic but exertion-related and improving at rest, associated with exertional and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea. Vital signs were: blood pressure 112/64 mmHg, heart rate 116 bpm, respiratory rate 19/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Examination showed no murmurs, fine bilateral crackles at the lung bases, no abdominal abnormalities, and no lower extremity edema. A D-dimer and chest X-ray were normal. ECG showed 2-mm ST depression in precordial leads. High-sensitivity troponin I rose from 0.06 to 2.25 ng/mL (normal 0-0.026). C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate were within normal limits. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a severely dilated left ventricle with mid-ventricular and apical dyskinesia, an ejection fraction of 20%, diastolic dysfunction, s

title: Polymer Emboli Complicating Transcatheter Aortic Valve Implantation
case_summary:
A 92-year-old woman with hypertension and type 2 diabetes was admitted with acute pulmonary edema and presyncope. Initial blood pressure was 80/44 mm Hg, heart rate 115 beats/min, with bilateral basal crackles and a crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur at the aortic area radiating to the carotids. Electrocardiography met criteria for left ventricular hypertrophy. Transthoracic echocardiography showed an ejection fraction of 55% and a heavily calcified aortic valve with critical stenosis (valve area 0.3 cm2; peak/mean gradient 139/89 mm Hg). Baseline creatinine was 105 ?mol/L. A limited?contrast coronary angiogram revealed calcifications but no flow?limiting lesions.  

Because of refractory cardiogenic shock and high surgical risk, the heart team performed emergent TAVI via a 14-F eSheath and deployed a 23-mm Sapien-3 valve without complications. On day 3 postprocedure, she developed severe abdominal pain, acute kidney injury, and blue discoloration of the left foot. Contrast CT of th

title: Takotsubo syndrome in a cancer patient treated with a combination of anti-cancer drugs including immune checkpoint inhibitors: a case report
case_summary:
A 66-year-old woman with recurrent lung cancer (on osimertinib for 2 years 9 months) had completed four courses of atezolizumab with bevacizumab, paclitaxel, and carboplatin, and was on maintenance atezolizumab plus bevacizumab. She experienced strong malaise after her last chemotherapy and emotional stress 4 days before presentation. Three days before arrival, she noted progressive dyspnea that progressed to orthopnea by the time of presentation.

On admission: BP 102/62 mmHg; HR 98 bpm; RR 22/min; O2 saturation 96% on 1 L nasal cannula. Physical exam showed slight leg edema, no jugular venous distention, and clear heart sounds. Chest X-ray revealed pulmonary congestion and a cardiothoracic ratio of 58%. ECG demonstrated poor R-wave progression in the precordial leads and T-wave inversions in leads II, III, and aVF.

Laboratory tests: CK 164 U/L (59-248); BNP 1305.6 pg/mL (<18.4); troponin I 284.5 pg/mL (<34.2); CRP 2.4 mg/dL; BUN 24.0 mg/dL; creatinine 0.91 mg/dL; eGFR 47.8 mL/min/1.

title: Long-term disease-free survival of patients with primary cardiac lymphoma treated with systemic chemotherapy and radiotherapy
case_summary: A 59-year-old man presented with a 1-month history of intermittent chest pain and exertional dyspnea. He had no past medical history, no risk factors for cardiac disease, and no family history of cardiac disease or malignancy. Vital signs were normal: blood pressure 121/79 mmHg, pulse 76/min, respiratory rate 18/min, temperature 36.4 degreesC. Cardiac examination showed a slightly accelerated regular rhythm and a fixed splitting of S2 without murmur. Lung and abdominal examinations were unremarkable; there was no lymphadenopathy or peripheral edema. Laboratory studies revealed WBC 8,500/uL, hemoglobin 14.4 g/dL, platelets 352,000/uL, negative viral serologies (HBsAg/Ab, HCV, HIV), and an elevated LDH of 248 IU/L. Chest radiograph showed mild cardiomegaly. Electrocardiography demonstrated first-degree atrioventricular block, right axis deviation, and possible right ventricular hypertrophy. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a 6?4 cm nonmobile ovoid mass with a broad base along the

title: Refractory ventricular tachycardia storm associated with severe hypokalemia in Fanconi syndrome
case_summary:
A 52-year-old woman was brought to the emergency department after a syncopal episode. Her medical history included Fanconi syndrome with renal tubular acidosis (stage IV chronic kidney disease) and primary biliary cirrhosis; she had no known cardiovascular disease. She had taken naproxen and ibuprofen for catarrhal symptoms. En route, telemetry showed ventricular ectopy, and the initial ECG demonstrated polymorphic ventricular tachycardia, a QTc of approximately 500 ms, and no evidence of ischemia.

Over the next 7 hours, she had continuous runs of polymorphic VT and ventricular fibrillation requiring approximately 150 external defibrillations. Episodes were initiated by short-coupled premature ventricular complexes of multiple morphologies and by sinus bradycardia; sinus rhythm returned only briefly.

Initial laboratory studies showed:  
? Potassium 2.5 mmol/L  
? Bicarbonate 13 mmol/L  
? Chloride 122 mmol/L  
? BUN 31 mg/dL  
? Creatinine 3.77 mg/dL  
? Magnesium 2.5 mg/dL  
? Calci

title: Severe functional ischaemic mitral regurgitation: is functional a misnomer for a dysfunctional valve? A case report
case_summary:
A 63-year-old man with ischaemic cardiomyopathy and New York Heart Association class IV symptoms, on maximal medical therapy, was referred after 22 days in an intensive care unit for inability to wean from dobutamine support (INTERMACS III), with evidence of low cardiac output (acute on chronic kidney injury, hepatic dysfunction, and respiratory failure). 

On transfer, he was bed-bound, afebrile, blood pressure 100/60 mmHg, heart rate 90 beats/min (on dobutamine), and oxygen saturation 95% on 40% FiO2 by facemask. Cardiovascular examination revealed a 2/6 apical systolic murmur and signs of volume overload with low output. 

His history included a myocardial infarction at age 48 (managed conservatively), coronary artery bypass grafting with left internal mammary artery grafts and left ventricular aneurysmectomy 6 months earlier, stage IV chronic kidney disease (peak creatinine 3.0 mg/dL, eGFR 21 mL/min/1.73 m?), and obesity (BMI 30.4 kg/m?). The most recent angiogram before surgery sh

title: Two cases of culture proven Mycobacterium tuberculosis presenting with a broad-complex tachycardia and non-caseating granulomas
case_summary:
A 38-year-old man from India presented with palpitations and chest pain.   
  
Vital signs and initial studies:  
? Electrocardiogram: ventricular tachycardia with 1 mm ST elevation in leads V1, V2, and aVL.  
? Troponin-I: 0.27 ?g/L.  
  
He was cardioverted with intravenous amiodarone and taken for primary PCI, which showed normal, right?dominant coronary arteries.  
   
Echocardiography revealed a mildly dilated left ventricle with basal anteroseptal akinesis and moderate systolic impairment. A CMR scan demonstrated pericardial enhancement and an enlarged mediastinal lymph node.  
   
CT of the thorax, abdomen, and pelvis showed right apical scarring with pretracheal and subcarinal lymphadenopathy.  
   
An endomyocardial biopsy was inconclusive, with "no features of myocarditis or granulomas," and acid?fast bacilli cultures were negative.  
   
He developed a global pericardial effusion requiring pericardiocentesis; fluid analysis was negative for bacteria, fungi, AFB, malignant ce

title: Primary Extramedullary Plasmacytoma of the Heart: A Rare Manifestation of Plasmacellular Tumor
case_summary: A 62-year-old man with no smoking history was admitted with several weeks of paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, arthralgia, and weakness. On examination, he was hemodynamically stable, with a pulmonary ejection murmur and an accentuated pulmonic second heart sound; heart rate was sinus tachycardia. There was no lymphadenopathy. Laboratory studies showed mild elevations in IgG (1398 mg/dL; normal 543-1360) and IgA (280 mg/dL; normal 58-259), with normal 24 h urine protein (115 mg), ESR (13 mm/h), and CRP (0.6 mg/dL). Chest radiography revealed a mildly enlarged cardiac silhouette, signs of pulmonary hypertension, congestion, and small bilateral pleural effusions. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a large right atrial mass and minimal pericardial effusion, with normal biventricular function. Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest confirmed a 3.2 cm ? 2.8 cm right atrial myocardial mass involving the auricle and atrial septum, without lymphadenopathy or osteolytic lesions. Transesophageal

title: Late leaflet dehiscence in a bovine bioprosthesis-mimicked COVID-19 infection
case_summary: A 71-year-old man with hypertension underwent implantation of a 25-mm Carpentier-Edwards Perimount bovine pericardial aortic bioprosthesis in 2008 for aortic stenosis. At a routine evaluation in May 2020, the transvalvular velocity was 3.3 m/s, there was no aortic regurgitation, left ventricular ejection fraction was 57%, and diastolic diameter was 50 mm. In September 2020, he presented with several days of progressive dyspnea, fever, cough, and chest pain. On examination, temperature was 39.0  degreesC, blood pressure 145/60 mmHg, pulmonary rhonchi were present, and no cardiac murmur was heard. Laboratory tests showed a white-cell count of 9.4 ? 10^9/L (reference < 8.3), a procalcitonin level of 0.33 ug/L (reference < 0.5), and an N-terminal pro-brain natriuretic peptide level of 7220 ng/L (reference < 194). Chest CT demonstrated ground-glass opacities and consolidation in the right lung, with no pulmonary emboli. Multislice CT coronary angiography showed no coronary obstructions. SAR

title: Unusual cause of chest pain, Bornholm disease, a forgotten entity; case report and review of literature
case_summary:
A 68-year-old woman was admitted with a 2-day history of acute onset, progressively worsening sharp epigastric and left-sided "gripping" chest pain of severe intensity. The pain was pleuritic in nature, worsened by deep breaths, had no radiation, and was not relieved by positioning. She denied trauma, recent travel, or similar symptoms in family members. Her medical history included hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and hypothyroidism; medications were aspirin, amlodipine, lovastatin, and levothyroxine.

On examination, she appeared uncomfortable, clutching her left chest and epigastrium. Blood pressure was normal; heart rate was 105 beats per minute; temperature 99.9 degreesF; and oxygen saturation 97% on room air. The pain was not reproducible with palpation, and there was no rash. Lung examination revealed a left-sided pleural rub; the right lung and the remainder of the examination were unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed a white blood cell count of 9.4 ? 10^3/uL with 54% neutroph

title: Transient central hypoxemia due to intermittent high-degree atrioventricular block in a heart-transplanted patient diagnosed during routine electroencephalography: a case report
case_summary:
A Swiss man in his 50s was hospitalized after a fall at home caused by recurrent short-lived episodes of generalized weakness, which over 8 years had increased to three or four times weekly and led to pelvic-ring fractures. He denied loss of consciousness, memory gaps, post-event confusion, tongue biting, or incontinence; there were no chest pain, dyspnea, or palpitations. His medical history included an orthotopic heart transplantation in 1991 for idiopathic dilated cardiomyopathy, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation (not anticoagulated), chronic immunosuppression with cyclosporin and mycophenolate mofetil, chronic kidney disease on dialysis, and a right pneumonectomy in 2006 for pulmonary carcinoma.  
Laboratory studies showed creatinine 513 ?mol/L, urea 8.6 mmol/L, and hemoglobin 86 g/L; electrolytes, thyroid function, and inflammatory markers were normal. Protein electrophoresis and immunofixation were unremarkable. Radiographs revealed nondisplaced fractures of the right ischiopubic an

title: Management of Pheochromocytoma in The Setting of Acute Stroke
case_summary:
A 53-year-old woman who suffered an ischemic stroke 13 days earlier was transferred for neurologic care. She had persistent light-headedness, left hemiparesis, aphasia, and cognitive impairment. Her medical history included hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus, supraventricular tachycardia, and coronary artery disease; she was taking a beta-blocker and hydralazine. On transfer, her blood pressure was 220/120 mm Hg. CT angiography showed hemorrhagic conversion of a left anterior and middle cerebral artery infarct with edema and midline shift. Continuous EEG monitoring revealed poor function over the infarcted hemisphere without seizures.

On hospital day 1, a nicardipine infusion (2.5 mg/hour) and labetalol (200 mg every 8 hours) were started, but she remained hypertensive (178/81 mm Hg) and developed an episode of supraventricular tachycardia treated with hydralazine (10-20 mg as needed) and metoprolol (5 mg as needed). By day 3, increasing labetalol to 300 mg every 8 hours and nicar

title: Caseous calcification of the mitral annulus presenting with symptomatic complete heart block
final_diagnosis: Caseous calcification of the mitral annulus
case_summary:
A 63-year-old man with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and obesity presented with worsening chest discomfort, dizziness, and palpitations. On examination he was euvolemic, with dual heart sounds and no murmurs. The electrocardiogram showed complete atrioventricular dissociation with a sinus rate of 100 beats per minute and a ventricular escape rate of 36 beats per minute; the QRS width was 100 ms. Serum troponin was not elevated, routine blood chemistry was within normal limits, and he was biochemically euthyroid.

Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a normal left ventricular ejection fraction (55-60%) and an echogenic mass measuring 3.1 ? 3.0 cm on the mitral annulus extending into the basal septum, with mild mitral leaflet thickening, mild regurgitation, and no stenosis.

Transesophageal echocardiography showed a well-circumscribed circular intramyocardial mass in the basal posterior medial aspect of the left ventricle extending into the posterior mitral annulus, measuring 2.5 ? 2

title: Hyepereosiniphilic syndrome and COVID-19: 2 case reports
case_summary:
A 27-year-old woman presented with gradually worsening dyspnea at rest and fatigue over several weeks, accompanied by nausea, poor appetite, and palpitations. She had a 10-year history of controlled asthma on inhaled corticosteroids. On examination, blood pressure was 100/60 mm Hg, heart rate 103 bpm, respiratory rate 30/min, temperature 36.7  degreesC, and oxygen saturation 96% on room air. Jugular venous pressure was elevated. Cardiac auscultation revealed a systolic murmur at the lower left sternal border and apex. Breath sounds were diminished at lung bases, and there was bilateral pitting edema of the lower limbs.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 12.7 g/dL, platelets 109 ? 10^9/L, and a white-cell count of 13.5 ? 10^9/L with 31% eosinophils (absolute eosinophil count 4.2 ? 10^9/L). Chest radiography demonstrated bilateral pleural effusions. Analysis of pleural fluid yielded a transudative profile. Spirometry revealed mixed obstructive and restrictive patterns. Transthoracic e

title: Hamartoma of mature cardiomyocytes presenting with atypical angina, 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose positron emission tomography uptake, and myocardial bridging: a case report
case_summary:
A 27-year-old woman presented with atypical chest pain that had worsened over recent weeks. Her medical and family histories were unremarkable. An electrocardiogram and serial troponin assays were not suggestive of acute coronary syndrome. Contrast-enhanced chest CT angiography excluded pulmonary embolism and aortic dissection. Transthoracic echocardiography showed marked interventricular septal hypertrophy (24 mm) without left ventricular outflow tract obstruction. A 24-hour Holter monitor demonstrated sinus rhythm with normal atrioventricular conduction and a normal corrected QT interval.

Cardiac MRI revealed a well-circumscribed mass in the interventricular septum measuring 42 ? 32 ? 50 mm, bulging into the right ventricle within an overall thickened septum. On MRI, the lesion was isointense on T1 with a hypointense central core; on T2-STIR it was inhomogeneous and slightly hypointense; early gadolinium enhancement was inhomogeneous, with intralesional late enhancement. To distingu

title: Late gadolinium enhancement in mid infero-septal area after left bundle branch area pacing in the setting of bifascicular block and syncope: a case report
case_summary:
A 60-year-old woman with a history of anterior myocardial infarction treated with stenting of the proximal left anterior descending artery in September 2018, poorly controlled type II diabetes (HbA1c 9%), and dyslipidemia began experiencing recurrent episodes of syncope in August 2023. On initial evaluation, her cardiorespiratory examination was unremarkable. A 12-lead ECG demonstrated a bifascicular block, and she underwent implantation of a dual-chamber pacemaker with leads positioned in the right atrium and in the interventricular septum targeting the left bundle branch; no cardiac magnetic resonance imaging was performed prior to device implantation.

In February 2024, she was admitted with suspected ST-elevation myocardial infarction after poorly defined chest discomfort, intermittent dyspnea, and syncope. On arrival, she no longer reported chest pain, and the cardiac and pulmonary examination remained normal. ECG revealed a paced ventricular rhythm with narrow QRS complexes, but

title: Euglycemic Diabetic Ketoacidosis in a Lung Cancer Patient Using Empagliflozin
case_summary:
A 58-year-old man with a history of stage 4 lung adenocarcinoma, type 2 diabetes mellitus, and hypertension was brought to the emergency department by his oncologist one day after starting chemotherapy because of generalized weakness and dyspnea. He had completed five weeks of radiation therapy and had a newly placed chemotherapy port.

Review of systems revealed weakness, shortness of breath, decreased appetite, polydipsia, polyuria, and chronic leg swelling. He denied fever, chest pain, cough, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, abdominal pain, dysuria, weight loss, and sick contacts.

Home medications included empagliflozin 10 mg once daily, insulin aspart, insulin lispro, sitagliptin, dexamethasone, and supportive therapies.

On examination, temperature was 97.3  degreesF, blood pressure 76/50 mmHg, heart rate 103 beats/min, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 100% on room air. He appeared lethargic yet was oriented. Cardiovascular examination showed tachycardia with an

title: A case of transfusion-related acute lung injury induced by anti-human leukocyte antigen antibodies in acute leukemia
case_summary: A 79-year-old man with a history of unstable angina treated with coronary stent, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and a 50 pack-year smoking history was admitted for evaluation of bicytopenia. Two months of facial and hand flushing had preceded admission. Medications included aspirin, a beta-blocker, a calcium-channel blocker, and prednisolone. On admission, vital signs were stable. He was alert and oriented. Examination showed purpura on the lower legs and pale conjunctiva. Heart sounds were regular without murmur. Breath sounds were clear without wheezing or crackles. Initial laboratory results included hemoglobin 7.1 g/dL, platelet count 7.0?10^9/L, white-cell count 4.0?10^9/L with 8% blasts, blood urea nitrogen 15.9 mg/dL, creatinine 1.2 mg/dL, prothrombin time 12 sec (INR 1.07), aPTT 36.2 sec, and lactate dehydrogenase 397 U/L. Acute leukemia was suspected, and because of severe thrombocytopenia and purpura, six units of platelet concentrates were transfused. Approximately 3

title: Exercise-induced Brugada pattern and ventricular tachycardia during Capecitabine treatment
case_summary:
A 59-year-old woman with no prior cardiac history and no smoking history was treated for ER-negative, PR-negative, HER2/neu 2+ equivocal, grade 3 right breast invasive ductal carcinoma with anthracycline chemotherapy, docetaxel, trastuzumab, pertuzumab, lumpectomy, and radiation. Because of persistent disease, capecitabine was started at 2500 mg orally twice daily. One day after initiation, she developed exertional, angina-like chest pain and intermittent shortness of breath. Her resting ECG before the stress test was normal. She underwent an exercise stress echocardiogram, during which at a heart rate of 161 bpm and 7.7 METS she developed a type I Brugada ECG pattern that deteriorated into recurrent, non-sustained, pleomorphic ventricular tachycardia, which spontaneously resolved when the stress test was stopped.  
A coronary angiogram was performed and showed normal coronary arteries.

title: Spontaneous Coronary Artery Dissection in a Male Patient With Fibromuscular Dysplasia
case_summary:
A 57-year-old man was brought by ambulance after sudden-onset crushing chest pain radiating to his jaw and left arm. A prehospital electrocardiogram showed anterior ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction, which degenerated into ventricular fibrillation, prompting defibrillation and cardiopulmonary resuscitation. On arrival, he was diaphoretic and in acute distress. His temperature was 36.1  degreesC, blood pressure 97/61 mm Hg, heart rate 112 beats/min, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 100% on room air. Lung fields were clear, and cardiovascular examination revealed a regular rhythm, normal heart sounds, strong peripheral pulses, and no jugular venous distention. 

His history was notable for hyperlipidemia, anxiety, depression, and ?-1 antitrypsin deficiency; he had no known cardiac disease but reported significant emotional stress related to his mother's recent enrollment in hospice care. Laboratory studies showed a high-sensitivity troponin level of 12 ng/L

title: Oxaliplatin-Associated Takotsubo Cardiomyopathy in a Patient with Metastatic Gastric Cancer: A Case Report
case_summary:
A 64-year-old woman with stage IV gastric adenocarcinoma metastatic to the lungs and abdominal wall was receiving a biweekly regimen of fluorouracil, leucovorin, oxaliplatin, and docetaxel. She had completed five cycles without incident. During the oxaliplatin infusion of her sixth cycle, she developed oppressive chest pain, diaphoresis, hypotension (blood pressure 60/42 mm Hg), bradycardia (heart rate 44 bpm), and hypoxemia (oxygen saturation 72% on room air). The infusion was stopped, and she was transferred to the emergency department, where she received intravenous 0.9% sodium chloride and supplemental oxygen by nasal cannula.  
On admission, an electrocardiogram showed ST-segment elevation in leads I and aVL. Serum troponin I was elevated at 75.71 ?g/L (normal range, 0-40 ?g/L). An interventional cardiologist performed emergency coronary angiography, which demonstrated coronary arteries without angiographically significant lesions. Left ventriculography revealed anteroapical ballo

title: ST-segment elevation myocardial infarction with refractory cardiogenic shock due to coronary spasm: a case report
case_summary:
A 67-year-old woman was admitted for revision of total hip arthroplasty due to increasing hip pain. She had no known cardiac history and denied exertional dyspnoea or chest pain. Cardiovascular risk factors included cigarette smoking and hypertension. On admission, her blood pressure was 156/81 mm Hg; other vital signs were normal. A baseline ECG was unremarkable. Medications included metoprolol, cholecalciferol, alendronic acid, folate, and cobalamin.

During induction of anesthesia with propofol, sufentanil, and rocuronium, she developed sudden hypotension. Initial treatment with theodrenaline/cafedrine had limited effect, and norepinephrine infusion was started. She then developed pulseless electrical activity with ST-segment elevation in leads II and III. Cardiopulmonary resuscitation, intubation, and transfer under mechanical chest compressions to the catheterization laboratory were performed. 

Transthoracic echocardiography showed a severely reduced left ventricular ejection fra

title: Cardiogenic shock in the context of newly diagnosed anomalous origin of the right coronary artery originating from the pulmonary artery: a case report
final_diagnosis: Anomalous right coronary artery from the pulmonary artery
case_summary:
A 76-year-old woman with hypertension and dyslipidaemia presented with two weeks of intermittent palpitations, orthopnoea, and NYHA class III-IV dyspnoea. On examination, her extremities were cool and clammy, jugular venous pressure was elevated to the angle of the jaw with a positive abdominojugular reflux, and auscultation revealed bilateral crackles. Heart sounds were normal without murmurs, but the rhythm was irregularly irregular at approximately 100 b.p.m. 

In the emergency department, she was found to be in atrial fibrillation with left bundle branch block on ECG and clinical evidence of decompensated heart failure, confirmed by chest X-ray showing pulmonary oedema and pleural effusions. Initial management included IV furosemide and rate control with metoprolol and digoxin. 

Over the next few hours, she became increasingly hypotensive (60/30 mmHg), tachycardic, diaphoretic, and developed anuric acute kidney injury and lactic acidosis. High-sensitivity troponin rose from 13 ng/

title: A case report of myocarditis masquerading as hypertrophic cardiomyopathy
case_summary:
A 16-year-old previously healthy female presented with syncope. Six days earlier she developed malaise, fever, and fatigue, later noting chest pain and shortness of breath. She experienced dizziness, visual changes, and sweating, followed by a true syncopal episode during which she chipped a tooth but regained consciousness within seconds. 

On arrival to the emergency department she was afebrile. Blood pressure was 93/72 mmHg, pulse 90-100 bpm, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 100% on room air. She appeared well, with mild pain from her fall, no head or facial bruising, was tachycardic but had normal heart sounds without murmurs, clear lungs, and no hepatosplenomegaly. 

An ECG showed normal sinus rhythm with low-voltage QRS complexes and T-wave inversions in the inferior and anterior leads. Laboratory studies revealed normal electrolytes, blood counts, and inflammatory markers (ESR and CRP). Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated mildly depressed left ventr

title: Finding the right pathway is the key to success
case_summary:
A 24?year?old woman was referred for management of recurrent, hemodynamically tolerated broad?complex tachycardia consistent with right ventricular outflow tract (RVOT) ventricular tachycardia (VT). Over the preceding 3 months, she had been hospitalized 10 times for symptomatic palpitations, requiring several direct current cardioversions. She had no family history of premature sudden cardiac death.
