Initial investigations included a normal echocardiogram, a normal 12?lead electrocardiogram, and cardiac magnetic resonance imaging showing only minor irregularities in the free wall of the right ventricle. A 24?hour Holter monitor revealed a high burden of ventricular ectopy correlating with her symptoms. She was treated sequentially with bisoprolol, diltiazem, and sotalol without satisfactory control.  
Two prior elective catheter ablations were performed using 3D mapping, both localizing the earliest activation to the RVOT. Radiofrequency ablation during VT termination was achieved eac

title: Cardiac manifestations of prolonged seizure: A case report on reverse takotsubo syndrome and neurogenic stunned myocardium
case_summary: A 66-year-old woman with hypertension, dyslipidemia, epilepsy, and a prior cerebrovascular accident presented after a 45-minute generalized tonic-clonic seizure, currently in the postictal phase. Blood glucose was 3 g/L. She was hemodynamically stable with an oxygen saturation of 95% on 6 L of oxygen. Laboratory tests showed a peak troponin I level of 1.5 ng/mL (normal <0.04 ng/mL). An ECG demonstrated sinus rhythm with T-wave inversions in the inferior leads. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a nonhypertrophied, nondilated left ventricle with severe hypokinesia of the basal segments, hyperkinesia of the apical segments, and an ejection fraction of 30%-35%; right ventricular function was normal. She was admitted to the cardiac care unit. Because of her altered sensorium, inability to report chest pain, and persistently elevated troponin levels, coronary angiography was performed, which showed no significant coronary artery lesions.

title: A case report reappraising the usefulness of Valsalva manoeuvre in drug-refractory ventricular tachycardia
case_summary:
A 74-year-old man with a history of remote myocardial infarction presented to the emergency department with dizziness and palpitations. He denied chest pain. One week earlier, he had outpatient Holter monitoring for syncopal palpitations without a diagnosis. On arrival, his heart rate was 180 bpm and blood pressure 102/78 mmHg. Physical examination, including cardiovascular and respiratory systems, was unremarkable.

A 12-lead ECG showed a broad complex tachycardia with features suggestive of ventricular tachycardia. Intravenous adenosine (6 mg, 6 mg, 12 mg) via a large antecubital cannula produced no change in rate, and transient side effects confirmed successful administration. Intravenous amiodarone was started, but the patient's blood pressure fell to 80/60 mmHg, necessitating synchronized DC cardioversion to sinus rhythm.

Two hours later, telemetry documented a recurrent broad complex tachycardia at similar rate but with a different QRS morphology. While he remained hemodynamical

title: A perfect mimic: the role of multimodality imaging in right ventricular cardiomyopathy--a case report
case_summary:
A 46?year?old man presented with palpitations. He had no significant medical history and no family history of cardiomyopathy or sudden cardiac death. An electrocardiogram showed T-wave inversion in leads V1-V3. Both transthoracic echocardiography and single-photon emission computed tomography were interpreted as normal. A 24-hour Holter monitor revealed episodes of non-sustained ventricular tachycardia with left bundle?branch-block morphology and a superior axis, and a premature ventricular contraction burden of 1.3%. 

Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging demonstrated a normal left ventricular size and function but a dilated right ventricle (114 mL/m2) with normal ejection fraction. Dyskinesia was noted in the mid segment of the right ventricular free wall along with extensive late gadolinium enhancement. Genetic testing for a panel of cardiomyopathy and arrhythmia genes was negative. He was started on bisoprolol and referred for further evaluation. 

On examination, he was hemodynamica

title: Life-threatening Vesicular Bronchial Injury Requiring Veno-venous Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation Rescue in an Electronic Nicotine Delivery System User
journal: Clinical Practice and Cases in Emergency Medicine
case_summary:
A 35-year-old woman presented with sudden-onset chest pain and dyspnea for two hours, with severe pain in the back of her neck and left arm worsened by inhalation. She denied fever, cough, leg pain, or swelling. Her medical history included three-vessel coronary artery bypass grafting, coronary artery disease, uncontrolled type 2 diabetes, obesity, deep vein thrombosis, dyslipidemia, gastroesophageal reflux, headaches, and hypertension. She was a former smoker and denied alcohol or recreational drug use.

On arrival she was tachycardic (126 bpm), tachypneic (18 breaths/min with labored effort), normothermic, hypertensive (140/90 mmHg), and 97% saturated on 2 L/min oxygen. Examination showed increased work of breathing without adventitious lung sounds, regular heart rhythm without murmurs, and no lower extremity edema.

Treatment included aspirin, hydromorphone, ondansetron, diphenhydramine, intravenous fluids, and supplemental oxygen. Initial ECG showed sinus tachycardia with nonspecif

title: Interventricular septum mass presenting as a late acute coronary syndrome with ST-segment elevation: a case report
case_summary:
An 81-year-old man, former smoker with known ischaemic heart disease treated by mid-left anterior descending artery stenting and an implantable cardioverter-defibrillator placed 2 months earlier for sustained ventricular tachycardia, was admitted with recent-onset dyspnoea and signs of decompensated heart failure.  
On arrival: blood pressure 130/70?mmHg, heart rate 72?b.p.m., oxygen saturation 95% on 3 L/min O?. Physical examination revealed no murmurs.  
An ECG showed sinus rhythm with abnormal R-wave progression and ST-segment elevation in leads V2-V5 and II, III, aVF. A prior ECG 2 months earlier had shown only negative T-waves in leads V2-V3 with normal R-wave progression.  
Laboratory tests revealed troponin I 83 ng/L (normal <26 ng/L), brain natriuretic peptide 1400 pg/L (normal <100 pg/L), C-reactive protein 93 mg/L (normal <4 mg/L), and white blood cell count 14 G/L (normal <10 G/L). Given a coronary angiogram 2 months earlier showing no new stenoses, emergent angiography was

title: Bullet-associated ventricular tachycardia: a case report
case_summary: A 44-year-old man presented with his first episode of a hemodynamically unstable sustained ventricular tachycardia (VT) with a cycle length of 250 ms, which required two electrical cardioversions by emergency medical services. He reported sudden palpitations, fatigue, and mild dyspnea while cycling; no chest pain. His medical history was remarkable only for a self-inflicted thoracic gunshot wound at age 16, with emergency cardiac surgery documenting a bullet perforation of the pericardium and contact with the anterolateral left ventricular surface. He had recovered fully, with no ongoing medications or illicit drug use, and maintained regular exercise. On admission, he was asymptomatic in stable sinus rhythm. Physical examination was unremarkable. Laboratory tests showed elevated troponin I (187 pg/mL; normal <14 pg/mL) with normal creatine kinase. Electrocardiogram demonstrated sinus rhythm with flattened or inverted T waves in leads I, II, aVL, and V5-V6. Transthoracic echocardiograp

title: Refractory Hypotension as an Initial Presentation of Bilateral Subclavian Artery Stenosis
case_summary: A 75-year-old woman with coronary artery disease (previous LAD stent), chronic diastolic heart failure, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, diabetes mellitus, prior carotid endarterectomy, and bilateral femoral bypass underwent transurethral bladder tumor resection. Postoperatively, she was persistently hypotensive but asymptomatic. Examination revealed a well-appearing, afebrile woman with blood pressure 66/50?mm?Hg (right arm), heart rate 96/min, respiratory rate 20/min, oxygen saturation 90% on 3?L nasal oxygen, and no jugular venous distension or peripheral edema; a bilateral carotid bruit was noted, and mild abdominal tenderness was present. After 3?L of normal saline, her blood pressure did not improve. Electrocardiogram showed poor R-wave progression, Q waves of an old inferior infarct, left ventricular hypertrophy, and nonspecific lateral ST abnormalities. Cardiac enzymes were elevated (troponin T 0.24?ng/mL; CK-MB 10.1?ng/mL). Echocardiography showed an ejection fraction of 37% (pre

title: Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma With Cardiac Invasion Presented as Acute Myocardial Infarction and Left Ventricular Hypertrophy: A Case Report
journal: Journal of Investigative Medicine High Impact Case Reports
case_summary:
A 51-year-old man presented with recurrent chest tightness for 4 months, worsened over the past 2 weeks. On initial evaluation, ECG showed ST-segment elevation in lead aVL and T-wave changes in leads V2-V6. Cardiac enzymes were elevated (troponin T 254.9 pg/mL, CK 440 U/L, CK-MB 30.3 U/L, myoglobin 103.1 ng/mL). Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a left ventricular ejection fraction of 70%, left ventricular wall thickening, regional wall motion abnormality, moderate mitral regurgitation, and a small pericardial effusion. Selective coronary angiography revealed no significant stenosis and TIMI grade 3 flow, and coronary spasm was considered.

One month later, he returned with chest tightness and dyspnea. Physical examination revealed wet rales. ECG showed sinus rhythm with ST-T changes. Echocardiography revealed local pericardial thickening and adhesion of the inferior and lateral left ventricular walls. Cardiac enzymes were normal, but NT-proBNP was elevated at 5407 pg/mL. He

title: MINOCA in a 15-year-old female with multiple genetic mutations: A case of myocardial infarction in the very young adult
final_diagnosis: Myocardial infarction with nonobstructive coronary arteries
case_summary:
A 15-year-old girl with a BMI of 30.2 presented with sudden, severe (10/10) substernal chest pain radiating to both arms and the epigastrium. Her history was notable for polycystic ovary syndrome treated briefly with oral contraceptives, and a strong family history of premature coronary artery disease (father died at age 36 of MI; uncle underwent CABG). She denied smoking, alcohol, or drug use. On arrival, she was hemodynamically stable and physical examination was unremarkable. Troponin levels were elevated. ECG showed sinus rhythm with T-wave inversion in leads II, III, aVF, V3, and V4. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed normal left ventricular function with mild inferior wall hypokinesis, and she was diagnosed with NSTEMI. She received aspirin, clopidogrel, heparin, and high-intensity statin therapy and was admitted to the CCU. 

Ten hours later, she developed recurrent substernal chest pain with sweating and nausea. ECG now showed ST-segment elevation in II, III, aVF, and post

title: "False" False Tendon: Fatal Intramyocardial Dissecting Hematoma
case_summary:
A 70-year-old man with hypertension, heart failure (LVEF 30-35%), prior LAD stent placement (2010), non-ST-elevation MI (2015), chronic kidney disease, and rheumatoid arthritis was admitted after an out-of-hospital pulseless electrical activity arrest. He had sudden nausea and nonbloody vomiting at home, then was found unresponsive. His wife, a nurse, began chest compressions; return of spontaneous circulation occurred after ~20 minutes. He denied preceding chest pain and had been functionally independent.

On arrival, he was comatose, intubated, with sinus rhythm at 20-30 beats/min and blood pressure 105/73 mm Hg. Lung auscultation revealed diffuse bilateral crackles; extremities were cool. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 10.9 g/dL, hematocrit 35.9%, platelets 56 ? 10^3/uL, INR 2.1, BUN 70 mg/dL, creatinine chronically elevated at 4.3 mg/dL, NT-proBNP >35 000 pg/mL, lactic acid 13.9 mmol/L, potassium 6.9 mmol/L, bicarbonate 12.5 mmol/L, pH 6.974, and a troponin I peak of 1.880 ng

title: Eosinophilic myocarditis complicated by permanent atrioventricular nodal block: a case report
case_summary:
A 44?year?old previously healthy man presented with chills for 3 days and arthralgias in the knees and hips, preceded by several days of unproductive cough and headache. One week before presentation, he had been treated with a macrolide antibiotic and an NSAID. His medical history was notable only for smoking; there was no family history of cardiovascular disease or long?term medications. 

On examination, temperature was 38.5 degreesC, heart rate 113/min, blood pressure 126/64 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Auscultation revealed no pericardial rub or crackles; there was epigastric tenderness. ECG showed sinus tachycardia with first?degree AV block (PQ 210 ms). Laboratory studies demonstrated WBC 13.4?10^3/uL, CRP 16.9 mg/dL, ESR 95 mm/h, and high?sensitivity troponin T 656.2 ng/L; the differential count showed no eosinophilia. Electrolytes, liver, and kidney function were normal. Blood cultures, serology, and PCR for pathogens were negative; vasculitis?associated autoant

title: Stiff right atrial syndrome? A complex clinical case report utilizing multimodality imaging and invasive hemodynamics
case_summary:
A 57-year-old woman with a history of orthotopic heart transplantation in 2008 for ventricular arrhythmias, complicated by antibody-mediated rejection in 2013, presented in December 2020 with refractory right heart failure. Over the prior four years she experienced progressive volume overload with worsening renal function. In 2018 she underwent tricuspid valve annuloplasty for severe tricuspid regurgitation; postoperatively she developed acute kidney injury, recurrent pleural effusions, and ascites. She progressed to stage 5 chronic kidney disease and underwent deceased-donor kidney transplantation in 2020. On admission she was taking tacrolimus, prednisone, valganciclovir, and high-dose torsemide; mycophenolic acid had been discontinued for leukopenia. NT-proBNP and high-sensitivity troponin were elevated but unchanged from prior measurements.

On examination, jugular venous pressure was elevated with a prominent v-wave, an S3 gallop was present, hepatomegaly with pulsatility was note

title: Can Hyperthermia Unveil Brugada Pattern?
case_summary:
A 61-year-old man with a history of congestive heart failure, dementia, intellectual disability, and hypertension was brought to the emergency department with altered mental status and shortness of breath. His home medications included aspirin, atorvastatin, chlorthalidone, and furosemide. On arrival, his temperature was 40.4  degreesC, heart rate 133 bpm, respiratory rate 24 breaths/min, blood pressure 116/61 mm Hg, and oxygen saturation 94% on 4 L/min. He appeared toxic and lethargic and was nonverbal. Examination revealed a regular tachycardia and left lower extremity circumferential erythema and warmth.

Laboratory studies showed a white blood cell count of 11.6?10^9/L, hemoglobin 11.5 g/dL, platelets 104?10^9/L, normal electrolytes, creatinine 1.9 mg/dL (baseline 1.2 mg/dL), normal TSH and free T4, and an initial high-sensitivity troponin of 126 ng/L that trended downward on repeat. A respiratory pathogen panel was positive for influenza B, lactic acid was 1.8 mmol/L, and one bloo

title: Idiopathic posterior papillary muscle rupture; a case report
case_summary:
A 73-year-old woman with hypertension presented with 30 minutes of severe, pressure-like substernal chest pain radiating to her left arm and jaw at rest, accompanied by nausea and acute dyspnea. She denied prior episodes, exertional dyspnea, trauma, new medications, sick contacts, or recent travel. Medical history included hypertension, generalized anxiety disorder, major depressive disorder, severe osteoarthritis, and an atypical polymyalgia rheumatica treated with a 6-month steroid taper. Family history was notable for a father who died suddenly from dilated cardiomyopathy. Home medications were buspirone, duloxetine, hydrochlorothiazide, valsartan, and meloxicam as needed.

On examination, she was normothermic, tachycardic (112 bpm), hypotensive (88/55 mmHg), and hypoxemic (SpO2 88% on 5 L nasal cannula). She was diaphoretic, in acute distress, with jugular venous distension, bibasilar crackles, a normal S1, diminished S2, and a holosystolic murmur radiating to the axilla. Extremiti

title: A case report of recurrent primary posterior mediastinal perivascular epithelioid cell tumour compressing the right inferior pulmonary vein, atria, and inferior vena cava
case_summary:
A 36-year-old man presented with a 1-week history of fevers, chills, productive cough, chest pain, dyspnea on exertion, loss of appetite, and generalized weakness. His chest pain was mid-back, pressure-like, constant, non-radiating, non-pleuritic, and rated 5/10. His medical history included resection of a "mediastinal PEComa" (initially misdiagnosed as rhabdomyoma), essential hypertension, and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation status post appendage ligation (off anticoagulation). 

On examination, vital signs were within normal limits. He had an irregularly irregular heart rhythm and a sternotomy scar. Laboratory data, including inflammatory markers, were unremarkable. Electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm, first-degree atrioventricular block, and left atrial enlargement. Chest radiograph was unremarkable.

Given concern for tumour recurrence, transthoracic echocardiography was performed and showed a globular mass involving the posterior left atrium and pulmonary veins measuring a

title: Early Left Ventricular Thrombus Formation in a COVID-19 Patient with ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction
case_summary:
A 50-year-old man with no previous medical history was admitted after being found unresponsive at home. On arrival, he was confused (GCS 11), hypotensive, mottled in the legs, and hypoperfused. Initial labs showed extreme hyperglycaemia (unrecordable), ketones 3.6?mmol/L, lactic acidosis, acute kidney injury (urea 41.3?mmol/L, creatinine 277?umol/L, eGFR 21?mL/min), hypernatraemia (Na 172?mmol/L), elevated CRP rising from 53 to 374?mg/L over 5 days, and a normal white cell count. He tested positive for SARS-CoV-2, developed respiratory failure requiring intubation and haemofiltration, and had findings of pulmonary aspergillosis treated with voriconazole.

One month after admission, he developed acute chest pain. ECG showed anterior ST-segment elevation. Emergency coronary angiography revealed an acute occlusion of the left anterior descending artery and moderate bystander atheroma elsewhere. He underwent primary PCI with a drug-eluting stent and restored TIMI 3 flow. During the procedu

title: Acute cholecystitis and myocardial infarction: a case study with coronary involvement
case_summary:
An 83-year-old woman with non-insulin-dependent diabetes, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, GERD, and chronic kidney disease presented with 3 months of intermittent sharp right upper quadrant pain, rated 6/10, accompanied by nausea and occasional constipation. She denied fever, diarrhea, bloody stools, dysuria, chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, or syncope. There had been no bowel movement for 2 days. Exercise tolerance was one block, limited by dyspepsia.

On examination, blood pressure was 156/80 mm Hg, pulse 93/min, respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 36  degreesC. The abdomen was soft with normal bowel sounds, no distention, tenderness, or masses. The remainder of the exam was unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed a white-cell count of 20,000/?L, normal liver function tests, hemoglobin A1C of 9.4%, and C-reactive protein of 15.5 mg/L. Electrocardiogram demonstrated normal sinus rhythm with left axis deviation and nonspecific ST-T-wave abnormalities.

An RUQ ultrasound revealed a 2.

title: A rare presentation of acute myocarditis as a manifestation of adult-onset Still's disease: a case report
case_summary:
A 22-year-old man presented with 4 days of fever, myalgia, arthralgia, and sore throat, and 2 days of vomiting and diarrhea. He had no past medical history, did not smoke or use recreational drugs, and had no notable family history. On examination, temperature was 38.4 degreesC, pulse 89/min, blood pressure 77/34 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. He had cool peripheries, normal heart sounds without murmurs or rub, and no signs of fluid overload.
Laboratory tests revealed leukocytosis (22.9?10^9/L) with neutrophilia, monocytosis, and lymphopenia; mild thrombocytopenia; creatinine 333 umol/L; urea 22.2 mmol/L; AST 123 U/L; ALT 46 U/L; high-sensitivity troponin T 4550 ng/L; NT-proBNP 1485 pmol/L; C-reactive protein 415 mg/L; lactate 3.6 mmol/L; and sodium 129 mmol/L. Electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm, right-axis deviation, and diffuse ST-segment elevation. Chest radiograph was unremarkable.
He was admitted to the intensive care unit for presumed sep

title: Acute myocardial infarction in twin pregnancy after assisted reproduction: A case report
case_summary: A 33-year-old primigravid woman at 31 weeks of a twin pregnancy conceived by in vitro fertilization presented with intermittent chest tightness for 12 weeks, which worsened over the prior week, and acute poststernal colic chest pain lasting 4 hours accompanied by sweating and nausea. She had no history of cardiovascular disease, hypertension, diabetes, smoking, or family history of coronary artery disease. Medications included nifedipine, dexamethasone, progestin, and prophylactic enoxaparin. On examination, temperature was 36.0  degreesC, pulse 95 bpm, blood pressure 119/73 mmHg, and respiratory rate 20/min; she appeared ill but had no murmurs or pulmonary findings. Laboratory tests showed elevated CK (2924 U/L), CK-MB (21.5 ng/mL), troponin I (47.7 ng/mL), LDH (558 U/L), and myoglobin (1442.7 ng/mL). Initial ECG demonstrated ST-segment elevation in leads II, III, aVF, V3R-V5R, and V7-V9. Echocardiography revealed akinesis of the inferior left ventricular wall and hypokinesis of the b

title: Multiple myeloma presenting with high-output heart failure and improving with anti-angiogenesis therapy: two case reports and a review of the literature
case_summary:
A 50?year?old man of Indian ancestry with a 3?year history of multiple myeloma presented with progressive abdominal and lower?extremity edema and dyspnea. He reported a 15?lb weight gain over 2 weeks. His only other chronic condition was mild hypertension. His myeloma had advanced despite corticosteroids, cyclophosphamide, etoposide, cisplatin, stem?cell transplant, thalidomide, and bortezomib.

On examination, he was afebrile, heart rate 100/min, blood pressure 97/50 mm?Hg, and oxygen saturation 96% on 3?L/min nasal oxygen. He had bilateral basal crackles, jugular venous distension to 12?cm, a 2/6 systolic flow murmur at the left upper sternal border, distended abdomen with shifting dullness, a liver edge 4?cm below the costal margin, warm extremities, and 3+ bilateral pitting edema with scrotal swelling.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 9.1?g/dL, platelets 10,000/uL, blood urea nitrogen 55?mg/dL, creatinine 1.0?mg/dL, albumin 3.6?g/dL, and calcium 13?mg/dL. ECG showed sinus tach

title: Esophageal perforation mimicking an acute inferior myocardial infarction: a case report
case_summary:
A 65-year-old man with a history of hypertension and a 40-year history of alcohol use presented after half an hour of exercise with persistent, oppressive chest pain in the anterior and posterior regions of the chest radiating to the shoulder and back. He denied any preceding gagging, vomiting, or retching. His home medications were none despite a 10-year history of untreated hypertension.

In the ambulance, an ECG showed Q waves and approximately 0.1 mV of ST-segment elevation in leads II and III. Sublingual nitroglycerin provided no relief. On arrival at the emergency department, his blood pressure was 130/90 mmHg, heart rate 90 beats/min, oxygen saturation 90%, and temperature 36.5 ?. Physical examination was unremarkable, with no upper abdominal tenderness and normal cardiac and pulmonary auscultation.

An 18-lead ECG obtained within 10 minutes of presentation demonstrated persistent Q waves in the inferior leads and low-amplitude T waves in all leads, with dynamic ST-segment evolu

title: Acute "Pseudoischemic" ECG Abnormalities after Right Pneumonectomy
case_summary: A 68-year-old man with no prior chronic disease underwent right intrapericardial pneumonectomy for non-small-cell lung cancer. The intraoperative and immediate postoperative courses were unremarkable. On postoperative day 2, he developed acute mid-chest pain of intermittent character lasting about one hour, with radiation to the back, accompanied by fatigue and sweating. On examination, he was pale and diaphoretic; respiratory rate was 19 /min and blood pressure 90/60 mm Hg. ECG showed supraventricular extrasystoles at 100 beats/min and slight ST-segment elevation in leads V4 to V6. He received oxygen, digoxin, hydration, and NSAIDs for analgesia. Symptoms persisted, and repeat ECG revealed absolute arrhythmia with a heart rate of 140 beats/min and ST-segment elevation in leads V2 to V6, I, and II. Transthoracic echocardiography showed no left-ventricular wall-motion abnormalities and no pericardial effusion. ECG at that time showed absolute arrhythmia with a heart rate of 140 beats/mi

title: Triple-Vessel Spontaneous Coronary Artery Dissection Managed Conservatively
case_summary:
A 54-year-old woman presented with recurrent episodes of sudden-onset sharp chest pain lasting minutes over 1 day, starting after she received distressing news of a family member's death. The pains culminated in a severe, persistent episode lasting hours. On arrival, her blood pressure was 180/100 mm Hg; other vital signs were normal. Physical examination was unremarkable, and she had no features of connective tissue disease (no joint laxity, arachnodactyly, skin hyperextensibility, or marfanoid habitus).

An initial electrocardiogram showed a chronic right bundle branch block and left anterior fascicular block, with new T-wave inversions in the anterior and inferior leads. Serial ECGs demonstrated progression of T-wave inversions. Initial high-sensitivity troponin was 207 ng/L (reference >14 ng/L in women) and later peaked at 1280 ng/L.

Her medical history was notable for obesity, sleeve gastrectomy 2 weeks earlier, heart failure with preserved ejection fraction, type 2 diabetes, hyp

title: Apical myocardial infarction with bizarre coronary images mimicking left ventricular apical ballooning syndrome: a case report
case_summary:
A 51-year-old Japanese woman with no prior medical history and no coronary risk factors presented to the emergency department with the sudden onset of anterior chest pain while shopping. On arrival, her blood pressure was 192/100 mm Hg and her pulse was 64 beats/min and regular. A 12-lead electrocardiogram demonstrated ST-segment elevation in leads V2 through V5. Transthoracic echocardiography showed akinesis of the left ventricular apex with preserved basal contraction. Laboratory studies revealed a white-blood-cell count of 10 480 /?L and a positive troponin T assay. 

Given her presentation--a postmenopausal woman with chest pain, ST-segment elevation, and apical wall-motion abnormality--left ventricular apical ballooning syndrome was initially suspected. Emergency left ventriculography confirmed apical akinesis with basal hyperkinesis, consistent with that diagnosis. Subsequent coronary angiography showed a normal right coronary artery and left circumflex artery, but the distal por

title: Small Bowel Obstruction Mimicking Acute ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction
case_summary:
A 42-year-old woman with a history of endometriosis and prior hysterectomy presented with a 3-day history of nausea and vomiting. 
Initial abdominal CT showed obstruction of the distal ileum, and she underwent emergent exploratory laparotomy with adhesiolysis and decompression. 
During her postoperative recovery, she developed watery diarrhea and persistent sinus tachycardia despite intravenous fluids and electrolyte repletion. 
On postoperative day 13, an ST-elevation "MI code" was called when a 12-lead ECG demonstrated inferolateral ST-segment elevations. The patient reported epigastric discomfort and shortness of breath but denied chest pain. 
A bedside transthoracic echocardiogram showed normal left ventricular systolic function with inferior wall hypokinesis. 
Subsequent cardiac catheterization revealed angiographically normal coronary arteries and normal left ventricular contractility on ventriculography. 
Serial cardiac enzyme levels were within normal limits.

title: Case report: marantic endocarditis in renal cell carcinoma: nephrectomy a treatment
case_summary:
A 65-year-old man presented with a 2-day history of sudden-onset headache, weakness, visual disturbance, and a 3-month history of unintentional 15 kg weight loss. He experienced gait disturbance, word-finding difficulty, left-sided visual impairment, nausea, and vomiting. His wife noted confusion and muddled speech. He had no history of head trauma. His medical history included type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, a left cataract, and right wet macular degeneration. 

On examination, heart rate was 69 bpm, blood pressure 177/95 mmHg, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 97% on room air, and temperature 36.7 degreesC. He was euvolemic with no peripheral stigmata of infective endocarditis. Cardiovascular examination revealed a new ejection systolic murmur loudest at the aortic area, radiating to the carotids. Neurologically, he had a left homonymous hemianopia, left visual inattention, an upgoing left plantar reflex, and impaired left-sided coordination; tone, power, and

title: Left main coronary artery compression by a large pulmonary artery aneurysm in the absence of pulmonary hypertension: a case report
case_summary:
A 64-year-old woman with a history of childhood pulmonary valve stenosis treated by commissurotomy and infundibulectomy--and residual significant pulmonary regurgitation--presented with rapid progression of exertional dyspnea and recent onset of effort-related chest pain. Her blood pressure was 147/81 mmHg, heart rate 80 b.p.m., and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Cardiovascular examination revealed a short, low-pitched diastolic murmur over the left upper sternal border without signs of heart failure. 

Electrocardiography showed sinus rhythm, right bundle branch block, and frequent supraventricular premature complexes. Chest radiography demonstrated a dextropositioned heart with mesocardia, enlargement of the right atrium and right ventricle, and a dilated pulmonary trunk.

Transthoracic echocardiography revealed moderate right ventricular and atrial dilatation with normal RV systolic function (TAPSE 19 mm, fractional area change 43%), significant pulmonary regurgitation (pressure

title: Coronary embolism due to possible thrombosis of prosthetic aortic valve - the role of optical coherence tomography: case report
case_summary: A 50-year-old man presented with ongoing chest pain. Eleven years earlier, he had undergone mechanical aortic valve replacement for a bicuspid valve; preoperative angiography had shown normal coronaries. His history included treated hypercholesterolaemia and current smoking. He had no known atrial fibrillation, ventricular dysfunction, coagulation disorders, or recent cancer. On arrival, blood pressure was 95/65?mmHg, heart rate 79?b.p.m., temperature 36.2 degreesC, and oxygen saturation 99% on room air. Cardiopulmonary examination was unremarkable. The ECG showed anterior ST-segment elevation. Emergent coronary angiography revealed total occlusion of the proximal left anterior descending artery with a large thrombus burden. Thrombus aspiration restored flow, and a glycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitor was administered. Aside from distal embolization to the apical LAD and a filling defect in the first diagonal branch, flow was fully re-established without evidence of stenosis or atherosclero

title: Anesthetic and surgical considerations of giant pericardial cyst: Case report and literature review
case_summary: A 46-year-old woman with a 5-year history of hypertension and hypothyroidism presented for preoperative assessment after the diagnosis of a left breast mass on screening mammography. She reported progressive shortness of breath and dry cough for 2 years, with orthopnea developing over the past 6 months. She denied palpitations, syncope, hemoptysis, chest trauma, or prior thoracic surgery. Her medications included levothyroxine and metoprolol; she was a nonsmoker without known allergies. Vital signs were normal. Cardiac examination revealed regular rhythm and normal heart sounds; physical examination was otherwise unremarkable except for grade 2 bilateral lower-limb edema. Electrocardiography showed normal sinus rhythm. A preoperative chest radiograph demonstrated a well-defined, oval opacity at the right cardiophrenic angle. Contrast-enhanced chest CT revealed a homogeneous mass measuring 7.2 ? 9.1 cm in the right lower thorax, most consistent with a pericardial cyst, although a bronch

title: An autopsy case report of fulminant myocarditis: Following mRNA COVID-19 vaccination
case_summary:
A 27-year-old man with a high level of athletic activity was found sitting unconscious during practice and was brought to the emergency department in cardiopulmonary arrest. Eight days earlier, he had received his first dose of the mRNA-1273 SARS-CoV-2 vaccine and had had no symptoms of chest pain or fatigue until this event. Preoperative evaluation for unrelated orthopedic surgery three months before admission had shown mild cardiac enlargement on chest radiograph and an electrocardiographic abnormality (sinus rhythm, right-axis deviation, poor R-wave progression, and monomorphic premature ventricular contractions), but he was asymptomatic and underwent no further workup.

On arrival in the emergency department, he was in asystole. Despite cardiopulmonary resuscitation, he developed recurrent ventricular tachyarrhythmias and was supported with venoarterial extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (VA-ECMO) and an Impella CP device after two hours. A chest radiograph showed an enlarged card

title: A Large Cardiac Mass: Diagnosis of Caseous Mitral Annular Calcification and Determining Optimum Management Strategy
case_summary:
A 64-year-old woman presented after acute visual deficits that resolved within 24 hours and were consistent with a transient ischemic attack. Her history was notable for paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, hypertension, and migraines; she was taking aspirin 325 mg daily for stroke prophylaxis. On examination, blood pressure was 170/116 mmHg, heart rate 88 bpm and irregularly irregular, respiratory rate 20, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. Cardiac auscultation revealed a soft midpeaking systolic murmur best heard at the right upper sternal border; there were no carotid bruits. 

Electrocardiography confirmed atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 88 bpm and no ischemic changes. Laboratory evaluation was notable for a prothrombin time of 11.4 s, creatinine 0.5 mg/dL, HDL 71 mg/dL, LDL 105 mg/dL, and triglycerides 103 mg/dL. CT angiography of the head and neck showed only mild carotid bulb calcifications. Brain MRI revealed periventricular and subcortical white-matter changes co

title:
Pulmonary hypertension and severe right heart failure following
lumbar spinal surgery
case_summary:
A 47-year-old man with no smoking history and previously good health presented with a 1-year history of progressive dyspnoea, presyncope, and leg swelling. One year earlier, he had undergone complex spinal surgery including L5-S1 discectomy, laminectomy, and bilateral nerve reconstruction. Since then, he had multiple admissions for right-sided heart failure requiring intravenous diuresis.

Echocardiography revealed preserved left ventricular function, a severely dilated right ventricle and right atrium, normal left atrial size, severe tricuspid regurgitation, and a small pericardial effusion. Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging showed a D-shaped left ventricle consistent with right ventricular pressure and volume overload and pulmonary hypertension.

On examination, jugular venous pressure was elevated, the second heart sound was loud, and a systolic murmur was heard diffusely. There was peripheral oedema, ascites, and an abdominal bruit. Laboratory studies showed haemoglobin 11.7?g/dL,

title: Surgical treatment of a giant unruptured aneurysm of the noncoronary sinus of Valsalva: a case report
case_summary: A 52-year-old woman presented with a 2-week history of progressive dyspnea, tachycardia, and jugular venous distension. She was in New York Heart Association functional class III. Vital signs were notable for tachycardia; blood pressure was normal. Cardiac examination revealed no aortic murmur, and the electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm. A chest radiograph demonstrated cardiomegaly with enlargement of the right atrium. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a pericardial effusion measuring approximately 35 mm and a large extracardiac mass above the left atrium that appeared to communicate with the ascending aorta. Transesophageal echocardiography identified a 61 ? 77 mm unruptured aneurysmal sac arising from the noncoronary sinus of Valsalva, without thrombus or intracardiac shunting; the aortic valve was competent and there was no annular dilation. Contrast-enhanced CT angiography confirmed a 74 ? 60 mm aneurysm of the noncoronary sinus of Valsalva, free of thrombus, compressing t

title: Coronary artery intramural hematoma, a rare complication of percutaneous coronary intervention
case_summary:
A 53?year?old man with hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and prediabetes presented with two weeks of nonexertional, left?sided chest pain relieved by isosorbide mononitrate and exertional dyspnea on walking seven to eight blocks. He was referred for elective left heart catheterization after a positive stress test. On admission he was asymptomatic, with a blood pressure of 156/98 mm Hg and otherwise stable vital signs; the examination was unremarkable. Laboratory studies--including a complete blood count, metabolic panel, coagulation profile, troponin I, and CK?MB--were normal. An electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm, and a chest radiograph was unremarkable. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated an LVEF of 60 to 65%. A nuclear stress test revealed moderate lateral?wall ischemia.

Coronary angiography via a right femoral approach showed an 80% stenosis of the mid left anterior descending artery (LAD), an 80% stenosis of the proximal ramus intermedius, and luminal irregularities

title: Diffuse LAD and LV Thrombus as Possible Manifestation of Protein-S Deficiency in a Young Patient
case_summary:
A 24-year-old black African man presented with 12 hours of ongoing chest pain. On admission, his blood pressure was 130/70 mm Hg and his heart rate was 100 beats/min. An electrocardiogram showed anterior ST-segment elevation. He received 500 mg of acetylsalicylic acid and 180 mg of ticagrelor, then underwent emergency coronary angiography. Point-of-care transthoracic echocardiography revealed akinesia of the apical segments, a left ventricular ejection fraction of 30%, and a large thrombus in the apical inferolateral wall. Initial differential diagnoses included myocardial infarction, pericarditis, pulmonary embolism, and herpes zoster. 

Coronary angiography demonstrated a large intracoronary thrombus in the proximal left anterior descending artery with total occlusion in its mid segment. A floppy wire was advanced distally without restoration of flow. Intravascular ultrasonography confirmed a large thrombus burden in the proximal segment but showed no evidence of endothelial destruct

title: Takotsubo cardiomyopathy in the setting of severe hyponatremia and beer potomania: A case report
case_summary:
A 56-year-old African American man with hypertension, hyperlipidemia, and alcohol dependence was brought to the emergency department with incoherent speech and altered mentation. He reported mild exertional dyspnea but denied chest pain, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, or leg swelling. He had been binge-drinking about 24 cans of beer per day for several days following his wife's incarceration, with his last drink 5 hours before presentation.

On examination, he was disheveled, confused, and inebriated. Vital signs were blood pressure 129/67 mmHg, pulse 73/min, and temperature 99.4 degreesF. He was somnolent but arousable and oriented to person and place only. Neurologic examination showed no focal deficits. The remainder of the physical examination was unremarkable.

Laboratory studies showed:  
? Serum sodium 102 mmol/L (134-144)  
? Serum osmolality 245 mOsm/kg (275-295)  
? Urine osmolality 44 mOsm/kg (50-1200)  
? Urine sodium 7 mmol/L (20-40)  
? Blood alcohol level 221 m

title: Infective endocarditis of an aortic bioprosthesis causing life-threatening incessant junctional tachycardia: a case report
case_summary:
A 68-year-old man with a history of bioprosthetic aortic?valve replacement, coronary artery disease, and persistent atrial fibrillation was treated conservatively for methicillin?sensitive Staphylococcus aureus endocarditis of his aortic prosthesis, which was complicated by a hemorrhagic stroke with Wernicke's aphasia. He was receiving intravenous cefazolin and rifampicin.

He presented to the emergency department with 2 hours of palpitations without chest pain. On examination, he was afebrile; heart rate 240 b.p.m., blood pressure 105/70 mmHg, respiratory rate 22 breaths/min, and oxygen saturation 93% on room air. Arterial blood gas showed normal lactate levels and no electrolyte abnormalities. White blood cells were 11 G/L and C-reactive protein was 45 mg/L.

An ECG showed a regular wide?QRS tachycardia at 240 b.p.m. with a right?bundle?branch?block pattern identical to his baseline ECG. Vagal maneuvers and intravenous adenosine (up to 18 mg) were ineffective. The patient developed h

title: An unusual presentation of non-infectious ascending aortitis: a case report of an asymptomatic murmur
case_summary:
A 72-year-old man was referred for evaluation of an incidentally detected murmur. He had a history of well-controlled hypertension and diet-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus. He denied chest pain, dyspnoea, constitutional symptoms, or any history of infections, including tuberculosis or syphilis. He had no family history of aortic disease or autoimmune disorders and reported no systemic symptoms suggestive of vasculitis or connective tissue disease.

On examination, blood pressure was 160/59 mm Hg and heart rate 59 beats per minute. There was prominent head-nodding (De Musset's sign), a grade IV/VI diastolic murmur at the left upper sternal border, and bounding carotid pulsations. Lung and other cardiovascular findings were unremarkable.

Transthoracic echocardiography showed a tricuspid aortic valve with severe aortic insufficiency and an enlarged ascending aorta; left ventricular ejection fraction was 60%. Contrast CT angiography confirmed a fusiform aneurysm of the ascending thora

title: Multimodality Imaging Supports Cardiac Lesion Diagnosis in Patient With Liver Carcinoma: A Case Report
case_summary: A 53-year-old man with untreated gallbladder stones presented with progressively worsening right-sided hemiparesis and difficulty speaking. He had no fever, chest pain, or dyspnea. On examination, his temperature was normal, pulse 82/min, blood pressure 130/80 mmHg. He had mild jaundice, a grade 3 systolic murmur best heard at the cardiac apex, and hepatomegaly. Neurologic evaluation showed motor dysphasia and right hemiparesis (muscle strength 3/5). Brain MRI revealed a left periventricular infarction with hemorrhagic transformation in the left temporal lobe. Routine transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a 10.7 ? 13.8 mm homogenous, slightly hypoechoic mass attached to the edge of the right coronary and noncoronary cusps of the aortic valve, with mild aortic regurgitation; an 11 ? 4.4 mm mass on the atrial side of the posterior mitral leaflet with mild mitral regurgitation; and a 43 ? 11 mm right ventricular mobile lesion of similar echogenicity. Transesophageal and 3D echocardi

title: Severe calcification in chronic constrictive pericarditis of tuberculous-related a case report and literature review
case_summary: A 64-year-old man from Northern Morocco with poorly controlled diabetes presented with 8 months of progressive dyspnea, pericarditis-like chest pain, night fevers, sweats, asthenia, and 10% weight loss. On examination, he was afebrile, blood pressure 117/63 mm Hg, heart rate 82 beats/min, respiratory rate 17/min, O? saturation 99% on room air. Signs of right heart failure included jugular venous distension, hepatomegaly, moderate ascites, and bilateral lower extremity edema. ECG showed low QRS voltage in peripheral leads and negative T waves from V1 to V6. Laboratory studies revealed cholestatic liver enzyme elevations (alkaline phosphatase 314 U/L, GGT 161 U/L, total bilirubin 36 mmol/L), negative sputum AFB, positive Quantiferon (TB1-Nil and TB2-Nil >0.35 IU/L), and elevated CA-125 at 1325 U/mL. Full blood count, renal function, and other liver tests were normal. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated pericardial thickening around both ventricles, a septal bounce, moderate tricu

title: Incidentally Discovered Endocarditis Leading to the Diagnosis of an Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor Mutant Metastatic Pulmonary Malignancy of Occult Primary Tumor
case_summary: A 62-year-old man with a history of heart failure with recovered ejection fraction and persistent atrial fibrillation presented for evaluation of new anemia. A routine transthoracic echocardiogram revealed a 12 mm ? 4 mm mobile echodensity on the noncoronary cusp of the aortic valve, suspicious for vegetation. He reported 2 months of progressive dyspnea on exertion, fatigue, anorexia, and a 16 kg unintentional weight loss, but denied fevers, night sweats, chills, chest pain, or palpitations. He had two ischemic strokes in the prior 2 months attributed to embolic atrial fibrillation; no echocardiogram had been obtained at that time. On admission he was afebrile and hemodynamically stable, with an unremarkable physical examination. Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 5.9 g/dL (MCV 90 fL), normal leukocyte and platelet counts, erythrocyte sedimentation rate <1 mm/h, normal B12, folate, iron studies, haptoglobin 269 mg/dL, and normal coagulation parameters. Antiphospholipid and lupus sero

title: Dissecting aneurysm of sinus of Valsalva into interventricular septum and rupturing into left ventricle through multiple sinuses: a rare case report
case_summary:
A 24-year-old man with no prior medical history presented with 6 months of fatigue and exertional palpitations and 8 days of chest pain. On examination, peripheral pulses were bounding and collapsing, and prominent carotid pulsations (Corrigan's sign) were noted. Blood pressure was 110/40 mmHg in both arms. The cardiac apex was displaced laterally and hyperdynamic. A palpable diastolic thrill was present in the left parasternal area, and a high-pitched, loud pan-diastolic murmur was heard best at the left parasternal border when sitting and leaning forward.  
Electrocardiography showed left bundle branch block, left-axis deviation, left ventricular enlargement, and Q waves in the lateral leads, consistent with volume overload. A chest radiograph demonstrated cardiomegaly with a left ventricular-type apex.  
Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a dilated left ventricle with severe systolic dysfunction (ejection fraction 20%) and global hypokinesia. In the parasternal long-axis view w

title: Cannabis-induced recurrent myocardial infarction in a 21-year-old man: a case report
case_summary:
A 21-year-old man presented with 2 h of constant substernal chest tightness radiating to the left shoulder, unrelated to respiration. He denied dyspnea. His heart rate was 67 bpm, blood pressure 130/75 mmHg, jugular venous pressure was normal, and there was no peripheral edema. Cardiac auscultation was unremarkable, and lung fields were clear. ECG showed sinus rhythm with ST-segment elevation in the precordial leads and leads II, III, and aVF. Initial troponin was 0.239 ng/mL (normal <0.1 ng/mL) and creatine kinase 469 U/L (peak 1563 U/L; normal <169 U/L). He received aspirin, heparin, and a ticagrelor loading dose. 

Coronary angiography revealed a thrombotic subtotal occlusion of the left anterior descending artery at the first diagonal branch take-off, with no other lesions or atherosclerotic changes in the remaining vessels. Thrombus aspiration and glycoprotein IIb/IIIa inhibitor infusion failed to restore full flow, so a 4.0 ? 18 mm drug-eluting stent was implanted with FilterWire

title: Isolated left ventricular apical hypoplasia: case report
case_summary:
A 24-year-old man presented to the emergency department with a 2-day history of persistent palpitations and dyspnea. He reported intermittent palpitations for 1 year, increasing in frequency over the past 2 months, and new exertional dyspnea. He had no prior medical history; he had used cannabis, alcohol, and cocaine but had discontinued all substances 2 months earlier. Family history was unremarkable.

On examination, he was hemodynamically stable (blood pressure 128/80 mmHg) with an irregular tachycardia and bilateral crackles. ECG showed atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and non-specific intraventricular conduction disturbance. Chest radiography confirmed pulmonary congestion. Laboratory tests, including a thyroid profile, were normal.

He received intravenous furosemide and deslanoside with clinical improvement. The next day, transthoracic echocardiography revealed:
? Severe left ventricular systolic dysfunction (ejection fraction 17%)  
? Elevated filling pressur

title: A Case of Congenitally Corrected Transposition of the Great Arteries Discovered on Coronary Computed Tomography
final_diagnosis: congenitally corrected transposition of the great arteries
case_summary: A 33?year?old man with a history of percutaneous closure of a secundum atrial septal defect in 2008 was referred for evaluation of valvular heart disease. A transthoracic echocardiogram performed for a murmur was technically difficult because of mesocardia and revealed grossly normal left ventricular wall motion, moderate posteriorly directed eccentric mitral regurgitation, mild prolapse of the anterior mitral leaflet, mild aortic insufficiency, and an echogenic structure contiguous to the lateral aspect of the tricuspid annulus; the right atrium and ventricle were poorly visualized. The patient reported overall well?being but experienced fatigue after walking 200-400 meters. An exercise stress test on a standard Bruce protocol lasted 12 minutes 26 seconds, achieving 14.2 METS and 96% of the age?predicted maximal heart rate; he had no chest pain or ischemic ECG changes. A chest radiograph suggested possible right ventricle on the left side of the chest, raising concern for dextrocardia

title: Beyond Cardiology: A Rare Encounter With Small Bowel Obstruction Disguised as Inferior Wall ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction
case_summary:
A 77-year-old White woman presented with chest pain, abdominal discomfort, nausea, and vomiting for one day. Her medical history included bioprosthetic aortic valve replacement for calcific aortic stenosis, hyperlipidemia, hypothyroidism, and obstructive sleep apnea. The chest pain was intermittent, nonexertional, and pressure-like, accompanied by four episodes of nonbloody bilious vomiting and dull, poorly localized abdominal discomfort. Vital signs were notable only for a blood pressure of 175/84 mm Hg. On examination, she appeared ill and diaphoretic. A systolic murmur was heard at the aortic area. The abdomen was tender diffusely without peritoneal signs, and bowel sounds were present. 

An initial electrocardiogram showed ST-segment elevations in leads II, III, and aVF with Q waves and reciprocal ST-segment depressions in leads I and aVL. High-sensitivity troponin I was 111 ng/L (normal < 54 ng/L). She was given aspirin, unfractionated heparin, ticagrelor, and a high-intensity sta

title: Remaining challenges in the diagnosis of early stage cardiac sarcoidosis
case_summary: A 73-year-old woman with chronic hypertension presented with a one-week history of gradually progressive exertional dyspnea and episodic lightheadedness. On arrival her blood pressure was 147/73 mm Hg and heart rate was 39 beats per minute. Physical examination revealed jugular venous distention, a holosystolic heart murmur, and bilateral coarse crackles. An electrocardiogram showed complete atrioventricular block without ischemic changes. She was not taking any medications known to cause atrioventricular block, and initial blood tests showed no electrolyte abnormalities. Cardiac biomarkers were elevated with troponin-I of 269.7 pg/mL (reference <26.0) and brain natriuretic peptide of 265.2 pg/mL (reference <18.4). Chest radiography demonstrated stage II bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy and pulmonary edema. A transvenous temporary pacing lead was inserted, and the patient was admitted to cardiology. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed no regional wall motion abnormalities or basal i

title: The electrocardiographic 'triangular QRS-ST-T waveform' pattern: a marker of severe haemodynamic compromise in Takotsubo syndrome--a case report
case_summary:
A 67-year-old woman with no prior medical history experienced emotional stress at a family funeral. Four days later, she developed sudden-onset chest pain and shortness of breath. In the local health centre, her blood pressure fell to 60/30 mmHg, and troponin-T was elevated. The ECG demonstrated widespread ST-elevations in the anterior and inferior leads consistent with Grade 3 ischaemia. Lung auscultation revealed fine crepitant rales; cardiac examination was otherwise unremarkable. She received ticagrelor, enoxaparin, and aspirin and was transferred for primary percutaneous coronary intervention.  

On arrival at the hospital, she remained hypotensive (60/30 mmHg) with an oxygen saturation of 75% and continued to report intense chest pain. The ECG showed extensive ST-elevations in all precordial leads and the inferior leads, with a triangular (lambda-shaped) appearance of the QRS-ST-T waveform in leads II, III, aVF, and V3-V6. Telemetry revealed repeated episodes of non-sustained ven

title: Constrictive pericarditis following atrial fibrillation ablation - A rare complication
case_summary: A 61-year-old man with persistent atrial fibrillation refractory to antiarrhythmic therapy underwent pulmonary vein isolation with roofline and cavotricuspid isthmus ablation. He had an uncomplicated immediate postprocedural course and was discharged on dabigatran. Approximately 3 months later, he presented with progressive dyspnea. His electrocardiogram showed low QRS voltage in the limb leads. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated preserved biventricular function and a mild circumferential pericardial effusion measuring 8 mm, without signs of hemodynamic compromise. Several examinations were performed for exclusion of phrenic nerve palsy, pulmonary vein stenosis, and anemia. Because of the possibility of effusive pericarditis, the patient was treated with colchicine and ibuprofen, and anticoagulation was continued. Over the next several months, his symptoms progressed, with clinical features of right-sided heart failure. A repeat transthoracic echocardiogram performed 9 months

title: Reversion of Severe Mitral Insufficiency in Peripartum Cardiomyopathy Using Levosimendan
case_summary:
A 35-year-old woman, 12 days after cesarean delivery for her first pregnancy, presented with a 3-day history of progressive shortness of breath, leg swelling, and orthopnea. Her prenatal course had been uncomplicated, and she had no known cardiac history. On examination, her pulse was 115 beats per minute, blood pressure 140/98 mm Hg, respiratory rate 18 breaths per minute, and she appeared pale. Cardiopulmonary auscultation was normal. Abdominal examination revealed a Pfannenstiel scar with serosanguineous drainage; the uterine fundus was palpable below the umbilicus. There was grade II pitting edema of the lower extremities; the neurologic examination was normal.

Laboratory studies showed hemoglobin 8.8 g/dL, hematocrit 28%, white-cell count 11,370/mm3, platelet count 645,000/mm3, and D-dimer 4722 ?g/dL; prothrombin time and thrombin clotting time were normal. Electrocardiography demonstrated sinus tachycardia with repolarization abnormalities. Chest radiography was unremarkable. Cl

title: Oliguric acute kidney injury as a main symptom of bradycardia and arteriosclerosis resolved by pacemaker implantation: a case report
case_summary:
A 76-year-old man was admitted with 3 days of nausea, vomiting, and dizziness. His history included non-alcoholic fatty liver disease-related cirrhosis (Child-Pugh A), type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and suspected Alzheimer-type dementia. Two years earlier, syncope had been attributed to hypovolemia; Holter monitoring then showed first-degree AV block and bradycardia down to 37 bpm, and AKI was ascribed to pre-renal causes. Medications on admission included spironolactone, propranolol, donepezil, amlodipine, and insulin.

On admission: blood pressure 150/80 mmHg, heart rate 62 bpm, temperature normal. Physical examination was unremarkable; no ascites or edema; he was oriented but psychomotor slowing was noted. Labs: serum creatinine elevated from a baseline of 139 ?mol/L to values consistent with AKI; mild transaminase elevations; albumin 31 g/L; normal inflammatory markers. Arterial blood gas showed metabolic acidosis and partial respiratory insufficiency. Urine microscopy revealed mic

title: Pseudo cardiac tamponade in the setting of excess pericardial fat
case_summary:
A 66-year-old woman presented with acute-onset dyspnea. She was diagnosed at an outside hospital with a non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction complicated by congestive heart failure and was stabilized with parenteral nitroglycerin and noninvasive positive-pressure ventilation. She was referred for coronary angiography.

Her history included type 2 diabetes mellitus, hypertension, smoking, and peripheral vascular disease. Coronary angiography revealed extensive disease, including a 95% lesion of the left main coronary artery; there were no procedural complications, and left ventricular pressure was 90/45 mm Hg.

While recovering, she developed acute dyspnea and tachycardia. Electrocardiography showed sinus tachycardia without low-voltage complexes or recurrent ischemia. Chest radiography demonstrated pulmonary edema, and she received intravenous furosemide and nitroglycerin.

An urgent transthoracic echocardiogram showed a mildly dilated left ventricle, severe global systolic dysfuncti

title: Successful Triplet Pregnancy Post-Allogeneic Stem Cell Transplant in a Patient With Doxorubicin-Induced Cardiomyopathy
case_summary: A 27-year-old Hispanic woman with a history of nasal-type natural killer/T-cell lymphoma treated 12 years earlier with 8 cycles of CHOP chemotherapy (cumulative doxorubicin dose 320 mg/m2) and mediastinal radiation therapy presented with a 2-3-month history of fever, cough, shortness of breath, and a 20-lb weight loss. She denied chest pain, palpitations, edema, or claudication. Physical examination was unremarkable: blood pressure 108/57 mm Hg, pulse 86 beats/min, respiratory rate 20 breaths/min, oxygen saturation 100% on room air, normal jugular venous pressure, clear lungs, no murmurs or gallops, and no peripheral edema. Initial laboratory tests, including cardiac enzymes, B-type natriuretic peptide, thyroid-stimulating hormone, and iron studies, were within normal limits. Chest imaging revealed moderate bilateral pleural effusions. Thoracentesis with flow cytometry confirmed lymphoma relapse. A pre-treatment transthoracic echocardiogram showed a left ventricular ejection fraction o

title: A case report of acute heart failure and cardiogenic shock caused by catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome and lupus myocarditis
case_summary: A 47-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus, antiphospholipid syndrome on warfarin, chronic kidney disease, and recent rapid weight loss after bariatric surgery was transferred for cardiogenic shock. Six months earlier her ejection fraction was 50% on echocardiography. Two weeks before transfer, warfarin was held after ureteral stent placement for nephrolithiasis complicated by a large perinephric hematoma; vitamin K, fresh frozen plasma, and red cells were given, and a pseudoaneurysm was treated with thrombin. Eleven days later she developed chest pain, dyspnea, and a cardiac arrest requiring intubation and epinephrine. Post-arrest ECG showed a new left bundle branch block. Emergent catheterization revealed nonobstructive coronary arteries and a ventriculogram-estimated ejection fraction of 15%. An intra-aortic balloon pump was placed and later upgraded to an Impella 5.0. Continuous norepinephrine, epinephrine, and vasopressin were initiated. Repeat echocardiography confirme

title: Cardiac calcified amorphous tumor as a potential cause of cerebral infarction: A clinical case report
case_summary:
A 62-year-old man on maintenance hemodialysis for 11 years was referred after multiple episodes of syncope and retrograde amnesia. He was asymptomatic on arrival. His medical history included hypertension, type 2 diabetes, dyslipidemia, peripheral arterial disease, bilateral carotid stenosis, and prior coronary artery bypass grafting. Medications included clopidogrel, amlodipine, valsartan, rosuvastatin, and linagliptin.  
Vital signs were stable. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations were unremarkable, and there were no focal neurologic deficits. Laboratory tests revealed hemoglobin 9.6 g/dL, BUN 45.5 mg/dL, creatinine 13.9 mg/dL; white blood cell count, platelets, electrolytes, C-reactive protein, and coagulation studies were within normal limits.  
Brain MRI showed multiple small subcortical infarctions in both hemispheres on diffusion?weighted imaging. ECG demonstrated normal sinus rhythm without arrhythmia. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a 2.5 cm highly echogenic ma

title: High output heart failure caused by a large pelvic arteriovenous malformation
case_summary: A 73-year-old woman was referred for mild breathlessness on exertion. She denied chest pain, fluid retention, orthopnoea, and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnoea. Her past history was significant for infra-renal abdominal aortic aneurysm repair 15 years earlier. On examination, she was in atrial fibrillation at 64 bpm, normotensive, and without pedal edema. Cardiovascular examination revealed a significantly elevated jugular venous pressure with systolic V waves, a pansystolic murmur at the left lower sternal edge, and a systolic flow murmur in the pulmonary area. Lung fields were clear. A continuous loud bruit was auscultated over the lower abdomen. Laboratory studies showed normocytic anaemia (hemoglobin 10.4 g/dL) with normal liver and renal function tests. The electrocardiogram confirmed rate-controlled atrial fibrillation without ischaemic changes. A chest radiograph demonstrated massive cardiomegaly. Transthoracic echocardiography showed normal left ventricular systolic and diastolic f

title: A fiery heart: case report of perimyocarditis in a patient with eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis
case_summary:
A 37-year-old African American woman, 5 months postpartum, with a history of hypertension, asthma, and sickle cell trait presented to the emergency department with palpitations and dyspnea of one day's duration. She reported baseline exercise tolerance of 7-8 blocks but now became short of breath after a few steps. She also experienced orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, and malaise. Her home medications--nifedipine, loratadine, and fluticasone--were unchanged.

Upon arrival, she described sharp, non-exertional substernal chest pain worsened by inspiration and movement. Vital signs showed normotension and tachycardia. Examination revealed fine crackles at both lung bases without peripheral edema.

Laboratory studies revealed a white blood cell count of 16 300/uL with 32% eosinophils, troponin I of 5.183 ng/mL, and brain natriuretic peptide of 1099 pg/mL. ESR was 59 mm/h and CRP was 5.55 mg/dL. Comprehensive metabolic panel, liver function, coagulation, and thyroid tests were norma

title: Electrocardiographic changes in right ventricular metastatic cardiac tumor mimicking acute ST elevation myocardial infarction: A case of misdiagnosis
case_summary: A 59-year-old man presented with acute onset of chest pain. Two years earlier, he had undergone complete resection and postoperative radiotherapy for tongue cancer, with no recurrence over the past three years. He had no history of diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. On arrival, he was in no distress. Vital signs were: blood pressure 130/75 mmHg, pulse 75 beats/min, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, temperature 36.7  degreesC, and oxygen saturation 94% on room air. Cardiac and pulmonary examinations were unremarkable. A 12-lead ECG showed sinus rhythm with ST-segment elevation in leads V1 through V6, findings compatible with acute anterior wall myocardial infarction. He underwent immediate coronary angiography, which revealed only noncritical stenoses in the coronary arteries. A chest radiograph showed clear lung fields and a mildly dilated cardiac silhouette.

title: Acute kidney injury due to?myoglobin cast nephropathy in the setting of coronavirus disease 2019-mediated rhabdomyolysis: a case report
case_summary:
A 67-year-old man with history of noninvasive papillary urothelial carcinoma, atrial fibrillation on warfarin, surgically corrected aortic stenosis with bioprosthetic valve, CAD status post CABG, and severe emphysematous COPD presented in mid-March 2021 with 3 weeks of malaise and poor oral intake. Over several days he noted dysgeusia, anorexia, generalized fatigue, myalgias, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, and oliguria (no urine for 3 days). He denied fever, cough, dyspnea, chest pain, or edema. Three months earlier he had been hospitalized for valve replacement and CABG complicated by shock, COPD exacerbation, and malnutrition; he had returned home a few weeks before this presentation. He had quit smoking 9 years ago; no alcohol or substance use; and had received one dose of mRNA-1273 COVID-19 vaccine one month earlier.

On arrival: temperature 97.6  degreesF, blood pressure 86/44 mmHg, heart rate 73 bpm, oxygen saturation 92% on room air. He appeared chronically ill; oral mucosa was dry

title: Iatrogenic aortic valve fenestration rupture following coronary angiography: a case report
case_summary:
A 66-year-old man with sleep apnoea and a history of junctional tachycardia ablation presented with acute dyspnoea shortly after undergoing urgent percutaneous coronary intervention for an inferior ST-elevation myocardial infarction. His medications included aspirin, clopidogrel, bisoprolol, atorvastatin, and ramipril. Initial examination prior to PCI was unremarkable, and transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) showed no valvular abnormalities. Coronary angiography revealed a complete occlusion of the distal right coronary artery, which was stented without difficulty, despite a shepherd's crook configuration of the proximal vessel.

Soon after the procedure, he developed shortness of breath and a short decrescendo early diastolic aortic murmur. NT-pro-brain natriuretic peptide was 1784 pg/mL. Chest radiograph was normal, and CT angiography excluded dissection and pulmonary embolism. Repeat TTE showed new moderate-to-severe aortic regurgitation (vena contracta 0.6 cm; jet >50% of outflow

title: Hydroxychloroquine-induced cardiomyopathy: role of cardiac magnetic resonance for the diagnosis and follow-up of a very rare entity--a case report
case_summary:
A 45-year-old woman with systemic lupus erythematosus presented with palpitations and shortness of breath for 2 weeks. Her history included rheumatic mitral valve disease treated with mechanical mitral valve replacement and tricuspid annuloplasty 6 years earlier, and a transient ischaemic attack 2 weeks ago. Current medications were hydroxychloroquine 400 mg/day (cumulative dose 2774 g), warfarin, prednisone, amiodarone, colchicine, and azathioprine. 

On examination, prosthetic valve sounds were normal and bilateral lung crackles were present. A chest radiograph showed pulmonary congestion. Laboratory tests revealed an N-terminal pro-brain natriuretic peptide peak of 16 040 pg/mL, high-sensitivity troponin I 597 ng/L (ULN < 54 ng/L), INR 2.7, and C-reactive protein 4.9 mg/L (ULN < 5.0 mg/L); biomarkers of lupus activity were normal. ECG showed atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and right bundle branch block. She was diagnosed with acute heart failure and received high

title: Exercise-induced right ventricular cardiomyopathy in an endurance cyclist: a case report
case_summary:
A 61-year-old man who cycles 40-60 miles continuously several times per week presented in 2014 with palpitations occurring at the end of a cycling session. He had no significant dyspnea or chest pain. An ambulatory ECG showed short runs of focal atrial tachycardia and unifocal ectopy, and no further investigation was undertaken as symptoms resolved.

In 2019, he returned with recurrent palpitations associated with exercise and mild lower-limb swelling. He denied presyncope, and exercise capacity was preserved. There was no family history of heart disease or sudden death. Resting ECG showed sinus rhythm with frequent unifocal ventricular ectopy of left bundle-branch morphology and inferior axis. A 5-day Holter monitor captured non-sustained ventricular tachycardia, sinus pauses up to 5.2 seconds, and runs of narrow-complex tachycardia, though he remained asymptomatic during monitoring.

He underwent an exercise treadmill test to 94% of target heart rate (10 minutes on Bruce protocol), d

title: A rare differential diagnosis of aortic stenosis with a black aortic valve: A case report
case_summary:
A 60-year-old woman with hypertension presented with headache and dizziness. On examination, her heart rate was 82 bpm, blood pressure 157/70 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 100%. A grade 4/6 ejection systolic murmur radiating to the carotid arteries was auscultated.  
Preoperative evaluation:  
? Chest radiograph: clear lung fields, cardiothoracic ratio 50%  
? ECG: sinus rhythm at 90 bpm, left ventricular hypertrophy  
? Transthoracic echocardiography: tricuspid aortic valve thickened and heavily calcified, peak-to-peak gradient 113/71 mmHg, ejection fraction 73%, concentric left ventricular hypertrophy, mild mitral and tricuspid regurgitation, aortic annulus 17 mm  
She was scheduled for elective aortic valve replacement with a bioprosthetic valve and possible aortic root enlargement due to the small annulus.  
Intraoperatively, the ascending aorta appeared calcified with unusual green-black discoloration of the adventitia. Transesophageal echocardiography showed no evidence of aortic d

title: Cardiac angiosarcoma in the right ventricle treated by surgical resection
case_summary: An 81-year-old Japanese woman presented with chest pain at rest for one month. She had a history of papillary thyroid carcinoma treated surgically 30 years earlier and was followed for psychiatric problems and hypothyroidism. On admission, blood pressure was 125/81 mm Hg, heart rate 78 beats per minute, and oxygen saturation 95% on room air. Physical examination revealed no heart murmurs, jugular venous distention, or pedal edema. An electrocardiogram showed sinus rhythm with an incomplete right bundle branch block. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated a heterogeneous, irregular mass-like lesion measuring approximately 45?40 mm on the right ventricular free wall, contacting the tricuspid valve in diastole, with a small pericardial effusion. Transesophageal echocardiography showed part of the mass was mobile. Contrast-enhanced computed tomography revealed a 51 mm lobular mass involving the right ventricle and pericardium with heterogeneous enhancement. Cardiac magnetic resonance

title: The role of hydroxychloroquine in catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome case: Series of two case reports and review of literature
case_summary: A 32-year-old woman with a history of epilepsy and recurrent abortions presented with 5 days of progressive dry cough, exertional dyspnea, and bilateral leg swelling. She denied fever, hemoptysis, chest pain, joint symptoms, or rash. On examination she was alert only to person, mildly febrile (max 38.5 degreesC), in mild respiratory distress, with a macular erythematous malar rash, pale conjunctivae, a grade II systolic murmur at the apex, bibasilar crackles, and bilateral pitting edema. Initial laboratory studies revealed hemoglobin 6.0 g/dL, MCV 81 fL, platelets 75 ? 10^3/uL, creatinine 253 umol/L, BUN 18.4 mmol/L, nephrotic-range proteinuria (4.81 g/L), aPTT 66.0 s, D-dimer 2.96 mg/L FEU, LDH 351 U/L, CRP 30 mg/L, and procalcitonin 0.83 ng/mL. Chest radiography showed bilateral lower-zone infiltrates. She received IV antibiotics, fluids, and two units of PRBCs. A peripheral smear showed microcytic hypochromic anemia and thrombocytopenia. Complement levels were low (C3 23.7 mg/dL, C4

title: Pleuritis with pleural effusion due to a Bacillus megaterium infection
case_summary:
A 77-year-old man was evaluated for acute dyspnea and tachyarrhythmia. He had a 150 pack-year smoking history and past occupations as a tailor and metalworker. His medical history included hypertension (on olmesartan), partial gastrectomy for peptic ulcer, and a recent stroke treated with aspirin. He denied heart disease, diabetes, fever, cough, or chest pain. 

On presentation, heart rate was 166 beats per minute in atrial fibrillation, blood pressure 120/80 mmHg, and arterial blood gas showed PaO? 57 mmHg with normal PaCO? and pH. He reported several months of exertional dyspnea. Laboratory tests revealed macrocytic anemia (hemoglobin 11 g/dL, MCV 104 fL), leukocytes 10.5 ? 10?/L (80% neutrophils), and CRP 28.7 mg/L. An electrocardiogram confirmed paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, managed with amiodarone and anticoagulation.

Chest radiography showed a right, organized pleural effusion. Chest CT confirmed a right pleural effusion with thickening of the right parietal and mediastinal pl

title: Trivalvular nonbacterial thrombotic endocarditis in a patient with colon adenocarcinoma: a case report
case_summary: A 54-year-old woman with known metastatic colon adenocarcinoma presented with left proximal deep venous thrombosis and dyspnea on exertion (NYHA class II). Three months earlier, she had started a modified FOLFOX6 chemotherapy regimen; she had no prior cardiac or pulmonary disease. On examination, blood pressure was 136/85 mm Hg, pulse 76/min, respiratory rate 17/min, temperature 37.1  degreesC, and oxygen saturation 98% on room air. There was left lower-extremity edema. Lung auscultation was clear. Cardiac examination revealed a 2/6 early-diastolic murmur at the left lower sternal border, unchanged from prior exams. Laboratory studies showed WBC 7900/?L, hemoglobin 7.3 g/dL, platelets 171 ? 10?/?L, D-dimer 36 400 ng/mL, creatinine 1.0 mg/dL, and C-reactive protein 5.26 mg/dL. Blood cultures were negative. Brain CT was unremarkable. Pulmonary CT angiography revealed a thrombus in the left lower lobe pulmonary artery. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated multiple vegetations on th

title: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation as a Possible Cause of Acute Coronary Stent Thrombosis: A Case Report and Literature Review
case_summary:
A 64-year-old Caucasian man presented with one day of left arm pain and numbness. He was not taking any medications and had no family history of coronary artery disease or sudden death. Physical examination was unremarkable. An electrocardiogram showed normal sinus rhythm with T-wave inversions in leads II, III, and aVF; chest radiography was normal. He was started on aspirin, intravenous heparin, nitroglycerin, and eptifibatide. Initial troponin I was 0.47?ng/mL, rising to 2.28?ng/mL, and he underwent left heart catheterization. Angiography of the right coronary artery revealed >90% stenosis, and a drug-eluting stent was placed with TIMI grade III flow. He received a 600-mg loading dose of clopidogrel. 

Fifteen hours later, he developed left-sided chest pain and ST-segment elevations in leads II, III, and aVF. Repeat catheterization showed acute thrombosis of the stent; thrombectomy was performed and a second drug-eluting stent was placed. His platelet count fell from 145,000/uL to 7

title: Acute Myocardial Injury in a Patient with Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder and History of Substance Abuse: A Multimodality Imaging Point of View
journal: Journal of Cardiovascular Development and Disease
case_summary:
A 39-year-old man was admitted with 6 hours of severe chest pain. He has a 14-year history of amphetamine and cannabis abuse and is prescribed lisdexamfetamine 70 mg daily for ADHD. He is a non-smoker, with no diabetes or hypertension, and a BMI of 33 kg/m2. On admission, heart rate was 65 bpm and blood pressure 123/78 mmHg. ECG showed minor ST-segment elevations in the anterior leads. Initial cardiac troponin T was 319 ng/L (normal < 16 ng/L), rising to 1148 ng/L at 4 hours and peaking at 2035 ng/L the next day before declining to 19 ng/L at discharge. Pro-BNP was 420 ng/L on admission, peaked at 2648 ng/L the following day, and fell to 992 ng/L before discharge.

Urgent coronary angiography revealed normal epicardial coronary arteries. Transthoracic echocardiography showed a mildly reduced left ventricular ejection fraction (48%) with hypokinesis of the apex and distal lateral and inferior walls; global longitudinal strain was reduced in these segments.
