title: Inverted left atrial appendage mimicking a left atrial mass after surgical repair of an atrial septal defect: a case report
case_summary:
A 3-year-old Caucasian girl was referred for surgical repair of an incidentally discovered large secundum atrial septal defect. She was asymptomatic, with a soft systolic murmur at the left lower sternal border and an oxygen saturation of 99% on room air. Chest radiography showed cardiomegaly and pulmonary plethora. Transthoracic echocardiography revealed a 14?12 mm multi-fenestrated atrial septal defect with significant left-to-right shunting, right ventricular volume overload with normal function, no pulmonary hypertension, no valvular pathology, and no intracardiac masses.

Under median sternotomy, cardiopulmonary bypass was established (34  degreesC) with aortic and bicaval cannulation and vacuum-assisted venous drainage. The aorta was cross-clamped, and cold blood cardioplegia was administered. During right atriotomy, air was noted in the inferior vena cava cannula, which was repositioned before completing the pericardial patch closure of the defect. After de-airing and cross-clam

title: Acute esophageal necrosis masquerading acute coronary syndrome
case_summary: An 80-year-old man with hypertension, dyslipidemia, and poorly controlled diabetes mellitus presented with a 1-hour history of right-sided, nonradiating, 10/10 chest pain accompanied by nausea and two episodes of maroon-colored emesis. He denied toxic ingestions or prior similar episodes. On arrival, he was afebrile; blood pressure 109/45 mm Hg; heart rate 75 beats/min; respiratory rate 18 breaths/min; SpO? 98% on room air. Physical examination was unremarkable. Laboratory studies showed sodium 125 mEq/L, potassium 5.1 mEq/L, chloride 91 mEq/L, bicarbonate 24 mEq/L, blood urea nitrogen 145.7 mg/dL, creatinine 2.95 mg/dL, blood glucose 492 mg/dL, WBC 13.5 ? 10?/mm?, hemoglobin 12.3 g/dL, platelets 242 000/mm?, BNP 154 pg/mL, and troponin 0.062 ng/mL. ECG revealed normal sinus rhythm at 94 beats/min without ST-segment or T-wave abnormalities. The patient received morphine and nitroglycerin without relief. Given persistent chest pain and risk factors for ischemic heart disease, he was sta

title: Biventricular thrombosis and survival after two different mechanical supports before heart transplantation
case_summary:
A 25-year-old man presents with 12 hours of precordial discomfort and dyspnea.  
Vital signs: blood pressure 132/76 mm Hg, heart rate 98 beats/min, respiratory rate 28 breaths/min, afebrile.  
Physical examination is normal.  
Electrocardiogram shows nonspecific ST-segment abnormalities; troponin is elevated.  
He receives aspirin, ticagrelor, and intravenous heparin for presumed non-ST-elevation myocardial infarction.  
Because of persistent severe chest pain, a CT scan of the thorax is performed and reveals a large ascending aortic aneurysm measuring 12.8 cm from the aortic valve to 7.5 cm before the brachiocephalic trunk, with right coronary artery compression.  
Transthoracic echocardiography shows severe aortic regurgitation.  
Transesophageal echocardiography confirms a bicuspid aortic valve, ascending aortic aneurysm with severe regurgitation, left ventricular ejection fraction of 20%, and possible intimal flap.  
He undergoes a Bentall procedure with a mechanical valve conduit.

title: Pulmonary embolism mimicking acute myocardial infarction: a case report and review of literature
case_summary: A 37-year-old woman with no past medical history presented with continuous chest pain for two hours. She denied dyspnea, syncope, or prior similar episodes. There was no personal or family history of cardiovascular or thromboembolic disease. Vital signs were: pulse 110/min, blood pressure 100/65 mmHg, respiratory rate 20/min, temperature 37  degreesC, oxygen saturation 95% on room air (99% on 2 L/min nasal cannula). Cardiac auscultation was normal, and neck veins were not distended. Initial ECG showed sinus rhythm with 2 mm of ST-segment elevation in leads V2 and V3. She was given aspirin, clopidogrel, and intravenous heparin and taken to the catheterization laboratory. Twenty minutes later, a second ECG showed a new right bundle branch block and resolution of the precordial ST-segment elevations. Forty-five minutes after presentation, coronary angiography revealed normal coronary arteries without stenosis. Transthoracic echocardiography demonstrated right ventricular dilatation and an

title: Aorta-right atrium tunnel and mitral valve prolapse complicated with flail leaflet: a case report of a multimodality approach for an accurate diagnosis
case_summary:
A 46-year-old man presented with palpitations and dyspnea on mild exertion. He had been noted to have an asymptomatic systolic murmur at age 17 but was lost to follow-up. On admission, he had dyspnea with minimal exertion and mild ankle swelling. Cardiac examination revealed an apical systolic murmur (grade 4/6) and a continuous murmur (grade 3/6) at the right parasternal border. Vital signs were: heart rate 85 bpm, blood pressure 125/80 mmHg, and oxygen saturation 97% on room air. Lung fields were clear, and the chest radiograph was unremarkable. Routine blood tests were normal except for an elevated NT-proBNP of 700 pg/mL. ECG showed sinus rhythm with premature supraventricular beats, left atrial enlargement, and right ventricular hypertrophy.
